query: Why is the idea of infinity such a hard concept to fathom ? I understand the idea of infinity but my brain keeps insisting that there is a boundary somewhere . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Our ability to fathom things is finite . Good luck using something finite to conceptualize something that is n't :)
Have you ever tried considering , nothing . I mean seriously , nothing .
We live in a finite environment . We can only see a certain distance that makes sense to us ( or even beyond that , but still very much finite ) . The food we eat is finite , the lives we live and the monuments we erect - all finite . The trouble with something that is not finite , is that we have no frame at all as to what that is . If you think about it , we have trouble visualizing how many glasses of water a tub would hold . Then picture a lake . Then a larger lake . Then the pacific ocean . We are so thoroughly lost in these abstract concepts because it does not matter to us . We are animals . This is where we came from and it is how evolution has shaped us . If we are walking around in the forest all we really need to know is as far as we can see and as far as we can hear . We have never had a reason to grasp something really big .
rejected: The idea of infinity is hard for many people to understand because it is very different from the things we experience in our everyday lives. In the real world, there are always limits and boundaries. For example, we can only count up to a certain number, and then we run out of numbers and have to start over at one. But infinity is something that goes on forever, without any end or limit. It can be hard to wrap our heads around this concept because it is so different from anything we experience in our daily lives.

query: Suggest treatment for pain and burning sensation in the ankleHello, I have been experiencing really bad pain in my right ankle for the last 2 months. It seems like it has gotten worse. its a throbbing and burning pain and in the morning it is very hard to walk on and it is swollen. it hurts to walk. I was just wondering what it might be.
chosen: Hi, It can be ankle sprain most probably developed due to trivial trauma or slipping. As the first line of management, you can take analgesics like Diclofenac for pain relief. Apply ice packs also. If pain persists, better to consult an orthopedician and get an MRI scan done. Hope I have answered your query. Let me know if I can assist you further. Regards,Dr. Shinas Hussain,General & Family Physician
rejected:
I'm sorry to hear that you've been experiencing pain and a burning sensation in your ankle. It's important to see a healthcare professional for an accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment. There are many possible causes of pain and a burning sensation in the ankle, such as an ankle sprain, a fracture, inflammation of the tendons or joints, nerve irritation, or an infection.

In the meantime, you can try the following to help manage your symptoms:

Rest your ankle: Avoid activities that put weight or pressure on your ankle, such as walking or running.

Elevate your ankle: Keep your ankle elevated above the level of your heart to reduce swelling and inflammation.

Use ice: Apply an ice pack wrapped in a towel to your ankle for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours to reduce swelling and numb the pain.

Wear supportive shoes: Wear shoes with good arch support and a firm heel to help reduce strain on your ankle.

Take over-the-counter pain medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or acetaminophen can help reduce pain and swelling.

It's also important to see a healthcare professional as soon as possible to determine the cause of your ankle pain and receive proper treatment. They may recommend additional treatment options, such as physical therapy or prescribed medications.

query: Why did the U.S. ever enter WW1 ? I know it sounds simple but I ca nt find a good and concise answer in the encyclopedia Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The largest contributing factors were Germany attacking US trade vessels at sea and Germany trying to bait Mexico into joining the war with the promise of giving them Texas , New Mexico , and Arizona .
The telegram that Germany sent to Mexico was the Zimmermann Telegram , in case you wanted the name . German U - Boats were also sinking US ships , killing Americans .
Because German U - boats sunk several US ships ( most famous Lucitania ) . The question remains why those ships were sent into waters known to have Uboat activity and why were they carying ammunition since US has bees declared neutrality .
rejected:
The United States entered World War 1 for a number of reasons. 
One reason was that the war had been going on for several years, and the U.S. wanted to help bring it to an end. The war had caused a lot of destruction and loss of life, and the U.S. believed it could make a positive contribution by joining the fight. 
Another reason was that Germany, one of the countries fighting in the war, was sinking ships that were carrying goods to the United Kingdom, which was an ally of the United States. This made it difficult for the U.K. to get the supplies it needed to continue fighting, and the U.S. wanted to help protect its ally. 
Finally, the U.S. was also motivated by the idea of protecting democracy. At the time, Germany was ruled by an autocratic government, while the U.S. and many of the other countries fighting in the war were democracies. The U.S. wanted to help defend democracy and prevent autocracy from spreading. 
So, in short, the U.S. entered World War 1 to help bring an end to the conflict, protect its ally the United Kingdom, and defend democracy.

query: How the Internet travels between countries Basically , how does my email travel to Japan if there is no wiring or wireless ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
[ There is wiring ] ( URL_0 )
There are these pipes under ground and under sea . When you put stuff in pipe in your house , it goes under the sea , in the pipes , to other side of the sea . then the recipient gets it to his pipe and reads it . Pipe is the line where bits go , the wiring . Stuff in pipe is data , like email . There is wiring , you just do n't see it .
If you want a deep dive into how undersea cables work from a physical , political , and economic perspective , check out [ this ] ( URL_0 ) .
rejected: The Internet is a vast network of interconnected computers and servers that can communicate with each other. When you send an email, it travels from your computer to a server that is connected to the Internet. From there, the email is sent through a series of interconnected servers, kind of like a relay race. Each server passes the email along to the next server until it reaches its destination. So if you send an email from the United States to Japan, the email would first travel to a server in the US that is connected to the Internet. Then it would be passed along to servers in other countries, such as Canada or Russia, until it finally reaches a server in Japan. The servers that the email travels through are connected to each other using a variety of methods, including wires, cables, and even satellites. These connections allow the email to travel from one place to another very quickly, even if the two places are on opposite sides of the world. I hope that helps to explain it! query: Why does n't English have gendered articles when all other languages do ? It seems odd that nearly every other language uses gendered articles in front of their words but English does n't . For instance , Die and Der in German of El and La in Spanish . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
" All " is a bit of a stretch . Lots of languages are ungendered : URL_0
Actually the majority of languages in the world do not have masculine and feminine grammatical genders , many European languages do because they share common roots . Old English had grammatical genders but it fell out of use starting in the 11th century . No one knows exactly why , there are few surviving texts that old , but one possibility is that it was because of Vikings . Vikings raided England between the 8th to 11th century and many of them actually settled in Northern England . Old Norse , the language spoken by Vikings , had a lot in common with Old English because the two languages have a common ancestor . So the two languages would have been similar but still with a lot of differences . The idea is that as the two languages got mixed together the grammatical genders were lost , and this form of English then slowly spread to the rest of England . English today still contain loan words from Old Norse , the names of several days in the week come from gods in Norse mythology for example . EDIT , the days : Tuesday : [ Týr 's ] ( URL_2 ) day . Wednesday : Wodan 's day , another name for [ Odin ] ( URL_0 ) . Thursday : [ Thor 's ] ( URL_3 ) day . Friday : [ Freyja 's ] ( URL_1 ) day . ( This one is not 100 % certain , there are other pagan gods Friday might be named after , but Freyja is my favourite candidate . )
Old English had three grammatical genders , much like modern German . Turns out grammatical gender is kind of useless ( in terms of aiding understanding ) , and it fell into disuse through Middle English . And now modern English does n't have grammatical gender . A corollary question is : why do so many Western languages bother with grammatical gender ? Edit : unimply causality .
rejected: In English, we don't have gendered articles like "die" and "der" in German or "el" and "la" in Spanish because English doesn't have grammatical gender. Grammatical gender is a system in which every noun is assigned a gender, either masculine, feminine, or neuter, and the gender of a noun affects the way other words in the sentence are inflected or declined. For example, in Spanish, "la casa" means "the house" (feminine), while "el perro" means "the dog" (masculine). The articles "la" and "el" change to match the gender of the noun they are referring to. In English, we don't have to worry about this because we don't have grammatical gender. Instead, we just use the same article "the" for all nouns, no matter what their gender is. Grammatical gender is a feature of some languages, but not all languages have it. English is one of the languages that doesn't have grammatical gender, so we don't have gendered articles like other languages do.

query: how does shorting a stock work ? I know you 're counting on the stock to lose value . I do not know how you can make money doing it . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I 'll sell you stock for ten dollars a share . The catch is , I do n't own them yet . So , I 'll borrow the shares . So I sell you shares I borrowed for $ 10 / share . Eventually , I have to give those shares back from where I borrowed them , though . So , I 'm hoping I can buy them back for less than $ 10 / share . If I succeed , I get to pocket the difference .
1 . borrow a share of stock from someone 2 . sell it 3 . wait for the stock to go down 4 . buy it for less 5 . give the stock back and keep the difference Steps 1 & 2 are kind of a convenient fiction that you broker handles for you , but that is essentially what is happening .
1 . I borrow your car for a year because you 're going to be working abroad 2 . I sell it on eBay for $ 10,000 3 . After a year the car 's value drops to $ 8,000 4 . I buy an identical car to yours for $ 8,000 5 . I give you back the identical car and pocket the $ 2,000 difference You do n't care because you 've got the same car you would have had anyway . The risk being that if your car goes up in value ( because someone discovers it 's absolutely irresistible to beautiful women , or something ) , I might have to buy you a car back for $ 12,000 rather than $ 10,000 , and I 'll lose $ 2,000 instead ! You still do n't care because you 've still got the same car you would 've had anyway . Obviously with the slight difference that with stocks the two really are identical , while with a car you 'd just be getting an identical one .
rejected: Sure! So when you "short" a stock, you are basically betting that the price of the stock is going to go down. Here's how it works:First, you borrow shares of the stock from someone else. Let's say you borrow 100 shares of XYZ stock, which is currently worth $50 per share. That means you have borrowed $5,000 worth of XYZ stock (100 shares x $50 per share).Next, you sell those borrowed shares on the stock market, hoping to be able to buy them back later at a lower price. So you sell the 100 shares of XYZ stock for $5,000, and you get the money in your account.Now, let's say the price of XYZ stock goes down to $40 per share. You can buy back the 100 shares for $4,000 (100 shares x $40 per share).When you buy back the shares, you return them to the person you borrowed them from, and you get to keep the difference between the price you sold them for and the price you bought them back for. In this case, you sold the shares for $5,000 and bought them back for $4,000, so you get to keep $1,000.So, in short (pun intended), you make money by shorting a stock when the price goes down, because you get to keep the difference between the price you sold the stock for and the price you bought it back for.

query: Zeno 's Paradox ( infinite halves ) * edit : thanks to everyone who commented ! your answers are exceptional . * Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Zeno said motion is impossible because in order to walk from here to there , you must first reach the half - way there point , but to get half - way there you first have to reach quarter - way there , and so on with infinitely smaller fractions , meaning there are infinite steps of getting here to there , so infinity steps means you 'll never get there . Zeno came up with this mind trick long before mathematics solved Zeno 's motion paradox by introducing calculus . Calculus says that just because you can slice a pie into infinitely smaller pieces does not make the pie bigger , it 's just makes the slices smaller .
Another way to look at it : step 1 ) let the destination be the kitchen step 2 ) realize that in order to get to the destination you have to first get half way there step 3 ) set your new destination to half way to the current one step 4 ) go to step 2 You can repeat the whole process infinitely and never figure out how to take the first step in the journey , so you ca n't really go anywhere at all . Yet , we go places all the time . That 's the paradox .
step 1 ) Walk half way to the kitchen step 2 ) Call this your new starting point . step 3 ) add 1 to your step counter step 4 ) goto step 1 . Zeno 's paradox is that you have to complete and infinite number of things , which is impossible .
rejected: Sure! Zeno's Paradox is a thought experiment that raises questions about how we perceive the world and how we can understand things that seem to be infinite. The idea is that if you want to travel a certain distance, you have to first travel half of that distance. But then, before you can reach the other half, you have to travel half of that distance, and so on. So you have to keep traveling an infinite number of smaller and smaller distances, and it seems like you could never actually reach your destination. This might seem confusing, but it's like if you have a piece of paper and you want to fold it in half, but before you can fold it in half, you have to fold each half in half again, and so on. You could keep folding the paper in half forever, but you would never be able to fold it all the way in half. Does that make sense?

query: What happened to the HeartBleed Bug ? Have we just stopped talking about it ? Or has it disappeared ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It was , at the time , a big thing . The company I worked for were aware of it before it hit the news - our IT security people were on it like a rash , and our vendors were also well aware . Within hours of it hitting the news , our customer were asking us what we were doing about it , and we were able to tell them our plans immediately . The answer is that when something like that blows up , the majority of people get it patched almost immediately . Are there servers out there that are still vulnerable ? No doubt . Do you have personal data on some of those servers ? Perhaps , perhaps not . It has n't gone away , but it 's less of an issue than it was , due to diligent IT security and admins who get stuff like this fixed as soon as humanly possible .
It was patched . Most installations replaced their systems with the patched version , so in that way it 's no longer a problem . What is a problem is the exposure of private data that heartbleed made possible for a long time before it was discovered . At my work , and most probably everywhere else , we are still in the middle of a campaign first suggesting , then requiring , then mandating that people change their passwords under penalty of being locked out of the network . We are currently at the " mandating " phase and there are still a few holdouts who are arguing that they just ca n't change their passwords for one reason or another . I wonder how they will feel when they learn that the next step will be mandating that everybody changes all their passwords every month .
It was patched pretty much immediately after it was revealed .
rejected: The Heartbleed bug was a serious security vulnerability that was discovered in April 2014. It affected certain versions of software called "OpenSSL," which is used to secure many websites and other online services. The bug allowed hackers to potentially steal sensitive information, such as passwords and other personal data, from the servers that used the affected software. The Heartbleed bug was quickly fixed after it was discovered, and most websites and online services that used the affected software updated their systems to protect against the vulnerability. However, it is still possible that some servers or systems may not have been updated and could still be vulnerable to the Heartbleed bug. It is important for everyone who uses the internet to take steps to protect their personal information, such as using strong and unique passwords, enabling two-factor authentication, and being cautious about the websites and services they use. It is also important for companies and organizations to regularly update their software and security systems to protect against new vulnerabilities.

query: Impact of RMD on credit worthiness
chosen: The actual policy will vary based on the specific bank.  But, if I were in your shoes I'd include RMDs in my stated income for credit card purposes.
rejected: Required minimum distributions (RMDs) are the minimum amount that an individual must withdraw from certain types of retirement accounts each year after reaching a certain age. RMDs apply to traditional individual retirement accounts (IRAs), 401(k)s, and other types of qualified retirement plans.RMDs do not directly impact an individual's credit worthiness, as they are not a type of debt or loan that must be repaid. However, if an individual fails to take their RMDs as required by law, they may be subject to a 50% tax penalty on the amount that they should have withdrawn but did not. This could potentially affect an individual's overall financial situation and, by extension, their credit worthiness.For example, if an individual has a large balance in a traditional IRA and fails to take their RMDs as required, they may face a significant tax liability and potentially have difficulty paying their other debts or bills. This could potentially lead to negative marks on their credit report and a lower credit score. On the other hand, if an individual takes their RMDs as required and uses them to meet their financial obligations, it could help to maintain a good credit score.It is important to note that RMDs are only required for certain types of retirement accounts, and not all retirement accounts have RMDs. In addition, there are certain exceptions and rules that may allow an individual to postpone or waive their RMDs in certain circumstances. It is important for individuals to understand their RMD requirements and to consult with a financial professional or tax advisor if they have any questions.

As a result, the demand for Chinese goods and services may decrease, and the demand for US goods and services may increase. This increased demand for Chinese assets can lead to an appreciation of the Chinese currency, which will also cause the real exchange rate between China and the US to rise.It's important to note that these are just a few of the factors that can affect the real exchange rate between two countries. There are many other economic and political factors that can also influence the real exchange rate.

query: How / Why is it that there are " swing states " each election ? What are the reasons that the same states are typically " key " ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
US politics are extremely divided along internal cultural lines . It is exceedingly rare in the US to change which party you vote for - they appeal to an entirely different group of people , and focus their policies around that group of people . Where many other democracies have parties who 's fortunes rise and fall with the economy or popular culture , in America you are either liberal or conservative , and you generally will shift at most once in your lifetime . Red States are states that almost always vote Republican . Places like Utah or Texas , where a majority of people are conservatives . Blue States are states that almost always vote Democrat . Places like New York or California , where a majority of people are liberals . Swing states are states that have about 50 % of each . This is a place where the ebb and flow of politics has an impact , because how ' energized ' conservatives or liberals feel will impact how many go to the polls . Because of that , these states change who they vote for from election to election - not because their citizens change parties , but because when 10 % fewer liberals vote than usual that electorate swings conservative . It 's a caustic problem that has lead to parties crafting policy to energize their bases , rather than trying to appeal to Americans as a whole . It 's one of the main reasons America sorely needs electoral reform .
Many states have a heavy bias towards one side or the other in an election . It 's unlikely for them to switch away from this bias , and even if they did that would only happen in a landslide election so it would n't really be important . Other states are simply not worth that many electoral college votes so winning them is n't in and of itself that important . Some states though ( Ohio , Florida , and Pennsylvania are the traditional 3 , but Virginia , Colorado , and a few others are sometimes included ) are large enough to be a big prize , and near enough to the center politically that they are open for either side to win . Winning the majority of these states will general mean you have won enough states to win the election ( because it 's presumed you won all the " easy " states if you also won the moderately difficult ones ) .
Most states have demographics that are pretty set . It 's quite unlikely for Utah or Idaho to vote Democratic or for California or Vermont to vote Republican . But some states are mixed or balanced demographics ... Florida is sort o a redneck state , except there 's a huge number of immigrants and retired people there who would vote Democrat , and its worth a lot of points . Ohio is another one , were the demographics just do n't lean one way or another . So whoever they vote for is very likely a good sample of how the populace as a whole is going to vote .
rejected:
A swing state is a state that does not consistently vote for the same political party in presidential elections. These states are considered important because the outcome of the election can be influenced by the results in these states. 
There are a few reasons why certain states are typically swing states. One reason is that the population of these states is evenly divided between Democrats and Republicans, so the outcome of the election in these states is often very close. 
Another reason is that these states have a large number of electoral votes, which are used to determine the winner of the presidential election. The more electoral votes a state has, the more influence it has on the outcome of the election. 
Finally, swing states are often targeted by both political parties because they can be won over with targeted campaign efforts. This means that both parties will spend a lot of time and resources trying to get people in these states to vote for their candidate.

rejected: There are a few reasons why health insurance rates increase disproportionately when you add a spouse or family member to a plan. query: Why is snow sometimes easy to make objects with , like snowmen . While other times it 's like fluff that just falls apart ? Is there different types of snow ? Explain like I'm five.

More water will make it the thicker snow that 's easy to clump together . Even more water makes it slush , where it 's very clearly water - like when you step on it .
There is light , fluffy snow that 's so cold it 's dry . This is good for shoveling , as it is light . Then there is ' Packing ' snow , which is good for building shapes . It is heavy , and a little more moist . Usually packing snow happens close to freezing temps , while the light , fluffy stuff happens in a hard freeze
Liquid Moisture content . Snow can be a lot like sand in a sandbox . Sure you can build stuff with dry sand but wet sand is best sand for making a sand castle .
rejected: Yes, there are different types of snow! Snowflakes are made of ice crystals, and the way the ice crystals form and group together can affect the texture and structure of the snow. When the temperature is cold enough, the ice crystals can form in a way that creates snowflakes with a lot of flat, tightly packed surfaces. This type of snow is called "packed snow" or "firm snow", and it is easy to make objects with because it sticks together well and doesn't fall apart easily. On the other hand, if the temperature is slightly warmer, the ice crystals might not form as tightly together and the snowflakes might have more rounded, fluffy shapes. This type of snow is called "fluffy snow" or "light snow", and it is not as easy to make objects with because it falls apart more easily. So the type of snow that you get depends on the temperature and humidity conditions when the snow is forming in the clouds.

query: What is the tax rate for selling stocks?
chosen: Assuming that taxes were withheld when you received the options, you would now only owe tax on the profit from the sale of the stock.  The cost basis would be whatever you bought the stock for (the strike price of the options in this case), and the profit will be the total amount received from the sale minus the total cost of those shares. Since you bought the stock more than one year ago, you will get taxed at the long-term capital gains rate of 15% (unless you are in the 39.6% tax bracket, in which case the rate is 20%). As with all tax advice on this site, you need to check with a tax specialist when you actually file, but that should give you a rough indication of what your tax liability is.
rejected: The tax rate for selling stocks depends on how long you held the stocks before selling them and your income tax bracket.If you sell stocks that you have held for less than one year, the profits you make from the sale are considered to be short-term capital gains and are taxed at your ordinary income tax rate.If you sell stocks that you have held for more than one year, the profits you make from the sale are considered to be long-term capital gains and are taxed at a lower rate than your ordinary income tax rate. The specific long-term capital gains tax rate that you will pay depends on your income tax bracket:For taxpayers in the 10% and 12% income tax brackets, the long-term capital gains tax rate is 0%.For taxpayers in the 22%, 24%, 32%, 35%, and 37% income tax brackets, the long-term capital gains tax rate is 15%.For taxpayers in the highest income tax bracket (37%), the long-term capital gains tax rate is 20%.It's worth noting that these tax rates are for federal taxes only. Some states also have their own taxes on capital gains. You may want to consult a tax professional or refer to IRS guidelines for more information about the tax rate for selling stocks.

query: Credit card transactions for personal finances
chosen: Other responses have focused on getting you software to use, but I'd like to attempt your literal question: how are such transactions managed in systems that handle them? I will answer for "double entry" bookkeeping software such as Quicken or GnuCash (my choice). (Disclaimer: I Am Not An Accountant and accountants will probably find error in my terminology.) Your credit card is a liability to you, and is tracked using a liability account (as opposed to an asset account, such as your bank accounts or cash in your pocket). A liability account is just like an asset except that it is subtracted from rather than added to your total assets (or, from another perspective, its balance is normally negative; the mathematics works out identically). When you make a purchase using your credit card, the transaction you record transfers money from the liability account (increasing the liability) to the expense account for your classification of the expense. When you make a payment on your credit card, the transaction you record transfers money from your checking account (for example) to the credit card account, reducing the liability. Whatever software you choose for tracking your money, I strongly recommend choosing something that is sufficiently powerful to handle representing this as I have described (transfers between accounts as the normal mode of operation, not simply lone increases/decreases of asset accounts).
rejected: Credit cards can be a convenient and safe way to pay for purchases, as well as a useful tool for managing your personal finances. Here are a few tips for using credit cards effectively for your personal finances:Pay your balance in full each month: To avoid paying interest on your credit card purchases, it's important to pay your balance in full each month. This will help you avoid accumulating debt, which can be expensive and difficult to pay off.Choose a card with rewards that align with your spending: If you're going to use a credit card for everyday expenses, it can be beneficial to choose a card that offers rewards for the types of purchases you make most frequently. For example, if you frequently travel, a credit card with travel rewards may be a good choice.Monitor your credit card activity: It's important to regularly review your credit card statements to ensure that all of the charges are accurate. This will help you catch any fraudulent activity or mistakes as soon as possible, so you can report them to your credit card issuer.Use your credit card wisely: Avoid using your credit card to make impulsive or unnecessary purchases, and try to pay off your balance as soon as possible to avoid accumulating debt. It's also a good idea to avoid using your credit card to withdraw cash from an ATM, as this often carries higher fees and interest rates.By following these tips, you can use your credit card to manage your personal finances effectively and avoid falling into debt.

query: why we need Internet service providers . Would it be possible , if net neutrality were to be abolished , that we simply find access to the Internet by ourselves with no supplier at all ? What does it actually take to * access * the Internet ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The Internet is just a bunch of computers connected together with cable . There 's more to the internals of the implementation , but at the most basic level , that 's really all it is . Now , to do anything USEFUL with that , you need : * Someone laying the cable from houses to connect to the internet * Some way of organizing and bridging all these cables so you do n't end up needing a physical cable running between each and every single computer in the world * Someone managing that bridge , and routing all the traffic from where it started to where it 's supposed to end * Someone to maintain and update those cables if they fail * If you do n't plan on implementing ALL cable - laying for the entire world , you need to set up access points for other people doing the same thing as you to connect to your cables to theirs . These are all things ISPs do . They set up and manage the connections between all the computers on their network and other ISPs networks .
[ If only this question had n't been asked over and over and over again in this sub . ] ( URL_0 ) The last time it was asked was * twenty - one hours ago * .
I 've been in network engineering for a long time , basically you ca n't be a major player , like a mom - n - pop version of Comcast . That takes many buildings full of very specialized equipment . And some of them are single boxes that cost over a million dollars . The power requirements alone to turn those boxes on are several thousand dollars a month , as well as a place to put them that can handle such power . A normal house can not provide enough power . A single box like that requires several times the amount of electricity than the main line going into a large home . However , you can be a smaller ISP , were you have a single room full of equipment maybe costing a $ 250,000 total , and have a big fiber uplink to a provider for about $ 5,000 a month . Lots of small companies do that in rural areas where the big ISPs do n't want to go because the customer density is n't worth it . You 'd basically get a big loan for the equipment and startup costs . And after a few years you might have 1,000 people paying you $ 30 - $ 50 per month and you can govern your ISP however you like . Google " data center pictures " to see some of the equipment locations that I 'm talking about .
rejected:
An Internet service provider (ISP) is a company that provides customers with access to the internet. Without an ISP, you wouldn't be able to connect to the internet from your home or business. ISPs offer different plans with varying speeds, data limits, and prices, so you can choose the one that's best for you.

Net neutrality is the idea that all internet traffic should be treated equally, without any data being favored or blocked by ISPs. If net neutrality were to be abolished, it is possible that ISPs could choose to block or slow down certain websites or services, or charge more for access to them.

To access the internet, you need a device that can connect to the internet (like a computer, phone, or tablet), an internet connection (which an ISP provides), and a way to access the internet (like a web browser). When you type in a website's address or click on a link, your device sends a request over the internet to the server that hosts that website. The server then sends the website back to your device, which displays it on your screen.

query: Doing arithmetic in your head quickly I sometimes feel so stupid . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I break everything down into smaller sums . Say 162\*7 . Times 7 is tricky , but \*5 is easier , I can times by ten ( add another nought ) and divide by two . So if I can work out 162\*5 , I just need to add another 162 twice . In other words , turn a sum you ca n't do into one you know you can do . As for the quickly part , I guess you just need to practice a lot .
I am not good at it but I do it enough that I know a few tricks . If you want to do something like add 86 and 37 , you know that 86 is 14 less than 100 , so call that 100 and take 14 off the 37 , so this is 123 perhaps a little easier than carrying in your head . I do n't know any real addition tricks that are n't some variation of this . There are lots of tricks when multiplying . To do 10 's you add a zero , obviously . To do 20 's you double and add a zero . 5 's is add a zero and halve . 18 you can do 20 and subtract 10 % , like 18 x 33 is 660 - 66 = 594 . There are lots of these .
It 's okay . Doing math in your head is never a good thing anyways . Once you get to higher math , such as calculus , doing derivatives and integrals in your head will be so much slower than just writing it on a piece of paper . There is no easy way to do math in your head , you are either born with a very large memory buffer , or you have to memorize certain things .
rejected: Sure! Doing arithmetic in your head quickly, or mental math, can be challenging for some people, but it's a skill that can be improved with practice. Here are a few tips that can help you get better at mental math: Start with basic math facts: Make sure you know your basic math facts, like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, so you can quickly solve problems in your head. Break down larger problems: If you're trying to solve a larger problem, try breaking it down into smaller pieces. For example, if you're trying to find the total cost of four items that cost $5, $10, $15, and $20, you can add the first two numbers ($5 + $10 = $15), then add the third number ($15 + $20 = $35), and then add the fourth number ($35 + $5 = $40). Use tricks and shortcuts: There are lots of tricks and shortcuts you can use to solve math problems more quickly in your head. For example, you can use the "double and add" method to quickly find the total cost of items that are all the same price. If four items cost $5 each, you can double the price (2 x $5 = $10), then add the price of one more item ($10 + $5 = $15). Practice, practice, practice: The more you practice mental math, the better you'll get at it. Try doing some mental math every day, even if it's just for a few minutes. You can also find math puzzles and games online that can help you improve your mental math skills. I hope these tips help! If you have any questions or need more help, just let me know.

query: Why are car batteries so big ? Car batteries are quite large and heavy and I was just wondering why the batteries in everything else have been able to shrink but car batteries have stayed the same size . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
They need that much size to generate the amount of current needed to turn the engine over to start . You can get 12 votes out of some small batteries but not the current . Because of advances in electronics the parts do not require as much current to run so those batteries can get samller . Also Car batteries use lead plate technology which does not produce the power of modern lithium - ion etc . But its cheap & so we will have them for the foreseeable future
Car batteries ( conventional lead - acid batteries ) have shrunk , actually . At one time batteries had thick glass cases and used solid lead plates suspended in acid ; they were extremely heavy and reliable . We call batteries like that " marine " or " deep cycle " batteries today . They were design - optimized for producing steady lower rates of current and they can have much of their capacity discharged without damage . Nowadays they have plastic cases so they 've gotten a little lighter but deep - cycle batteries are basically the same as they were in the early 20th century . But somebody figured out that if the lead plates in the battery had more surface area , all other things being equal , a battery could discharge far more amperage in a shorter period of time . They developed ways to make battery plates that were " foamed " instead of solid lead , so they weigh less but have far more surface area . These batteries were ideal for starting cars , which requires a huge jolt of amperage all at once but no significant demand after the car is running . They recover quickly and they weigh less -- but if they are discharged past a certain point the plates are damaged , and if they are stored in a discharged condition they will be ruined very quickly .
Lead - acid batteries are an extremely old technology and are big and heavy relative to the current they product . They are also much cheaper than newer technologies . Unlike say a notebook computer or an iPod there 's not a lot of reason to increase the expense in order to make them smaller .
rejected: Car batteries are large because they need to store a lot of energy. This is because cars use a lot of energy to start the engine and to power the car's electronics and accessories when the engine is not running. Car batteries also need to be able to supply a lot of power very quickly, which requires a larger battery. Imagine that you are trying to fill up a bucket with water. If you have a small bucket, it will take a long time to fill up because it can only hold a small amount of water at a time. But if you have a large bucket, it will be able to hold more water and it will take less time to fill up. The same is true for car batteries. A larger battery can store more energy, so it can provide power to the car for a longer time. In addition, car batteries are heavy because they are made with lead, which is a very dense metal. Lead is used in car batteries because it is very good at storing and releasing electricity. So, to sum it up, car batteries are large and heavy because they need to store a lot of energy and be able to supply a lot of power very quickly, and they are made with lead, which is a dense metal.

query: What is the difference between murder , homicide , and manslaughter ? I searched the thread and learned the difference between murder and homicide is intent , but what about manslaughter ? And how does the first degree , second degree , etc factor into the meaning ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Murder comes in 3 degrees . 1st Degree Murder - Homicide * Premeditated , you thought about what you where going to do , made preparations , went out and did it . 2nd Degree Murder - Heat of the moment * You walked into a room , saw your wife in bed with another man , you go super angry and shot him in the face . You did n't plan to kill him , but you still killed him . * You get in a fist fight with someone , you hit them to hard and they black out and hit their head on a curb . They die . Again you did n't mean to kill them , but you did . 3rd Degree Murder - Manslaughter * This one is a bit more complicated but generally something you did lead to someone dying . For example you run a red light and hit a pedestrian who dies , that is vehicular manslaughter .
Homicide is * any * killing of another human being , justified or not . Murder is typically a killing with deliberate intent to kill , and with no legal justification for the killing . Manslaughter is a killing without legal justification , but without deliberate intent to kill . In some jurisdictions , manslaughter is the crime charged when someone kills another in self - defense , but the court finds that the defendant was n't justified in believing deadly force was necessary . If someone kills another person in justified self - defense , it is * still homicide * . It 's just not a crime in that case .
Homicide is the general term : someone dying by another 's cause . Murder is an illegal act , to kill with intent or extreme negligence . Manslaughter is killing by negligence , but not to the degree of murder .
rejected:
Murder, homicide, and manslaughter are all crimes that involve the killing of a person, but they are not the same thing. Here's a simple explanation of the difference between these three types of crimes:

Murder is the most serious of these crimes. It involves intentionally or deliberately killing someone. For example, if someone plans to kill someone and then carries out that plan, that would be murder.

Homicide is a general term that refers to the killing of one person by another. This includes both murder and manslaughter.

Manslaughter is a less serious crime than murder. It involves the killing of a person, but not with the same level of intent as murder. There are two main types of manslaughter: voluntary and involuntary.

Voluntary manslaughter involves the intentional killing of a person in the heat of the moment, such as in a fit of rage or passion. For example, if someone finds out that their spouse has been cheating on them and then kills the spouse in a fit of anger, that would be voluntary manslaughter.

Involuntary manslaughter occurs when someone unintentionally kills another person while doing something reckless or negligent. For example, if someone is texting while driving and causes a fatal accident, that could be involuntary manslaughter.

In terms of degrees, first degree, second degree, and so on refer to the severity of the crime. First degree murder is the most serious type of murder, and it usually involves premeditation or planning. Second degree murder is less serious than first degree, and it usually involves the intentional killing of a person without premeditation or planning. The degree of a manslaughter charge will depend on the specific circumstances of the case.

query: How much is one “lot” of EUR/USD?
chosen: A lot (sometimes called a round lot) always refers to the quantity of physical good that you're getting, like a carton of eggs or a barrel of oil.  The tricky thing in the case of forex is that the physical good also happens to be a currency. A spot currency product trades in the denomination on the right-hand side (RHS) of the product name.  So if you're buying EUR/USD you are paying USD currency to get EUR "units", and if you're selling EUR/USD you are receiving USD by giving away EUR "units".  The EUR is the "physical good" in this case. The way I remember it is to think of all products (not just currencies) as trading pairs.  So AAPL in my mind is AAPL/USD.  When I buy AAPL/USD I am paying USD to get AAPL units.  When I sell AAPL/USD I am receiving USD by giving away AAPL units. The thing on the left is the physical good (even if it happens to be money) that you are exchanging, and the thing on the right is the money that you are exchanging. So, when I buy a lot of AAPL, I am buying 100 shares at their current price in dollars. Similarly, when I buy a lot of EUR/USD, I am buying 100K Euros at their current price in dollars.
rejected:
In the foreign exchange (forex) market, a "lot" is a standard unit of measure for a trade. The size of a lot can vary depending on the broker and the type of account that you have.A standard lot is typically 100,000 units of the base currency in a forex trade. For example, if you are trading EUR/USD, a standard lot would be 100,000 EUR. If you are trading a mini lot, the size of the trade would be 10,000 units of the base currency, and if you are trading a micro lot, the size of the trade would be 1,000 units of the base currency.It's important to note that the value of a lot will vary depending on the exchange rate between the two currencies being traded. For example, if the exchange rate for EUR/USD is 1.20, a standard lot of 100,000 EUR would be worth 120,000 USD.
In the foreign exchange market, a "lot" is a standard unit of measurement for currency trades. One "lot" is equal to 100,000 units of the base currency in a currency pair. So in the case of the EUR/USD currency pair, one "lot" would be equal to 100,000 euros.There are also smaller units of measurement, known as "mini lots" and "micro lots," which are equal to 10,000 and 1,000 units of the base currency, respectively.The value of a "lot" can vary depending on the leverage being used and the current exchange rate of the currency pair. For example, if you are trading EUR/USD with a leverage of 1:100 and the exchange rate is 1.20, one "lot" would be worth approximately $120,000 (100,000 x 1.20 x 100).It's important to note that trading in the foreign exchange market carries significant risks, and it is not suitable for everyone. query: Why do n't constellations change noticeably on a daily or even yearly basis ? The earth moves around the sun , the sun moves around the galaxy , the galaxy moves around the universe . Everything is moving and changing . Why do n't the constellations and change ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The stars that make up the constellations are very , very , very far away . The Earth 's orbit around the sun is tiny in comparison . So the slightly different view of the stars at different times of year is not noticeable . And yes , they are all moving , and moving quite fast . But they are so far away , and the distances they have to move to make an apparent difference are vast . Over thousands of years you 'd be able to see the changes .
because those stars are so mind - boggling far away that there is virtually no perceivable parallax . its like if you put your thumb a foot in front of your face and look at it while alternating between closing your left and right eye your thumb will shift pretty far between eyes but when you extend your arm out fully you see your thumb change positions less and less
Well , they do move out of position . However , because they are so far away , we observe these changes at a very slow rate to our naked eye . If you know your history of astronomy well , you will note that the positions of our constellations today are however fairly different compared to say , what the ancients saw when they viewed the sky , or even from the time of Jesus . Forensic astronomy uses this concept of position change to make inferences on the chronology of historical events . On a daily or yearly basis as you note , the changes are not very significant , but on the order of centuries and millennium , the constellations do appear to more significantly change and be different .
rejected:
The constellations do change, but it's hard for us to notice because the changes happen very slowly over a long period of time. 
Think about it this way: imagine you are standing on a very tall ladder, looking at the stars. You see a group of stars that form a shape, like a bear or a hunter. Now imagine that the ladder is slowly turning around. The stars are still there, but they seem to move in the sky as you change your perspective. 
The same thing happens with the constellations. The Earth is moving around the sun, and the sun is moving around the galaxy, so the stars seem to move in the sky. But because the Earth takes a whole year to go around the sun, and the sun takes even longer to go around the galaxy, it takes a long time for the constellations to change. So, even though the constellations are always moving, it's hard for us to notice the changes because they happen so slowly.

query: What do people mean by " The Republic " ? Is it different than a democracy , or a country ? I often hear this stated in American TV shows or movies , where a secret government agency is tasked with " defending the republic " Please explain like I'm five.

Because or this most countries elect individuals to represent them and make laws for them . This form of government is called a republic . The United States as well as every other decomocratic government in the world is not a true democracy but a republic . When people talk of " The Republic " they mean the country . rejected: "The Republic" is a term that is often used to refer to a government that is a representative democracy. These leaders are supposed to represent the interests of the people and make decisions that are in the best interests of the country and its citizens.A republic is different from a pure democracy, in which all citizens have an equal say in the decision-making process. query: How can having one vaccine shot cover you for life ? I just had a rabies shot , and the doc told me I am now covered for life ? Does the vaccination stay in your system forever ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
PLEASE READ THIS . What everyone else is saying is true about how vaccines build a long term immune response by developing " memory " cells that recognize the virus / bacteria in the future . BUT please remember that you are NOT covered for life with the rabies vaccine . Your doctor is incorrect in telling you this ( and you should tell him that so he does n't spread this to his other patients ) . If you EVER get exposed to rabies in the future , you MUST get the shot again . It does n't matter if this current shot was for pre - exposure or post - exposure protection . A.k.a if you get bit by a rabid dog today , you MUST go get another rabies vaccine shot . This protocol has been made by the Committee for Disease Control and Prevention ( CDC ) which is the governing body for medical protocol related to infectious diseases . [ Here is their site ] ( URL_0 ) where they explain the protocol . Look for the chart that says " Postexposure Prophylaxis for Previously Immunized Individuals " .
I 'm no expert , but i always understood it that when you are injected with a vaccine it contains a harmless amount of the illness . This small amount is then attacked by your body 's white blood cells which then make a note of how to kill the disease which is kept within your white blood cells for the rest of your life therefore if it ever happens to enter the body again the disease can be defeated . Again I 'm no expert , deffinetly someone with knowledge of subject can give a better explanation . Just remembered we were taught to think of it like a lock and key . The disease being the lock and the white blood cells being the object to open the lock . The vaccine allows the white blood cells to cut the correct key to open the lock . Aka kill the disease . And because they will always have the key they can always kill the disease .
When you 're vaccinated you 're given the antigens which sets off the primary immune response which is what happens when the body is presented with a pathogen it has n't encountered before . The primary immune response is a bit shit and does n't get rid of the pathogen quickly enough however as a result of the primary immune response memory cells are produced . These memory cells can divide rapidly when the pathogen is encountered again resulting in a much stronger secondary immune response that can actually deal with the pathogen . So the vaccine causes the production of memory cells and you 're covered as long as you have the memory cells . The cells can reduce in number over time which is why sometimes you need booster jabs but as long as you have memory cells for that pathogen you are immune .
rejected:
Vaccines work by teaching your body how to defend itself against a particular disease. When you get a vaccine, your body produces immunity to that disease, which means that your body is able to fight off the disease if you are exposed to it. Some vaccines provide immunity that lasts for many years, while others may need to be given more frequently to maintain immunity.

Rabies is a viral disease that can be fatal if it is not treated promptly. The rabies vaccine works by introducing a small amount of a killed or weakened form of the rabies virus into your body. Your body's immune system recognizes the virus as foreign and produces antibodies to fight it off. These antibodies remain in your body and provide protection against the disease.

The rabies vaccine is usually given as a series of shots over a period of several weeks. After you have received all of the necessary shots, your body should be able to produce enough antibodies to protect you against the disease for a long time. Some people may need to get booster shots to maintain their immunity to rabies, but for most people, the vaccine provides lifelong protection.

query: What is DirectX and why do you need it ? I never really understood it in the first place . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
These explanations are all brutal if you 're supposed to be actually explaining DX to laymen . I will put everything in point form because I 'm a software engineer and do n't believe in wasting my time with stupid crap like grammar , haha . - DirectX is a piece of software ( called an API ) developed by Microsoft for their Windows Operating System - It allows programs on your computer , such as video games , CAD software , etc to access your computer 's hardware ( such as graphics , sound , keyboard and mouse ) in a very lightweight manner - Very importantly , it allows hardware independence . This means that the same video game will work on every computer without having to be rewritten for every single machine it 's expected to run on - It is actually composed of a couple of components : Direct3D , DirectShow , DirectSound , DirectInput , and a few more I 'm probably missing . - By far , the most important of these ( and the one MS has sunk the most time and money into ) is Direct3D. - As you may know , everything you see in a 3D video game is made of a bunch of polygons . Even if you see a sphere , it 's actually made of a bunch of tiny triangles that are so small that they give the illusion of a smooth curve . - Here is an example of Leon from Resident Evil 4 to help you visualize this fact : URL_0 - Direct3D allows video games to say to the 3D graphics hardware , " Hey man , here are a bunch of triangles and here are instructions on where to put them , what color they should be , what textures they should have , how to light them up , etc " - DirectSound allows video games to say to the sound card , " Hey dude , here is a sound wave I want to you play through the speakers at this volume , with this speed , and I want you to mix it with several other sounds " - A version of DirectX has shipped with Windows ever since Windows 95 , but they were horrible . - Windows XP shipped with DirectX 9 , which was considered to be the first version of DX that did n't totally suck . - Ever since then , DirectX 10 came with Vista and DirectX 11 with Windows 7 . - Video games are generally tested with very specific subversions of DirectX , Ex . DirectX Version 10.2.2345 - Since they do n't want to take a risk with a version they were n't tested on , they almost always install DirectX on your computer whether they need it or not . - Luckily , Microsoft was smart enough to come up with a versioning system that makes it so they do n't all step on each other 's feet .
DirectX is a set of programming libraries which interact with the drivers for the hardware on your computer . The idea is that when writing an application , a developer can use DirectX algorithms instead of having to reinvent them to save time and effort .
DirectX is a collection of multimedia APIs for programmers . Mostly for gaming but also a / v players and editors . They abstract the really grimy details of how to interact with the hardware on a system so a developer does n't need to add code to their programs to deal with different ways of accomplishing something or different capabilities of various pieces of hardware like video or sound cards . You need DirectX installed because when a developer uses DirectX in their program , their program just contains a reference to code in the DirectX library and not the actual code . So you just install it once on your system and then everything that needs it knows where to find it .
rejected:
DirectX is a set of software tools that developers can use to make games and other programs that run on Microsoft Windows. It helps developers create more realistic graphics, sounds, and other interactive elements that make games and programs more fun and engaging.

Imagine that you want to play a video game on your computer. You would need a way to get the game's images and sounds from the game's software onto your screen and speakers. DirectX helps make this happen by providing a set of standard rules and tools that game developers can use to make their games work smoothly on your computer.

So, you need DirectX because it helps make sure that games and other programs run smoothly and look great on your computer.

query: Why do dogs lick people ? Does it represent a particular emotion on their part ? Explain like I'm five.

query: How do banks generate profit , adjust for inflation , and can reimburse their customers fully without collapsing ? I did n't pay enough attention in economics . EDIT : Thanks for the replies guys , they were all very helpful . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The ELI5 is that you give your money to a bank to hold . In return for this the bank pays you interest , we 'll say 1 % per year . The bank then loans out your money to someone else at 5 % per year . They take the 4 % difference as profit .
Just to add to what s been already been said in the other comments , modern day banks run on a system called fractional reserve banking and they have what s known as a reserve limit , say 5 % , and have to legally keep this amount ( of what s been deposited ) in reserves for day to day running and withdrawals . This means they can lend out 95 % of deposits but it does n't stop there , without going into to much detail this money gets " multiplied " in the economy and this initial deposit actually leads to a lot more money lent out than the original deposit , see this link if you wanted a bit more detail : URL_0

rejected:
Banks make money by borrowing money from some customers, such as individuals or businesses, and lending it to other customers at a higher rate of interest. The difference between the rate at which the bank borrows money and the rate at which it lends money is called the "spread," and it is the main source of the bank's profit.

To adjust for inflation, banks can increase the interest rates they charge on loans. Inflation is when the general price level of goods and services increases over time, which means that the same amount of money is worth less in the future. By charging higher interest rates, banks can compensate for the fact that the money they lend will be worth less in the future due to inflation.

Banks can also compensate for inflation by investing in assets that are expected to increase in value over time, such as stocks or real estate.

To reimburse their customers fully without collapsing, banks need to make sure that they have enough money on hand to cover all of the money that their customers want to withdraw. They do this by setting aside a certain amount of money as reserves, which they can use to pay customers if necessary. In addition, banks are regulated by government agencies, which require them to maintain a certain level of financial stability and to hold a certain amount of capital as a buffer against losses.

I hope this helps to explain how banks make money, adjust for inflation, and remain financially stable. Let me know if you have any other questions!

query: why did only Japan use Kamikaze Pilots in WW2 and was there some special kind of nationalism going on in Japan ? Doing some research for a history paper and this made me curious Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Kamikaze pilots was a strategy that evolved out of the growing gap in air power between America and Japan during WWII . After Pearl Harbor and in the early part of the war , Japan had arguably one of the best air forces around , at least in terms of numbers and experience . However , due to a number of mitigating factors , Japan essentially could not replace the losses that accumulated as the War in the Pacific progressed . As you drew into the later part of the campaign , the position had essentially reversed with the American air force now possessing superiority in numbers , training , experience , and resources available . With dwindling supplies ( especially fuel ) and scores of inexperienced pilots ( who ca n't get sufficient training because you 're rationing fuel ) , the principle of the Kamikaze was to maximize the amount of damage a single inexperienced pilot could inflict . In short , their pilots were so poorly trained and equipped , kamikaze was seen as one of the few ways they could inflict any damage on the Americans .
No , there was n't really any " nationalism " thing going on although the Japanese warrior code did play a small role . Unfortunately , despite any mythological ideas of kamikaze pilots , the real fact is that Japan was losing the battle in the air ( and sea ) for pilots very badly , and for many pilots , a battle with american forces meant you were n't coming home anyways ... as in , if you were forced to fight against US naval forces , especially if combined with US planes , you were dead in the water anyways ( excuse the pun ) . Kamikaze attacks begin making sense , when your potential to destroy a ship or cause major damage by regualr flying and fighting ( and eventually dying ) is low , but as a human guided missile you could actually do damage before you died . The kamikaze attacks grow more as Japan 's ability to field experienced pilots who might have a reasonable chance - despite everything against them - dwindled down , as such many inexperienced pilots , a kamikaze attack again made sense , they definitely were n't gon na survive a battle anyways , and at least they could be useful . [ As a side note , at this point in the war , Japanese planes were sub standard , not properly maintained and such , which means yeah , just more notches against the Japanese pilots ] tl;dr : You were probably going to die anyways in the battle . Take the bastards out with you

query: How to get information about historical stock option prices for a defunct company?
chosen: Though you're looking to repeat this review with multiple securities and events at different times, I've taken liberty in assuming you are not looking to conduct backtests with hundreds of events.  I've answered below assuming it's an ad hoc review for a single event pertaining to one security. Had the event occurred more recently, your full-service broker could often get it for you for free.  Even some discount brokers will offer it so.  If the stock and its options were actively traded, you can request "time and sales," or "TNS," data for the dates you have in mind.  If not active, then request "time and quotes," or "TNQ" data. If the event happened long ago, as seems to be the case, then your choices become much more limited and possibly costly.  Below are some suggestions: Wall Street Journal and Investors' Business Daily print copies have daily stock options trading data.  They are best for trading data on actively traded options.  Since the event sounds like it was a major one for the company, it may have been actively traded that day and hence reported in the papers' listings.  Some of the print pages have been digitized; otherwise you'll need to review the archived printed copies. Bloomberg has these data and access to them will depend on whether the account you use has that particular subscription.  I've used it to get detailed equity trading data on defunct and delisted companies on specific dates and times and for and futures trading data.  If you don't have personal access to Bloomberg, as many do not, you can try to request access from a public, commercial or business school library. The stock options exchanges sell their data; some strictly to resellers and others to anyone willing to pay.  If you know which exchange(s) the options traded on, you can contact the exchange's market data services department and request TNS and / or TNQ data and a list of resellers, as the resellers may be cheaper for single queries.
rejected: There are a few potential sources of information about historical stock option prices for a defunct company. Here are a few options you could consider:Financial databases: Some financial databases, such as Bloomberg or FactSet, may have historical stock option price data for defunct companies. However, access to these databases can be expensive, and it's possible that they may not have the data you're looking for.Historical stock price websites: There are several websites that provide historical stock price data, including Yahoo Finance and Google Finance. These sites may have stock option price data for defunct companies, but it's possible that the data may not be complete or up to date.Archival news articles: If the defunct company was publicly traded, it's possible that news articles from the time period when the company was in operation may contain information about stock option prices. You may be able to find these articles through a search engine or by looking through the archives of financial news websites.Company records: If the defunct company has records that are publicly available, it's possible that they may contain information about stock option prices. However, it may be difficult to access these records, particularly if the company is no longer in operation.Overall, it may be difficult to find complete and reliable information about historical stock option prices for a defunct company. You may need to consult multiple sources and cross-reference the information to get a comprehensive picture.

For example, if you have a cold or the flu, inflammation in your sinuses or upper respiratory system can cause pressure and pain in your head.
