query: What purpose did inventing a new language fulfil ? Why were so many languages invented if many of them were derived from existing ones ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Almost never does someone deliberately invent a new language . Language change slowly over time , and isolation allows them to diverge to the point where they are no longer mutually comprehensible . Sort of like speciation in biology . Bear in mind that until quite recently , almost nobody travelled . If a town 50 km away pronounced a word differently , most people in your town would never know about it . We 've gotten used to knowing how other dialects and accents sound , but that is a very recent development in the history of language . Incidentally , accent shifts are still happening . Canadian accents and American accents in the Great Lakes region are currently undergoing vowel shifts in opposite directions . If it were n't for easy travel and movies , etc , it 's entirely possible we 'd not be able to understand each other in 300 years ...
The only language I 'm aware of being deliberately " invented " , and used with any modicum of success , is Esperanto , which was an idealistic invention of the late 19th century . It was hoped that Esperanto would provide a simple , global language that would encourage peace and harmony around the world by giving all nations a common tongue . That hope , clearly , has been unfulfilled .
I would like to know the reasoning behind your question . The second part your sentence contradicts the first part . Most languages are , as you state , derived from existing ones , so it 's not really possible to speak about purposes . Nowadays , there are 6909 living languages as documented by SIL Ethnologue . > A " living language " is simply one that is widely used as a primary form of communication by a specific group of living people . The exact number of known living languages varies from 5,000 to 10,000 , depending generally on the precision of one 's definition of " language " , and in particular on how one classifies dialects . There are also many dead and extinct languages . Those living languages are mostly derived from what are called [ proto - languages ] ( URL_4 ) . A list of known proto - languages can be found [ here ] ( URL_0 ) . Those proto - languages are the languages most modern languages / the families of modern languages are derived from . On [ this picture ] ( URL_1 ) you can see the 25 modern language families . As you can see , most of the families are concentrated in one specific area . The reason behind this is isolation ; thousands of years ago , people lived very isolated lives ; they did n't travel to other areas for multiple reasons ( e.g. natural obstacles as mountains ) and mostly communicated only with people in their own area . Is it safe to say that those proto - languages were invented ? The answer is that we do nt know . We do nt know how the first language(s ) came to be . It 's a really difficult subject to study , because there is no evidence / proof . There are two different views . * [ Polygenesis ] ( URL_3 ): different languages were created on different places , probably because of isolation . Think of China vs. South America . * [ Monogenesis ] ( URL_2 ): all languages originate in one proto - Human language . Explaining the purpose of language is way to broad for this comment , but it comes down to one thing ; easier communication .
rejected:
Languages are invented for a variety of reasons. One reason is to help people communicate with each other. When people speak the same language, they can understand each other and share their thoughts and ideas.

Languages can also be invented to express new ideas or concepts that may not have existed in previous languages. For example, when people invent new technologies or scientific concepts, they may need new words to describe these things.

Languages can also be invented for artistic or creative purposes. Some people invent languages as a way to express themselves or to create a unique way of communicating with others.

There are many different languages in the world because people have different cultures and histories. Each language reflects the experiences and traditions of the people who speak it. Some languages are derived from other languages, while others are invented from scratch.

query: How can light not escape a black hole If light is made of photons , and photons have no mass how does a black hole 's gravitational pull affect said light ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Gravity does not come solely from mass . That may be what Newton thought , but as it turns out , it comes mostly from energy , and matter is a type of energy . Light is not matter . But it * is * energy , so it is affected by gravity . When body A orbits body B at a particular height , A must circle B at the correct speed to stay in that orbit . Lower orbits are faster orbits , and vice versa . With black holes , there is a particular height(called the Event Horizon ) at which the orbital velocity is the speed of light . If anything is closer to the black hole than that height , it can not orbit , and will inevitably spiral to the center .
By warping space itself so that the straight line light is traveling is curved around inside the event horizon .
gravity does n't just " pull " on an object , it actually warps space . If you imagine light as a swimmer and space as a river , gravity is n't a rope pulling the swimmer back , it 's more like the current . Inside the event horizon of a black hole , the current is so strong that even the fastest swimmer ca n't get enough speed to overcome the current .
rejected:
Black holes are objects with extremely strong gravitational pull, so strong that not even light can escape from them. This is because the gravitational pull of a black hole is determined by its mass, and the more massive an object is, the stronger its gravitational pull will be. Since photons (which make up light) have no mass, you might be wondering how they can be affected by the gravitational pull of a black hole.

Well, even though photons don't have mass, they do have energy, and energy can be affected by gravity. So when a photon gets too close to a black hole, the black hole's strong gravitational pull can bend the path of the photon and cause it to be sucked into the black hole. This is what makes it so difficult for light to escape from a black hole - it's being pulled in by the black hole's powerful gravity.

I hope that helps to explain it! Let me know if you have any other questions.

query: How do polaroid pictures work ? How do the pictures just slowly come in there etc ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Freakin ' magic , that 's how ! In a nutshell , a polaroid is a little envelope that contains all the chemicals needed to develop and fix the picture . Light hits chemicals that are sensitive to light , which react with reagents in a different layer that cause dyes to migrate into the first layer and become visible . Developing a photograph takes time ( the chemical reactions are n't instantaneous ) , and the first process to do instant color prints did n't work very well . The solution was to add another layer of chemicals that opacify soon after the exposure to light . This makes it dark inside the layers , and allows the time necessary for the developing and fixing process to happen without being exposed to more light . That opacifier breaks down and becomes clear after a period of time . That 's what you 're actually seeing when the polaroid comes out grey and image slowly shows up .
Many of the other posters have covered the developing process well , but I would like to add one thing . The white section down at the bottom contains many of the chemicals in a little pouch . When the photo is taken and comes out of the camera , it passes through rollers which pop this pouch and press the chemicals along the picture . The chemicals are still contained within internal layers so they do n't get everywhere , though .
/u / bazmonkey covered it well , but two things I will add : Polaroid photos only need time to develop . " Shake it like a Polaroid picture " was something of a nervous tick we developed as a society . Shaking it did not help the photo develop at all . Another thing ; if you use something with a point to draw on the photo as it 's developing ( a screw driver , a nail , etc ) you can get a pretty crazy effect .
rejected:
Polaroid pictures are a type of instant film photography. They use a special type of film that contains all the chemicals needed to develop the photograph right inside the film itself.

To take a polaroid picture, you first need a camera that is designed to use polaroid film. You load the film into the camera, just like you would with any other type of film.

Then, when you take a picture, the camera uses a flash to expose the film to light. This causes a chemical reaction inside the film, which starts to develop the photograph.

As the chemical reaction continues, the picture slowly starts to appear on the film. This can take anywhere from a few seconds to a few minutes, depending on the type of film you are using.

Once the picture is fully developed, you can gently peel it off the film and hold it in your hand. It's a magical moment, especially for kids!

query: Why well known Windows CTRL+C / CTRL+V does not work in command line ? Why well known Windows CTRL+C / CTRL+V does not work in command line ? Every time I am using command line I am wondering why Microsoft had to make it " different " ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
They did n't make the command line different . They made * Windows * different . The command line came first . DOS ( Disk Operating System ) , which Windows was built on top of , was just a command line , and there was no CTRL+C / CTRL+V for copy and paste . As for why they have n't modified the command line to use standard Windows keyboard shortcuts , CTRL - C is the " break " key in the command line ( it forcibly stops the currently - executing program ) , and changing it to do something else would potentially cause tons of programs that interact with the command line to have to be rewritten , which is a headache that Microsoft does n't want to deal with the backlash from .
Before there was Windows , there was MS - DOS , which used Ctrl - C as the break key , carrying over from CP / M , which got it from Unix . Then the Macintosh appeared . The Mac had no command - line at all , and GUIs were a brand - new idea , so Apple created keyboard shortcuts ( possibly based on Xerox Alto ? ) , and with the all - new platform they created a special Command key on the keyboard for that purpose . The x - c - v keys were assigned to Delete , Copy , and Paste , because they were somewhat mnemonic ( x - scratch out - delete , C for copy , but no mnemonic for V ) and next to each other . Then Windows came out , and copied as much of the Mac as it could . Microsoft saw that these shortcuts were nifty , and added the same thing to Windows . But there was a problem . Unlike the entirely new Mac platform , Windows was built for the existing PC platform , so they could n't create a new command key . So they just used the closest thing , the Control key . Even so , Windows provide two sets of shortcut keys , with the Ctrl - Insert and Shift - Insert style shortcuts as well as the Mac - like Ctrl shortcuts Maybe Microsoft really hoped that the Insert - style shortcuts would eventually prevail and the Ctrl XCV shortcuts were just for user compatibility for migrating Mac users , so the comflict with the existing DOS Ctrl keys was ignored . Once Windows completely dominated the PC platform , MS was finally able to add the Windows key , which is in the same location as the Mac 's command key . But it was too late to migrate shortcuts to the Windows key , and to this day the Windows key languishes , seeing little use . As with so many Microsoft things , a combination of an unfortunate early decision coming back to bite them when the platform 's own success made it impossible to fix in the face of backward imcompatibility .
On the command line , ctrl+c is a signal to kill the program that 's running . To copy and paste , you must right click , select " mark " , then highlight your text . When you have highlighted what you want , press enter . It will now be on your clipboard , to paste in command line , you right click and select paste , shift+insert might work as well
rejected: The reason that the familiar copy and paste keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V) don't work in the command line is because the command line was designed to use different keyboard shortcuts for these actions. In the command line, the keyboard shortcuts Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V are used for different purposes. Ctrl+C is used to terminate a running command or process. For example, if you run a command that takes a long time to complete, you can use Ctrl+C to stop it. Ctrl+V is not a valid keyboard shortcut in the command line. The command line was designed to be a powerful and efficient way to work with your computer, and the use of different keyboard shortcuts is part of that design. It might be a little bit confusing at first, but you'll get used to it with time and practice.

query: If household electricity is AC , why do we use DC so much ? Just wondering why we resort to wall warts and giant power bricks and all , instead of designing devices to run on AC ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Most modern electronic devices require DC to operate . Simple devices like motors and heaters ( light bulbs , vacuum cleaners , washing machines , etc ) can run on AC but electronics ca n't . You ca n't design electronic devices to run on AC ; it 's fundamental to how they work that they run on low - voltage DC . Houses have AC supplies because high voltage electricity is more efficient to transmit , and it 's easy to convert high voltage AC to low voltage AC using a transformer , but high voltage DC is more difficult and expensive to convert to low voltage DC .
Up until the late 1950 's just about everything in a house ran on AC . A lot of things still do . motors ( as in a washing machine , refrigerator , an air conditioning unit etc ) Light bulbs , heating elements ( electric heaters , stove burners , ovens ) . DC circuits are typically less than 24 volts while household AC circuits ( in the US are 120v and 240)--a standard wall outlet is 120 volts and 15 - 20amps . In simple terms , more voltage / amps translates into more power . Refrigerators , washing mashing machines , dryers , furnaces etc require a lot of power . A computer circuit board does n't . DC is necessary for modern electronic circuits ( think circuit boards ) to operate , which means that even appliances that use AC for motors , use DC for controls , displays , and other on board electronics .
Modern electronics usually require rather low voltages , and somewhat low current . For the most part , adding the PSU would add bulk , heat , and require devices be approved by safety agencies .

So they can make most of their money on regular pizza sales , and a little extra from 5 dollar pizzas , garlic sticks drinks etc . and retaining you as a customer when it comes time to order a 20 dollar pizza .

query: The Anti - Vaccine Movement Thanks for the downvotes everyone ! Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
People think they 're smarter than scientists , and then hear an authority figure who 's " just like them " repeat some stupid debunked study that makes them feel justified in their smug belief . They then go looking for things that confirm their point of view and consciously filter out anything that challenges their world view .
Read the wikipedia page for Andrew Wakefield . Basically , he published a study linking a certain brand of MMR vaccine to autism . It got a lot of hype , but then it was found that he had a financial interest in a competing brand of vaccine , and that his results were fraudulent ; his results could n't be replicated , and he was found to have been ignoring key data that opposed his conclusion . As a result , his medical license was revoked .
Some people think that vaccines cause or predispose people to autism . Other object to some of the chemicals found in some vaccines . Still others have religious objections . Yet others think that vaccinations should still be performed , but only for disease of various levels of plausibility .
rejected: The anti-vaccine movement refers to people who are opposed to vaccination. They may believe that vaccines are dangerous, that they are not effective at preventing diseases, or that they are not necessary because the diseases that vaccines prevent are no longer a threat. Some people in the anti-vaccine movement also believe that the ingredients in vaccines are harmful, or that vaccines can cause autism or other developmental disorders. It's important to understand that vaccines are one of the most effective and safe ways to prevent diseases. They work by exposing the body to a small, harmless version of a disease-causing virus or bacteria, which prompts the body to build up immunity to that disease. This means that if a person is exposed to the real virus or bacteria in the future, their body will be able to fight it off and they won't get sick. Vaccines have helped to prevent countless cases of illness and death, and they are a vital tool for protecting public health.

query: Why won't my retirement account let me write a “covered put”?
chosen: A "covered put" of the form of being short, and buying at the strike price if the "put ... is put" (excercise), is off the table simply because you can't do shorts in the retirement account. Even if you feel you "win" the argument that you're hedged by being short, any broker can say, "we simply forbid shorts" and that's that. A "covered put" of the form of posting the cash, and spending it to buy at the strike price if the "put ... is put" (excercise), might be forbidden by brokerages because, frankly, how do you account for the "dedicated" cash? Is it locked down like margin is, or escrow, or what? I don't know offhand how I would address that in my very own firm. Thus, any broker could say, "we forbid it" and that's that. The other answers are very interesting in conjunction with this. JoeTaxpayer says, very paraphrased, 'just cuz it's legal doesn't mean we have to offer it.' Jaydles says (again, completely paraphrasing), 'complex stuff for a safe little retirement savings account;' 'difficult to administer' (as I said, how do you account for it); and 'tradition' So maybe look at Scott, per Thorn's answer, LOL. It appears that you can shop around on this issue.
rejected:
It is possible that your retirement account, such as an IRA (Individual Retirement Account) or 401(k), does not allow you to write covered puts. This is because covered puts, also known as short puts, involve selling a put option on a security that you own, or are "covered" by, in your portfolio. The sale of the put option obligates you to potentially buy the underlying security at a certain price (the strike price) on or before a certain date (the expiration date). This strategy can be used to generate income or to hedge against a potential decline in the value of the underlying security.However, some retirement accounts have restrictions on the types of investment activities that are allowed. These restrictions may be in place to protect the tax-advantaged status of the account or to ensure that the account is used for its intended purpose of saving for retirement. For example, some retirement accounts may not allow you to engage in short selling or other types of options trading.It is important to review the terms and conditions of your retirement account to understand what types of investment activities are allowed. You should also consider consulting with a financial professional or tax advisor to determine whether any particular investment strategy is appropriate for your retirement account.
There are a few reasons why a retirement account, such as a 401(k) or an Individual Retirement Account (IRA), might not allow you to write a covered put. Here are some possible reasons:The account may have restrictions on the types of investments that can be made. Some retirement accounts may not allow you to trade options or engage in other complex investment strategies.The account may have limitations on the use of margin. Writing a covered put involves the use of margin, which is borrowing money from the brokerage to purchase the underlying stock. Some retirement accounts may not allow the use of margin or may have restrictions on the amount of margin that can be used.The account may have tax implications. In a retirement account, any income or capital gains earned on investments are tax-deferred until the funds are withdrawn at retirement. If you write a covered put and earn a profit, that profit may be subject to taxes, which could impact your retirement savings.It's important to understand the rules and limitations of your retirement account before making any investment decisions. If you have questions about what you can and cannot do with your retirement account, it's a good idea to consult with a financial advisor or the administrator of your account.

query: How is Canadian healthcare different from Obamacare ? Canadian healthcare , along with many other countries who provide healthcare via the government , is working perfectly fine , so how is it working when American healthcare is n't ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Canadian healthcare is , more or less , single - payer . Basically , you pay a tax to the government and the government insures you . American healthcare is a big old mess . We have single - payer for some people ( Medicare ) , full - on nationalized healthcare for some ( the Veterans ' administration , where doctors are government employees ) , private insurance for many , and no insurance ( fuck you and die ) for some . Obamacare is actually working -- it 's fixing some of the worst problems with private insurance and reducing the number of people with no insurance . But our health care system is still a big old mess . Obamacare did n't create the mess , but it does sort of freeze it in place . If you want more detail , I created a comic to explain Obamacare here : URL_0 .
The ' why ( and if ) American healthcare is n't working ' would very much break the bias rule if I went on a rant about it , so here 's just some bare bones differences between the two . In Canada we have " single payer " healthcare- the government buys all the equipment and employs healthcare workers . This is seen as a good thing because it [ lowers the cost of procedures ] ( URL_0 ) by giving one entity ( the government ) bargaining power ( and eliminates the ' hidden costs ' your insurance is supposed to haggle down in American health care . ) We do n't ever have to put money down to get health care- it 's not regular insurance , so you never have a doctors visit only covered 80 % . ( There are certain things not covered , of course . ) Single - payer would never have passed in the US , so they compromised and used a partially privatised solution . Americans still use private insurance , but the government introduced subsidies to lower the cost of it , and enacted a large amount of reform , including mandating a minimum amount of coverage they have to offer , laws against different pricing based on sex or pre - existing conditions . There are a lot of similarities between the two- the ACA tried to take healthcare to a similar place , covering everyone , through market competition ( private hospitals and insurance companies ) plus subsidies rather than through a government monopoly on it . Now whether or not they 've succeeded ... Generally everyone on both sides is unhappy about it , but at least more people have some coverage in the mean time .
Our healthcare system is run like a business . Affordable Care Act or " Obamacare " requires citizens to have health insurance , and requires health insurance companies to cover everyone ( There is obviously [ more to it than ] ( URL_0 ) that , but that will suffice for this ELI5 ) . That is the difference : we still pay for the health insurance and health care .
rejected:
Canadian healthcare, also known as "Medicare," is a publicly funded healthcare system that provides universal coverage to all Canadian citizens and permanent residents. This means that all Canadian residents have access to a range of healthcare services, including hospital care, physician services, and prescription medications, at no direct cost to the patient. The healthcare system is funded through taxes paid by Canadians, and the services are provided by a combination of public and private hospitals and healthcare providers. 
Obamacare, also known as the Affordable Care Act (ACA), is a healthcare reform law that was passed in the United States in 2010. It was designed to make healthcare more accessible and affordable for Americans by expanding coverage to more people and requiring insurance companies to offer certain minimum benefits. However, unlike in Canada, the United States does not have a universal healthcare system, and many Americans still do not have access to affordable healthcare. Instead, the ACA relies on a combination of private insurance companies and government-funded programs, such as Medicaid, to provide coverage to Americans. 
There are a number of differences between the Canadian healthcare system and Obamacare. One of the main differences is that the Canadian system is universal, while the ACA only covers certain individuals. Another difference is that the Canadian system is funded through taxes, while the ACA relies on a combination of private insurance premiums and government funding. Finally, the Canadian system is administered by the government, while the ACA involves both private insurance companies and government programs.

query: What is used in movies to replicate druguse etc . ? I mean , they probably do n't let actors chain - smoke real cigarettes and snort real coke the whole time the movie is being made . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Cigarettes are usually pretty easy . Herbal cigarettes give the look of the real thing and are about as safe as inhaling smoke into your lungs can be . Depending on the actors involved and the area 's smoking laws , real ones can be used , but I doubt it 's common . If something like cocaine is snorted , it 's usually lactose powder or some other vitamin powder . If it 's just sitting on a table , but nobody snorts it on screen , it 's usually corn starch and baby powder . Source : was in a play in college , we had to smoke and snort some things . Asked a professor from the film department . University rules would n't let us light anything in the theater , so we ended up just holding a lot of unlit cigarettes .
I used to work for the art department for a crime recreation show , so this is just perfect . We used catnip for weed if it 's not going to be smoked , gutted herbal cigarettes put in rolling paper if it is . Various white powders for cochise , rock candy for meth . I think brown sugar for heroine , that one did n't come up as often , if at all .
A lot of actors / actresses smoke cigarettes in real life . For marijuana , I know in Traffic and American Beauty , in order to replicate the red eye effect , they blew peppermint dust in the actors ' faces .
rejected: In movies, actors usually do not use real drugs to portray drug use. Instead, they use fake or simulated drugs that are made to look like the real thing. These fake drugs are often made from food items or other safe and legal substances that are made to look like drugs, but do not have the same effects as the real drugs. For example, a piece of candy or a small amount of flour might be used to simulate cocaine, while a vegetable or a piece of bread might be used to simulate a pill. Similarly, actors often use fake cigarettes or electronic cigarettes, which do not contain tobacco, to portray smoking in movies. This is because smoking real cigarettes can be harmful to the actors' health and can also produce a strong smell that can interfere with filming. It's important to remember that using drugs and smoking are not safe or healthy behaviors, and they should not be portrayed as such in movies or other media.

query: Why do cars only have defroster grids in the rear window ? I am thinking it has something to do with what might happen if the windshield shattered , but it would be convenient a lot of the time . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Rear window is far enough back that the lines of the defroster grid are not too distracting during the brief glances back drivers make during driving . Putting those lines in the front window would prove to be hazardous to many drivers by being visual distractions . This is the same reason LEO 's will issue tickets for driving with large cracks in your front windows , and that hanging stuff from your rear - view mirror is ticketable as well ( usually only happens if you 've ticked off the officer ) .
Most cars have their climate control system centralized in the front dashboard . It is easier to duct warm air to the front windshield than it is to pass the treated air back to the rear window since this would require lengths of tubes with turns and bends that present flow losses to the gas flow . So electrical grids are a better option for the rear windows .
The latest models do have defroster in the front . Ford for example has trademarked its heated windshield under the name " Quickclear " . So , its coming . Check [ this ] ( URL_0 ) for example . Plus , you do n't wanna look to / thru all those little wires .
rejected:
Cars only have defroster grids in the rear window because the front windshield is made of different material than the rear window. The front windshield is made of laminated safety glass, which is two layers of glass with a plastic layer in between. The laminated safety glass is designed to stay in one piece even if it cracks or breaks, so it doesn't need a defroster grid to help clear it. 
The rear window, on the other hand, is usually made of tempered glass, which is a type of glass that shatters into small pieces if it breaks. Tempered glass is used in the rear window because it is stronger and more durable than regular glass, but it is also more prone to fogging up or getting covered in condensation. That's why the rear window has a defroster grid, which is a grid of heating elements that help clear the window of fog or condensation. 
So, in short, cars have defroster grids in the rear window because it is made of a different type of glass that is more prone to fogging up, and because the front windshield is made of laminated safety glass that doesn't need a defroster grid.

query: Should I buy my house from my landlord?
chosen: There are probably thousands of houses that you could buy. If you want to buy a house, it is very unlikely that the one you are renting right now is the best possible buy.  Usually people living in the houses they own are more interested in the quality of their property and the quality of their neigborhood than people who are renting, so I'd say that you are generally better off finding a home to buy in an area where the majority own their homes.
rejected: There are a few things to consider when deciding whether to buy your house from your landlord. Here are a few points to consider:Affordability: Can you afford to buy the house? Consider your income, debts, and other financial obligations when determining whether you can afford the monthly mortgage payments and other costs associated with homeownership.Financing: How will you finance the purchase of the house? Will you need to take out a mortgage or will you be able to pay cash? If you will need to take out a mortgage, consider whether you will be able to qualify for a loan and what the terms of the loan will be.Location: Is the house in a location that you want to live in long-term? Consider factors such as proximity to work, schools, and amenities when deciding whether the house is a good fit for your needs.Condition: Is the house in good condition? If not, are you willing and able to make any necessary repairs or renovations?Market conditions: What is the current real estate market like in the area where the house is located? Is it a buyer's market or a seller's market? This can affect the price of the house and your negotiating power.Overall, purchasing a house from your landlord can be a good option if you can afford it, the financing and location are right for you, and the house is in good condition. However, it's important to carefully consider all of these factors before making a decision.

query: Islamic banking From what I understand , islamic banks do not charge interest , which raises the question of how they make their money ? Very hard concept to grasp . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
There are a number of methods used that allow the bank to earn money , but not actually , technically earn interest . One of the most common is the " repurchase agreement " . So say you want a car loan , then car costs like ... $ 15000 . If that was a 5 year loan at 5 % , your payments would be 283 a month , total repayment is $ 16980 . We can round that off to 17 . That 's the western version . The Islamic version is that , if you are approved for the loan , the BANK will buy the car for $ 15k , and you will sign a contract to buy the car FROM THE BANK for $ 17k , in monthly installments . Until you 've paid off the " loan " , the bank legally owns the car . Once you 've paid it off , you have actually completed the purchase of the car . It 's a " repurchase " because the bank buys it , on the promise that you 're going to buy it . URL_0 That has most of the methods , described in fairly simple terms
If my brief time in Pakistan is any example , the major differences are no interest rates ( that is considered usury ) and there is a monthly portion withdrawn for Zahat ( alms giving ) . Basically it is a state sponsored charitable donation . Most of the bank 's profits come from conservative investments and fees . Though to be honest , I do n't recall the fees to open and operate an account being all that substantial . I asked the exact same question when I opened my modest account in Pakistan . Honestly , I wish my American bank would adopt some of these policies .
As mentioned : * The bank owns the house until you 've paid it off . * There 's a fixed , one - off service fee * There 's a minimum monthly payment to keep up . * There 's no early - exit penalties - if you pay it all off in one hit , you pay exactly as much as if you 'd taken 20 years . They may have a funny way of handling defaults , too , but I forget the details . All in all , it seems a lot more equable . Personally , I want to do some contracting for a western bank . I 'll offer them excellent terms . My rates are $ 50 an hour for the first six months , but are subject to unlimited change without notice after that , and they 're obliged to employ me for the next 20 years , or pay me a significant majority of the remainder of my contract in order to break it . If they stop paying me , I 'll destructively undo all the work I 've performed for them in the past , and ensure that nobody else will ever work for them again .
rejected:
In traditional banking, banks make money by charging interest on loans they give out to borrowers. This means that the more money a bank loans out, the more money it makes.

Islamic banks, on the other hand, do not charge interest because charging interest is considered to be against the principles of Islam. Instead of charging interest, Islamic banks make money by sharing in the profits or losses of the businesses they finance.

For example, let's say that an Islamic bank provides financing to a farmer to help him buy new equipment for his farm. The bank might enter into a partnership with the farmer, where the bank and the farmer share the profits or losses of the farm. If the farm does well and makes a profit, the bank and the farmer would share the profits. If the farm doesn't do well and loses money, the bank and the farmer would both lose money.

In this way, Islamic banks make money by investing in and partnering with businesses, rather than by charging interest.

query: NYSE & NASDAQ: Mkt Cap: $1 billion+
chosen: Try Google Finance Screener ; you will be able to filter for NASDAQ and NYSE exchanges.
rejected: The NYSE (New York Stock Exchange) and NASDAQ (National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations) are both stock exchanges, where publicly traded companies' stocks are listed and traded. The market capitalization (mkt cap) of a company is the value of the company's outstanding shares of stock, and it is calculated by multiplying the number of the company's outstanding shares by the current market price of one share.Companies with a market capitalization of $1 billion or more are often referred to as "large cap" companies. These companies tend to have a long track record of stability and financial strength, and they are often well-established and influential within their industry. Large cap companies may be more resistant to market fluctuations and may offer investors a degree of stability, but they may also have slower growth potential compared to smaller, emerging companies.It's worth noting that market capitalization is just one factor to consider when evaluating a company as an investment. Other factors, such as the company's financial performance, management team, competitive landscape, and future growth prospects, are also important to consider.

query: Are there extra fees for a PayPal Premier account?
chosen: Summary: the fees used to differ but no longer do. Fees are the same. If you have a personal account, feel free to upgrade it to premier to get access to more features. Longer answer: the two account types USED to differ but that changed a few years ago (maybe circa 2011?). PayPal wants person-to-person payments to be free (except where they must pass along credit card charges or else they would loose their shirt) but wants to charge merchants for receiving payments. Originally PayPal required merchants to have premier (or business) accounts, and charged fees for payments made to those account types. Personal accounts had significant limitations on receiving payments, but did not pay fees upon receiving payments.  Eventually PayPal decoupled the question of "is this a person-to-person payment or a payment to a merchant for goods and services?" from the paypal account type. So now the same account can receive both a p2p payment (e.g. splitting lunch costs), on which it will NOT pay fees, and can receive a payment for goods or services e.g. from a web checkout, on which it WILL pay fees. Regardless of the account type.
rejected: Yes, there are fees for using a PayPal Premier account. PayPal charges a fee for each transaction you make using your Premier account. This fee is a percentage of the total amount of the transaction, and it varies depending on the country you are located in and the type of payment you are receiving. For example, in the United States, PayPal charges a fee of 2.9% + $0.30 for each transaction you make using your Premier account. This fee is automatically deducted from the amount of the transaction before it is deposited into your account.In addition to transaction fees, PayPal also charges fees for certain other services, such as receiving payments from international buyers, making a currency conversion, or using certain features, like PayPal's fraud protection service. You can find more information about these fees on the PayPal website.It's important to note that fees may change over time, so it's a good idea to check the PayPal website periodically to stay up to date on the latest fees for using a Premier account.

query: Black holes How do they form ? Do they ever " go away " or dissipate ? What happens to things that get sucked inside of them ? What 's on the other side of the " hole " ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Calling them black holes is a bit of a misnomer , since there is no hole in them . What they are are bits of stuff that has become so squashed by its own weight that it does n't take up any space whatsoever . There is an area around this called the " event horizon " . Go beyond this and even light can not escape from the gravity of the black hole . This is what is usually used to define a black hole The normal way that they are formed is when a very large(about 3 times as large as the sun ) star dies . The nuclear fire in the star stops counteracting it 's gravity , and the star collapses in on itself . Black holes can , and do dissipate- due to quantum effects . The less mass the black hole has , the faster it dissipates . As for things sucked into them , they simply become part of the singularity , swelling it 's mass . Sometimes due to quantum effects they can be shot out again at high speed , which is responsible for most of the light emmitted by quasars- extremely active supermassive black holes at the center of some galaxies ( some galaxies have less active black holes at their centre and so are not quasars ) . There is nothing on the other sie of the hole , as there is no hole . This travelling occurs in wormholes , which are hypothesised , but have never been seen outsideof science fiction . Hopefully this explanation was good enough .
Black hole are probably the least understood and most misunderstood objects in the cosmos . Take everything you hear or think about them with a grain of salt . There is some very complicated mathematics describing how ( in some cases ) the can form and what should happen to them and what should happen around them . But simple descriptions will never do them justice so it is best to not try and imagine what you think would happen . ( like " what if I get sucked inside " ) without first seeing what the math is telling you . All that said , there are many interpretations of black holes and most are , for now , equally valid . The one I like is to imagine and wall so strong and powerful it shatters and scatters anything that touches it . That is what a black hole event horizon is like anything that encounters the event horizon is utterly destroyed and its energy it scattered back into the Universe . The Event horizon is also strongly attractive , if you get too close it can become impossible for you to avoid hitting the wall , you have used up all your available resources to avoid the contact and all that is left for you is to contact the event horizon and to have what remains of you scattered back . Some far away from the event horizon will have a completely different interpretation of what is happening but that is the nature of different frames of reference , especially in curved space - time .
Black holes form when very , very , very big stars end their life cycle . After these big stars " blow up " , all that is left is a super - heavy thing we call the black hole . Because it is very heavy , it has a very strong gravitational pull . So strong that not even light , which travels very quickly , can not escape . Hence the name black hole .
rejected: Black holes are very dense and heavy objects in space that have such strong gravitational pull that nothing, not even light, can escape from them. They are formed when a very large star, much larger than our Sun, runs out of fuel and collapses under its own weight. When this happens, the star's core becomes so dense that it creates a deep well in space-time, or the fabric of the universe. This well is what we call a black hole. Black holes do not "go away." They are forever present in the universe and can only get bigger over time as they continue to swallow up matter and energy. Anything that gets too close to a black hole will be pulled inside and can never escape. Scientists are not sure what happens to objects that fall into a black hole, but they think that the objects may be crushed into a very small and dense point, called a singularity. There is no "other side" of a black hole. Think of it like a bottomless pit that you could fall into but could never climb back out of. Black holes are mysterious and fascinating objects, and scientists are still trying to learn more about them.

query: If somebody shot a gun directly at you , how far away from the shooter would you have to be to realistically jump out of the way of the bullet in time ? I realize it also depends on the kind of gun . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:

If you knew exactly when the shot was fired , you 'd probably need about 600 m to get out the way . Your problem is that you 've no reliable way of knowing when the shot is fired , as with most rifle rounds , the sound of the gun will arrive after the bullet does .
Mythbusters did this . Under controlled circumstances 500 m ( provided you know the instant the bullet leaves the barrel ) . Under " normal " conditions you ca nt see the muzzleflash over 200 m distance and the bullet travels 3 times the speed of sound .... FYI They used a sniper rifle in the 30cal range since less powerfull weapons would not reliably hit the target at that distance .
rejected: It is not realistic for a person to be able to jump out of the way of a bullet in any situation. Bullets travel at very high speeds, and it is not possible for a person to react and move quickly enough to avoid being hit. Even if you were standing just a few feet away from the shooter, you would not have enough time to react and jump out of the way before the bullet reached you. It is important to always stay as far away as possible from guns and avoid any situation where someone might be shooting a gun. If you are ever in a situation where someone has a gun and is threatening to use it, it is important to try to find a way to get to safety as quickly as possible, rather than trying to jump out of the way of the bullet.

query: What is the best and most optimal way to use margin
chosen: This essentially depends on how you prefer to measure your performance. I will just give a few simple examples to start. Let me know if you're looking for something more. If you just want to achieve maximum $ return, then you should always use maximum margin, so long as your expected return (%) is higher than your cost to borrow. For example, suppose you can use margin to double your investment, and the cost to borrow is 7%. If you're investing in some security that expects to return 10%, then your annual return on an account opened with $100 is: (2 * $100 * 10% - $100 * 7%) / $100 = 13% So, you see the expected return, amount of leverage, and cost to borrow will all factor in to your return. Suppose you want to also account for the additional risk you're incurring. Then you could use the Sharpe Ratio. For example, suppose the same security has volatility of 20%, and the risk free rate is 5%. Then the Sharpe Ratio without leverage is: (10% - 5%) / 20% = 0.25 The Sharpe Ratio using maximum margin is then: (13% - 5%) / (2 * 20%) = 0.2, where the 13% comes from the above formula. So on a risk-adjusted basis, it's better not to utilize margin in this particular example.
rejected: Using margin can be a good way to increase your buying power and potentially maximize your investment returns. However, it is important to use margin carefully, as it also increases your risk of losses. Here are some tips for using margin optimally:Understand the risks: Margin trading involves borrowing money from your broker to buy securities. This can magnify your profits, but it can also magnify your losses if the value of the securities you bought declines.Use stop-loss orders: A stop-loss order is a type of order that closes your position automatically if it reaches a certain price level. This can help you minimize your losses if the market moves against you.Monitor your margin balance: Keep an eye on your margin balance and make sure it doesn't fall below the minimum required level, or your broker may force you to sell securities or deposit more money.Don't overleverage: It can be tempting to use a high amount of margin, but this increases your risk of significant losses. Consider using less margin and taking a more conservative approach.Use margin responsibly: Only use margin if you have a clear investment strategy and can afford to take on the additional risk.

query: when you stretch thoroughly , why does your hearing momentarily cut out ? Sometimes it can just numb the hearing considerably but more often than not my hearing goes when I stretch , like the ear hole is getting covered over or something . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
This is actually fascinating process . First off , your inner ear is an air chamber filled with sensitive instruments and sealed off from the world by your ear drum . It is , however , connected through your Eustachian tubes to your sinus , allowing it to equalize with changing air pressures . When you have a cold and these tubes get blocked off , diving under water or flying in an airplane can create pressure changes that are truly painful . You need to " pop " your ears to make it go away , or massage the Eustachians by chewing some gum . ( They are effected by moving your jaw around ) Now what does this have to do with stretching ? When you yawn , your jaw goes way back and your Eustachian tubes open up really wide . ( They 're not normally wide open , otherwise you 'd feel it in your ears every time you swallow . ) And then you pinch them off . As you pinch them off , the continued yawning and stretching actions squeeze the tissues around your ear , and your tongue will back against your nasal passage changing the air pressure in your sinus . All of this causes flutters of pressure changes within your inner ear , as you open it , seal it off , squeeze it and release . This momentary pressure in your inner ear pushes against the ear drum and messes with your ability to pick up sound waves during the stretch . Now sometimes something really awesome happens . Your ear actually has a built - in hearing aid , and it can get a feedback loop and have to reset ! There are tiny hairs ( ? ) in your inner ear that will vibrate in response to sound , amplifying it . Now , just like when someone gets that horrible feedback squeal between a microphone and speakers , sometimes these hairs pick up a random vibration and runaway with it . You 'll actually hear a very high - pitched ringing that slowly fades away to silence , with diminished hearing ability for a few minutes until the system has reset . Stretching sometimes sets off this feedback loop of hearing - aid hairs and can cause you to lose hearing volume for a couple minutes . More commonly you may just need to " pop " your ears after yawning to release any pressure that did n't come back out . ( Do this by pushing your jaw back and swallowing ) ( Ninja edit for clarity )
I 'm not sure where everyone 's getting their answers from , but i 'll add my two cents . There is a tiny muscle inside the MIDDLE EAR cavity called the tensor tympani . This contracts when your chewing ( trying playing some white noise through your headphones and periodically bite down to hear it working ) . This muscle tightens when your stretching , and interestingly also as part of a general startle reflex when your suprised or scared . Source : Qualified Audiologist for 6 years woop
Pretty sure it 's just the Eustachian tube ( tube between middle ear and nasal cavity ) opening from muscle activation . Same muscle activation you get when you yawn or chew sometimes and get that ear popping sensation . You 're just feeling the sensation of pressure equalizing .
rejected:
When you stretch, your body is physically moving and changing position. When you stretch your neck or head, for example, you may be tilting your head or moving it in a way that changes the position of your ears. This can temporarily affect the way that sound waves travel into your ear and reach your eardrum.

The eardrum is a thin, sensitive membrane that vibrates when sound waves hit it. These vibrations are then converted into electrical signals that your brain can understand. When you stretch and move your head or neck, it can change the position of your eardrum and the way that it vibrates, which can affect your hearing.

It's also possible that stretching certain muscles in your neck or head could put pressure on the blood vessels that supply blood to your ears, which could also affect your hearing.

Overall, stretching can temporarily affect your hearing because it changes the position of your ears and the way that sound waves travel into them, and it can also potentially affect the blood flow to your ears.

query: How do films played at a frame rate higher than intended remain synced with the audio ? Say a movie recorded at 24 fps is played back at 60fps , either analog or through digital streaming , how does the sound keep up without being distorted ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
How does it sync normally ? You stream a video service , which contains both video and audio . The two are kept completely separate with only one thing to share : A time stamp . So a video system has a reference clock , and each picture and audio ' frame ' have something called a display time stamp . So digital audio is not embedded inside the digital video frames . They are running alongside with a time stamp that the player can use to sync the video up against the audio . It just receives both , waits until the display time and show the image and play the sound . If you are inserting more video frames , it does n't really matter , as you have to give them new time stamps that fit in between where the existing video frames were . So the video keeps the same time stamps , it just has more of them . And the audio never even realises that something is going on .
When a movie is recorded at 24 fps , that 's the highest frame rate it can be played back at . When played on equipment that refreshes at 60 Hz , the frames have to be duplicated , so you 're seeing each frame two or three times . It 's " two or three times " , because if you simply double each frame you get 48 frames a second . If you triple each frame , you get 72 frames a second . To get 60 fps , you have to double some frames and triple others . That 's if you 're using the NTSC system common in North America and some other countries , which works at a fraction less than 60 fps . If you 're in Europe , you 'll be using the PAL system , which has better colours but a lower frame rate : exactly 50 fps . Movies shot at 24 fps are then simply shown with each frame doubled . This means that an average movie , when shown on TV , will run a few minutes shorter . The sound track will also be speeded up to match so that it does n't go out of sync : this does mean that the sound is very slightly higher in pitch , but if that 's a problem modern digitial sound processing means it can be artificially lowered back to its original pitch . However , most audiences wo n't notice either the * very * slightly increased speed or the * very * slightly higher - pitched audio , so it 's not a problem .
It depends what you mean by played back at 60 fps . Usually all this means is that the the frames of the video are each played multiple times , so the video still looks normal but can play on 60fps screens . Otherwise , it would mean the video is playing back at more than double speed in which case the audio would be out of sync , or need to be sped up .
rejected:
When a movie is played back at a higher frame rate than it was recorded, the extra frames are typically created by duplicating some of the original frames. So for example, if a movie was recorded at 24 frames per second and is played back at 60 frames per second, each original frame would be shown on screen for a shorter period of time, and then repeated two or three more times before the next original frame is shown.

The audio track of the movie is not affected by this process, so it will still be played back at the same rate it was recorded. This means that the audio and the video will be slightly out of sync, with the audio running slightly ahead of the video.

To fix this, the movie's audio track can be sped up slightly, so that it matches the faster frame rate of the video. This will cause the audio to sound slightly higher pitched, but it will still be in sync with the video. Alternatively, the video can be slowed down slightly, so that it matches the audio. This will cause the video to appear slightly slower, but the audio and video will be in sync.

Overall, the process of keeping the audio and video in sync when playing a movie at a higher frame rate involves making slight adjustments to either the audio or the video, so that they both match the desired frame rate.

query: Why are dogs intrigued with anything that smells bad ? I do n't get it . Everything that we find revolting , dogs love . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Humans have around 5 million scent receptors . Dogs have upwards of 100 million or more scent receptors . They can smell * more things * , so what might smell * bad * to us , might smell astoundingly * good * to them . query: The first computer programs How were the first computer programs made without having the benefit from coding platforms ? Were the " card " computers able to make simple programs on the fly ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The bootstrapping problem . What did we compile the first compiler with ? A program no matter how complicated eventually gets transformed into binary opcodes that the CPU understands . Before compilers and assemblers people would use punch cards or even manually flip switches on the computer to enter the binary ones and zeros that they wanted the computer to run . in other words whatever ones and zeros the compiler would have spit out , assuming it actually existed , the programmer used to have to enter into the computer manually to program the computer . They realized this sucks so they made very small and simple assemblers and compilers by hand . Manually entering ones and zeros in that when ran by the computer would act as an assembler . Once they had that they could use the assembler to write a more advanced compiler and so on . The best way to understand it is if you were to write your own compiler today . How do I compile it and get it to run if I have n't written my compiler yet ? Simple use an older compiler that already exists like gcc or something to write your compiler . Once your new more advanced compiler is up and running and well developed you may decide to stop using gcc or whatever you 're using to compile your compiler with and say well hey , my compiler has come far enough it can compile its own code now . Once that occurs and the compiler can compile itself , it is said that the compiler can now bootstrap itself and is referred to as a self hosting compiler .
The first programming languages predate the modern computer . The 19th century saw the invention of " programmable " looms and player piano scrolls , both of which implemented examples of domain - specific languages . By the beginning of the twentieth century , punch cards encoded data and directed mechanical processing . In the 1930s and 1940s , the formalisms of Alonzo Church 's lambda calculus and Alan Turing 's Turing machines provided mathematical abstractions for expressing algorithms ; the lambda calculus remains influential in language design . In the 1940s , the first electrically powered digital computers were created . Grace Hopper , one of the first programmers of the Harvard Mark I computer , developed the first compiler , around 1952 , for a computer programming language .
All it is really is a step by step progression on making the computer do something for you . first it was manual punch cards / assembly , lets have the computer automate that .. than there was whatever ... lets have the computer automate that as well ... and here we are today with a plethora of different languages and/or compilers . so instead of all your really legible code that you would see in most languages today ... you would write the code the function / method would return ... wish i could be more clear .... anyone care to add ?

The first computer programs were written using machine language, which is a set of instructions that can be understood directly by the computer's hardware. These instructions were typically written using a series of 0s and 1s, which represented specific operations that the computer could perform.

To write a program using machine language, a person would have to manually enter the series of 0s and 1s into the computer using punch cards, or by manually flipping switches on the computer's hardware. This was a very tedious and time-consuming process, and it was difficult for people to write complex programs this way.

As computers became more powerful and sophisticated, programming languages were developed that allowed people to write programs using more human-readable instructions. These programming languages were then translated into machine language by a compiler, which made it much easier for people to write and understand computer programs. Today, there are many different programming languages and tools available that make it much easier for people to write and run computer programs.

query: Please explain what is "Data aggregation"
chosen: Data aggregation is the compiling of information from databases with intent to prepare combined datasets for data processing.
rejected:
Data aggregation is the process of collecting, combining, and organizing data from multiple sources. The goal of data aggregation is to provide a summary or overview of the data, which can then be used for various purposes such as analysis, reporting, or decision-making.



There are many different ways to aggregate data, depending on the specific needs of the user. For example, data can be aggregated by summing up values, averaging them, counting the number of occurrences, or finding the maximum or minimum value. Data can also be aggregated by grouping it according to certain criteria, such as location, time period, or category.



Data aggregation is a common practice in many fields, including business, finance, marketing, and scientific research. It can be done manually or using specialized software or tools. Data aggregation is often an important step in the data analysis process, as it allows users to identify patterns, trends, and relationships in the data that might not be immediately apparent when looking at the raw data.

query: Does a wired pc " hog " bandwidth before wifi devices get to it ? Inspired by this post : URL_0 Hope this follows all rules , tried searching but could n't find this question Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
That would depend on the architecture of the router you are using . In any case the router will find it easier to communicate over wire .
I 'm not sure on if it would have priority over sheer bandwidth if someone on wifi was trying to stream HD Netflix at the same time as someone doing it over Ethernet . However , the wired Ethernet * will * have priority in obtaining a connection if there are a dozen wifi devices all trying to get onto a home network that only is typically designed to handle 5 or so WiFi connections at once . This happens at my parent 's house during Christmas when there are a dozen guests over and everyone has a phone and/or iPad all trying to get on the house 's WiFi . The cheap home router ca n't host 20 wireless devices at once so everyone is constantly getting dropped from the signal . Meanwhile , anyone on a wired Ethernet has a solid connection because those are dedicated ports and the router will never " drop " that uplink .
Basically : If your router does that , manufacturers were lazy and it is a bad router . EDIT to add : people do n't seem to understand that a router has software on it that handles these things . Changing a line of code in that software could give wired devices absolute priority , or give wireless devices absolute priority . . . having a more sophisticated and appropriate ( I call it " fair " ) way of handling traffic is the better design . It is an issue of software design quality and will vary from router to router . Fairly dividing traffic over different interfaces ( wired vs wireless ) is a problem for the manufacturer to solve . In some sense , it is their job to make sure it works well . But the practical reality may be that making it work " pretty well " is seen as " good enough " and they leave it at " pretty good , " not perfect . To be honest , it depends on the router . I would call a router badly designed , if it gives wired devices 100 % priority , to the point that wireless devices maybe ca n't even connect to a basic webpage . A lot of the time , your router is not sending or receiving at its maximum capacity . So a lot of the time any device can do its thing on the network regardless of the fair or unfair design . The concept you 're asking about is called " Quality of Service " in the IT industry . More sophisticated and configurable QoS is treated as a special feature and is not necessarily a given .
rejected: It's possible for a wired device to "hog" bandwidth, but it's not a matter of the device itself being wired or not. Instead, it's about how much data the device is trying to transfer at a given time.Imagine that you have a bucket of water, and you want to fill it up as fast as possible. If you have a really big hose, the bucket will fill up faster because more water can flow through the hose at once. Similarly, if you have a really small hose, it will take longer to fill up the bucket because less water can flow through at a time.When it comes to computers and the internet, data is like the water in the bucket and the internet connection is like the hose. If one device is trying to transfer a lot of data all at once (like downloading a big movie), it will use up more of the internet connection, just like a big hose would fill up the bucket faster. This means that other devices might have to wait longer to get their turn to use the internet.So, to answer your question, a wired device might "hog" bandwidth if it's trying to transfer a lot of data all at once, but it's not because the device is wired. It could be a wired device or a wireless device. It just depends on how much data it's trying to transfer.

query: Computer programming and languages Why are there so many different languages , and where should I begin to learn programming ? I have zero experience in programming . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Languages are exactly what they are . Different ways of expressing your desire to communicate something and get some work done ( by a computer ) . Some languages expect you to know every possible detail of what you are doing . Some expect you to know nothing apart from spelling commands correctly . Let 's choose this activity for example : You want your computer to check if a memory ( EEPROM ) chip inside on its motherboard is ready , and report back . In a fictitious assembly language , you might have to do something like this : 1 . Locate address of device driver routine . 2 . Move to routine 3 . Lower chip select by clearing Input / Output pin 35 . 4 . Transmit operation code for " Read " 5 . Transmit address code for " Status word " 6 . Transmit dummy word during which you will receive status word . 6 . Release Chip Select by setting Input / Output pin 35 . 7 . Perform an AND operation with the Status Word to get the Ready Bit . 8 . Depending on the bit , return 1 ( Ready ) or 0 ( not ready ) . In a slightly higher ( abstracted ) language like C , you could do the same thing by calling functions that someone already prepared for you : int Check_Ready ( ) { int ready ; ready = read_EE(statusWord ) & 0x1 ; return ready ; } Now there might be people who would n't want to bother even with this level of knowledge , so they would use a very high level language which promises the highest levels of abstraction and larger library of functions which do lots of things including checking EEPROM 's readiness , and they would write : Start MemoryManager Read a word called " EE Ready " whenever they need status . As you might see , the higher up you go the abstraction scale , the language becomes simpler and more intuitive to use . However its like using a multipurpose screwdriver . You have a lot of tools at your disposal , none of them is as heavy duty and quickly usable as a set of single size screwdrivers . You also end up carrying a lot of weight even when you want to do a simple specific task which needed only two or three sizes of screwdrivers . So in the end , it is up to you to decide what you want to do before picking a programming language . 1 . If the world of microprocessors and hardware fascinates you , please learn general Assembly coding techniques , and likely C programming . 2 . If you do nt want to anything with hardware , but want to develop software that people install on their computers , learn C++ . 3 . If you do nt want to do anything with software , but want to write websites , learn HTML ( for basics ) , Java ( for intermediate ) .. and .. the advanced users are not reading this post anyway . 4 . If you want to do mathematical computing , and fascinating scientific analysis , learn Matlab programming language . There is a screwdriver for every screw out there . Hope this helped .
There are so many programming languages for the same reason there are so many kinds of automobiles . Why does n't everyone in the world drive a simple sedan like a Honda Civic ? * Well what if you wanted to haul a sofa from point A to point B. You could tie it to the roof of your Honda Civic but that 's a lot of work and might not work out so well . So people made pickup trucks . * What if you had to drive across a lot of mud and sand to get to where you want to go . A sedan might get stuck or damaged . So people made Jeeps and Land Rovers . * What if you wanted to get from point A to point B as fast as possible and you knew the roads were very clean and well maintained . So people made sports cars . Why does almost every car maker in the world make a sedan if there already is a company that makes one . Because they are all a little different and they often compete with each other to get better and be more useful . Those are the same reasons there are so many programming languages and why no one programming language is " the " language to learn or the be all end all . They all do different things well . And for example C # and Java are based on similar concepts but are made by sort of competing groups and they push each other to add new features and get better . Most modern programming languages are high level languages . High level languages are abstractions that make it easier for humans to tell a CPU what to do when and how . Every program written in a high level language eventually gets translated into the lowest level language for a particular computer which is machine code ( instruction for a CPU ) . A simple line in a C++ program such as " x = x +1 " gets translated into lots of machine code instructions . For the example I gave the machine code might look something like ( this is very simplified ) : 1 . Fetch x from memory 2 . Put x in box A 3 . Load 1 4 . Add 1 to box A 5 . Write box A to memory Man , this taking too long . I hope this helps . I 'm sure there are better explanations out there . People often suggest C++ and Java as good learning languages because they are close to general purpose languages , tools to write and compile them are free and they cover a lot of broad programming concepts .
/r / learnprogramming This has also been asked a few times before . As for which to begin with , this can differ depending on a few things . For example , if you are a student with a reasonably OK Windows PC , get Visual Studio from DreamSpark and start with C # - or get Visual Studio Express . Other possibilities include PHP , or Java ( and you 'd use NetBeans or Eclipse for Java ) . Why so many ? Different languages are suited to different purposes . C # is a " general purpose " language for example .
rejected:
There are many different programming languages because different languages are better suited for different tasks. For example, some programming languages are better for making websites, while others are better for making apps or operating systems.

If you're just starting out with programming, a good language to begin with is Python. Python is a popular language that is easy to learn and is used in a wide variety of applications, including web development, data analysis, and artificial intelligence.

To get started with Python, you'll need to install it on your computer and then write some code using a text editor or an integrated development environment (IDE). You can find online tutorials and resources to help you learn the basics of Python and how to write simple programs. As you become more comfortable with Python, you can learn more advanced concepts and explore different areas of programming.

query: How are we able to distinguish between loud sounds that are far away and quieter sounds that are nearby ? For example , how can I know that a sound is a firework in the distance and not just a balloon popping outside my house ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
We can not always tell . However , typically distant sounds have a slight echo ( more precisely , they arrive at your ear through multiple paths after bouncing off different things ) , so the sound is n't quite as sharp and clear as if it were a nearby sound .
Your brain is amazingly complex and integrates information from all your senses , as well as past knowledge and assumptions , without you even realizing . For example , your past experience tells you that fireworks are loud up close , and quieter at a distance . When you hear a quieter firework , your brain may automatically interpret it as farther away . Or if you see the firework and know it 's far away , the same thing may occur . Sight and sound , after all , talk to each other .
Also relevant are the frequencies of the sound . Higher frequencies will dissipate more quickly than lower sound . That 's one reason why thunder cracks when it is close and rumbles when distant . Firecrackers close by will have more of the sizzle than those far away .
rejected: The loudness of a sound is called its volume. When a sound is far away, it will usually be quieter than when it is close by. This is because the sound waves have to travel through the air, and they can get weaker as they go. When you hear a sound, your ear can tell how loud it is, and it can also tell how close the sound is coming from.Sometimes it can be hard to tell the difference between a loud sound that is far away and a quieter sound that is nearby. But there are a few things you can look for to help you figure it out.One thing you can try is to cover one ear and listen with the other. When you cover one ear, it is harder to tell where the sound is coming from. If the sound seems to be coming from all around you, it might be a quieter sound that is close by. But if the sound seems to be coming from one direction, it might be a louder sound that is farther away.Another thing you can try is to look for other clues. For example, if you see a bright flash of light when you hear the sound, it might be a firework. If you don't see anything, it might be a balloon popping.I hope that helps! query: How do soccer players curve the parabolic trajectory of the soccer sphere ? Scratch that : ELI4 Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
A ball only follows a parabolic trajectory in the absence of air resistance . A spinning ball deflects airflow around the ball , which creates a force on the ball . This is called the [ Magnus effect ] ( URL_0 ) .
spin on a ball causes a slight difference in pressure which will create a lift force . its the same principle as the lift on an aeroplane wing , except in the case of a wing the pressure difference is created by different surface lengths on the top and bottom of the wing . say a tennis player hits a ground stroke with top spin , the ball is travelling along its trajectory towards the other side of the court but it is also rotating towards the opposing player and down . because the top surface of the ball is rotating " forward " and the bottom surface is rotating " backward " , the top surface is generating more air resistance , creates a high pressure zone compared to the bottom and a force is applied to the ball moving from high to low pressure , that is the force is applied on the ball in a downwards direction because of the high pressure air flow on top of the ball , and the ball drops to the ground faster than it would with no spin or backspin e : the concept is the same in soccer , except the axis of rotation of the ball is perpendicular to the topspin example , and the force generated is also perpendicular

rejected: To curve the ball in soccer, a player kicks the ball with the inside of their foot (the part closest to their other foot) and applies spin to the ball. This makes the ball spin around its center as it moves through the air. Because the ball is spinning, the air flows differently over one side of the ball compared to the other. This difference in airflow causes the ball to curve, or bend, in the direction of the spin. Just like a spinning top, the ball will lean towards the direction it is spinning in. Another way to curve the ball is to kick it with the outside of the foot (the part furthest away from the other foot). This also causes the ball to spin, but in the opposite direction. Kicking the ball with the outside of the foot will cause it to curve in the opposite direction of the spin. It takes a lot of practice and skill to be able to kick the ball with the right amount of spin and force to make it curve the way you want it to. Some players are better at this than others, and it's one of the things that makes soccer such a fun and exciting sport to watch.
