query: Inkblots as psychological test Are inkblots still used for testing ? How does this exactly work and what can be the result ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Originally the idea was what other people are saying . You can infer something about a person by the way in which they interpret an ambiguous image . Doing this now is dangerous and just bad science . I had a number of instructors that were really skilled at using the test and making it useful . I 'm not an apologist in any means , but more so was just heavily exposed to it . Are they used today ? Absolutely . The Rorschach system is heavily misunderstood because it needs to keep the scoring rules and methods of administration secret from the general public . This is referred to as the Exner scoring system and is a synthesis of a time when there were multiple scoring systems . Now there is only one . Wikipedia put all ten of the cards up as well as how the current scoring method works . Personality and IQ tests work hard to keep their materials out of the hands of the general public in order to prevent faking . Most tests including the Rorschach have mechanisms in scoring that check for faking though . You can probably guess why someone would want to fake a test . Contrary to popular belief , interpreting responses is not based on subjective responses using a sort of theory that you think fits . Instead , there is a large amount of statistics used to see what are the most common responses to cards . A number of the cards will get bat or bug responses . Other parts are concerned with what the image or images might be doing . Is the image passive or actively engaged ? Is it a generic item or something more specific ? All of these can speak to a person 's level of cognitive abilities . How detailed are responses ? All of these things are based on norms collected . I believe the norm set has something of over 900 responses collected and established conditions . Some of the images also have variations in shading and color which suggest some neurological conditions , although I ca n't speak to this as much . The system used to score responses is very complex and often simply ignored by psychologists in favor of other types of personality tests . It is a real pain in the ass to score , as in correctly code the responses into the quantitative data necessary to get results . Usually once , you code it you put it into a computer which gives out the the most commonly associated issues with the data . Just about every large personality test works like this . There is a sophisticated amount of statistical analyses run in the software . I 've used the test mostly to confirm diagnostic questions regarding serious mental illness such as psychotic types of behavior including hallucinations . They are also used often in forensic settings to see if there is some suggestion of a Personality Disorder diagnosis . On a side note : You never ever give a diagnosis based on one test . Good diagnostics includes a battery of tests including actually talking to the person in a clinical interview as well as talking to people who know the person . All diagnoses given are ultimately determined by the DSM and nothing else . Tests just support certain ideas and help answer questions .
They 've mostly been forsaken by modern psychologists . Some still use it , but the general consensus is that they 're not really useful for producing an accurate psychological outline of a person . sorry
You show someone something ambiguous , and you observe how they interpret it . The rational is that what they see at a perceptual level will be representative of who they are at " subconscious " level . It is popular in the psychodynamic tradition , which has lost favour with modern psychologist mainly because interpretation is subjective .
rejected:
Inkblots, also known as the Rorschach test, are a type of psychological test that involves showing a person a series of inkblots and asking them to describe what they see in each one. The test is designed to reveal a person's thoughts, feelings, and personality characteristics by analyzing their responses to the inkblots.

The test is administered by a trained professional, who presents the inkblots to the person being tested one at a time and asks them to describe what they see. The person being tested is asked to provide a spontaneous response to each inkblot, without being prompted or influenced by the tester.

The results of the Rorschach test are used to help understand a person's emotional functioning, personality, and cognitive style. The test can provide insight into a person's thoughts, feelings, and behaviors, and may be used to diagnose mental health conditions or to guide treatment.

While the Rorschach test is still used by some mental health professionals, it is not as widely used as it once was, and there is some debate about its reliability and validity as a psychological assessment tool. Some people believe that the test is useful for understanding a person's inner thoughts and feelings, while others argue that the results of the test can be influenced by the tester's own biases and expectations. So, it's important to keep in mind that the Rorschach test is just one tool among many that mental health professionals may use to assess and understand a person's mental health and well-being.

query: If a novel is originally written in a foreign language , how much influence does the translator have on impacting the quality of the book ? I 'm never sure how much credit is owed to the translator . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
A lot . You can rarely just directly translate a language from one into another . If there 's an expression that does n't translate then something else will have to be chosen to make up for it .
Strongly depends on the language , how related the two languages are and the translator . As an interpreter & translator I know that oftentimes you can not translate for example a joke or some sentences , because some languages have words that other languages do n't have and also because stylistically a directly translated text just does n't sound so pretty . Every language has it 's own flow and the trick is to preserve the language flow while translating as correctly and as close to the original as possible . Oftentimes you have to change the words a bit , find equivalents in the language you 're translating ( like for example for common expressions , word - plays ) so that a national would find the text flow to have a natural feel . That 's a lot of fun though :)
Plenty and tons . Most translated works that are popular have been translated multiple times and by multiple people / groups . The translator 's name is usually on the front cover , right next to the original author . If the translation is bad , wrong , different , or otherwise , the subtle word nuances that make good writing good are completely lost , and you go from " Quoth the raven , nevermore " to " The bird said not again " . A proper literal translation , yes , but devoid of any of the literal nuance that makes the writing notable .

query: Is it true that glands in the breast that have produced milk can not get cancer ? How so ? My professor told the class this tidbit . Never heard it before . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
ANY cell can get cancer . Your professor is likely citing a statistic that suggests that breastfeeding lowers chances of cancer significantly , but I think it 's naive to say milk production completely stops cancer development . Cancer is a specific mutation of your cells that basically means your cells are n't told to die anymore . They grow uncontrollably . I 'm not sure of the correlation between breastfeeding ( or milk production ) and cancer growth , but * all * cells can mutate in this way . It 's just that ; like smoking increases the risk of this mutation , so can breastfeeding reduce that risk . Frighteningly , * anyone * can develop cancer . But , to bring this full circle , I do n't see how producing milk necessarily would stop cancer development on its own .
Your professor may be testing to see if you 'll actually call them on their shit . Either that , or they 're full of shit . I say you should thoroughly research the topic and call BS . Either they will be impressed and congratulate you or be pissed and reveal just what sort of asshole they are .
Not true . Had a grandmother die of breast cancer after menopause . She obviously had birthed a child , my mother .
rejected:
It is not true that glands in the breast that have produced milk cannot get cancer. Any part of the body, including the breasts, can potentially develop cancer. 
The breast is made up of various types of cells, including glandular cells, which produce milk, and connective tissue cells. Cancer can develop in either of these types of cells, as well as in other cells in the breast. The most common type of breast cancer is ductal carcinoma, which starts in the cells lining the milk ducts. 
It is important for all individuals to be aware of the signs and symptoms of breast cancer and to practice regular self-exams and seek medical care if they notice any changes in their breasts. It is also important to get regular mammograms and other screenings, as recommended by a healthcare provider, in order to detect breast cancer early and increase the chances of successful treatment.

query: The Great Digg Migration  . I 've seen this phrase several times , concerning a movement of users from " URL_0 " to reddit . Why and what happened ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Digg was pretty much what Reddit is now with a fancier stock interface . Then they made some shitty mistakes , first being the banning of people posting the HD - DVD key . But what really made it all come crumbling down was when they " re launched " digg . They basically said fuck your votes and user generated content , pay us money and we will put your shit on the front page . That 's not sarcasm , that 's what they actually did . There was no point in ever using digg again .
Ok , here 's what went down straight from a former digger . Digg has done a lot of shit in the past that has n't jived well with the users but the biggest gripe that users had for the longest while were the presence of power users . Power users were users who submitted huge amounts of content to the site and thus their submissions were more likely to get to the front page . Also , articles that they dugg would also be more likely to get to the front page . This was a problem as power users would just digg content from other power users and often steal original content and resubmit as their own . All this made it almost impossible for the average user to get any content to the front page . There were also claims that power users were " selling " their diggs , basically accepting payment from a website in order to push their content to the front page . The breaking point finally came when " New Digg " was rolled out . Everyone 's comment and submission history was wiped out . A whole slew of sloppy social networking tools were implemented and instead of being able to browse newly submitted content , all your news feeds would be aggregated by whatever your " friends " were digging ( though it seems they have brought back the new content feed ) . It was clunky and stupid and further reinforced the network that power users utilized to push their content to the front page . On top of that , Digg allowed outside websites like URL_0 and URL_1 to become their * own * power user on the site and push their own content to the front page . It all seemed like it had become just one giant advertising scheme and that website " created by users for users " was no more and so I and many like me jumped ship and washed up here .
The issues were highly complex , in the end , Digg was started as a " get rich quick " scam where they had investors bring in about 70 mil to start the site . They hired like 50 people to run / design the site and then their investors were like " where the fuck is our money ? " when the site was not profitable . This chase for profits led them astray from enriching the user experience and towards grabbing for cash any way possible , including sponsored links and whatnot . This was called " Version 4 " which was basically more or less an entire re - write . What else did they do ... Oh , stories could n't be buried , since they wanted to embrace some sort of hippy culture where there 's no negativity , just like facebook . And they fired their CEO . And Kevin rose tookover and plowed it into the ground even more . Basically , a huge CF related to investors and them taking money which they never should 've gotten . Which was all part of Kevin Roses plan anyway . It seems most business models kind of go like this : Be an internet celebrity / attract a lot of attention . Help rich folk / investors part with their money by giving you their money under the auspices that you are using it to find a company to turn $ X into $ X + $ Y. Remove money from company in the form of salary / subsistence and hope to some day go public where you can really cash in . Retire , not giving 2 fucks whether your contribution to society was positive or not , just revel in the money . People in this class of " entrepeneur " : Kevin Rose Michael Arrington Gizmodo guys you know the lot of them . cult of personality types , attention whores . nothing really of any use . Just an audience and really really bad ideas that other people think are good enough to throw money at .
rejected: The Great Digg Migration refers to a mass movement of users from a website called Digg to a website called Reddit. Digg and Reddit are both online communities where people can share and discuss interesting news and topics. At some point, many users who had been using Digg decided to switch to using Reddit instead. There could have been a number of reasons for this. For example, some users might have preferred the way that Reddit was designed or the types of content that were being shared on the site. Others might have been unhappy with changes that were made to Digg, or with the way that the site was being run. Regardless of the specific reasons, the Great Digg Migration refers to a large number of users moving from Digg to Reddit in a short period of time. This migration likely had a significant impact on both Digg and Reddit, as the movement of so many users would have changed the overall dynamic of each site.

query: Why is global warming a political issue ? I do n't understand why this issue has become so highly politicized . Why is the dialogue aimed more towards party politics than science ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Well , a lot of it stems from the praised / dreaded word : regulation . The praise or dread really depends on which side of the debate you 're on . In a nutshell , Conservative minded people tend to feel that regulation stifles business , growth , and the economy in general . The logic goes that if a company has to jump through a lot of hoops ( regulations ) to do business , they 're going to have to spend more time / effort / resources / money on that , which takes away from other things like providing jobs and boosting the economy . On the other hand , Liberal minded people are n't strictly pro - regulation in all forms ( as in , they are n't just the opposite side of the coin here ) , but in this case , many Liberals see environmental protection as an important concept to integrate in our rules and laws . They see regulation as a necessary step to protect the environment as most companies with free reign are notably careless about their impact on the environment . So on the subject of global warming , it 's often accepted as fact by most Liberals , which encourages their push to create regulations on business -- such as allowed amounts of carbon emissions , waste disposal , etc . So it becomes politicized -- if things are as Conservatives claim , that global warming does n't exist or is over - hyped , then there 's no need for regulation . This is the heart of the debate and why it has gone political .
because anything that effects public policy , tax collection and spending , the use of public natural resources , creates or destroys jobs or anything a voter might have an opinion about is a political issue .
2 reasons : 1 . Money . If pollution causes global warming then industries which pollute are reasonably upset when they 're accused of heating the globe . They 're even more upset when legislation is introduced to slow / stop global warming since they 're the main targets . How to prevent this ? Lobbying , funding studies which lean towards your point of view , etc . Suddenly it 's political . 2 . Regulation . If we , as a people , agree we have a problem with * anything * which is universal , or nearly so , the debate becomes how much should the government have to do with it . Should all citizens have health care ? Should marriage be defined , and , if so , by whom ? You see where I 'm going with this . When we say " Global Warming Is A Global Problem ! " we then have to fight over the best way to control it - through oversight , taxes , incentives , or even if we should try to control it at all . Feeling political yet ? I , for one , feel pretty slimy just typing all this out .
rejected: Global warming is a political issue because it involves the way that people, governments, and societies choose to address the problem of climate change. Climate change is a complex issue that affects many different aspects of our lives, including our economy, our environment, and our health. It requires decisions about how we use energy, how we produce and consume goods, and how we live our lives. Because these decisions have such a big impact on society, they are often influenced by political considerations. Some people may have strong beliefs about the role of government in addressing climate change, and they may support or oppose certain policies based on those beliefs. Others may be more concerned with the economic impacts of climate change and how it could affect their businesses or industries. In short, global warming is a political issue because it involves making choices about how we want to address a complex problem that affects many different aspects of our lives. These choices can be influenced by a variety of factors, including political beliefs, economic interests, and cultural values.

query: Do money markets fluctuate during market crashes?
chosen: Wikipedia has a solid article on Money Market Funds which includes a section on "Breaking the Buck" when the money market fund fails to return its full dollar.  Money market funds smoothing out the daily (generally small) fluctuations of investing in short-term treasuries directly but have similar risk over longer periods.  Some funds can and have lost money in market crashes, though even the worst performers still returned 95+ cents on the dollar. While few investments are guaranteed and likely none in your retirement account,  money-market funds are likely the choice you have with the least fluctuation and similar minimal risk to short term treasuries. However, a second important risk to consider is inflation.  Money market funds generally have returns similar or less than the inflation rate.  While money markets funds help you avoid the fluctuations of the stock market the value of your retirement account falls behind the cost of goods over time.  Unless the investor is fairly old most financial professionals would recommend only a small portion of a retirement account be in money market instruments.  Vanguard also has a set of target retirement investment funds that are close to what many professionals would recommend.  Consulting a financial professional to discuss your particular needs is a good option as well.
rejected: Yes, money markets can fluctuate during market crashes. Money markets refer to financial markets that deal with short-term borrowing and lending of funds, usually with maturities of one year or less. These markets are characterized by low risk and relatively low returns, as the funds are typically invested in highly liquid, low-risk assets such as government securities or high-quality commercial paper.During a market crash, the value of many assets, including those held in money markets, can decline significantly. This can lead to fluctuations in the value of money market instruments, such as certificates of deposit, commercial paper, and Treasury bills. In addition, market participants may become more risk-averse during a market crash, which could lead to a reduction in demand for money market instruments and further fluctuations in their value.It's important to note that money markets are generally considered to be less volatile than other financial markets, such as the stock market, and are often seen as a safer place to park funds during times of market uncertainty. However, they are not completely immune to market fluctuations, and investors should be aware of the potential risks involved in investing in money market instruments.

query: What forms do I need to fill out for a super basic LLC closing?
chosen: If it is a sole proprietorship and  you didn't make another mistake by explicitly asking the IRS to treat it as a corporation - there are no IRS forms to fill. You'll need to dissolve the LLC with your State, though, check the State's department of State/Corporations (depending on the State, the names of the departments dealing with business entities vary).
rejected: To close a limited liability company (LLC), you will need to follow the procedures outlined in your state's LLC laws and your LLC's operating agreement. Depending on your state and the specifics of your LLC, you may need to take some or all of the following steps:Dissolve the LLC: Depending on your state, you may need to file articles of dissolution with the state government to formally dissolve the LLC. You may also need to send a notice of dissolution to any creditors or claimants of the LLC.Settle any outstanding debts: Make sure that all debts and obligations of the LLC have been paid or otherwise settled before you close the LLC. This may include paying off any outstanding loans or bills, as well as distributing any remaining assets to the LLC's members.File final tax returns: You may need to file final tax returns for the LLC, as well as any required state or local tax returns.Cancel any licenses or permits: If your LLC had any licenses or permits, you will need to cancel them. This may include business licenses, liquor licenses, or other permits required to operate your business.Notify any partners or contractors: If you have any partners or contractors who were working with your LLC, you will need to notify them that the LLC is being closed.Close any bank accounts or credit lines: Make sure to close any bank accounts or credit lines that are in the name of the LLC.It's important to note that the specific steps you need to take to close an LLC may vary depending on your state's laws and the specifics of your LLC. It's a good idea to consult with a lawyer or an accountant to make sure you are following all the necessary steps to properly close your LLC.

query: What forms (S-1, 8-K, etc) and keywords in news headlines signify dilution?
chosen: Possibles: stock offering, secondary placement, increase authorized number of shares, shelf registration.
rejected: Dilution refers to a decrease in the ownership interest or control of a company's shareholders as a result of the issuance of new shares of the company's stock. There are several forms and keywords that may indicate dilution in a company's stock:Form S-1: This is a registration statement that a company files with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) when it plans to issue securities, such as stocks or bonds, to the public for the first time through an initial public offering (IPO). The S-1 includes information about the company's financials, business operations, and use of proceeds from the offering. The issuance of new shares of stock through an IPO can lead to dilution for existing shareholders.Form 8-K: This is a report that a company must file with the SEC to disclose certain material events or changes that may be important to shareholders or the market. One type of event that may be disclosed on an 8-K is the issuance of new shares of stock, which could result in dilution for existing shareholders."Stock dilution" or "share dilution": These terms may be used in news headlines or other public statements to indicate that a company is issuing new shares of stock, which may lead to dilution for existing shareholders.It's important to note that dilution is not always negative, and in some cases it may be necessary for a company to issue new shares of stock to raise capital or finance growth. However, dilution can also dilute the value of existing shares and may be viewed negatively by shareholders.

query: How does a citizens arrest work ? I keep hearing about it , but I 'm not sure what gives someone the legal right to do this , or or under what circumstance . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Essentially it 's the citizen 's right to detain someone that has obviously broken the law until the authorities arrive . It can range from a security guard detaining a trespasser until the cops show up , to a homeowner locking a burglar in the pantry , to my personal favourite of a concerned citizen using a zip - tie to essentially handcuff a guy who left a kid and dog in his truck on a hot day . For the most part , if you stop a criminal that you know is committing a crime , without using excessive force , then it 's unlikely that there are going to be any charges pressed .
* * In Canada * * , you may only make a citizen 's arrest if you find someone who is / has ( i ) escaping from police ( ii ) committed an indictable offence ( more serious offence , like a felony in the US ) or ( iii ) having committed any crime against your property , property you are in possession of , or property in which the owner has given you the authority to protect ( think mall security ) . From the URL_0 site : In most cases , you must find a person either in the act of committing a crime , or escaping from and freshly pursued by persons who have lawful authority to arrest that person , in order to lawfully make a citizen 's arrest . In particular , if you are arresting a person for an indictable offence , which is the most serious type of offence and includes violent offences , you can only make the arrest at the time you witness the person committing the offence . It is against the law to arrest a person after any lapse in time for having committed an indictable offence , unless it is relation to your property . Extreme care should be taken while making a citizen 's arrest .
How Stuff Works . Com has a great podcast on it and they make it really easy to understand :)
rejected: A citizen's arrest is when a private citizen detains or arrests someone who they have probable cause to believe has committed a crime. In most places, a citizen's arrest is only allowed if the crime is a felony (a serious crime) and if it is happening right in front of you. Here's an example: Let's say you are at a store and you see someone steal something. You have probable cause to believe that the person committed a crime because you saw it happen. In this case, you could make a citizen's arrest by detaining the person until the police arrive. It's important to remember that a citizen's arrest is a serious matter and can be dangerous. If you are thinking about making a citizen's arrest, you should consider whether it is safe for you to do so and whether it is necessary. If you are unsure, it is always a good idea to call the police and let them handle the situation.

query: Could someone please provide an example of a portfolio similiar to the GFP or Couch potato, but for Australia?
chosen: The portfolio described in that post has a blend of small slices of Vanguard sector funds, such as Vanguard Pacific Stock Index (VPACX).  And the theory is that rebalancing across them will give you a good risk-return tradeoff.  (Caveat: I haven't read the book, only the post you link to.) Similar ETFs are available from Vanguard, iShares, and State Street.  If you want to replicate the GFP exactly, pick from them.  (If you have questions about how to match specific funds in Australia, just ask another question.)  So I think you could match it fairly exactly if you wanted to. However, I think trying to exactly replicate the Gone Fishin Portfolio in Australia would not be a good move for most people, for a few reasons: Brokerage and management fees are generally higher in Australia (smaller market), so dividing your investment across ten different securities, and rebalancing, is going to be somewhat more expensive. If you have a "middle-class-sized" portfolio of somewhere in the tens of thousands to low millions of dollars, you're cutting it into fairly small slices to manually allocate 5% to various sectors.  To keep brokerage costs low you probably want to buy each ETF only once every one-two years or so.  You also need to keep track of the tax consequences of each of them. If you are earning and spending Australian dollars, and looking at the portfolio in Australian dollars, a lot of those assets are going to move together as the Australian dollar moves, regardless of changes in the underlying assets.  So there is effectively less diversification than you would have in the US. The post doesn't mention the GFP's approach to tax.  I expect they do consider it, but it's not going to be directly applicable to Australia. If you are more interested in implementing the general approach of GFP rather than the specific details, what I would recommend is: The Vanguard and superannuation diversified funds have a very similar internal split to the GFP with a mix of local, first-world and emerging market shares, bonds, and property trusts.  This is pretty much fire-and-forget: contribute every month and they will take care of rebalancing, spreading across asset classes, and tax calculations.  By my calculations the cost is very similar, the diversification is very similar, and it's much easier. The only thing they don't generally cover is a precious metals allocation, and if you want that, just put 5% of your money into the ASX:GOLD ETF, or something similar.
rejected: The Australian version of the Couch Potato portfolio is a portfolio that is made up of index funds that track the Australian stock market and the Australian bond market. A typical Couch Potato portfolio for Australia might include the following funds:Vanguard Australian Shares Index FundVanguard Australian Fixed Interest Index FundTo create a portfolio similar to the Global Couch Potato, you could include international index funds in addition to the above funds. For example:Vanguard International Shares Index Fund (hedged)This portfolio would be well-diversified, low-cost, and easy to manage. It would be suitable for a long-term, buy-and-hold investment strategy.

query: The " Fiscal Cliff " for us that are totally confused . I keep hearing about it on news shows and see some articles on it but I 'm still pretty lost . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Last year , when the congress was so deadlocked , they could n't even agree on what should have been a routine debt ceiling increase . The Democrats wanted to increase revenue and continue the stimulus , the Republicans wanted to cut taxes and spending . So they struck a perverse deal . They would pass the debt ceiling bill , and agree that a super - committee would try to come up with a deal for the other stuff . And to motivate the committee , if it did n't reach a decision , a bunch of across the board tax increases and spending cuts would automatically take effect . To no one 's surprise , the committee did n't reach an agreement , and so no one would look bad , our brave politicians waited until after the election to deal with it . So now we call the automatic stuff the Fiscal Cliff .
If you followed the Presidential Campaigns , you saw both candidates talking about how they would cut the deficit . Cutting the deficit gets you a lot of votes and you put a time limit on it so that , hopefully , cooler heads will prevail and a better proposal will get accepted . If it helps , think of it in terms of the players strikes and the lockouts in professional sports . Well , one of these proposals is about to become approved because the date it is due to become law is going to arrive and soon . Congressmen have not tried to change the upcoming deficit reduction plan because they are either the ones who voted to approve it , or they ca n't come up with a politically correct way to justify the huge deficit spending . What will result if another plan is not approved in the next twenty days or so is that most middle class people will pay about $ 2000 more a year in taxes . What will happen is that at the last minute Congress will approve a plan to delay the start of the deficit reduction bill for a few more years or at least months to give the problem to someone else . Same as anyone with a hot potato in his lap .
Congress negotiated a deal last summer that would have deep cuts in federal budgets should they be unable to come to a more reasonable agreement with regard to spending . These cuts are coinciding with an existing expiration of tax cuts . So we 're looking at a HUGE amount of money about to be taken out of an economy that 's starting to get it 's wits about it again . This could cause another economic downturn if it 's not avoided .
rejected:
The "fiscal cliff" refers to a set of tax increases and spending cuts that were set to take effect at the beginning of 2013. These measures were put in place as part of a previous budget agreement, but they were designed to be so unpopular that they would force politicians to come up with a new budget plan before they took effect.

The main concern was that the tax increases and spending cuts would have a negative impact on the economy, potentially leading to a recession. For example, the tax increases would have meant that many people would have had to pay more in taxes, which would have reduced their disposable income and potentially led to a decrease in consumer spending. Similarly, the spending cuts would have reduced government spending, which could have led to job losses and a decrease in economic activity.

In the end, politicians did come up with a new budget plan that avoided the fiscal cliff, but the issue continues to be a source of debate and concern in the United States.

query: Nuclear Fission Reactors : How is there a net gain in energy from both fusion AND fission ? I thought one of the processes resulted in a net gain , and the other resulted in a net loss . What am I missing here ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
You gain energy from fusing elements lighter than iron . You gain energy from * splitting * elements * heavier * than iron . Iron is at the peak of [ nuclear binding energy ] ( URL_0 ) ( the energy need to break nuclear bonds ) , which means that moving closer to iron , by either fission or fusion depending on the direction you 're coming from , releases energy .
You 're missing the idea that fusion processes that give energy and fission processes that give energy * start with different fuel nuclei * . Iron-56 is the isotope that is the most strongly bound : it has the lowest energy per nucleon ( proton or neutron ) of any possible isotope . If you imagine all possible nuclei as a surface , Iron-56 is the lowest part of the valley . No matter what kind of nucleus you start with , moving towards Iron-56 will release energy because you 're moving from higher energies to lower energies : you 're rolling down the valley towards the lowest spot . On the other hand , moving * away * from Iron-56 costs energy . You need to add energy to go up the hills surrounding the valley . If you are lighter than Iron-56 , then adding mass ( fusion ) usually results in a heavier nucleus and releases energy , while fission takes you farther away and costs energy . If you are more than twice as heavy as Iron-56 , then * fission * results in 2 lighter nuclei that are closer to Iron-56 and so releases energy , but trying to add ( fusion ) more mass onto the nucleus takes you farther away from the lowest energy point .
If you 're using one particular element , yes , one results in a net gain and one in a net loss . But you 're not ; fusion reactors use elements that release energy when they fuse , and fission reactors use elements that release energy when they split .
rejected:
In a nuclear fission reaction, a heavy atom such as uranium or plutonium is split into two smaller atoms. This process releases a large amount of energy, because the combined mass of the two smaller atoms is less than the mass of the original, heavier atom. The difference in mass is converted into energy, according to Einstein's famous equation, E=mc^2.

In a nuclear fusion reaction, two light atoms such as hydrogen are combined to form a heavier atom. This process also releases a large amount of energy, because the mass of the heavier atom is less than the combined mass of the two lighter atoms. Again, the difference in mass is converted into energy.

Both nuclear fission and nuclear fusion reactions release energy because they involve the conversion of a small amount of matter into a large amount of energy. The net result is a gain in energy. However, it is more difficult to achieve and maintain the conditions necessary for nuclear fusion to occur, so most nuclear power plants use nuclear fission reactions to generate electricity.

There are a few different ways that freshwater fish species can disperse to new areas:

query: Please explain what is "Soar (cognitive architecture)"
chosen:
Soar is a cognitive architecture, originally created by John Laird, Allen Newell, and Paul Rosenbloom at Carnegie Mellon University. (Rosenbloom continued to serve as co-principal investigator after moving to Stanford University, then to the University of Southern California's Information Sciences Institute.) It is now maintained and developed by John Laird's research group at the University of Michigan.
The goal of the Soar project is to develop the fixed computational building blocks necessary for general intelligent agents – agents that can perform a wide range of tasks and encode, use, and learn all types of knowledge to realize the full range of cognitive capabilities found in humans, such as decision making, problem solving, planning, and natural-language understanding. It is both a theory of what cognition is and a computational implementation of that theory. Since its beginnings in 1983 as John Laird’s thesis, it has been widely used by AI researchers to create intelligent agents and cognitive models of different aspects of human behavior. The most current and comprehensive description of Soar is the 2012 book, The Soar Cognitive Architecture.

Soar is a cognitive architecture, which is a framework for building intelligent systems. It was developed by John Laird, Allan Newell, and Paul Rosenbloom at Carnegie Mellon University in the 1980s and has been further developed and refined since then.



The main idea behind Soar is to model the way that the human mind works, with the goal of creating intelligent systems that can think and learn in a way that is similar to humans. Soar models human cognition at a high level of abstraction, including processes such as problem solving, decision making, and learning.



Soar represents knowledge in a hierarchical structure called a "production system," which consists of a set of "rules" that can be triggered by specific conditions. These rules can be thought of as if-then statements, with the "if" part being the condition and the "then" part being the action that is taken. When a rule is triggered, it can execute a sequence of actions and also modify the current state of the system.



Soar has been applied to a wide range of problems, including natural language processing, robotics, and simulation. It has also been used to build intelligent tutoring systems and to study the way that humans solve problems.

query: what is logic ? Almost everyone throws it around in a debate and claims to use it but do you really know what it is ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
In the debating sense , using logical arguments basically means that you 're able to provide a reasoned explanation for your point of view which does n't rely on logical fallacies . Some examples of logical fallacies include : * * Strawman * * arguments : When you misrepresent the other person 's argument so that it 's easier to attack , e.g. " You 're saying that < misrepresentation of what the person said > , but clearly that 's not true because < argument of why misrepresented statement is false > . " * * Ad Hominem * * arguments : When you attack the individual 's character rather than the argument . E.g. bringing up the fact that the individual has used illegal drugs before , and trying to make it sound like he / she is therefore not credible . * * Loaded Questions * * : When you ask your opponent a question which has a loaded assumption built into it and ca n't reasonably be answered without appearing guilty . E.g. asking someone about their drug problems makes other people ( observers of the debate ) assume the person has a substance abuse problem and it makes it difficult for the person to answer without appearing guilty of being involved in drug problems ( even if they do n't even use drugs ) . * * Slippery Slope * * arguments : An example of this is saying something like " If we allow gays to get married , it 's going to cause our children to become gay . " The argument does n't really make sense , the false presumption is that allowing one thing to happen will cause another extreme thing to happen , so the first thing should never be allowed to happen . * * ' False Dichotomy ' or ' Black or White ' or ' Either - or ' * * arguments : Where you present two sides of an argument and claim that everyone must either be on one side of the argument or the other and that no middle - ground exists . E.g. If you support this new national - security legislation you are patriotic , if you do n't support the bill you must be unpatriotic . * * Anecdotal * * arguments : Using personal ( isolated ) stories / experience as the basis for your argument while ( typically ) denying any scientific or statistical evidence that counters your position . * * False Cause * * arguments : Arguing that one thing must cause the other because they are correlated . For example , students with high self - esteem tend to have higher IQs , so you may argue that self - esteem affects your IQ and that by increasing self - esteem we can boost students ' IQs . In practice , this does n't work . Boosting self - esteem just causes students to become over - confident in their abilities and no more intelligent than they were before . In reality , students who get high grades tend to have higher self - esteem and higher IQs than students who have low grades , thus self - esteem seems to be related to IQ , but self - esteem does not cause IQ . There are many more examples of logical fallacies , these are just some of the common ones .

query: Why is the Tenth Amendment ignored ? When did it go by the wayside ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
For the lazy : " The powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution , nor prohibited by it to the States , are reserved to the States respectively , or to the people . " Basically- if some sort of power is n't explicitly given to the executive branch then it belongs to the states ( unless the Constitution says its prohibited , like slavery ) .
It 's all about how you define " by the Constitution " . Basically it comes down to whether or not you think the Constitution should be interpreted and precedent should be considered or the wording should be explicitly followed . For instance , the federal government regulated the production of ADHD medication . This is usually done underneath the auspices of the Commerce Clause ( Article I , Section 8 , Clause 3 ) , saying that since the medicine can ( but might not ) be produced in one state and sold in another that the federal government can regulate the product . The constitution does n't actually mention the regulation of medicine or Adderall and an extremely strict constructionist would insist that the US government has no ability or right to decide what can be in the medication or who can receive it . They would leave this up to the states individually to regulate this . This would seem ridiculous , but it 's one example of skirting around the 10th amendment .
The Civil War was basically the very violent verdict on the scope of the 10th Amendment being delivered .
rejected: The Tenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is part of the Bill of Rights and it states that any powers not delegated to the federal government by the Constitution, nor prohibited to it by the Constitution, are reserved for the states or the people. This means that the federal government only has the powers that are specifically listed in the Constitution, and any other powers are reserved for the states or the people. The Tenth Amendment is not necessarily ignored, but it is sometimes interpreted differently by different people. Some people believe that the federal government has the power to do certain things that are not specifically listed in the Constitution, while others believe that the states or the people have more power. This can lead to disagreement and debate about the proper role of the federal government in different areas of life. It is also worth noting that the Constitution and the Bill of Rights, including the Tenth Amendment, are not necessarily set in stone and can be amended or changed over time through the process of constitutional amendment. This process is intended to allow for the Constitution to be amended in order to keep it relevant and responsive to the needs and concerns of the people.

query: What does Big Data really do with our data ? I want honest , true answers please . No fear - mongering or anti - corporate propaganda . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
" Big Data " is n't an entity so it does nothing with our data . " Big Data " refers to the concept of using large amounts of data to draw conclusions or direct action . Some company might collect " Big Data " about their customers and then try to draw conclusions about their customer base , such as what products to carry or advertise . Similarly selling that data to other companies would be profitable , and buying such data could in turn help grow a business .
Big Data is n't an entity . It 's just the phrase used to refer to the existence of comprehensive collections of data , most of it taken from public record or purchased legally from companies you 've allowed to have the info . Data brokers develop lists which incorporate info from many sources to create profiles of individuals , so they can sell the names and addresses back to companies that want to reach , say , married women with no kids and a house worth > $ 100 K ( or whatever ) . Basically , this is all about selling you more stuff , more efficiently . It 's not a plot . There is another use of the word , which involves reporters synthesizing huge public databases to investigate whether public agencies and politicians are working for the public or for themselves .
They mostly use it to figure out what kind of person you are so they can advertise best to . If they know you are a parent and know your income and know the age of your kids , then they will make sure you are getting an ad for the brand new toy for your kid 's age . Target did an interesting thing regarding that . There are certain items that newly pregnant women will buy . So if you buy those things , you 'd get a special advertisement in the mail with a bunch of prenatal items . They [ got in trouble ] ( URL_0 ) a few years ago for it . What they do now is they 'll send an ad with a bunch of sale prices for random things with a bunch of pregnancy stuff thrown in there so it does n't look so creepy .

Big data refers to the large amounts of data that companies and organizations collect about their customers and operations. This data is often collected from various sources, such as social media, websites, and sensors, and is used to help organizations make better business decisions.

There are many different ways that organizations use big data. Some examples include:

Personalization: Companies can use big data to personalize their products or services for individual customers. For example, a retail website might use big data to recommend products to a customer based on their past purchases and browsing history.

Predictive analytics: Organizations can use big data to make predictions about future events or trends. For example, a bank might use big data to predict which of its customers are most likely to default on a loan.

Improving efficiency: Big data can be used to identify inefficiencies in business processes and help organizations become more efficient. For example, a shipping company might use big data to optimize its delivery routes and reduce fuel costs.

Fraud detection: Big data can be used to identify fraudulent activity, such as credit card fraud or insurance fraud.

Overall, big data is used to help organizations make more informed decisions and improve their operations. It is important to note that organizations are generally required to follow privacy laws and regulations when collecting and using big data.

query: What s the big deal about HTML5 Also , any sites that use it already ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
[ NLY5 ] Lots of sites are using it at least a little . You would n't necessarily know it to see it though . In HTML4 , if you wanted a video , you had to reach for Flash or Silverlight and embed the video in a site with code ( think about the old cut - and - paste stuff to embed content that YouTube would provide ) . In HTML5 , there are audio and video elements integrated into the language - no external plugins required . There 's drag - and - drop capability ( like how you can drag and drop a file into SkyDrive in your browser , or the way gmail has integrated drag - and - drop ) , so there 's more interaction programmed in . There 's also a move towards offline stuff . For example , you could write something in Office365 or GoogleDocs even with no internet connection and then it would sync the next time you 're online .
This is a bit of a complex question so I 'll answer it as best I can . --- * * New " semantic " tags * * HTML5 is the next step in creating a more " semantic web , " meaning that browsers can more easily tell what format certain data is in . In the past , web pages have been structured using something called " divs " ( which , in code is the < div > tag ) . In order to differentiate them , most coders use an attribute within the < div > tag , known as " i d " or " class " . This is like giving a label to that div so it 's known by a specific name or group . ( For example : if I have a div that will serve as the header of my page , I might use the tag < div id="header " > . ) This is all fine and well , but when a browser reads this code , it ca n't understand that this specific div is meant to be the header of the page . It just knows that it 's called " header . " In HTML5 , there is actually a tag now called < header > . This adds " semantic value , " or meaning , to the tag itself , meaning that it 's easier for a browser that reads the code to know that this section is the header of a page . So , why is that important ? It 's for the sake of search engines like Google , who can filter through certain parts of a page when you 're looking for something and improve the quality and the accuracy of your search results , making it easier for you to find what you 're looking for . This same concept applies to a lot of other elements of the page . Instead of using a generic < object > tag that could be one of many things ( images , audio , video , Flash , etc . ) , you can now use semantic tags called < video > or < audio > . So , instead of the browser reading the code and saying " okay , this is an object , now what type is it ? " , it will say " okay , this is clearly a video . " --- * * The canvas tag * * Think about all of the fancy animations and such that Flash webpages are able to do . The canvas tag will allow web designers / developers to achieve many of those same effects using just HTML and JavaScript code ( JavaScript being another powerful web language that works with HTML to create complex actions and applications ) instead of needing to use Flash , which can be slow to load and bandwidth - heavy . Why is this a good thing ? It simplifies the process of designing rich graphics and interactive elements without needing to learn and resort to a third - party technology like Flash . It 's also more portable and scalable to mobile browsing , whereas Flash has had well - documented problems making the jump to smartphones . --- * * Application caching * * Ever been doing something on a webpage ( like using an application or filling out a form ) and your internet connection went out , so you lose your data and ca n't finish what you were doing ? HTML5 solves that problem by using what 's called " application caching . " Basically , when the browser reads the code and comes across a tag that looks like this : < html manifest="filename.manifest " > ... it will read the manifest file to figure out what files a specific webapp needs to run . It will then download them and store them on your computer so that if you need to use the webapp offline , it will still function . Why is this a good thing ? Obviously , it allows for a lot more flexibility in terms of how webapps are used . If you 're on the go and you ca n't get WiFi or 3 G coverage , you can still use those webapps to accomplish various things without needing an internet connection . --- Those are just a couple of reasons , but they 're what I 'd call the major ones . It 's really about helping code writers write cleaner , simpler and less code , while improving HTML 's capabilities and organizing data better so it 's easier to sort and find . HTML5 is still in development and is n't scheduled to be finalized until around 2022 , but many of its abilities are already being implemented into more progressive webpages . The drawback is that browsers do n't have full capability to understand everything that HTML5 tries to do , so some of it breaks . When you 're designing a webpage , you need to consider " cross - browser compatibility , " meaning that you make sure the page will look the same and work the same , no matter what browser or version someone is using . Internet Explorer 6 is notoriously bad for developers because it does n't support many of the newer features and many people ( usually older people or non - computer savvy people ) do n't understand the importance of upgrading their browser , so web developers will need to cater to the lowest common denominator in many cases . Hope this explanation helps . I did n't cover everything , but maybe this will explain the general reason why HTML5 is a " big deal . "
[ Facetheduke ] ( URL_0 ) explains it pretty well . As for websites that use it , Youtube actually has an [ HTML5 beta ] ( URL_1 ) that you can opt into . That 's the only one I know that has HTML5 support , I know Apple has an " HTML5 Demo " but they restricted it to Safari , despite Chrome and Firefox now supporting HTML5 .

HTML5 is a big deal because it allows web developers to create more interactive and engaging websites. query: Why do chef 's cook seafood alive ? I 'm no PETA activist , I was just wondering if it actually changes the taste , texture , etc . * * Edit 1 :* * Shellfood * * Edit 2 :* * Omg grammar , chefs * Explain like I'm five.

query: Why are the three primary colours on video red , blue and green ? As aposed to red , blue , yellow like with something like paint ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Hey , this is the first time i 've come into one of these threads and all the answer posts have been wrong . I 'm not an expert , but I 'll try to muddle through . The light receptor cells in the human eye do not correspond to red , green , and blue ; they would be better described as yellow green and violet . Our brain then identifies what color the light is by looking at the relative responses of those three types of cells . [ here ] ( URL_0 ) is an image that shows the absorption spectra of the three types of human cone cells . So , if light entered your eye in around the 555 nm range , the M(green ) and L(yellow ) cells would be strongly activated , while the S(blue ) cells would not . Your brain would therefore interpret this as yellowish green light . There is no cell that corresponds to red light , but when red light strikes the retina the L cells are most strongly activated , then the M cells less strongly , and the S cells least of all . The brain interprets this as red light . Incidentally , I believe the fact that the L and M cells are so " close " on the spectrum explains why the green to orange portion of the spectrum is the easiest to differentiate . query: Why can we easily distinguish between faces in photorealistic games and real faces ? Graphics have improved a lot , I admit . Many photorealistic games are indistinguishable from actual photos , except when human faces are there . What exactly is the difference ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:

Humans are highly adapted to recognize slight differences in human faces because it is very useful for humans to tell humans apart . Crows for example might look pretty much all the same to humans but crows have no trouble telling each other apart as that is what they are adapted to do . So the reason human faces are so hard to replicate is because humans are unusually picky about them in particular .
Skin , and faces in particular , are very hard to model accurately . Skin is n't opaque - there 's a sheen and surface color but a lot of light goes into the skin and comes out again in interesting ways . See URL_0 Eyes have * very * complex optics , also in multiple layers . Muscles underly the skin and have to be modeled accurately for different facial expressions to look right . Wrinkles ca n't really be modeled , they have to be captured and cleaned up into " wrinkle maps " for key expressions and then interpolated . Blood flow ( flushing ) moves around the face as the muscles move around and with emotion . Flush maps are also manually authored by artists . Teeth are complex optically as well , and I do n't think a lot of attention has been paid to that yet . They always look a bit wooden .
While other answers has their logic in it . I think , in video games or CGs at least , it 's the animation . If it 's just a still image . Good artists can easily fool people into thinking it 's a real person . With animation , it 's much harder . Human face has 42 muscles in that tiny space ( compare to body ) . They move together when you talk or make facial expressions . Even tho textures can be drawn basically identical to real human skin . It 's extremely hard to animate all these muscle movement accurately , which result in unnatural facial movements and that 's how we notice it .

There are a few reasons why it might be easier to tell the difference between a face in a photorealistic game and a real face, even though the graphics in some games are very realistic. One reason is that the way light reflects off of a real face is often more complex and subtle than the way it is simulated in a game. In real life, the way light interacts with the various textures and shapes of a face (such as wrinkles, pores, and bumps) creates a unique set of highlights and shadows that help give a face its distinctive appearance. These subtle lighting effects are often difficult to reproduce accurately in a game, so even if the overall shape and color of a face in a game look realistic, the lighting might not be quite right.

Another reason is that faces in real life are often more expressive and animated than those in games. Real faces move and change in response to emotions, speech, and other factors, which can make them more distinctive and easier to recognize. In contrast, the faces of characters in games are often more static and less expressive, which can make them seem less realistic and more artificial.

Finally, it's also worth noting that our brains are very good at recognizing and distinguishing between different faces, and we have a lot of practice doing so. This might make it easier for us to spot the differences between a real face and a simulated one, even if the simulation is very realistic. So, even though photorealistic games can look very convincing, there are still some subtle differences that our brains are able to pick up on and use to distinguish between real and simulated faces.

query: Please explain what is "Software engineering"

Engineering techniques are used to inform[clarification needed] the software development process which involves the definition, implementation, assessment, measurement, management, change, and improvement of the software life cycle process itself. Modern processes use software versioning.
rejected:

Software engineering involves the use of a variety of tools, techniques, and processes to design, implement, test, and maintain software systems. It encompasses a wide range of activities, including requirements gathering, design, implementation, testing, debugging, and deployment.



Software engineers work in a variety of settings, including software development companies, IT departments, and consulting firms. They may be involved in the development of applications for a variety of platforms, including desktop, web, mobile, and embedded systems.



Some of the key principles of software engineering include the use of a systematic and disciplined approach to software development, the use of modeling and design techniques to create and communicate software designs, and the use of testing and debugging techniques to ensure the quality and reliability of software products.



Overall, the goal of software engineering is to produce high-quality software systems that are reliable, efficient, maintainable, and scalable, and that meet the needs of the users in a cost-effective manner.

This means that you won't have to pay back most of your debts.

However, student loans are generally not dischargeable in bankruptcy. This means that you will still have to pay back your student loans, even if you file for bankruptcy. Your credit score will also be affected by bankruptcy, and it can be difficult to get approved for loans or credit cards in the future.

When you file for bankruptcy, your personal belongings are not usually seized. However, if you have valuable assets, such as a house or a car, the bankruptcy trustee may be able to sell them to pay off your debts.

In short, filing for bankruptcy can help you get relief from some of your debts, but it won't eliminate all of your debts and it can have long-term effects on your finances. It's important to consider all of your options and to seek professional advice before making a decision about whether to file for bankruptcy.

query: The strawman fallacy Heard numerous times and I swear every time I read it from somewhere I never get it . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Your mom says you ca n't have any cookies before dinner because you will spoil your appetite . You complain to your dad that your mom will not let you eat anything because she wants you to starve . This was not your mother 's argument , but your dad hears this and storms into the kitchen wanting to know why she insists on starving you . Your mom tells him that 's not what she said at all , that her position was simply that you could not have any cookies before dinner , and your dad nods sagely at this . Realizing you misrepresented your mother 's claims , your father sentences you to stand in the field to scare off crows while you think about what you 've done . \*Au . Thank you .
The best way to think about it is instead of arguing what the person is saying , you argue a stereotype of that position . It involves making assumptions about their positions and arguing against those assumptions . I tried to come up with my own example but wikipedia 's is pretty good " A : We should relax the laws on beer . B : ' No , any society with unrestricted access to intoxicants loses its work ethic and goes only for immediate gratification . " So A is saying we should relax beer laws , but B is arguing * as if * A said we should eliminate laws regulating intoxicants entirely . B is setting up a man made of straw because that 's easier to beat than a real man ( or woman or whatever ) . EDIT : Now this is not to be confused with attacking the underlying assumptions or implicit arguments of your opponent , which can also be important in debate and there is sometimes a fine line there .
A Straw Man is a misrepresentation or complete invention of your opponents argument in order to make dismantling of that argument easier . A common example is a person who thinks we need tighter border regulation and need to crack down on illegal immigration . The Straw Man argument often brought up against this position is , " So and so is against immigration . Does n't he know we were a nation founded on immigrants ? " The Straw Man in this case is that person A is not against immigration , but a specific type , namely illegal immigration . So person B misrepresents his position and creates the lie that person A is opposed to all immigration .
rejected: Sure! A strawman fallacy is when someone argues against a weaker version of someone else's argument, rather than the actual argument that was made. This is often done to make it easier to win the argument, because the weaker version of the argument is easier to attack.For example, imagine that you and I are having a debate about whether or not people should be allowed to own guns. You argue that people should be allowed to own guns because it is their constitutional right. I might respond by saying something like "well, owning a gun doesn't guarantee your safety, so there's no point in letting people have them." This response doesn't address your actual argument that owning guns is a constitutional right. Instead, it attacks a weaker version of your argument (that owning a gun will make you safe). This is a strawman fallacy.
