query: How does electricity always travel the path of least resistance ? How do the electrons " know " which path will offer the least resistance ? or do they go everywhere , but only flow in the path of least resistance ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
This is probably best explained by analogy . Think of electrons flowing through a circuit like water flowing through pipes . The resistance of the wire the electrons see is like the diameter of the pipes the water flows through . Now , take a single wire / pipe and have it come to a junction . One path contains a 100 Ohm resistor/10 inch pipe diameter pipe . The second path contains a 500 Ohm resistor/1 inch pipe . After diverging , the wires / pipes eventually join back together . The same amount of electrons / water is going to flow in the wire / pipe before the divergence as after the convergence . So , all the electrons / water will flow through either the first or second path . What will happen is that the majority will flow through the first path ( path of least resistance ) simply because it is easier to do so . But , at the same time a little bit will flow through the second path simply because it is also available to be used . Why not send a little bit down the second path and the majority down the first since it will speed up the overall flow . The electrons / water do n't actually know which is the path of least resistance . If all the water tried to flow through the second path there would be a buildup and things would slow down ( ignoring pressure/ flow rate changes that would normally occur ) . If all the water went through the first path it would also experience a slight backup and buildup but to a much lesser extent than the second path . In reality the water will flow through both paths with the majority going down the first because it is easiest and a little bit going down the second because it it also available .
> How do the electrons " know " which path will offer the least resistance ? They do n't . > or do they go everywhere , but only flow in the path of least resistance ? They attempt to flow through all paths , but the path of least resistance can accommodate more electrons per second ( that 's what makes it the path of least resistance ) , so most of the electrons go that way .
Current flows through all available paths . If you wire up a 100 - ohm resistor and a 300 - ohm resistor in parallel , the current does not magically select the 100 - ohm path . Instead , it flows through both paths and the circuit offers a net 75 - ohm resistance .
rejected:
Electricity is the flow of tiny particles called electrons through materials that allow them to move freely, called conductors. These materials can be metals like copper or aluminum, or other substances like water or saltwater. When electricity flows through a conductor, it is like a river flowing through a pipe. The path of least resistance is like a wide, smooth pipe that the water can flow through easily, while a path with more resistance is like a narrow, rough pipe that the water has to push through.

When electricity is flowing through a circuit, it will always try to find the path of least resistance to follow. This is because it is easier for the electrons to flow through a conductor that has a low resistance. If there is a choice between two paths, the electricity will choose the one with the lower resistance. This is because the electrons can flow more easily and use less energy to get to their destination.

So to answer your question, the electrons do not "know" which path will offer the least resistance. They just naturally follow the path that is easiest for them to flow through, and that is usually the path with the lowest resistance.

query: Google v. Oracle It sounds like there have been several such bouts of fisticuffs between the two companies . Can someone explain the latest court victory and put it in context for me ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Google helped develop android and some of the coders * may * have used some code from the Java language ( some test files that do n't ship in android and 9 lines of code ) that was purchased by oracle . They sued Google , and the jurors could n't definitely say that Google infringed . I * think * oracle is awarded no damages except for a deal struck between the two companies . Oracle will appeal the decision and it will now be drug on in the courtroom for the next year or so .
There were two phases of the suit so far in court - a copyrights phase , and a patents phase . The copyright phase had mostly to do with the fact that google wrote it 's own stuff for Android that was a re - implementation of Java 's APIs , and Google was found guilty on one part , and the jury could n't come up with an answer for the second . Even this could be meaningless , however , since a core issue is whether APIs are " copyrightable " and the judge has n't decided it yet - if he finds that APIs * are n't * " copyrightable , " then the win Oracle got here might be void . The patent phase had to do with a couple software patents Oracle owned , that it thought Google had used in their implementation . The software patents were kind of vague and very general , and Google was not found by the jury to have violated them . This leaves two things to be decided - whether software APIs are copyrightable ( this ruling can have an enormous effect , like making all instances of Linux copyright infringement ) , and if so , how much money Google should pay Oracle for violating Java copyrights when they developed android . The money penalty could range from millions of dollars , to like $ 1 as a token penalty . Like homelesstatertot said , either party could appeal the ruling or ask for it declared a mistrial , especially given the failure of the jury to rule on every part of the Copyrights phase .
Oracle wants money . Everybody wants money .
rejected:
Google and Oracle are two large technology companies that have been involved in several legal disputes over the years. One of the most recent and well-known disputes between the two companies was a case that went to the Supreme Court of the United States, which is the highest court in the country.

In this case, Oracle claimed that Google had used Oracle's software, called Java, in the development of the Android operating system without proper permission. Google argued that it had the right to use the software because it was necessary for the development of Android and because it was freely available to developers.

After several years of legal proceedings, the Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of Google. The Court held that Google's use of Java was a "fair use" under copyright law, which means that Google was allowed to use the software without permission because it was necessary for the development of Android and because it did not harm the value of the software for Oracle.

In other words, the Court ruled that Google was allowed to use Java in Android because it was important for the development of the operating system, and because it did not take away from the value of the software for Oracle. This was a significant victory for Google, as it allowed the company to continue using Java in Android without having to pay any damages to Oracle.

query: Is there ever an advantage for an average person to lease a new car vs buying it ? The care dealerships always seem to push leasing , so I would assume they make more money that way . is this true ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Previously worked at a car dealership and still work in the automotive industry and currently lease a vehicle . There is a lot of wrong comments in this thread . Let me give you some clarification . - Whether you Lease or Purchase a vehicle unless you write a check for the car the bank OWNS the car . Not You . - Leasing is another way to finance the vehicle where you are paying a little extra to remove yourself from liability of the cars equity . - You can still negotiate and sell the vehicle at any point while on a lease contract , you do n't just have to turn the vehicle in at the end . If you have equity you can use it as a negotiation tool when you trade for another vehicle . - when you turn a vehicle in it 's the banks vehicle not the dealership , usually the bank will offer it to the dealership first but you can turn it into any dealership any of them can negotiate . This means the dealership does n't necessarily care whether you lease or buy . That being said ... - manufacturers want people leasing because it is more likely you will want a new car In a few years even tho you have the option of buying the vehicle first at the end of your lease and refinancing it . - dealers want you to lease because it is a lot easier for a dealership to add profit places . they can fudge the Cap cost , they can fudge the residual value , this is where it 's an afvantage for a dealer to have you lease . It has to do with the contracts , it 's easier to hide money in a lease contract . TLDR : Just to wrap up because I think I 'm writing a lot . Lease is just another way to finance . Any way you finance you do n't own the car . If you are going to Lease do lots of research go to more places and look closely at your contract . If you have any questions just ask , I love helping people buy cars because after selling them I know there are a lot of ways you do n't know where I can still make money .
The main advantage of leasing to the consumer is you can get a better car for the same monthly payment , at least in the short term . If you want to drive a Mercedes but have a Toyota income , leasing can make that happen . Dealers like it because it helps them sell more expensive cars . They make about the same money whether you buy or lease a $ 20 K car ... but they make more from you leasing a $ 40 K car . That said , leasing is almost always a poor financial choice for the consumer . If you have a Toyota income , you should drive a Toyota and save up until you can afford to buy that Mercedes .
IIT there seems to be an awful lot of assumptions and " that sounds right " logic , but no insider knowledge . I used to agree that there leasing was basically for suckers who wanted to own a car beyond their means , but my brother ( who works in a dealership ) convinced me otherwise . Like everything , there is a tradeoff but according to him , leasing is actually kind of win win for both parties . Buying : When you buy a car , it is yours and you do whatever you want with it . Personally , this is a big plus , but financially , maybe not the wisest move . You see , your car loses value as soon as it goes from new to used so one month in , you owe most of the car which has lost a substantial amount of value . With homes , people tend to sell them ( and panic ) but for some reason , they do n't think about it with cars . Leasing : This benefits the dealership because , like some assumed , they still own the car at the end of the day . However , they own a 2 - 5 yr old car which is worth a whole lot less than when you first started using it . The reason dealerships will take this deal anyways is because there is a definite market for used cars and a need to have a used car inventory . Leasing often ties you to the dealership for maintenance , so they have definite records of how good the car is ( important for warranties etc ) . Believe it or not , this actually benefits the consumer many times too . Whether we realize it or not , people who tend to buy a new car , also tend to want an even newer car 5 - 7 years down the road . If they bought , they paid a lot more than a lease , for a car that is worth way less now . For people who buy a car until it breaks down , the old car often further drains you with maintenance after the 100k mark where more expensive parts need to be purchased and cars tend to become unreliable . Things to know : Older cars become less valuable , and more so at high mileage points . For this reason , the dealership puts strict rules and steep fees for driving more than the allotted miles . If you have no choice and KNOW you will use more than the miles provided , my brother recommends you BUY and not lease . However , he does suggest you buy a certified used car as the economic drop of a car going from new to used is simply not worth it . This is obviously , his advice to me as his brother , but often tells this to customers too . However , intangibles like piece of mind do n't have a set value , and many customers decide to buy new anyways . Deals : Some people ITT mentioned about deals . The profit margin for a new car ( lease or sold ) is actually quite small so saying " give me your best price " is nt going to work . For this reason , you will rarely find an amazing deal from dealership to dealership as they are all buying from the same company . Essentially , a 2015 Honda Accord will be the same base price in any Honda dealership . The biggest difference is in financing which can vary depending on how big of a risk a dealership wants to take on your creditworthiness ( and this is actually what you probably want to focus on if getting a new car ) . Deals do exist with used cars though . For some reason , many dealerships will sell a used car for a loss if they have had it for too long . It is pretty much just taking up space . Trade ins : We did n't discuss this ( my brother and I ) , but generally , you will get less than your car is worth for a trade in . However , it is usually likely they will take it , so some people are okay with eating the loss . He always sells his own cars to private parties . Loyalty : With this 5 - 7 year new car trend , dealerships rely A LOT on return customers and referrals . While of course , the dealership wants to make as much money as possible , they would also rather sell you two cars than one . For this reason , dealerships generally play it safe across the board and would rather make their money with add ons than with totally ripping you off on the base price of a new car . Source : Brother works in a dealership and is a car enthusiast .
rejected:
Leasing a car can be a good option for some people, but it's not necessarily the best choice for everyone. Here are a few pros and cons to consider when deciding whether to lease or buy a new car: Pros of leasing: Lower monthly payments: Because you're only paying for the portion of the car's value that you use during the lease term (usually 2-4 years), your monthly payments will be lower than if you were to buy the car and finance it over a longer period of time.
Lower upfront costs: When you lease a car, you typically only need to pay a small upfront fee (called a "down payment") and any applicable taxes. This can be a good option if you don't have a lot of money saved up to put towards a down payment on a car.
No need to worry about selling the car: When you lease a car, you don't have to worry about selling it when you're ready to get a new one. You can simply turn the car back in to the dealership and start a new lease on a different vehicle. Cons of leasing: Limited mileage: Most leases come with a mileage limit (usually around 10,000-15,000 miles per year). If you go over this limit, you'll have to pay extra fees. This can be a problem if you do a lot of driving or take long road trips.
No ownership: When you lease a car, you don't actually own it. This means you don't have the freedom to make any modifications or upgrades to the car, and you'll have to turn it back in to the dealership when the lease is up.
Higher total cost: Although your monthly payments may be lower when you lease a car, you'll end up paying more in total over the long run because you never actually own the car. So, is leasing a good option for an average person? It really depends on your individual circumstances. If you don't drive very much, don't mind turning the car in at the end of the lease, and don't have a lot of money saved up for a down payment, leasing might be a good choice for you. However, if you do a lot of driving, want the freedom to customize your car, or want to eventually own the car outright, it might be better to buy it instead.

query: How does a gas stove prevent the flame from going in ? Is n't gas highly flammable ? What stops it from shooting back and causing a chain reaction within the line itself ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Combustion requires three ingredients : fuel , oxidizer , and heat . Only when all three are present can a flame occur . This happens at a stovetop burner where gas from a pressurized pipeline mixes with oxygen from the air in the room , with a spark to ignite it . Inside the pipeline , however , there 's only gas . You can heat it all you want but it wo n't burn if there 's no oxygen to react with .
You need three things to make fire : fuel , heat , and oxygen . The gas line is pressurized and full of just fuel . With no oxygen ( or at least not the correct amount of oxygen ) , the heat at the end ca n't ignite the gas inside . That 's also why there 's a gap between the exit and the flame ; the gas has to disperse and mix with oxygen before it can ignite .
Flame needs oxygen and fuel to work . The fuel is the gas , the oxygen is in the air . Inside the cylinder there is no oxygen , only fuel . In the air there is no fuel , only oxygen . This means flame can only burn where fuel meets air : the top of a lighter ( butane meets air ) ; the surface of a log ( the wood being the fuel ) ; and indeed the top of your gas stove . The exception is when fuel and oxygen are premixed inside the container , then a lot of fire is produced very quickly inside the container this is how most bombs work . Your gas cylinder is especially designed NOT to explode , so has no oxygen inside it .
rejected:
When you turn on a gas stove, a small amount of gas is released into the burner area. This gas is mixed with air and ignited by a spark from the ignition system. The flame from the ignition system lights the gas, and the gas burns to create heat.

The gas stove is designed so that the flame is contained within the burner area and doesn't spread beyond it. This is done through the use of a burner cap and burner base, which help to keep the flame contained. The burner cap sits on top of the burner base and has small holes in it that allow the gas to escape. The gas mixes with air and is ignited by the flame, and the heat from the burning gas is used to cook the food.

The gas stove also has a safety feature called a gas shut-off valve, which is designed to stop the flow of gas if the flame goes out. This helps to prevent the gas from accumulating and potentially causing a fire or explosion.

So, to sum it up: the gas stove is designed to contain the flame within the burner area and has a safety feature that shuts off the gas if the flame goes out, which helps to prevent accidents and keep you safe while cooking.

query: How come some planes ca n't fly during high temperatures ? I 've read that some flights were canceled in Phoenix due to high temperatures . Why exactly ca n't they fly ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Pilot here . While most of the comments here about air density are correct it is not the actual reason why a lot of flights are being grounded right now . The aircraft are not certified to operate outside of their charted performance limits . That is not to say they will just blow up and not get off the ground , but rather the cost - benefit for an airliner to fly a jet full of people in * * uncertified conditions * * is not worth it . ELI20 below : When aircraft go through certification the engineers go through an expensive process of drawing up performance charts based on typical weather conditions , fuel states and other factors . The actual flight testing , modeling , and administrative process ( paperwork , signatures , FAA signoff , etc . ) is EXTREMELY expensive and so what aircraft manufacturers will typically do is just produce a minimum number of charts for a common range of conditions ( for example , say 10 charts depicting takeoff performance ranging from -40degs up to 20deg celsius . So on a day with 10deg C you would look up takeoff performance , cruise , landing performance , etc . all the relevant 10deg charts and figure out how much fuel you can take , how many passengers can be loaded , how long of a runway you need to takeoff , etc ... ) . So on an abnormally hot day that does not have certified charted performance , even if the aircrew and engineers think the aircraft can get off the ground , airliner management says " well we do n't have certification for these conditions so we ca n't risk a lawsuit if anything were to happen " .
Additionally , the less dense air means the engine power is also reduced so the plane needs more runway to get up to speed . This is probably more important than the slight loss of wing lift since engine losses are substantially higher with rising temperatures .
As temperature increases , the density of air decreases . This has a negative impact on the amount of lift that can be generated , which can prevent some planes from taking off safely .
rejected:
Sure! So, planes need to generate a lot of lift in order to be able to fly. Lift is what helps them rise into the air and stay up there. One of the main things that helps a plane generate lift is its wings. The shape of the wings is designed to produce lift as the plane moves through the air.

Now, the air around us is made up of tiny molecules that are constantly moving and bouncing around. The faster these molecules are moving, the more heat there is. So, when the temperature is really high, the molecules in the air are moving really fast, and that can make it harder for a plane to generate enough lift to get off the ground.

It's kind of like trying to jump really high on a trampoline when it's really hot outside. The trampoline is bouncier because the heat has made the springs expand, so it's harder to get as much lift as you normally would. Planes work in a similar way, except they use their wings instead of springs to generate lift.

So, if it's too hot outside, the air might not be bouncy enough to give the plane the lift it needs to get off the ground. That's why some planes can't fly when it's really hot outside.

query: Why does a 20x30 rectangle have less area than a 25x25 square , when the perimeter of each is the same ? I can do the math and understand that this is the case , but I simply can not wrap my head around this . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
> I can do the math and understand that this is the case , but I simply can not wrap my head around this . The shape with the smallest perimeter - to - area ratio is the circle . It 's not possible to cram more space in a smaller perimeter . Thus the closer a shape is to being a circle , the more area it covers for the same perimeter . A square is closer to being a circle than a rectangle is , and thus a square of a given perimeter will cover a larger area than a rectangle of the same perimeter .
Imagine a rope with a length of 100 cm ( which is the perimeter of your shapes ) . Set that rope out so that it forms a square with 25 cm sides . Now , take two opposite corners of that square , and pull them apart from each other so you get a diamond shape . Keep pulling until the corners are 50 cm apart from each other ... As you pull , you gradually reduce the area of the shape , so that eventually it reaches zero . You end up with two lines , each 50 cm long , each made up from what used to be two sides of the square , lying next to each other with no area in between them . You should hopefully be able to see from this that the area of a shape can vary quite a lot even if the perimeter remains constant .
Look at the proportional change in the individual dimensions . Your first dimension goes from 20 to 25 . This is a 25 % increase . Your second dimension decreases from 30 to 25 . This is a 16.7 % decrease . While the perimeter is the same the area has changed be cause the scale has changed .

The 20x30 rectangle would have two lines that are 10 units long and the 25x25 square would have one line that is 25 units long. If we cut along these lines, we would get two pieces of paper from the 20x30 rectangle, and four pieces of paper from the 25x25 square. If we laid the pieces of paper out flat, we would see that the 20x30 rectangle has a bigger area because it has more space to cover. The 25x25 square, on the other hand, has less space to cover because it has been cut into smaller pieces. I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any more questions.

query: ‘ Net Neutrality ’ Debate Why is this a big deal ? How would the average users internet experience change by either side of the debate ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Net neutrality is a principle currently in place that makes it so governments and Internet Service Providers treat each piece of data on the Internet as equal . This means it is a violation if your ISP charges you more money for , say , access to YouTube as opposed to access to Reddit . Basically , no matter what site you use , you pay the same price . Verizon is suing the FCC to change that , because they want to start filtering websites into categories , and charging more money for sites with large amounts of data . The reason this is a bad thing is because they can use this proposed method to restrict access to certain parts of the Internet , and stop consumers from reaching websites they do n't like , such as Netflix , which presents competition to Verizon 's video on demand service , due to its competitive pricing . TL;DR : Abolishing net neutrality will allow your Internet company to decide what websites you can / can't see , and how much each one costs to access .
Another important aspect to consider is how the neutrality of the internet has changed the world for the better already and how getting rid of neutrality would stop that . Lets say that net neutrality never existed , you get this idea to make a website where people can submit links to things they see and read on the internet and users of this site can vote up or down these stories and links . Sounds like a great idea right ? lets call it Hmmm ? ... how 'bout " Reddit " yeah has a nice ring to it ! If people see it , it can really take off right ? But in that world , you have to pay verizon , comcast and others huge sums of money if you want people online to have the same access to your brand new site as they do a big company 's site . So if you do nt have that kind of cash flow no one can see your site or it 's too much of a headache to access so it will never take off . Reddit would have died before it ever had a chance . One of the best things about the ( net neutral ) internet is that young people with fresh ideas have the same chance of success as the big companies . The difference is that the young fresh ideas usually are innovating , shake up the world and make thing better by showing us a new way to do things which , in turn , pushes the world forward . While , on the other hand , the big companies want to keep thing the way they are generally , keeping themselves rich and stopping what they do n't understand or might challenge their profit gain . Think about how the world has changed with Reddit , Facebook , Twitter , blogs the list goes on and on . Imagine how the world would have been if these companies never had a chance to start ? Now imagine all the countless possible ideas that young people who do n't have tons of funds have yet to imagine and how those , yet to be discovered , ideas could further change things . Then finally Imagine a world where these ideas never get the traction to take off . That would be our future with no net neutrality . ( stepping down from soap box )
There are very good reasons to want net neutrality , all of which you 'll hear ad nauseam here on reddit . The problem is that , because the internet is not considered a necessary resource , like telephone or over the air media broadcasts , the FCC does not have the ability to regulate it as such . So , yes , net neutrality is good but there appears to be no legal grounds for the FCC to regulate and enforce it , therefore it is unlikely to remain until there are more serious changes made to the regulation of the internet .
rejected: Net neutrality is the idea that all internet traffic should be treated equally. This means that internet service providers (ISPs) should not be able to slow down or block any websites or services, or charge more money for faster access to them. The debate about net neutrality is a big deal because it affects how you can use the internet. If net neutrality rules are in place, you can use the internet however you want and access any websites or services you want without your ISP getting in the way. But if net neutrality is not in place, your ISP could decide to block or slow down certain websites or services, or charge you more money to access them. For example, let's say you like to watch videos on a website called "VideoLand." Without net neutrality, your ISP might slow down VideoLand so it takes a long time to load, or they might block it completely so you can't access it at all. On the other hand, if your ISP has a deal with another video website called "WatchVideos," they might make that website load faster and be easier to use. Overall, the average user's internet experience would change depending on whether or not there are net neutrality rules in place. With net neutrality, everyone would have the same access to all websites and services. Without net neutrality, some websites and services might be harder to use or not available at all, depending on what your ISP allows.

query: Good yield vs. safer route (Checking vs. Savings)
chosen: In personal finance, most of your success is determined by personal habit rather than financial savvy.  Getting in the habit of making regular deposits to your savings account will have a much larger effect on your situation than worrying about which account pays the highest interest rate (particularly as neither one of them matches the current inflation rate, which is over 3%). So go ahead and put your money in a savings account, but not because of the interest or safety, but because it's a "savings" account.
rejected: When it comes to saving and investing money, it's important to consider both the potential return on your investment and the level of risk you are willing to take on. Checking and savings accounts are typically considered to be relatively safe options for storing and growing your money, as they are FDIC-insured and generally offer low or no risk of losing your principal investment. However, these accounts also tend to have relatively low interest rates, which means that the potential return on your money may be lower compared to other options.On the other hand, investments that have the potential for higher returns, such as stocks, bonds, or real estate, also carry a higher level of risk. These types of investments can fluctuate in value and there is a possibility of losing some or all of your principal investment.Ultimately, the choice between a higher yield and a safer route will depend on your individual financial goals and risk tolerance. It's important to carefully consider your options and make a decision that is appropriate for your specific situation. It may be helpful to speak with a financial advisor or professional to get guidance on the best way to save and invest your money.

query: Next option(s) after house is not selling on market?
chosen: EDIT: new ideas based on the full story. I wouldn't worry about the price history.  While it is certainly true that some buyers might try to leverage that information against you, the bottom line is the price is the price.  Both the buyer and the seller have to agree.   If the initial listing was too high, then lower the price.  If that isn't low enough, then readjust down.  I see no harm in moving the price down over time repeatedly.  In fact, I thin that is a good tactic to getting the most for the house.  If you happen to have the luxury of time, then keep lowering that price until it sells.  Don't fret how that behavior appears. You can lower the price as often as you like until it sells.  I am not a real estate agent, and I am a terrible negotiator, but I would lower the price every quarter until it sells.  You can't go down to fast (a buyer might wait you out) and you can't wait to long as you stated.  Also, if you house is priced inline with the neighborhood, you can at least get offers and negotiate. Buy asking for such a premium (25%) folks might not even make an offer. You simply need to decide what is more important, the selling price or the time frame in getting it sold. If you house doesn't sell because the market doesn't support your price, then consider keeping it as a rental.  You can do it yourself, or if you are not interested in that (large) amount of work, then hire a rental management company to do it for a fee. Renting a home is hard work and requires attention to detail, a good amount of your time and much labor.  If you just need to wait a couple of years before selling, renting it can be a good option to cover your costs while you wait for the market to reach you. You should get advice on how to handle the money, how to rent it, how to deal with renters, and the the laws are in your jurisdiction. Rent it out to a trusted friend or family member for a steal of a deal.  They save money, and you get the luxury of time waiting for the sale. With a real estate lawyer you hire, get a contract for a lease option or owner finance deal on the house.  Sometimes you can expand the market of people looking to buy your house.  If you have a willing purchaser will bad credit, you can be doing them a favor and solving your own issue.  It costs money and you will make less on the sale, but it could be better than nothing. Take heed, there is a reason some people cannot get a traditional loan on their own.  Before you extend your good name or credit think about it.  It is another hassle for sure. This won't help if you have to pay off a mortgage, but you could donate it.  This is another tricky deal that you really need to speak with a lawyer who specialize in charitable giving.  There are tax benefits, but I would make any kind of a deal where tax deductions are the only benefit. This is common enough these days.  If you are unable to pay for the mortgage, it benefits you and the bank to get into a short sale arrangement.  They bank gets probably more money than if they have to foreclose (and they save money on legal fees) and you can get rid of the obligation. You will do a deed in lieu or the short sale depending on how the market it and what the house can be sold for.  You and the bank will have to work it out. This will ruin for a credit for a while, and you will not likely qualify to get a new mortgage for at least a few years. You can stop paying your mortgage, tell the bank and they will foreclose.  This is going to ruin your credit for a long time as well as disqualify you from mortgages in the near future.  Don't do this.  If you are planning a foreclosure, take the time to contact your bank and arrange a short sale or a deed in lieu.  There isn't really any excuse to go into foreclosure if you are having problems.  Talk to the bank and work out a deal.

query: Why the sky appears to be blue , when actually the air has no color ? Well , the title says it all . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's due to [ Rayleigh scattering ] ( URL_0 ) - sunlight passing through the atmosphere is scattered as a function of wavelength . Shorter wavelength light , such as those near the blue end of the spectrum , is scattered more . When you look up at the sky , what you 're seeing is light from the sun that 's travelling tangential to you ( i.e. , not hitting you ) that has been scattered . This phenomenon is also responsible for red sunrise and sunsets - the blue light has been scattered away so the light that reaches your eye is predominantly higher wavelength light .
When I was 5 , I asked my mom this question . She told me to ask my dad . My dad said , " It 's because if it was n't blue , I 'd beat it 's ass . " So there you have it . The sky is blue out of fear of getting it 's ass kicked by my dad .
FALSE : the sky is blue because light entering any medium , in this case our atmosphere , causes it to refract . when this happens , it only refracts a certain portion of the spectrum of light which is the blue you see . the sun gives off white light which is the entire color spectrum . that s why if u notice a sunset , it actually looks like a rainbow , becoming redder closer to the horizon . this is because the angle the light hits the atmosphere is different , and thus refracting a different part of the spectrum for us to see ... was that clear enough ?
rejected: The sky appears to be blue because when sunlight hits the Earth's atmosphere, the short-wavelength blue light is scattered in every direction by the gases and particles in the air. When we look up at the sky, we are seeing all of the blue light that has been scattered in every direction. This is why the sky appears to be blue most of the time. The amount of scattering that occurs depends on the size and number of the particles in the air, so the color of the sky can vary. Sometimes the sky can appear to be other colors, like red or orange, due to different atmospheric conditions. But blue is the most common color of the sky because blue light is scattered more easily than other colors of light.

query: Why are injured horses put down instead of looked after and healed ? I mean , we 're perfectly capable of healing people and other animals if they break a limb ; why do we not do the same for horses ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It depends on the type of fracture the particular horse has . The idea that all horses are put down is n't as common a practice as different types of media like to portray . One main factor in determining the horse 's destiny is their quality of life . If it 's just a hairline , usually they 'll put the horse under and set the leg . For more serious breaks that could result in life - long suffering with arthritis , they 'll put the horse down . If they are going to brace it , it 's rarely just a cast ; but more often plates and screws . With an injured leg , the horse wo n't stand on it which can lead to [ laminitis ] ( URL_0 ) , which is life threatening as well .
People and other animals do not have the stresses put on their limbs likes horses do . Average quarter - horses weigh between 1000 - 1200 pounds , and large draughts ( or drafts ) weigh about 2,000 pounds . All of that weight sits on the equivalent of you standing on the balls of your feet at all times . If you get injured , you can understand that you ca n't run , or hardly walk , you can use crutches , and you can describe what kind of pain you 're feeling . A horse can not do any of that . Because of that , a horse is likely to run and play , without thought to their injury , and they will continually re - break the leg . So , quality of life will never be the same . And to keep a horse in its stall for three months would destroy the rest of its body .
The costs are n't worth it . Often times when a horse breaks a leg , it can be healed , the problem is that it takes a long time , the horse will be bed ridden for much of that time , and there will be lots of physio therapy in order to get the horse running again . And even then , that leg is always going to be at risk for another break , it rarely heals perfectly , this often leaves a limp or lame horse , and the cost of the healing process is just too high . In the end it 's easier to put the horse down and send it to the butcher . That being said , there are farms that specialize in injured horses and often use them for therapy with people . These often include troubled children , the developmentally challenged , etc who relate to these horses and find a hobby and possibly a way to a trade .
rejected:
Injured horses are often put down because their injuries can be severe and may not be able to be fully healed. For example, if a horse breaks a leg, it may not be able to walk or stand properly again, even with medical treatment. This would cause the horse a lot of pain and suffering, and it would not be able to lead a normal, healthy life. In these cases, it is often considered more humane to euthanize the horse, which means to put the horse to sleep in a way that is painless and peaceful. 
In other cases, a horse's injuries may be able to be treated and the horse may be able to recover. In these cases, the horse may be given medical care and rehabilitation to help it heal. However, this can be expensive and time-consuming, and it may not always be possible to fully heal the horse. 
Overall, the decision to euthanize an injured horse or to try to treat it is often based on the severity of the injury and the chances of a full recovery. The decision is usually made with the well-being of the horse in mind and is intended to prevent the horse from suffering unnecessarily.

query: Negative deposit rates So i was browsing the Financial times yesterday and they mentioned something called negative deposit rates , and i did nt understand what it was . So my question is , what are they and what function do they serve . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
You know how when you deposit money in a bank , they pay you some amount of interest ( these days , something piddling like 0.35 % ) ? A negative deposit rate would be if * you * had to pay * them * for the privilege of having them hold your money .
* * * Summary * * * The government wants banks to loan money to people instead of keeping it . So the government makes banks pay money if they try to store money instead of loaning it . ----------------------- First , central banking . In the US the central banking system is called the Federal Reserve System or the Fed . It 's really different from a normal bank . The Fed influences what " normal " banks do . One of the things that the Fed does is influence is how much money banks loan out vs how much money banks keep " in the vault " . That is , the Fed can ( basically ) do stuff to make banks loan out more money * or * make it so that banks loan out less money . Let 's back up . Banks ( basically ) store a lot of their money at the Fed . Naturally , a high interest rate means that banks want to do that ; a low interest rate means that banks do n't want to do that . So imagine if you lent me $ 10 today in exchange for $ 20 in a week . * It 's a good deal . * Because it 's a good deal you 're gon na to do it . Imagine if you lent me $ 10 today in exchange for $ 8 in a week . * It 's a shitty deal . * Because it 's a shitty deal you 're gon na try not to do it . Like , unless you 're expecting to be robbed and end up with $ 0 then you probably are n't going to lend me $ 10 today in exchange for $ 8 in a week . It 's kinda the same . The Fed can offer a positive rate ( i.e. , if you give me $ 10 today i 'll give you $ 20 in a week ) . Or the Fed can offer a negative rate ( i.e. , if you give me $ 10 today I 'll give you $ 8 in a week ) . If it 's a negative rate then you 're gon na work hard not to lend me the money . Which means you 're gon na try to lend the money to other people . Therefore , by offering a negative rate I 've basically caused you to go ahead an loan out the money to other people .
Many banks park their money in a Central Bank ( Hub ) and earn Interest ( additional money ) on it . With Negative deposit rates , the banks would have to pay the Central Bank to hold their money . This means that banks would be encouraged to lend that money and make investments in start - up companies rather than hoarding all that Interest . It is a good thing for the Economy .
rejected:
Negative deposit rates refer to a situation where a central bank, such as the Federal Reserve in the United States or the European Central Bank, charges banks to keep their money in the central bank's accounts. Normally, banks are paid a small amount of interest for keeping their money in these accounts, but with negative deposit rates, they are charged a fee instead.

The main reason central banks might use negative deposit rates is to try to encourage banks to lend more money to households and businesses. When banks have to pay a fee to keep their money at the central bank, they may be more likely to lend it out instead, in order to earn some income. This can help stimulate economic activity and encourage growth.

It's important to note that negative deposit rates are not very common and are usually only used in unusual economic circumstances, such as during a recession or financial crisis. Most of the time, central banks use other tools, such as setting interest rates, to try to influence the economy.

query: How do we not run out of Oxygen in closed rooms ? Are there any small ventilation holes to let oxygen in buildings and cars ? Do architects need to take this into account ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Generally , nothing is going to be airtight unless * it 's specifically designed to be * . Making stuff airtight is very difficult , and very expensive . It requires special materials and precision . There are lots of places for air to get in , such as a window frame , underneath a door , vents ( even if the furnace is n't running ) . If you were in a room sealed well enough to prevent much air from entering , an average size room has * a lot * of oxygen . It would take many days , even weeks , to use it all up and asphyxiate . Chances are you will have left by then .
Architecture school graduate here . Google " air changes per hour " an old house may have two air changes per hour , meaning whatever cracks exist allow the entire volume of air to be replaced twice an hour . Even a modern house will still have 1 to 1.5 air changes per hour . But yeah , in a room with a lot of people , I think a classroom wants at least 4 ACH , so we definitely pump fresh air in . Although I do n't think kids would be suffocating , but the air would get stale and germs would spread even more .
In a completely airtight small room of 3 meters by 4 meters and average height , one person can live 2 or 3 days and nights without running out of oxygen . They will reach toxic levels of CO2 before then , though . Architects simply avoid making rooms truly airtight .
rejected: We don't run out of oxygen in closed rooms because the air inside the room is constantly circulated by an air exchange system. In buildings, this system is usually made up of a network of ducts and vents that bring fresh air in from outside and remove stale air from inside. In cars, the air exchange system is usually made up of small vents that allow fresh air to enter the car and a exhaust pipe that removes the stale air. Architects do need to take this into account when designing buildings and cars because it is important for people to have access to fresh air in order to breathe and stay healthy.

query: How did West Berlin operate when it was located on East Germany during the Cold War ? Just watching a show now talking about the wall and I never actually realised Berlin was so far behind ' enemy lines ' . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
There was a network of transit routes people from West Berlin could use to go to nearby countries , and back to West Germany . Leaving those roads was forbidden , and you needed a visa to get in or out . There was also a few railroads with interzonal trains , which would go straight through Eastern Germany , though never stopped ( except for border controls ) . At one point , those were blocked due to an economic dispute . This led to a massive airlift operation , delivering 4700 tons of supplies per day , with almost 200 000 flights per day . Total cost of the operation is estimated to be between 2 to 5 billion , and 25 planes crashed .
Adding to what others stated , there would be rail trains or convoys that were allowed to pass , from West Germany , through East Germany to West Berlin . But these routes could , and were , shut down whenever a political spat occurred between East and West . Also , it was very dangerous to divert from that road as the Stasi & soldiers patrolled that area and would use deadly force if they thought you were a spy . As for the air routes , there were three routes to three airports in West Berlin ( Tegel , which will be shut down when the new airport is built , Berlin Templehof , Nazi era airport and recently shut down and turned into historical site , and Gatow , a British RAF airbase that is now suburbs and a Luftwaffe museum ) that had strict routes where diversion from them meant a shootdown by the East German air force . Before the wall , there were arguments over giving West Berlin to the Soviets . But the Berlin Airlift , and support for local leaders there , was the decisive factor in keeping West Berlin within NATO 's enforcement until late 1989 .
Before the Berlin Wall was built , it functioned as simply another part of East Germany , albeit one where the East German government had no authority . In other words people were free to cross from east to west Berlin to meet friends , buy goods and services , and so on . Once the Berlin Wall was built everything changed . In fact one of the main purposes of the Berlin Wall 's creation was to basically besiege west Berlin by preventing supplies getting in . That problem was overcome by the western powers launching a non - stop 24 - hour - a - day airdrop of supplies to west Berliners , continuing for weeks . Once the East German government relented a little and allowed supplies into west Berlin , it then functioned just as any other small and relatively isolated city did .
rejected:
During the Cold War, the city of Berlin was located deep within the territory of the German Democratic Republic (GDR), also known as East Germany. The city was divided into two parts: West Berlin, which was controlled by the Western powers (the United States, the United Kingdom, and France), and East Berlin, which was controlled by the Soviet Union. 
West Berlin was located on the western side of the Berlin Wall, which was a heavily fortified barrier that separated the two parts of the city. The wall was built in 1961 to prevent people from East Germany from fleeing to the West. It was guarded by East German soldiers, and anyone who tried to cross the wall illegally could be shot. 
Despite being surrounded by East Germany, West Berlin was not controlled by the GDR or the Soviet Union. Instead, it was a free and democratic city, similar to other cities in Western Europe. The Western powers provided the city with food, fuel, and other supplies to help it survive. They also allowed people from West Berlin to travel freely to other parts of Western Europe. 
East Berlin, on the other hand, was a communist city and was controlled by the GDR and the Soviet Union. It was not as open or free as West Berlin, and people who lived there had fewer freedoms and rights. 
The situation in Berlin remained like this until the end of the Cold War in the 1990s, when the Berlin Wall was finally taken down and the city was reunited.

query: Why do computers heat up ? Why do we need coolers for some older laptops but not for smartphones that may have as much computing power ? What makes materials in computers warm up ? Does it depend on " how hard " the computer " works " ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
When you open a computer , you 'll see a lot of little black rectangles . Those are called " chips " , and they are what makes a computer work . They notice when you move the mouse & let you create the pretty pictures mommy pins to the fridge . But how do they work you ask ? Good question . They consist of even smaller parts , called " transistors " , and work like a valve . With just a little bit of work they can regulate a lot of water . But unlike a valve , they usually are either open or closed . And any switch between open and closed takes time , and during that time they resist the flow , and begin to heat up from the energy , like the old valve in the basement makes noise when turning on and off . Now , in a computer these transistors switch between on an off about 800 million times per second . Each switch generates only a little bit of heat , but over time it gets more . In old computers , the transistors were bigger and slower , so there was more time for heat to build up , and they also needed more energy to switch around , which is why they needed coolers , while your new phone does n't . Now go up to your room & pack your swimming stuff , we 're going to Stinson beach , I have something to show you .
Electrons are pushed through the circuits . This pushing generates heat . More electrons flowing through the computer = more heat . More electrons are needed if you perform more calculations each second , and less electrons if you calculate less .
Yes , computers run hotter under load than they do while idle . when the computer is idling it can turn off some features and use fewer transistors to save power and generate less heat .
rejected: Computers generate heat because they have a lot of small parts inside them that are constantly working and moving. These parts, like the processor and the graphics card, use electricity to function, and as a byproduct of this process, they give off heat. The more work a computer does, the more heat it generates. Cooling systems, like fans or heat sinks, are used to keep a computer from getting too hot. Some computers, like smartphones, are designed to be small and portable, so they don't have room for large cooling systems. As a result, they may not generate as much heat, or they may have special materials and designs that help them dissipate heat more efficiently. In general, the materials used in computers are not naturally warm. It's the process of using electricity to power the computer's parts that causes them to heat up. That's why it's important to use a cooling system to keep a computer from getting too hot, especially if it's being used for tasks that require a lot of processing power.

query: How come only certain animals can be domesticated ? How come we ca n't domesticate lions or other animals like we did with the dog or cat ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:

query: How native americans lost so much of their territory so quickly to colonists Was it really just diseases that decimated their numbers or were they just too trusting of the colonist until it was too late and their numbers and technology was just too great to fight back ? Many native groups were also at war with each other . There was never a point at which all natives took direction from a single leader or followed a single policy . Trying to look at millions of diverse people as if they were a single Borg - like entity is a huge mistake .

There were several factors that contributed to Native Americans losing much of their territory to colonists. One factor was the spread of diseases, such as smallpox and measles, which Native Americans had no immunity to. These diseases were often brought to the Americas by Europeans and were transmitted through close contact. Many Native Americans died as a result of these diseases, which made it easier for colonists to take over their land.

Another factor was the use of superior technology and weapons by the colonists. The colonists had access to guns, cannons, and other advanced weapons, which gave them an advantage in battles with Native Americans.

Finally, some Native American tribes were less prepared to defend themselves against the colonists because they were more trusting and did not expect the colonists to try to take their land. This led to some Native American tribes making agreements with the colonists that they later regretted, as the colonists did not always keep their promises.

Overall, the loss of Native American territory was a complex process that was influenced by a variety of factors, including disease, technology, and the actions of both Native Americans and colonists.

query: Do abusive parents produce abusive children , or submissive ones ? It would make sense that an abused child would live life fearfully , but it also makes sense that abuse happens in unbroken cycles . Is one of these right ? Or possibly both ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
In cases of abused children , generally you end up with both situations arising . If a child is abused they can start to self identify as a victim and start to display learned helplessness - a state where they are convinced that no matter what they do , they 're going to still be victimized and terrible things will continue to happen . As an example of this consider a child that is beaten under the pretense of not cleaning their room . After the beating they clean their room and make sure everything is nice and tidy and start to feel pride in their accomplishment . After this they are again beaten or otherwise abused , the abuser using another pretense to justify the abuse . The child was expecting to be rewarded ( even subtly ) and was punished again . As this continues to happen the child will learn that no matter what punishment will happen and end up resigned to their sad predicament . This explains why they can be seen as more passive and often times do n't work to try and make their situations better / end up exhibiting self - destructive tendencies . On the other side of the coin , an abused child is being taught ( incorrectly ) that the abusive behavior is the response for a wide range of situations . Consider that you 're a small child and you touched your father 's prize bowling trophy . You thought it was just a toy and wanted to play with it , but he beat you for touching it . After enough incidents of things like this happening you start to associate touching other people 's things with a beating and you accept it as fact . Now , one day in school a classmate touches one of your toys when you did n't want them to . Since you have been taught that physical attacks are the response in this situation , you hit them . Being abused is a double edged sword . Not only are you more likely to be violent , you 're also less likely to seek help since you do n't believe you 'll get help or that the help will be useful . You 've learned that nothing will please your abusers except for their abuse . You begin to learn that using abuse is the appropriate response to a lot of situations and thus start to emulate abusive behavior .
It 's offensive that you assume that there is n't a third option , and that people who were abused as children are utterly helpless to get better . We 're not . We 're in control of our own lives . It 's hard for us , but we can do it , and regularly do . Just because some of us use that misconception as an excuse for what they find themselves doing , does n't make it true .
They can produce fucked up kids who can be submissive and/or abusive at different places in their lives .
rejected:
It is possible for both abusive parents to produce abusive children and for abusive parents to produce submissive children. It is also possible for non-abusive parents to produce abusive children. The way that a child responds to abuse can depend on many factors, including their personality, their coping mechanisms, the support they have from other people, and the severity of the abuse.

It is important to note that abuse can have serious negative effects on children, including physical, emotional, and psychological harm. Children who experience abuse may struggle with low self-esteem, trust issues, and difficulty forming healthy relationships. It is not uncommon for people who have experienced abuse to struggle with mental health issues later in life, such as depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder.

It is important to seek help if you or someone you know is experiencing abuse. There are many resources available to provide support and assistance to people who have experienced abuse, including hotlines, counseling services, and shelters.

query: Scientists say that the universe may be flat , but obviously we live in 3 - dimensional space . How is this possible ? If the universe is flat , why can we move along three axes ? Is the flatness of the universe just an analogy ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
" Flat " in this context refers to space not appearing to be bent in such a way that a straight path would loop back around in some way to itself . For example if you went one direction you would n't ever end up back where you started .
Flat is meant in a geometrical sense . In math , we can often generalize properties for arbitrary dimensions . For example , we can generalize the idea of a plane the following way : In three dimensions , a plane is a two dimensional surface . Thus , generally , a plane in an n dimensional space is an n-1 dimensional surface . * ( For the more mathematically versed , the exact definitions is the following : a hyperplane of an n - dimensional affine space is a flat subset with dimension n − 1 . By its nature , it separates the space into two half spaces.)*^[Source ] ( URL_1 ) The same can be done for the property of flatness . A two dimensional plane is flat if the sum of all angles in a triangle is 180 ° . On a curved surface , like the surface of a sphere , this is not the case . [ This ] ( URL_0 ) triangle , for example , has three 90 ° angles . Another property of flat surfaces is , that parallel lines stay parallel for all eternity . That this fact does not hold true on curved surfaces can be seen from the image above as well : both lines start orthogonal to the equator , yet they meet on the north pole . This property turns out to be a very useful metric to quantify " flatness " , even in higher dimensions . A universe , in which two lines that are parallel in one point stay parallel in all points is ( globally ) flat . If that is not the case , we live in a curved universe .
This is a great question ! Part of it has to do with different definitions of the word " universe " . What we perceive is 3 dimensional space . All of three dimensional space at the present time can be referred to as the nowverse , or lets just call it space ( as in , a 3 dimensional space ) . We know with certainty that there is a 4th dimension , time - let 's call that spacetime . The 3D space ( the nowverse ) is basically a slice of that . String theory ( and others ) postulate many other dimensions as well . We know that spacetime curves - general relativity gives us that . Any large mass will cause a curve . In other words , if you were to move a massless object past a large gravitational force , you would see it curves , even though it 's not because of some direct attraction of the large mass and your object , it 's because gravity has distorted that area of space . The way this is usually pictured is to imagine having three spaceships and having them shoot lasers at each other , forming a triangle . A triangle should have the degree of angles between the sides add up to 180 degrees . If they do , that space is flat . If they add up to more than 180 degrees , there is positive curvature . If it is less , there is negative curvature . So since we know there is curving , why would we say the universe is flat ? Because you need to average out the whole thing . In other words , if you made an infinite number of triangles in the universe , would they average out to 180 degrees , or more or less ? the BOOMerang experiment used this method to determine the universe appears to be flat , with a plus or minus of .12 margin of error . That 's still fairly big . We know that mass causes the curvatures because of General Relativity . The calculation for it is mass over critical energy density . So what we can do is add up all the mass in the observable universe , and calculate out an average mass density , and divide that by the critical energy density . If you get 1 , the universe is flat . Last year the Planck mission determined the average mass density of the observable universe , and the Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe determined the critical energy density . When calculated together , they determined the universe was flat , with a margin of error of .005 . That 's our experimental proof of a flat universe . edit : tried to clarify gravity
rejected: The idea that the universe may be flat is actually a bit more complicated than it may seem at first. When scientists talk about the universe being flat, they are referring to the overall shape of the entire universe. In other words, they are trying to understand whether the universe is infinite in size and goes on forever, or whether it is finite and has an edge.If the universe is infinite, then it would be flat, in the sense that it would be infinitely large and would not curve back on itself. On the other hand, if the universe is finite, it could be either curved or flat.Even though the universe may be flat, we still live in a 3-dimensional space. This is because, at the scale that we experience everyday life, the universe looks and behaves as if it is 3-dimensional. We can move up and down, left and right, and forward and backward, and these are all movements that happen in 3-dimensional space.The flatness of the universe is not just an analogy, but it is based on scientific observations and theories. Scientists use a variety of techniques to study the shape of the universe, including analyzing the distribution of galaxies and the cosmic microwave background radiation, which is a faint glow of light that fills the entire universe. A government shutdown occurs when there is a disagreement between the President and Congress (which is made up of the Senate and the House of Representatives) about how to fund the government. This means that certain parts of the government stop working because they do not have the money to pay for their operations.

In the United States, the government relies on Congress to pass a budget that determines how much money each government agency will receive. If Congress cannot agree on a budget, or if the President refuses to sign a budget that Congress has passed, then the government can shut down.

During a government shutdown, many government employees may not be able to work and may not get paid. This can cause problems for people who rely on government services, such as national parks being closed, or delays in processing social security or unemployment benefits.

It's called "part deux" because it is the second time this has happened. The first government shutdown happened earlier and was resolved, but now it has happened again.

query: Shorting versus selling to hedge risk
chosen: It's not quite identical, due to fees, stock rights, and reporting & tax obligations.  But the primary difference is that a person could have voting rights in a company while maintaining zero economic exposure to the company, sometimes known as empty voting. As an abstract matter, it's identical in that you reduce your financial exposure whether you sell your stock or short it.  So the essence of your question is fundamentally true.  But the details make it different.  Of course there are fee differences in how your broker will handle it, and also margin requirements for shorting.  Somebody playing games with overlapping features of ownership, sales, and purchases, may have tax and reporting obligations for straddles, wash sales, and related issues.  A straight sale is generally less complicated for tax reporting purposes, and a loss is more likely to be respected than someone playing games with sales and purchases. But the empty voting issue is an important difference.  You could buy stock with rights such as voting, engage in other behavior such as forwards, shorts, or options to negate your economic exposure to the stock, while maintaining the right to vote.  Of course in some cases this may have to be disclosed or may be covered by contract, and most people engaging in stock trades are unlikely to have meaningful voting power in a public company.  But the principle is still there. As explained in the article by Henry Hu and Bernie Black: Hedge funds have been especially creative in decoupling voting rights from economic ownership. Sometimes they hold more votes than economic ownership - a pattern we call empty voting. In an extreme situation, a vote holder can have a negative economic interest and, thus, an incentive to vote in ways that reduce the company's share price. Sometimes investors hold more economic ownership than votes, though often with morphable voting rights - the de facto ability to acquire the votes if needed. We call this situation hidden (morphable) ownership because the economic ownership and (de facto) voting ownership are often not disclosed.
rejected:
Short selling and selling to hedge risk are similar in that they both involve selling securities in order to reduce the risk of loss in an investment portfolio. However, there are some important differences between the two.Short selling involves selling securities that the seller does not own, with the expectation that the price of the securities will fall. The seller borrows the securities from someone else and sells them, hoping to buy them back later at a lower price and return them to the lender. The seller profits from the difference between the price at which the securities were sold and the price at which they were bought back. Short selling is a way to hedge against the risk of loss on a long position, or an investment that profits if the price of the securities increases.Selling to hedge risk, on the other hand, involves selling securities that the investor owns in order to reduce the risk of loss on those securities. For example, an investor who owns a large number of shares of a particular stock may decide to sell some of those shares in order to reduce the risk of loss if the price of the stock falls.Overall, short selling is a more advanced investment strategy that involves taking on additional risk in order to potentially profit from a decline in the price of securities, while selling to hedge risk is a more straightforward way to reduce the risk of loss on an existing investment.
Selling a security and shorting a security are both ways to hedge risk.When you sell a security, you are getting rid of an asset that you own in order to reduce your exposure to the risks associated with that asset. For example, if you own stock in a company and you are worried that the stock price is going to go down, you could sell the stock to minimize your potential losses.On the other hand, shorting a security involves selling a security that you do not own, with the expectation that the price will go down. You borrow the security from someone else and sell it, hoping to buy it back at a lower price in the future and return it to the lender. If the price goes down as you expect, you will make a profit. If the price goes up, you will incur a loss.Both selling a security and shorting a security can be used to hedge risk, but they involve different approaches and have different potential outcomes. It is important to carefully consider your investment strategy and risk tolerance before deciding which approach is best for you.

query: Where does the oxygen come from to feed the Centralia fire ? It 's underground so it seems like it would run out of fuel * * ( * * * oxygen - wise * * * ) * * . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
When you burn something , the complex carbon in the fuel source ( in this case coal ) combines with the oxygen in the air to produce water vapor , carbon dioxide , and sooty remains . This means that oxygen is coming out of the surrounding air and being bound into CO2 , which has a lower volume . This reduction in volume creates a low - pressure zone around the fire , which acts like a straw , sucking in surrounding air . In this case , it means that all of the mineshafts in the area pull air in to equalize the pressure difference . Since that air then immediately has its oxygen stripped from it , it keeps happening , and will keep happening for as long as there is exposed coal in those mines .

The fire in Centralia is burning deep underground in coal deposits, and it needs oxygen to continue burning. 
Oxygen is all around us, in the air we breathe. When the fire in Centralia started, there was plenty of oxygen in the air above the ground, and the fire was able to burn through the coal seam and release heat. As the fire burns, it uses up the oxygen in the air, and it needs a new supply of oxygen to keep going. 
The oxygen that the fire in Centralia uses comes from the air above the ground. There are vents and cracks in the ground that allow air to flow down into the underground coal seam, where the fire is burning. As long as there is oxygen in the air above the ground, the fire in Centralia will continue to burn.
