query: What is DNS and DHCP . I have no idea . Please help . Thanks in advance . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
* * DNS is like a phone book . * * You either know the name or the phone number [ IP ] and need to know the other . Example : I know John Doe , but I do n't know his phone number . The phone book tells me John Doe 's at 555 - 555 - 5555 . [ I know COMPUTERNAME but do n't know the IP address . DNS tells me COMPUTERNAME is assigned 192.168.0.3 . ] * * DHCP is like the phone company * * , doling out phone numbers [ IPs ] to people [ computers ] . Example : Hey , AT & T , I just moved to the area and need a phone number . [ Hey , DHCP - SERVER , I just booted up and need an IP address . ] EDIT : I accidentally some formatting .
Both are means for your computer to find its way on the Internet . When your computer starts up , it wants to connect to a computer network , usually the Internet but sometimes a corporate network . The reason it wants to connect is so that you can check your mailbox and surf the web . In order to be able to connect to a network , your computer needs a unique address assigned to it . Compare this to sending a Valentine card through the postal system to your sweetheart . The postal system requires you to write the address of your sweetheart on the envelope . In much the same way , every computer that wants to use a computer network needs an address . For the postal system , an address is a street name , house number , city , state and zip code . For a computer network , an address is a number , called the IP address ( IP is short for Internet Protocol ) . Now , back to your question . When your computer starts up and connects to the network , it needs an IP address . There are several ways of getting one . The most simple one is that your computer already knows what its IP address is ( stored somewhere in a local configuration file ) and it simply uses that . This is called a static IP address and is mostly used for small networks , for instance a home network . Another way of getting an IP address is to ask the network you are connecting to for an address . This is where DHCP is used . What happens here is that on startup , your computer essentially sends a " Hello ! What is my IP address ? " question to all the computers on the network . However , it does not speak English but DHCP gibberish instead . Most of the other computers on the network are not able to understand DHCP gibberish and will simply ignore the question . However , one or maybe two computers on the network * * do * * speak DHCP gibberish and are able to answer your question : " Your IP address is 192.168.1.101 " ( this is the way IP addresses are written down ) . Your computer understands this answer and will adopt this IP address for as long as you stay connected to the network . If you switch off the computer , it will not be able to remember what its address was the next time you power it up and the whole Q : " Hello ! What is my IP address " A : " Your address is 198.172.1.99 " will be repeated . Notice that the second time , you receive a slightly different address ( ending in 99 instead of 101 ) . This is due to the " dynamic " part of DHCP . When you shut down your computer , the address that it had ( 192.168.1.101 ) is no longer occupied by your computer . So if someone else comes along and asks for an address , the DHCP computer may decide to recycle your old and now free IP address . Since an IP address must be unique , when your computer starts up again and requests an IP address , the old address will be already given to another computer so you receive another address in response . Now , for DNS . On the Internet , each and every computer must have a unique IP address . This IP address is a number . People in general are not very adept at recalling numbers . For instance , the IP address of the Google computer is 173.194.65.105 . I had to look it up . But URL_0 is something that I can recall from memory . What DNS does , is it translates human - readable website names to computer - readable IP addresses . So when you are in your web browser and decide to go to Google , what happens is that your computer poses a question to a special other computer . The question is " Hey , can you tell me the IP address for URL_0 ? " The special other computer looks up the name URL_0 and returns the IP address for that name . The " special computer " part in this case is a DNS server . It exists to translate names into numbers . When you connect to the Internet through your provider , your provider tells your computer what the IP address is for the special other computer that provides this name - to - IP - address translation service . So every time your computers needs to translate a website name to an IP address , it uses the IP address for the DNS server to pose the question . That is what DNS does
Imagine your computer network is an office with lots of cubicles separated by low walls . Now , there are no " fixed " cubicles assigned to individual employees : Everyone comes to work with their things in a briefcase and has to find an empty one to use for the day . It 's hard to tell if an empty cubicle is actually empty or whether the worker using it for the day is just in the bathroom or at lunch . How do you manage it ? The company assigns one guy as " DHCP server " . So when you walk in , and you need to find a cubicle you know nobody else is using . You * * yell * * ( to the whole room ) " Hey ! I need a cubicle ! Who manages this dump ! ? Where do I go ? " The DHCP server yells back , across the office and over the walls : " I 'm the cube manager from Cubicle 4 . Cubicle 17 was open , now it 's yours . The DNS guy is in cubicle 58 . " So you go over to Cubicle 17 and sit down and get ready for work . Now you need to talk to another employee , " Bob " . How do you find where Bob is today ? That 's where the DNS guy comes in . The DNS guy knows which cubicle different people are in based on their * names * . So you walk over to the DNS guy 's cubicle , have a short chat , and he tells you to go to Cubicle 45 to find Bob . The next time you want to talk to Bob , you might go straight to 45 because you remembered from last time and do n't want to bug DNS guy without a good reason .
rejected: DNS (Domain Name System) is like a phone book for the internet. When you type a website's name into your browser (like www.google.com), your computer needs to know what IP address to send that request to so it can find the website. DNS helps by converting the website's name into a numerical IP address that computers can understand. DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) is like a hotel concierge for devices connected to a network. When a new device connects to a network, it needs to be told what its IP address is, what the gateway is, and other network information. DHCP helps by automatically assigning these settings to the new device so it can communicate with other devices on the network. Here's a simple example: Imagine you're a computer trying to visit the website "www.google.com". First, you ask your DNS server (the phone book) for the IP address of www.google.com. The DNS server looks it up and tells you that the IP address is "74.125.224.72". Your computer then sends a request to that IP address, which is like calling the phone number for Google. When you reach Google, their DHCP server (the hotel concierge) assigns you a room number (IP address) and tells you where the lobby (gateway) is so you can find your way around the hotel (network).

Some things are so horrible that they will convince neutral countries to join the struggle against you . 4 . rejected: Laws of war, also known as the rules of armed conflict, are a set of guidelines that are followed during times of war or armed conflict. These laws are designed to protect civilians, to ensure that soldiers are treated humanely, and to minimize the damage caused by war.There are several reasons why laws of war exist. One reason is to protect civilians from harm. During times of war, it is easy for civilians to get caught in the middle of fighting and to be harmed or killed. The laws of war aim to minimize the harm to civilians and to ensure that they are not targeted by either side.Another reason why laws of war exist is to ensure that soldiers are treated humanely. The laws of war dictate how soldiers should be treated, including things like how they should be treated if they are captured and how they should be treated if they are injured. These laws are in place to make sure that soldiers are treated with respect and dignity, even in the midst of war.Finally, laws of war exist to minimize the damage caused by war. War can be extremely destructive, and the laws of war aim to limit the amount of damage that is done to buildings, infrastructure, and the environment. This helps to reduce the long-term effects of war and to make it easier for countries to rebuild and recover after a conflict ends.War crimes are actions that are considered to be serious violations of the laws of war. These can include things like targeting civilians or soldiers who have surrendered, torturing prisoners, or using weapons that are banned under international law. War crimes are considered to be very serious offenses and those who are found guilty of committing war crimes can be punished severely.

query: Why are propellers on the back of boats , but the front of airplanes ? Never made sense to me . Seems like airplanes play by their own set of rules Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
For reasons of balance , it 's much easier to have the engine at the front of a plane than at the back - unless you 're flying a canard , which has the wings at the back and the " tailplane " at the front . Boats , on the other hand , have a lot less problems with balance , and it 's easier to put the engine amidships or towards the back . Add to that water being more viscous so you do n't want high - speed flow dragging all along the hull , it makes sense to put the prop at the back .
Lots of reasons- If you put the propeller at the back of the plane- then when you rotate on takeoff or flare for landing- you put the propeller dangerously close to the ground . Airflow over the fuselage and wings gets pretty turbulent and when that air interacts with the propeller the propeller becomes noisier and less efficient . Waterflow over a boat hull tends to be a lot less turbulent and the propellers are either spinning much slower ( large boat ) or located below the hull where there is even less turbulence ( smaller boats ) . Cooling an air cooled engine can be difficult when the fuselage is in the way . Putting the engine in front greatly simplifies cooling baffles and such . The airflow from the propeller flowing over the wings helps generate additional lift you would n't have in a pusher configuration . Weight and balance is an issue . Putting the engine at the back generally means moving a lot of weight aft or using long drive shafts . Control surfaces are simpler and arguably better tested when they 're in the back and the engine would get in the way of putting them there . Edit : Almost forgot the most important reason ! Anything kicked up by the tires in a pusher configuration is going to pass right through the propeller and nick and otherwise damage it . This is a serious issue on unimproved airstrips- but even on a paved runway you kick up debris . Look at what happened to the Concorde when it kicked up a small metal wear strip . This is n't a factor for a boat . There are notable exceptions that do use a pusher configuration- The rear engine on a [ Cessna Skymaster ] ( URL_0 ) is a pusher ( and that engine has an overheating problem ) . Many small seaplanes are pushers with the engine mounted up high in a pod on the wing or on a pylon . The [ Beech Starship ] ( URL_2 ) was a twin engine pusher canard . It was the first real production composite airframe and the first business aircraft to have an all glass cockpit . The [ Piaggio P180 Avanti ] ( URL_1 ) is another twin engine pusher aircraft with a canard- but it also has a convention T tail as well . The P180 makes a very distinctive sound due to the interference between the propellers and the airflow over the wings and from the exuast .
The shape of the boat is why . ( Though , some airplanes in " pusher " configuratiin have had their prop(s ) in back . ) With a boat , it is easier to install a prop in back , with a shorter driveshaft while keeping the center of gravity more rearward . You do n't want the front of your boat to be lower than the back . Boats generally move faster with the bow angled up .
rejected: The reason that propellers are mounted on the back of boats and the front of airplanes is because of the way that these vehicles move through water and air.Boats move through water, which is a dense, viscous fluid. When a boat moves through water, the water pushes against the hull of the boat, providing a forward force that propels the boat forward. To move the boat, the propeller is mounted on the back of the boat and turns, pushing the water behind the boat and propelling the boat forward.Airplanes, on the other hand, fly through the air, which is a much less dense fluid than water. When an airplane moves through the air, it generates lift, which is a force that acts perpendicular to the direction of motion and lifts the airplane off the ground. The lift is generated by the wings of the airplane, which are specially shaped to produce lift when the airplane moves through the air. To move the airplane forward, the airplane has an engine that powers the propeller, which is mounted on the front of the airplane. The propeller turns, pulling the airplane through the air and propelling it forward.So, in short, the reason that propellers are mounted on the back of boats and the front of airplanes is because of the way that these vehicles move through their respective environments. Boats use the propeller to push against the water to move forward, while airplanes use the propeller to pull the airplane through the air to move forward.

query: Why do famous people get to live in luxury while being in debt ( tens of ) millions of dollars ? I do n't get how they are allowed to keep all of their assets while being in debilitating debt ... Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
What do you mean " allowed to keep all of their assets " ? So long as they are current on their payments , it does n't matter how much they are in debt . That 's no different than a middle class person with a big mortgage or credit card -- also living beyond their means , but able to do so if they keep current . If they go overdue , or even declare bankruptcy , they may have to sell some of their assets to pay creditors .
Just because someone has tens of millions of dollars of debt does n't mean it 's debilitating . That could be a completely manageable level of debt for a wealthy person .
Let 's say you want to buy a house . A reasonably expensive house , say , $ 100,000 . Do you have that much money sitting in the bank ? Very likely , no . Very few people do . Instead , the bank buys the house , and you pay the bank off slowly ( a mortgage ) . You 're now $ 100,000 in debt . If you 're paying $ 10,000 a year , but you make $ 50,000 a year ... you're fine . You 're in debt , yes , but you 're paying it off . A good credit rating is n't defined by a complete lack of debt , but rather a healthy management of debt . If you 're talking about Kanye West ... well ... Kanye is an idiot . He probably is grossly overstating the significance of his debt ( again , see the house analogy - how much he owes is n't as important as how much he owes * relative to how much he makes * ) .
rejected: Famous people often have a lot of money and assets, but they can also have a lot of expenses. For example, they might have to pay for a team of people to help them with their work, or they might have to pay for expensive things like houses and cars. Sometimes, even if they have a lot of money, they might not have enough to pay for all of their expenses. When this happens, they might have to borrow money from a bank or someone else. Even if they owe a lot of money, they can still keep their assets as long as they are able to make their loan payments. Do you understand?

query: Why is it that when a propeller starts spinning fast enough it appears to change direction ? What I 'm talking about : URL_0 Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
There was a great explanation of this the other day that I ca n't find , but that I will steal , so credit to whoever came up with this ( in the context of car tires ): Instead of a propeller , imagine a clock that you are watching through a video monitor , with the minute hand spinning around the center . Because of the biology of your eye and the speed of the camera watching the clock , you do n't really see it continuously moving , but instead you see it in flashes at a certain point on the face of the clock . Now , if the speed of the minute hand is such that it " moves " two hours every time you see it , it will look to you like it is going " forward . " First you will see it at 2 , then at 4 , then at 6 , etc ... But , imagine we make it much higher , so instead of going 2 hours every time it goes 11 . Now , the first time you see it , the minute hand is at 11 , but the next time it is at * * 10 * * , because 11 " hours " after 11 o'clock is 10 . And it continues like that , so now you see it at 11 , 10 , 9 , 8 , etc ... like it is counting down instead of up .
This is just a camera effect . Most cameras take 30 pictures per second . A propeller that spins at 1800 rpm ( or 30 revolutions per second ) will appear to stand still , because it always happens to be in the same position when the camera takes a new picture . At just over 1800 rpm , it will appear to rotate forwards very slowly , and at just under 1800 rpm it will appear to rotate backwards . So when the engine spins up , you get these effects whenever the camera 's shutter and the propeller speed are in sync . When the engine speeds up , this is what you 're going to see : 1700 rpm - appears to spin forwards really fast 1790 rpm - appears to spin backwards very slowly 1800 rpm - appears to stand still 1810 rpm - appears to spin forwards very slowly 1900 rpm - appears to spin forwards really fast By the way , I 'm just using 1800 rpm as an example , for a 3 - bladed prop and a 30 fps camera , this would also happen at 600 , 1200 , 1800 , 2400 rpm and so on . For a 4 - bladed prop it would be 450 , 900 , 1350 , 1800 , 2250 rpm etc .
Any video or monitor has a set refresh rate , meaning it shows a still image X times per second . If the propeller is spinning at the same speed or any multiple of that speed , it will appear to stand still in the video ( such as the 11 second mark ) . This is because by the time it takes another frame , the propeller has spun enough so that one blade matches the position of the blade in the previous frame . If it moves just a bit faster , it will appear to rotate forward . If it moves just a bit slower , it will appear to rotate backward .
rejected:
When a propeller starts spinning fast enough, it can appear to change direction because of the way our eyes and brains process the information.

Imagine you are standing in front of a spinning fan. The blades of the fan are moving around in a circle very quickly. As they move, they pass in front of your eyes one by one. Your brain is able to process this information and understand that the blades are moving in a circle.

Now, imagine that the fan is spinning even faster. The blades are now moving so quickly that they start to blur together. Your brain can still process the information, but it has a harder time understanding exactly what is happening. It might seem like the blades are moving in a different direction or that the whole fan is moving in a different direction.

This is known as the "stroboscopic effect." It happens when an object is moving so quickly that it appears to be moving in a different way to our brains.

So, when a propeller starts spinning fast enough, it can appear to change direction because our brains are not able to process the information about the movement of the blades as easily as they can when the propeller is moving more slowly.

query: How can we have the technology to complete safe assisted suicides but lack the technology for ' safe ' lethal injections ? In what way is killing by lethal injection medically different from killing by assisted suicide ? Please explain like I'm five.

> In what way is killing by lethal injection medically different from killing by assisted suicide ? It 's * legally * different , and the legality affects what methods can be used , and may not be based on(or current with , or capable of ) the most advantageous methods .
rejected:
Assisted suicide and lethal injection are two different things. Assisted suicide refers to a situation where a person chooses to end their own life with the help of another person, usually a doctor, who provides the means for the person to do so. Lethal injection, on the other hand, is a method of execution used by some governments to carry out the death penalty on people who have been convicted of certain crimes. 
In assisted suicide, the goal is to help a person who is suffering from a terminal illness or other severe medical condition to end their life in a peaceful and dignified manner. This can be done through the use of medication that the person takes themselves, or through other methods such as inhaling gases. The goal is to cause the person to fall asleep and then pass away peacefully, without experiencing any pain or suffering. 
Lethal injection, on the other hand, is a method of execution that is intended to quickly and painlessly cause the death of the person being executed. It involves injecting the person with a combination of drugs that are designed to stop their heart and other vital functions. The goal is to cause the person to pass away quickly and without experiencing any pain or suffering. 
The main difference between assisted suicide and lethal injection is the context in which they are used. Assisted suicide is used to help people who are suffering from terminal illnesses or other severe medical conditions to end their lives in a peaceful and dignified manner, while lethal injection is used as a means of carrying out the death penalty on people who have been convicted of certain crimes.

Personal loans and home equity loans typically have fixed interest rates and fixed monthly payments, while FHA 203(k) loans are specifically designed for home renovations and can be used to finance both the purchase and the improvements.Home equity line of credit (HELOC): A HELOC is a type of loan that is secured by the equity in your home. query: What causes thrombocytopenia?I'm 17 years old (150 lbs, 5'6") and was diagnosed with thrombocytopenia about two weeks ago.  I'm also suffering from mild depression. Is there any vitamins or supplements I can take to increase the platelet count? Upon admission into the hospital it was 50, then 40, 30 and a week ago, 75. They haven't yet figured out what is causing this but I am curious myself. Thanks.
chosen: hi, there are many causes of thrombocytopenia. most common cause is simple viral infection. many other infection like malaria dengue also causes thrombocytopenia. peripheral destruction or decreased production may be responsible for that. so kindly investigate for the cause and then take treatment accordingly. you will definitly get benifits. thanks for using health care magic.
rejected:
Thrombocytopenia is a condition in which there is a low number of platelets in the blood. Platelets are small cells in the blood that help the blood to clot when you are injured. A normal platelet count is usually between 150,000 and 450,000 platelets per microliter of blood. A platelet count below 150,000 is considered low and may lead to an increased risk of bleeding.

There are many potential causes of thrombocytopenia, including:

Infections: viral or bacterial infections can cause a decrease in platelet production or increase in platelet destruction.

Medications: certain medications, such as heparin, aspirin, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), can cause thrombocytopenia.

Autoimmune disorders: autoimmune disorders, such as lupus or rheumatoid arthritis, can cause the body to produce antibodies that attack and destroy platelets.

Cancer: certain types of cancer, such as leukemia and lymphoma, can cause thrombocytopenia by disrupting platelet production or increasing platelet destruction.

Nutritional deficiency: a deficiency in certain nutrients, such as iron or folic acid, can cause thrombocytopenia.

Pregnancy: pregnant women may develop thrombocytopenia due to the increased demand for platelets during pregnancy.

Alcohol abuse: excessive alcohol consumption can lead to thrombocytopenia.

It is important to consult with your healthcare provider to determine the specific cause of your thrombocytopenia and to receive appropriate treatment. In some cases, vitamin or mineral supplements may be recommended to help increase platelet count, but it is important to only take supplements under the guidance of a healthcare provider.

query: Should I pay a company who failed to collect VAT from me over 6 months ago?
chosen: Note: I am not a lawyer. This is my personal opinion and interpretation. First, your source is European Law, which obviously doesn't apply outside of the EU. Second, if you owe something, you owe it; it doesn't matter if it was forgotten to be mentioned. At best, you can say that under those circumstances you don't want the software anymore, and i would assume you can send it back and get your money back (minus a fee for having it used for a while...) - this gets quite difficult to calculate clearly, so it's probably not a good avenue to follow for you. As the company has to send the VAT to your country (they will not be allowed to keep a dime of it, and have to bear the complete cost for the handling), it is a debt you have to your government; they are just the entity responsible for collecting it. Still, if you just ignore them, they will probably suck it up, and your government will also not do a thing to you. If they only have your email address, they have no way of knowing if you even still have/use this address; for all they know, it could be you never got it. They also cannot simply charge your card, as they probably don't have the card data any more (they are not supposed to keep it after the transaction is complete, and they thought it was complete at the time). All in all, you should be safe to ignore it. It's between you and your god/consciousness, if you feel obliged to pay it, as technically you owe it.
rejected: It is generally advisable to pay any taxes that are owed, including value-added tax (VAT). They should be able to provide you with details about why they failed to collect the VAT and whether you are responsible for paying it.If you do owe VAT and you do not pay it, you may be subject to penalties and interest charges. It is generally best to pay any taxes that are owed as soon as possible to avoid these additional costs.If you have concerns about the amount of VAT that you have been charged or about the company's failure to collect it, you may want to seek the advice of a tax professional or contact your local tax authority for further guidance.

query: why it 's so hard to think about nothing ? You know what i mean , why its so hard to get no thoughts or memories in your mind . I always feel like my mind is a boiling pot of thoughts when i try to fall asleep . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Look into meditation , even taking basic steps ( breathing control , finding out * what * mental silence is ) helps out alot .

In my experience a lot of people find the first instance of ' quiet mind ' rather profound when they learn meditation . With a bunch of hindsight what I think is actually profound about it is realizing that the mind gets quiet * naturally * if the conditions are right . For my answer to your question the phrase " the mind gets quiet naturally if the conditions are right " is key . query: Why does the stock market exist , and why is it such a big deal in our economy ? Woah , first page . Did n't expect that . o.O Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Well , before explaining the stock market , we need to understand stocks . Thankfully , it 's not that hard . To start a corporation , a group of people each put in the initial money . Each of these persons gets a number of stock shares proportional to the amount of money they put in . Owning shares of the corporation means that if and when the corporation pays out some of its profits ( if there are ever any ; this is not guaranteed ! ) , the shareholder will receive some of it , in proportion to the number of shares they own . Now , stock is also * transferable * . It means that if you own stock in one corporation , you 're not stuck with it forever ; you may give it to other people in exchange for money , as a gift , or your children may inherit it . When this happens , the corporation is notified that the number of shares in question changed ownership , and they write it down in their records . Anytime the corporation pays out some of its profits to its shareholders , it uses these records to determine who to pay and how much . Now , the stock market is just a set of financial institutions to make it easy for people to buy and sell stock . Why is this so valuable ? Because the existence of the stock market makes it easier to find people willing to invest money to start corporations . If there was no stock market , and you gave some money to start a company , you 'd be stuck with that investment forever ; if you had an unexpected family emergency you 'd have no way to get any of that money back quickly , so you 'd be very unwilling to invest your money that way unless you were very , very rich and could spare it . If there is a stock market , however , and the stock may be sold freely and easily in it , then that lowers the bar a lot , and people who would otherwise find it too risky to invest into a corporation are now able to . It 's still risky , because the corporation may lose money , but you have much better chances of being able to get some of your money back for an emergency .
Companies have to have owners . For smaller companies this is usually one person or a group of people . These are called private companies . When companies start to get really big the owners decide to sell stock in their company . Each stock represents a small part of the company and anyone who owns stock actually owns a small part of the company . These companies are known as public companies . Because these public companies are so big and complicated , they register with a stock market to regulate how shares are bought and sold . Since all the big companies are public they have a big impact on the economy .
The following answers are paraphrased from the standard textbook , * Foundations of Financial Markets and Institutions * . These answers are more concise , complete , and authoritative than others . * * Main answer :* * The * * stock market * * serves three primary purposes . * * First , * * it determines the current price of a given asset based on the interactions of buyers and sellers of that asset . This is called the * price discovery process * . * * Second , * * financial markets provide a mechanism for an investor to sell an asset , without having to search at the local pub for a buyer . This is called * liquidity * . * * Third , * * financial markets reduce the cost of such transactions by reducing * search costs * and * information costs * . Search costs are the price in advertising , time , etc . that would be needed to find assets , buyers , and sellers without an established market . Information costs are the price of research that would be needed to establish the value of a financial asset if there was no market to determine it . To answer the second part of your question , " why is it such a big deal , " you have to think about the purpose of financial assets . ( Note : financial assets like stocks could , in theory , exist without a centralized stock market , but your question was about the " stock market , " which is why I handled it first . ) So ... * * Financial assets , * * such as stocks and bonds , have * two main functions . * * * First , * * they enable the transfer of funds from those who have surplus cash they wish to invest to those who need funds to invest in tangible assets . A * tangible asset * , unlike a financial asset , is tangible -- like , for instance , the tables you 've got to buy before opening a restaurant . * * Second , * * financial assets * redistribute the risk * associated with the cash flow generated by tangible assets ( like the stuff inside a restaurant or factory ) . For example , if my life savings is $ 200,000 , and I need that much to open a new business , I might prefer to sell a 50 % ownership of the business for $ 100,000 instead of investing every penny I have -- so that I wo n't be broke if the business fails . This is a redistribution of risk . Or if I own a restaurant but am worried that I 'll go broke if the restaurant burns down , then I buy fire insurance -- another redistribution of risk . Finally , what determines the * * price * * of a stock or bond ? Per above , its settled by market consensus , but the price does reflect something real . It reflects the * present value of the expected future cash flow * to which that financial asset entitles you . A * bond * , for instance , is a loan : you are entitled to be repaid the value of the loan plus interest . A * stock * , meanwhile , is partial ownership of a company : you are entitled to the earnings of the company , in the form of dividends and an increase in the company 's value . In both cases , there is some risk that the * issuer*--the company ( or government ) that sold you the asset -- will not meet the expectations . That 's why the price of a financial asset is the current value of the * expected * future cash flow . If there 's a big probability that the company will go belly - up , that risk is built into the expectation of future cash flow and thus built into the price .
rejected: The stock market is a place where people can buy and sell shares of stock in public companies. These companies sell shares of stock to raise money to grow and operate their business. When you buy a share of stock, you become a part owner of the company. The stock market is a big deal because it helps companies raise the money they need to operate and grow, and it also gives people the opportunity to make money by buying and selling shares of stock. The stock market is an important part of the economy because it helps to provide funding for businesses, which in turn can create jobs and help the economy grow.

query: What is quotational loss in stock market?
chosen: https://www.fool.com/investing/general/2013/07/30/2-types-of-risk-2-types-of-bubbles.aspx (mirror): The Wall Street Journal reviews: What Mr. Bernstein calls "shallow risk" is a temporary drop in an asset's market price; decades ago, the great investment analyst Benjamin Graham referred to such an interim decline as "quotational loss." "Deep risk," on the other hand, is an irretrievable real loss of capital, meaning that after inflation you won't recover for decades -- if ever. So quotational loss = loss not explained by change of actual value of a firm.
rejected: Quotational loss, also known as quotation loss or quote loss, refers to the difference between the price at which a trader bought or sold a security and the next available price quoted by the market. It can occur when there is a delay in executing a trade, such as when a trader places a market order or when there is a large discrepancy between the bid and ask prices.For example, consider a trader who wants to sell 100 shares of a stock at the current market price. The current bid price is $50 per share, and the ask price is $50.50 per share. The trader places a market order to sell the shares at the current market price, expecting to receive $50 per share. However, due to delays in the market, the trade does not execute until the next available quote, which is now $49.50 per share. In this case, the trader would experience a quotational loss of $0.50 per share, or a total loss of $50 on the trade.Quotational loss can be a significant risk for traders, particularly in fast-moving or highly volatile markets. It can also be a source of frustration for traders, as they may feel that they missed out on an opportunity to execute a trade at a more favorable price. To minimize the risk of quotational loss, traders may use limit orders or other trade execution strategies that allow them to specify the price at which they are willing to buy or sell a security.

query: The Helium Shortage What is causing the shortage ? What are some of the consequences of having no helium ? Is it 100 % inevitable that we 'll run out ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:

That caused helium to become very cheap , so cheap it was no longer profitable to extract it from natural gas , so people stopped trying and let it escape into the atmosphere . Well , the US stockpile is now completely sold off , and helium is getting more expensive . People are starting to collect it again , but it will take a few years for them to catch up with demand . Until then , helium will be expensive . Bottom line , there really is n't a shortage , just a massive fluctuation in supply due to other factors .

When it comes out of the ground , if it is n't captured , helium is so light that it will float right into outer space , so it 's not like we can skim it out of the air , either . A long time ago , America 's government started to save up large amounts of helium because they thought it would be useful for zeppelins and such . It 's not , but helium is used for many high tech purposes . Anyway , the government has this huge stockpile and has been selling it over the years . Then sometime in the 90 's , congress decided that they did n't want to keep this helium anymore and made a law that said it all had to be sold off by 2015 ( it costs a lot of money to keep the helium there ) . Because of this law , the helium is being sold off for much cheaper than it should , especially because it 's not something we can just make more of . Liquid helium is used to cool parts in things like MRI machines and experiments with strong magnets . Helium is also used in the manufacture of computer chips ( though honestly I do n't know how off the top of my head ) , so it is very important even though most people do n't know .

Helium is a rare gas that is found in small amounts in the Earth's atmosphere and in certain natural gas deposits. It is used in a variety of applications, including balloons, airships, diving tanks, and MRI machines.

There are a few factors that have contributed to the current helium shortage:

Limited supply: Helium is a finite resource, and there are only a few places in the world where it can be mined or extracted from natural gas. This means that there is a limited amount of helium available.

High demand: Helium is used in a lot of different industries, and demand for it has been increasing in recent years. This has put pressure on the available supply.

Infrastructure issues: Some of the facilities that produce helium have experienced technical problems or disruptions, which has also contributed to the shortage.

If we run out of helium, it could have some serious consequences. For example, MRI machines, which use helium to cool the magnets that create images of the body, might not be able to function. Helium is also used in airships and balloons, so these industries could be affected as well.

It is not inevitable that we will completely run out of helium, but it is important to use it responsibly and to find ways to recycle and reuse it to help conserve this valuable resource.

query: Why does our belly get " fat " but our backs do n't ? I know that there is a layer of fat under my back - skin too , but somehow it seems to amass on the frontside of my body ... Why ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I believe that a lot of it is not actually fat under your skin , but fat around your guts that causes them to stick out . This is actually a different kind of fat . The stuff under your skin is called * subcutaneous fat , * while the stuff around your guts is called * visceral fat . * They are actually different types , and can be affected by your * hormones*- chemicals in your body that tell it what to do . Stress , a lack of exercise , genetics , diabetes ( too much sugar in your blood ) , and other factors can cause an increase in the fat around your guts relative to fat under your skin , which is why some people can be skinny and have big bellies , and others can have a lot of body fat but not have their tummies stick out so much . Your skeletal system and muscles keep it from sticking out of your back , although if you get enough fat on your body , you can get a decent amount of ' back fat ' also . The actual distribution of fat underneath your skin is , as I understand it , mostly determined by your genetics . This means that ' spot reduction'- the idea that you can lose fat in a specific area more than others say by exercising muscles near that far more- is a myth . Some people 's genes make them put more of their fat in their faces or stomachs or arms or legs than others . We 're all a little different in that way . Situps , for example , wo n't reduce your tummy fat in a larger proportion than anywhere else on your body ( or at all if you do n't burn more Calories / kilojoules { energy } than you eat ) , although they can strengthen muscles that hold it in more . And doing squats or lunges wo n't take off leg fat , either . Sorry if I 'm going a little too far in explaining some terms- they did say EXPLAINLIKEIMFIVE , but more importantly , some terms may be difficult for people whose first language is not English .

query: What limits CPU speed ? Also what makes processors at the same GHz and same number of cores faster than one another ? Furthermore the differences between CPU and GPU . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
A CPU is based on transistors switching on or off depending on an input signal . And the transistors are connected to each other to create the logic . So a simple addition of two numbers might require a signal to go though a few hundred transistors to switch them to the correct states for the correct output to show . And because electricity takes a certain amount of time this cascading of state will take a tiny bit of time . The exact time it takes depends on the layout of the design , the materials used , the supply voltage and the temperature . To avoid miscalculations the clock in the CPU which signals the CPU to go into the next state have to be slow enough that all the changes have cascaded properly . Overclockers will increase the voltage and increase the cooling to make the CPU states cascade faster so they can safely increase the clock speed . A CPU takes machine code instructions to tell it how it should operate . However there is no direct relation between the number of instructions it executes and the clock cycles . Sometimes an instruction might take several clock cycles to complete and sometimes the CPU can execute two or more instructions at the same time . CPU manufacturers are constantly coming up with new ways to make the code take the fewest possible clock cycles to complete . A GPU is quite different from a CPU . In graphics processing it is very common to do the same computations on a lot of different data . For example calculating where on the screen every single point in a scene is supposed to be or changing the color of every pixel on the entire screen . So to do this they have a lot of cores . And the cores are often less capable then their CPU counterparts with some even unable to calculate integers , only floating points . And all the logic that is dealing with the instructions and transferring data to and from memory is removed from the cores and placed in a common supervisor location . So all cores will run the exact same instructions but on different data . This means that you can have a lot simpler cores and therefore a lot more cores on the same chip for the same price .
Heat Heat is the great limiter . You 'll occasionally see overclocking competitions where they get a CPU up to 8 GHz but have to use liquid nitrogen to keep it cool in the process . The faster the processor runs the more heat it makes Back in the day when we were mostly cranking up the MHz it was helpful . A 486 66MHz was twice as fast as the 486 33MHz . These days most things come down to architecture . Now the specs are n't nearly as helpful . Saying you have 3.6 GHz quad core processor tells me about as much about overall performance as saying your car has a 3.8L V6 that redlines at 7k RPM . Is it the one from the GT - R that provides over 500HP or the one from an old Ford Taurus that gives just 210HP ? The real speeds these days come from rearranging how it handles the tasks to match modern workloads . While you only have 1 Floating point unit , maybe it is longer so it can be working on multiplying multiple sets of numbers at the same time . Maybe your chip is better at guessing what its going to need next so it can stay busy instead of waiting for it to be read off the hard drive(hard drive reads take * forever * in CPU time ) . A GPU is really good at doing tons of really simple things at the same time while a CPU is really good at doing a really hard thing , but only one at a time . Lets take two examples 1.A=((5.18384-(42.323 ^ 27 ) ) /(34843 ^ 342847 % 1284234 ) 2 . for ( i=0;i < 100,000;i++){C[i]=A[i]+B[i ] } The first one is obviously very computationally intense and you ca n't really split it up too much . If you wrote a script to do that your CPU would likely spend a few minutes chugging away and then spit out an answer at you . Your GPU would issue that problem to 1 of its 4000 cores and sit at 0.1 % utilization for the next couple days before it got you an answer back The second one is very simple math and very easy to split up . Just issue a different value of i to each core and work them at the same time . Your CPU will work on i=0 , then i=1 , then i=2 , ..... and give you an answer in a little while when its done . Your GPU will issue i=0 through i=3999 to its 4000 cores , work on it in parallel , then issue i=4000 through i=7999 to them .... Your GPU will sit at 100 % utilization for a couple seconds and spit an answer at you . Your CPU likely performs each math operation faster , maybe 10x as fast as the GPU can , but the GPU is doing 4000 at the same time so it still has a 400x speed advantage ! CPU - good for hard things , one at a time GPU - good for easy things , lots and lots of easy things all at the same time
CPU speed is mostly limited by heat . One thing that is n't mentioned a whole lot is that most of that heat comes from processor interconnects -- the wiring between the transistors . Not only do processors contain billions of transistors , those things have to be wired together with more than 60 miles of copper wire . To push signals really quickly through all that wire requires a decent voltage . Heat is roughly proportional to voltage squared . So that really limits things . As a simple example to think about clock speed ( GHz ) vs. performance , a processor that can only add will take more time to do a multiplication than a processor that can do multiplication in one step . Given that a step is a clock cycle , the processor with more and more useful features will be faster per GHz . Basically some processors can do more work in one step than others . I 'm less knowledgeable about GPUs so I wo n't address that part of your question .
rejected:
CPU stands for Central Processing Unit, and it is the brain of a computer that performs most of the processing tasks. The speed of a CPU is measured in GHz, which stands for gigahertz. One GHz is equal to one billion cycles per second.

There are several factors that can limit the speed of a CPU. One of these is the clock speed, which is the rate at which the CPU processes instructions. The faster the clock speed, the faster the CPU can perform tasks. Another factor that can limit the speed of a CPU is the number of cores it has. Cores are essentially small processors that can work independently to perform different tasks at the same time. The more cores a CPU has, the more tasks it can perform simultaneously, which can make it faster overall.

There are also other factors that can affect the speed of a CPU, such as the efficiency of its design, the quality of its manufacturing, and the amount of power it uses.

GPU stands for Graphics Processing Unit, and it is a type of processor that is specifically designed to handle tasks related to graphics and video processing. A GPU is often faster at these types of tasks than a CPU, because it has many more cores and is specifically designed to perform them quickly.

In general, a CPU is responsible for performing a wide range of tasks, including running the operating system, running applications, and handling input and output. A GPU, on the other hand, is specifically designed to handle tasks related to graphics and video processing, such as rendering 3D graphics, processing video, and displaying images on a screen.

query: Why are n't people woken up by their own loud snoring ? Please help me understand so that I do n't smother my husband to death tomorrow evening . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:

Our nervous system is continuously bombarded with information that it uses to control the many processes that keep us alive and healthy and out of danger . For example , it receives information from sensors that measure oxygen and carbon dioxide to regulate breathing . The brain also handles sensory information so that we can see , hear , feel , taste , and smell and are able to respond to these sensations . When we sleep , the brain continues to process vital information ( for example , breathing is still controlled ) , but it ignores the information from the sensory organs . We are disengaged from our sensations . The information is still bombarding the nervous system , but brain centers actively filter the information . Essentially , the brain filters it out as it 's a function of the body .
I used to be , periodically . I 've been mistaken for a bear when at a campsite before ... I second /u / CommitteeofOne here - he may have sleep apnea . I do . Getting a CPAP machine changed my life .

When this happens, the air has to work harder to get through the throat, which can cause the tissues in the throat to vibrate. This vibration is what we hear as snoring.The reason why people don't wake up when they snore is because the brain is in a deep sleep and doesn't respond to the noise. It's not uncommon for people to snore and not realize it. However, if you're worried about your husband's snoring, it might be a good idea to talk to a doctor. They can help determine if there is a medical reason for the snoring and recommend treatment if necessary.

query: Why do sports games sometimes get blacked out , even close to the team 's city ? I was in Buffalo and the Red Sox / Indians ( in Cleveland ) game was blacked out for some reason . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
They black out games in local markets to make sure they get enough attendance at the games . It 's the leagues way of selling tickets . The thought process is , people are n't going to the games because they can watch it at home , if we do n't get enough people at the games we will take the home option away .
It depends on the sport and their local terms . The NFL 's policy supports game attendance , and is simply a base distance from the city , but the MLB 's rules protect local broadcasters and are based on the team 's [ ' territory ' ] ( URL_0 ) ( so El Paso is considered part of the territory for the Houston Astros ) . The NBA 's blackout policy only applies to NBA TV . For the NFL , the idea is to keep ticket sales high . The profit off a ticket sale ( and the related parking , concessions , and memorabilia sales ) is far higher than the profit from a single viewer . However , teams and broadcasters do n't want to lose the money from the broadcast , so they have the option to tape off sections of the stadium for the season ( something the Jacksonville Jaguars have been forced to do ) or buy the remaining tickets and give them to charity . It does , however , mean that struggling teams run into further problems , because they lose even more money if their local broadcast never shows .
One , to keep ticket sales up . Two , it protects the smaller sports channels that have rights to the team 's games and air all or most of their games . For example , I am an Atlanta Braves fan , and most of their games are on FoxSports South . So if they are blacked out on ESPN , it 's because FoxSports South is airing the game .
rejected:
Blackouts in sports refer to the practice of preventing certain games from being broadcast on television or other media in certain locations. This is often done to protect the exclusive rights of local broadcasters to show certain games, or to encourage people to attend the games in person rather than watching them on television.

For example, if the Red Sox are playing the Indians in Cleveland, and the game is being broadcast on TV in the Cleveland area, it might be blacked out in the Boston area to protect the exclusive rights of the local Boston broadcaster to show the game. Similarly, the game might be blacked out in the Buffalo area if it is being shown on a local Buffalo station.

Blackouts can be frustrating for fans who want to watch their favorite team play, but they are a common practice in professional sports and are used to protect the financial interests of the teams, leagues, and broadcasters involved.

query: How are huge prime numbers usefull in cryptography ? I recently stumbled upon this article : URL_0 and had no clue why it was so revolutionary to find such a number and why do those people spend so much time and resources for that ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Huge primes are useful in public key cryptography , specifically RSA . Basically , the reason for this is that we ( collectively as human beings ) know how to multiply together two numbers very efficiently . Given two numbers of essentially arbitrary size , we can compute their product in very little time . However , we do not know how to do the reverse efficiently . Given some huge number x , that is a product of two huge primes p and q ( so x = pq ) , we do not know how to factor x into p and q in a way that does n't take an incredibly long time -- and by incredibly long I mean unimaginably long . Certainly longer than any human life . Various cryptosystems ( RSA is the big one ) are constructed in such a way that to decrypt an encrypted message * without * knowing the proper decryption key , you would need to factor some huge number into its two prime factors . Edit : Just noticed the article that you linked . Primes of * that * size are not particularly useful . The security of cryptography is basically guaranteed with much , much smaller primes . This particular result is more of a mathematical novelty than a cryptographic success .
Huge prime numbers are useful for cryptography as multiplying them together very easy calculation to do one way and really hard to do the other . For example , 13 times 9 equals 117 . That is simple to do . Let 's say you make a password system that does an equation to unlock the login , and the answer has to match 117 . in your system you store a person 's password as 13 rather than the actual password , and then times that by 9 . If it 's correct , you will have 13 times 9 which equals 117 . It is much safer than matching password123 to see if it directly matches password123 . If someone gets into your system , they can just see everyone 's password , while if you used prime number equation they would only see the number 117 . Let 's take it from the hackers point of view with a different number . What two prime numbers , multiplied together , equals 7781 ? The only one you can eliminate straight off the bat is that it ca n't be number 2 , as it 's an odd number , . Well , what happens when I divide it by three ? No , I get 2593.66666 ... , so that is n't correct , maybe if I tried seven ... The only way to find out what two prime numbers equal 7781 is to go through and divide it by every single prime number till you get a result that is also a prime number . It will take a bit of time to figure out it 's 31 times 251 . Longer the prime number you are dealing with , the more computational time it will take for any hackers to figure out what prime numbers are being used .
Finding ever - larger primes is just showing off ; they 're already well outside the bounds of practicality . In cryptography , primes are useful because you can multiply two large primes together and let people use the resulting number to encrypt messages , and use the primes to decrypt them . Only you know the primes , and it would take an extremely long time to factor the number you shared and find them out . If you just used two random numbers , people would usually be able to use ( 2 and half the number ) , ( 3 and 1/3 the number ) , ( 5 and 1/5 the number ) or a similar easy answer to decrypt your message .
rejected: In cryptography, prime numbers are used to create secure communication channels. A prime number is a whole number that is greater than 1 and is not divisible by any other number except for 1 and itself.One way that prime numbers are used in cryptography is through the use of public key infrastructure (PKI). PKI is a system that uses a pair of keys - a public key and a private key - to secure communication between two parties. The public key is used to encrypt a message, and the private key is used to decrypt it.The security of PKI relies on the fact that it is computationally infeasible to determine the private key from the public key. This is because the private key is generated from the product of two large prime numbers, and it is very difficult to factorize this product back into the original prime numbers.Therefore, if someone wants to send a secure message to someone else, they can use the recipient's public key to encrypt the message, and only the recipient, who has the private key, can decrypt and read the message. This allows for secure communication even if the message is intercepted by someone else.Finding very large prime numbers is important for cryptography because the larger the prime numbers used to generate the private key, the more secure the key is. It takes more time and resources to factorize larger prime numbers, so finding a very large prime number helps to make the PKI system more secure.

query: Why does the world feel quieter when it snows ? Just a Canadian waiting for the bus when it dawned on me how quiet it was . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's harder for sound to travel through the air when there is snow in it . Same thing is true when it rains ( but you might not notice it as the rain itself makes a lot of noise ) Also the snow already on the ground also works like sound dampening . As the sound can bounce much easier on the hard ground than the soft snow .
/u / GamGreger 's answer is correct from a physics standpoint , but the other reason is simply less stuff going on . Cars travel slower , less people are out and about , so less total noise+higher dampening effect = pure heaven .
I 'm Irish so I 've probably only experienced this like 4 times in my life , but snow covered grounds on a moonlit night , may be one of the most magical things in my opinion . Everything is so peaceful , only the sound of you 're breath hanging in the air and boots crunching in the shimmering snow . It really feels like time is standing still , or you 're in another world . I wish we got it more .
rejected: Snow can make the world feel quieter because it absorbs sound waves. When sound waves hit a solid object, they can either be reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through the object. Snow is a soft, fluffy material that is able to absorb sound waves, rather than reflecting them like a hard surface would. This means that the sound waves are absorbed by the snow, rather than bouncing off of it and traveling through the air. As a result, there is less noise in the air, and the world may seem quieter.

query: Please explain what is "Artificial neuron"
chosen:
An artificial neuron is a mathematical function conceived as a model of biological neurons, a neural network. Artificial neurons are elementary units in an artificial neural network. The artificial neuron receives one or more inputs (representing excitatory postsynaptic potentials and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials at neural dendrites) and sums them to produce an output (or activation, representing a neuron's action potential which is transmitted along its axon). Usually each input is separately weighted, and the sum is passed through a non-linear function known as an activation function or transfer function[clarification needed]. The transfer functions usually have a sigmoid shape, but they may also take the form of other non-linear functions, piecewise linear functions, or step functions. They are also often  monotonically increasing, continuous, differentiable and bounded. Non-monotonic, unbounded and oscillating activation functions with multiple zeros that outperform sigmoidal and ReLU like activation functions on many tasks have also been recently explored. The thresholding function has inspired building logic gates referred to as threshold logic; applicable to building logic circuits resembling brain processing. For example, new devices such as memristors have been extensively used to develop such logic in recent times.
The artificial neuron transfer function should not be confused with a linear system's transfer function.
Artificial neurons can also refer to artificial cells in neuromorphic engineering (see below) that are similar to natural physical neurons.
rejected:
An artificial neuron is a mathematical function that is designed to mimic the behavior of a biological neuron. A biological neuron is a cell that is found in the nervous system of animals, and it is responsible for receiving, processing, and transmitting information. An artificial neuron receives input from other neurons or external sources, processes that input using a set of weights and biases, and then produces an output. The output of an artificial neuron is typically binary (either 0 or 1), but it can also be a continuous value between 0 and 1.



Artificial neurons are the basic building blocks of artificial neural networks, which are computer algorithms that are inspired by the structure and function of the brain. Artificial neural networks are used for a variety of tasks, including image recognition, language translation, and decision-making. They are particularly useful for tasks that require the processing of large amounts of data or for tasks that are too complex for traditional algorithms to solve.

query: What 's holding us back from truly photorealistic cgi ? Lack of computing power or artistic skill ? Both ? How will we exit the uncanny valley ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I think we have both the computing power and the artistic skill . Conquering the uncanny valley is going to require a perfect understanding of exactly how we perceive the movements in a human face . It 's taking the time to encode all the subtlety properly that 's making it a non - profitable venture at the moment . But we are [ getting insanely close ] ( URL_0 ) .
Your brain does an incredibly weird thing all the time . It takes your memories and superimposes them onto the environment you see . What you actually , actively see is a small ring of color vision , a larger ring of black and white vision and a tiny blind spot in the middle . That is what your eyes actually perceive . Your brain takes this information , superimposes your remembered environment in full color , and does so in such a seamless manner that other parts of your brain do n't notice what is going on . This means that a few simple things will trick all the systems that go on to simplify this information transfer . A lot of optical illusions just hijack the optical center 's cost - cutting measures like someone using the default Linksys password to get into someone else 's wifi ( if they 're smart they changed the password , but you can bank on people being lazy a lot of the time ) . So when people are making CGI , they are producing the optical illusion of reality that is so complex that your brain is able to catch the little mistakes and gets confused by certain mixed signals or out of place cues that put us into the uncanny valley , where what we are seeing looks more like a poorly animated corpse than a living human face .
This is actually an interesting question . We already * have * truely photorealistic CGI . 99 % of movies these days have CG used in many major ways - even romantic comedies . Things like putting a crowd in a stadium , adding and removing things or people from a scene after the fact , or replacing a stuntman 's face with the main actor is very common . We never notice this because it 's perfect . It was actually perfected quite a while ago . If you 've seen the first Jurrasic Park , the scene towards the end where the girl falls through the ductwork and the raptor jumps to bite her leg - she looks right at the camera . This is actually the stunt woman , and they were able to paste the main actress 's face right on top ! So , there will always be an uncanny valley , but at this point we can blame it on artistic skill . There is nothing , technologically , keeping CG from looking completely real . The rendering engines can trace light with complete accuracy , and photography and motion capture can animate the models with perfect accuracy . We even have skeletal and muscle simulation !
rejected: Creating truly photorealistic computer-generated imagery (CGI) requires a combination of factors, including powerful computing resources, advanced algorithms, and artistic skill.One of the main challenges in creating photorealistic CGI is the need to simulate a wide range of physical phenomena, such as lighting, shading, and materials, in order to create realistic-looking images. This requires a lot of computing power, as the computer needs to calculate all of these different effects for every pixel in the image.In addition to computational resources, creating photorealistic CGI also requires a high level of artistic skill. Artists must be able to create 3D models and textures that look realistic and believable, and they must also be able to use software tools to light and render the scene in a way that looks natural.The "uncanny valley" refers to the phenomenon where an image or animation of a human-like entity looks almost, but not quite, real. This can be unsettling to viewers and can make it difficult for them to fully suspend their disbelief. To exit the uncanny valley, we will need to continue to improve both our computational resources and our artistic skills, as well as our understanding of the factors that contribute to the realism of an image or animation.

query: what does a conductor for a orchestra actually do ? i understand they would help hem start on time and maybe keep in time but i honestly do n't get it ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It helps a lot to have a visual aid that everyone can look at and sync with . You can know a composition inside and out , but it 's hard to keep perfect rhythm , on your own . The conductor provides this visual aid as well , and by his movements and actions the orchestra / band is clued into when to play powerfully , when to play softly , when only the strings kick in , or when only the brass comes in , and he can direct sections at a time . It 's hard to explain but you feel the music as the musician , but you feel it even more when someone in front of you is feeling it in real time as well . Like for the climax of a song , when you see the conductor really getting into it , sweating , look of rapture on his face , like the pre cum face ... it just makes you want to play more passionately , because you know that climax is coming . Do we NEED him ? not really ... not technically I should say , but the conductor does serve a purpose .
I was in band in high school . Yes , they start them and keep the tempo . They also give cues to when different parts of the song are coming up . The instrumentalists follow the sheet music , so they should be ready , but the cue from the conductor ensures everyone stays together . They also give more subtle cues to imply certain members or sections should , for example , play louder . Most of their work is done during rehearsals . They lead the sessions and tell the band how to play .
Definitely to make sure everyone in the group knows the time . The time speeds up and slows down , so there would be no other way to make sure everyone knew that exact time from those changes . Also the conductor signals changes in volume , and can exagerrate those changes for higher emotional responses from the audience . Beyond that , signaling a section for prominent parts of the song and/or soloists . As a performer , I have questioned whether I 've counted the rests correctly , and that signal can mean the world .
rejected: A conductor is the person who leads an orchestra during a performance. They stand in front of the orchestra and use hand gestures and facial expressions to help the musicians know when to start playing and when to stop. The conductor also helps the orchestra play together as a group by keeping everyone in time with each other. Think of the conductor like a traffic cop. They help everyone know when to go and when to stop, and they make sure everyone stays in their own lanes so that everyone gets where they need to go safely. During a performance, the conductor will usually have a copy of the music that the orchestra is playing. They will look at the music and then give cues to the musicians to help them know when to start playing and when to stop. The conductor will also make sure that the orchestra is playing at the right speed and with the right dynamics (loud or soft). In addition to leading the orchestra, the conductor is also responsible for rehearsing the music with the orchestra before the performance. They will work with the musicians to make sure that everyone knows their parts and that the music sounds good. The conductor is also responsible for choosing the music that the orchestra will play and for making sure that the orchestra is ready for each performance. I hope this helps! query: Explain Reddit to my sister . Like she 's 5 I just ca n't do it . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's like a big plaza . People walk into it and share something ( a picture , a video , whatever ) , and see if other people like it . If someone likes it , they 'll gather a crowd of people around it . The biggest crowds move near the entrance of the plaza . Naturally , these crowds get more attention , since they 're at the front . People also start to talk inside the crowds , and start discussions . The most popular discussions gather a group of people around them ( but they 're all still in the same crowd ) . After a while , though , people get bored of it ( usually within a day ) , and they all leave to the next big thing . Points keep track of how many big crowds you 've had for all time . Basically , plaza(frontpage ) > crowds(posts ) > groups(comments ) . Number of people in crowds / groups is karma points . There are also smaller plazas for people who like certain things . Let 's say it 's ponies . In this plaza , you can only talk about ponies , and the most popular things about ponies get a big crowd .
Its like recess for adults . Over by the monkey bars we got some kids talking about politics . On the swings we got a group of kids talking about starcraft . Some kids are out playing basketball on the courts and some are playing football in the field . Every once in a while the teacher will gather all the kids in the middle and make an announcement . Yet when she got sick , she was whisked to the top cardiologist in New York in one of the Vatican 's private jets . Her orphanages refused to adopt kids out to non - Catholic parents . BOTH parents had to be Catholic , and you had to have documents . That kept countless orphans from being adopted . The faults were not so much hers , they were just indicative of the general hypocrisy of the Catholic Church .

If I recall correctly , the money did n't go much towards the hospitals , but towards trying to convert more people to Catholicism . Then we take a look at the hospitals she built , and see that they were more closely related to a home for those about to die than an actual hospital for healing . She seemed rather sadistic in her ideas that suffering makes people better . If I 'm correct , the reason that she seen as terrible is because behind what she 's believed to have done is actually atrocious acts .

query: Why do I always have nightmares ? I 'm 35 yrs old , ever since I 've been able to dream I 've never have a good dream . I always seem to just have horrible dreams . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
