Also , here is something else maybe some of you guys are familiar with : Despite the fact that I 'm an adult who has lived alone for the last 2 years , I find that when I am feeling particularly anxious , I am also afraid of the dark . I 'm not embarrassed to admit that because I deal with it and do n't let it get to me . But yeah , anxiety = nightmares , irrational fear of the dark for me .
I 'm 65 . I 've gone thru periods where I was murdered ( in my dreams ) 4 or 5 times a night . I have n't been able to abolish every bad dream , but I can tell you there is some help if you relax your body carefully before sleeping . I also recommend telling yourself you are going to have ' good ' dreams as you lay there waiting for sleep .
In my experience dreams are emotional vehicals that help someone make sense of their waking hours . Nightmares come when someone trys to make sense out of something they have nt experiencec before . Children usually suffer nightmares more than adults as they do nt have enough experiences to rely on or use to understand similiar events . Perhaps if a person has frequent nightmares then this person is growing emotional further than average adults . Maybe they are expereincing more depth in their waking lives than others and thus is seeking out an understanding of thes new views ?
rejected:
It's normal to have nightmares from time to time, and it's not uncommon for people to have them throughout their lives. There are many different things that can cause nightmares, and it's possible that you have some specific things in your life that are causing you to have more nightmares than other people. Here are a few things that might be contributing to your nightmares:

Stress: If you're feeling stressed or anxious, it can make you more likely to have nightmares.

Certain medications: Some medications can cause nightmares as a side effect.

Substance abuse: Using drugs or alcohol can lead to nightmares.

Medical conditions: Certain medical conditions, such as sleep apnea or post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), can cause nightmares.

Lack of sleep: Not getting enough sleep can make you more likely to have nightmares.

It's important to try to get enough sleep and to practice good sleep hygiene, such as going to bed and waking up at the same time every day and avoiding screens before bedtime. If you're still having a lot of nightmares, it might be helpful to talk to a doctor or a therapist about ways to manage them.

query: If you 're not supposed to button the bottom button on a suit , why do suits have more than one button ? Has /r / malefashionadvice lied to me ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
How would you know which button was the bottom button if they did n't put one on ?
The tradition of leaving the bottom button of a coat undone started as a fashion choice . Coats are designed to flare away at the bottom , since a man 's hips are generally broader than the waist , where a coat should be fastened . Modern suits are all cut with this in mind . So if you button the bottom button it throws off the tailoring of the suit , turning a man 's silhouette into a cylinder and making you look weird . Ideally , three - button suits are n't supposed to have the top button fastened , either . Leaving it undone balances the look and permits the lapel to roll closer to the waist , where it should be . The two - button suit takes care of this nicely by removing the offending button altogether . There are a few exceptions . The bottom button on some double - breasted jacket is buttoned . There is also a type of custom jacket called a " paddock coat , " where the placement of the buttons is altered and both are buttoned . John F. Kennedy was known to wear such a jacket . The custom of leaving the bottom button on a vest is historical , coming from those imitating a too fat King Edward VII . source : URL_0
To distinguish people like me , who button all the buttons , from people who know better .
rejected:
Suits often have more than one button because it can give the suit a more tailored, streamlined appearance. However, it is generally considered proper etiquette to leave the bottom button of a suit jacket unbuttoned. This is because buttoning the bottom button can cause the fabric of the jacket to pull and bunch in an unflattering way, and can also make it more difficult to sit down comfortably.

It's important to note that fashion rules are often flexible and can vary depending on personal style and the specific situation. Some people may prefer to button the bottom button of their suit jacket, and that's okay as long as they feel confident and comfortable. Ultimately, the most important thing is to wear what makes you feel good and represents your personal style.

query: Why do car batteries take so long to die if cars require a lot of power ? First time poster . Any explanations would be great ! Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Cars have an [ alternator ] ( URL_0 ) which charges the car battery when the engine is on . That 's why even if your battery is dead or no longer holding a charge , you can ' jump start ' it with another battery , providing just enough juice to kick the engine on , and the alternator will take care of pushing enough electricity into the battery to keep systems running . If your battery AND alternator are dead , then you 're in trouble , and a new battery will get you just far enough to get to a shop for a new alternator .
The alternator charges it when your car is running .
The battery is only in use when the car is off . Otherwise , you 're running on the alternator . The clock in your radio takes only a few microvolts to power , and a lead acid battery would probably decay from chemical reactions and oxidation on its own before wearing out from drain . The radio , lights , and other electronics are a heavy load if you 're using them with the car off , but even then , it could take hours to drain a battery dead . Most people do n't do that . The most load a battery gets is to start the engine , which takes seconds , and then it gets charged in a couple minutes of the engine running . If a car dies in the street and the battery is dead , that 's an indication that there is a problem with the alternator , as it 's not producing power to keep the car running and the battery was drained for that purpose instead . If the car wo n't start in the morning , it 's either a short in the electrical system draining the battery , or the battery is old and ca n't reliably hold a charge . An electrical tester should show you if there 's an unreasonable load on the battery while the car is off ( you should just be powering the clock , maybe an alarm , maybe a key fob receiver ) , then there 's a short . Same thing if you buy a brand new battery and it 's dead the next day . A battery across the terminals should register something like 12 volts , usually close to 13 volts . This will degrade with age . If it 's ~11 volts or less , it 's time for a new battery .
rejected:
Car batteries are designed to provide a steady source of power to the car's electrical systems. They are able to do this because they are able to store a lot of energy in the form of chemical reactions that can be easily converted into electricity.

When you start your car, the battery provides a burst of power to the starter motor, which turns over the engine and gets it running. Once the engine is running, the alternator takes over and provides power to the car's electrical systems.

The alternator is a device that uses the movement of the engine to generate electricity, and it is able to generate much more power than the battery alone. This means that even though the car requires a lot of power to run, the battery is only used for a short period of time and then the alternator takes over and provides the majority of the power needed.

The battery is able to last a long time because it is only called upon to provide a burst of power when the car is started, and then the alternator takes over and provides power for the rest of the time the car is running.

I hope this helps to explain why car batteries are able to last a long time even though cars require a lot of power! Is there anything else you would like to know?

query: Risk tolerance as I age
chosen: You say you have 90% in stocks. I'll assume that you have the other 10% in bonds. For the sake of simplicity, I'll assume that your investments in stocks are in nice, passive indexed mutual funds and ETFs, rather than in individual stocks. A 90% allocation in stocks is considered aggressive. The problem is that if the stock market crashes, you may lose 40% or more of your investment in a single year. As you point out, you are investing for the long term. That's great, it means you can rest easy if the stock market crashes, safe in the hope that you have many years for it to recover. So long as you have the emotional willpower to stick with it. Would you be better off with a 100% allocation in stocks? You'd think so, wouldn't you. After all, the stock market as a whole gives better expected returns than the bond market. But keep in mind, the stock market and the bond market are (somewhat) negatively correlated. That means when the stock market goes down, the bond market often goes up, and vice versa. Investing some of your money in bonds will slightly reduce your expected return but will also reduce your standard deviation and your maximum annual loss. Canadian Couch Potato has an interesting write-up on how to estimate stock and bond returns. It's based on your stocks being invested equally in the Canadian, U.S., and international markets. As you live in the U.S., that likely doesn't directly apply to you; you probably ignore the Canadian stock market, but your returns will be fairly similar. I've reproduced part of that table here: As you can see, your expected return is highest with a 100% allocation in stocks. With a 20 year window, you likely can recover from any crash. If you have the stomach for it, it's the allocation with the highest expected return. Once you get closer to retirement, though, you have less time to wait for the stock market to recover. If you still have 90% or 100% of your investment in stocks and the market crashes by 44%, it might well take you more than 6 years to recover. Canadian Couch Potato has another article, Does a 60/40 Portfolio Still Make Sense? A 60/40 portfolio is a fairly common split for regular investors. Typically considered not too aggressive, not too conservative. The article references an AP article that suggests, in the current financial climate, 60/40 isn't enough. Even they aren't recommending a 90/10 or a 100/0 split, though. Personally, I think 60/40 is too conservative. However, I don't have the stomach for a 100/0 split or even a 90/10 split. Okay, to get back to your question. So long as your time horizon is far enough out, the expected return is highest with a 100% allocation in stocks. Be sure that you can tolerate the risk, though. A 30% or 40% hit to your investments is enough to make anyone jittery. Investing a portion of your money in bonds slightly lowers your expected return but can measurably reduce your risk. As you get closer to retirement and your time horizon narrows, you have less time to recover from a stock market crash and do need to be more conservative. 6 years is probably too short to keep all your money in stocks. Is your stated approach reasonable? Well, only you can answer that. :)
rejected: As people age, their risk tolerance tends to decrease. This is because as we get older, we have more to lose and may be more financially responsible for others, such as children or grandchildren. We may also be closer to retirement and therefore have less time to recover from any financial losses. Additionally, as we age, our physical and mental abilities may not be as strong as they were in our younger years, which can also affect our risk tolerance.That being said, everyone's risk tolerance is different and can be influenced by a variety of factors, including personal circumstances, financial goals, and individual personality traits. It's important to carefully consider your own risk tolerance and to make investment decisions that align with your overall financial plan and goals.

query: If the Sun is closer to the Earth during Winter , why is it not hot as hell ? It 's a question that 's recently sprung into my mind . I just do n't understand . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
If you do the math , it turns out that the few percent distance difference between summer and winter , is massively outweighed by the different amounts of daylight the earth sees , as well as the angle of the sunlight . You can think of it sort of like this ... imagine having your hand about 3 feet away from a campfire , for 1 minute . It 'll get quite hot . Now imagine putting it 2 feet and 11 inches away for 30 seconds . Much less hot .
This would be true if the Earth 's orbit was far too elliptical like that of Pluto . But Earth 's orbit is very close to the actual circle . The distance at perihelion ( the closest point to the Sun ) is about 147 million km , the distance at aphelion ( the farthest point to the Sun ) is some 152 million km . The difference , on a cosmic scale , is really small . But it makes up for something , winters on the Northern hemisphere are actually warmer and last less compared to summers which are colder and last longer . The Earth is travelling faster at perihelion and slower at aphelion ( one of the Kepler 's laws , ca n't recall which one )
If I am correct , winter is produced not by the sun 's proximity but by the angle at which the sun 's rays hit us . The tilt of the planet influences the temperature and because of our different exposure we get seasons . And because the tilt influences temperature you get different season - specific weather in different parts of the globe ( this and the geography of the area ) .
rejected:
The Sun is actually farther away from the Earth during the winter, not closer. The Earth's orbit around the Sun is not a perfect circle, it's more like an oval. That means that the distance between the Earth and the Sun changes slightly over the course of the year. The Earth is closest to the Sun in January and farthest from the Sun in July.

Even though the Earth is farther from the Sun during the winter, the Sun still appears to be in the same place in the sky because the Earth is tilted on its axis. This tilt is what causes the seasons. During the winter, the North Pole is tilted away from the Sun, so the Sun's rays have to travel through more of the Earth's atmosphere to reach the surface. This causes the sunlight to be spread out over a larger area, which makes it appear less bright and less warm.

So even though the Sun is farther away during the winter, the Earth's tilt and the fact that the sunlight has to travel through more of the atmosphere make it feel colder.

query: Economic Bubbles Specifically in reference to real estate bubbles , but also the concept in general . EDIT : And how ( if possible ) is a bubble avoidable ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Think about how the price of something is determined . Part of it is intrinsic value , obviously . You 'll pay more for something if it makes you comfortable , or can be used to make you more money , or has some other quality that you think is cool and ... well , worth paying for . If you do n't know better , you might believe this is the only thing that determines price . But there * is * another component . I 'll pay for something if I believe i can sell it back to someone else for more than I paid . And as the price gets higher , this second component gets more important , since I can make more and more profit from reselling it . So say the price of tulip bulbs starts rising . As it rises , more and more people buy the bulbs , not because of their intrinsic value , but because they plan to resell the bulbs for more money . Eventually ( and usually quickly ) , you reach the point where * nobody * is buying the bulbs for their intrinsic value ; everyone is just planning to sell them when the price goes further up . Once this happens , the market is screwed . The price is now rising only because everyone thinks it 's going to keep rising . If a significant number of people ever stop thinking this , the price will fall , making even more people stop thinking it . This spirals down and down , until tulip bulbs are back to being worth no more than their intrinsic value . Which would be fine ... except for all the people that staked a fortune on the belief that they were worth many times more .
Like you 're five ? Okay . Let 's say you saw your classmate Dave playing around with his toy truck the other day . It 's all colorful and in a good condition , and you really really want it . You come to Dave and offer him 6 jellybeans for his truck . A fair deal for both side . Dave agrees and you get the truck . Several days later , after playing with the truck almost non - stop , you get bored of it and wish to trade it away . You come up to another classmate of yours named Amy and ask her if she wants the truck . She says yes and offers 6 jellybeans for it . You , however , are good and math , and know that you have a chance to earn some extra jellybeans here , so you ask for 8 jellybeans instead . Amy , having no choice , agrees and you both leave happily . A week after that you overhear a transaction in which Amy sells the truck to John for 11 jellybeans . Soon after that , John sells the truck to Mike for 15 jellybeans and Mike sells it to Jack for 20 jellybeans . You soon spot an opportunity to make easy jelly here . You buy the truck off Jack for 20 jellybeans and sell it to Bob for 25 jellybeans . It 's not long before everyone follow and start buying the truck not in order to play with it but to sell it later for profit . At some point , you buy the truck again , this time from a kid named Kyle , for 57 ( ! ! ! ) jellybeans , way more than the original price of 6 jellybeans . You then proceed to try and find something to sell it to for a profit , but at this point everyone in class had a turn with the truck and nobody is interested . You end up selling the truck back to Dave for mere 5 jellybeans , resulting in major losses . * * In real life , * * each classmate represent a very big group of people rather than just one individual , and the truck is a whole sector of economical goods ( be it real estate or electronics or wood or whatever ) .
A bubble happens because of speculation . If people notice that the price of something has been rising consistently , they will think " hey , I can buy this now and then sell it again after the price has risen to make some money . " They make the investment decision based on the assumption that the price will keep rising . Let 's say the price does keep rising . More and more people will catch on that they can make some money from this investment , and will act accordingly . However , what happens with a bubble is that the price is no longer rising because the real value of the investment is increasing ; instead , the price is rising because all these people keep investing in it . So , eventually whatever they 're investing in becomes WAY overvalued . Once enough people start realizing that it 's overvalued , there will be a rush to sell the investment ( because they do n't think the price will rise anymore ) . This starts a chain reaction of more and more selling . With all these sellers panicking and not enough buyers , the whole thing becomes unstable and collapses . Hope this is helpful !
rejected: An economic bubble is a situation where the price of an asset, like real estate, becomes artificially inflated due to a lot of people buying it without considering its true value. This can happen when people get caught up in the excitement of buying and selling and start to believe that prices will always keep going up. To avoid an economic bubble, it's important to be careful and not get swept up in the hype. This means looking at the true value of an asset and not just buying it because everyone else is. It's also a good idea to be diversified, which means not putting all of your money into one type of asset, like real estate. This can help protect you if the value of that asset drops. Another way to avoid an economic bubble is to have strong government regulations in place to prevent fraud and manipulation of asset prices. This can help make sure that prices reflect the true value of an asset and not just speculation. Overall, the best way to avoid an economic bubble is to be cautious and make informed decisions when investing your money.

query: Is the very long-term growth of the stock market bound by aggregate net income?
chosen: I am a believer in that theory. My opinion is that over the long term, we can expect 25% of income to reflect the payment on one's mortgage, and if you drew a line over time reflecting the mortgage this represents plus the downpayment, you'd be very close to a median home price. The bubble that occurred was real, but not as dramatic as Schiller's chart implies. $1000 will support a $124K 30yr mortgage, but $209K at 4%. This is with no hype, and exact same supply/demand pressures.  The market cap of all US companies adds to about $18T. The total wealth in the US, about $60T. Of course US stocks aren't just held by US citizens, it's a big world.  Let me suggest two things - the world is poor in comparison to much of the US. A $100,000 net worth puts you in the top 8% in the world. The implication of this is that as the poorer 90% work their way up from poverty, money will seek investments, and there's room for growth.  Even if you looked at a closed system, the US only, the limit, absent bubbles, would be one that would have to put a cap on productivity. In today's dollars we produce more than we did years ago, and less than we will in the future. We invent new things faster than the old ones are obsoleted. So any prognostication that our $18T market can grow to say, $30T, does not need to discuss P/Es or bubbles, but rather the creation of new products and businesses that will increase the total market.  To summarize -  Population growth (not really discussed), Productivity, and long term reduced Poverty will all keep that boundary to be a growing number.  That said, this question may be economic, and not PF, in which case my analysis is bound for the Off-Topic barrel. Fascinating question.
rejected: The long-term growth of the stock market is ultimately driven by the underlying earnings and profits of the companies whose stocks are being traded. In general, as companies' earnings and profits grow, their stock prices tend to rise, which can drive overall market growth. However, there are many other factors that can also influence stock prices and market performance, such as economic conditions, investor sentiment, and market competition, among others.It is worth noting that aggregate net income is just one measure of a company's financial performance and may not always accurately reflect the underlying strength of a business. For example, a company may have strong revenue growth but weak profitability due to high expenses or other factors. As such, it is important for investors to consider a range of financial metrics and factors when evaluating potential investments.

query: Why does a web page that takes too long to load suddenly pop up for a split second when I hit the back button ? It 's happened to all of us . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's a setting in your browser that prevents the page from displaying until enough has been downloaded to cache or else images like banners would pop in after text has loaded . This is undesirable because it could cause the text to move around while your browser suddenly makes space for the image . A good visual would be to imagine copy - pasting a picture into a Word document . You can actually go into a browser like Firefox and turn this feature off to see the page elements render as soon as they are downloaded , but then pages that load slowly can be unusable as clickable elements get shoved around as room is made for things like images or flash windows . When you press the Back button , the flash of the webpage that you see is probably the portion of the page that has been cached . tl;dr : The browser is waiting for all the pieces of the page to download before lifting the curtain and showing you the whole page in all its glory . When you press ' back ' the curtain flickers and you are seeing the elements of the incomplete page . [ edit : Forgot to add in the part about the Back button ]
Most webpages have several parts that needs to load before your browser will display the page . Most of the time , when a page takes too long to load , the fault lies with a third - party plugin like an adtracker or some bullshit . So you have actually loaded the page you want to see , but your browser does n't want to display it before it has loaded * * everything * * . * tl;dr Your browser is like an 8 yo girl that does n't want you to see the xmas tree she decorated , until she has put * * EVERYTHING ON * * tip : Hit the ' X ' for ' stop loading ' and it will display whatever it managed to load , which is often the entire actual page
I would think it 's whatever the cache has stored before you about the loading
rejected: When you visit a web page, your web browser sends a request to the server that hosts the website, asking it to send the content of the page to your computer. This process can take some time, especially if the web page is large or the server is busy. While the browser is waiting for the content to arrive, it may show a blank or loading screen. If you navigate away from the page before it has finished loading, the browser will stop waiting for the content and move on to the next page you've requested. However, if the content has already started to arrive, the browser may briefly display the partially loaded page before moving on to the next one. This is what you're seeing when the page "pops up" for a split second when you hit the back button. It's important to note that this is just a temporary issue and shouldn't affect your ability to browse the web. If you're having trouble with pages taking too long to load or experiencing other issues with your web browser, you may want to try clearing your cache and cookies, or even resetting your browser to its default settings.

query: How does David Blaine perform his levitation illusion ? I always wondered how he performed his tricks , but the levitation illusion is my favorite . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
That 's the Balducci levitation , and he did n't invent it . What he DID do was use special effects and editing to make it look a LOT more spectacular on TV than it is in real life , and in doing so , broke a decades - long rule among magicians to NEVER use FX or editing tricks on TV .
Misdirection . He is actually standing on his toes on the foot away from the observer and blocking their view with the other foot
Unfortunately he also resorted to camera tricks when presenting this illusion on TV . To the people who are there in person , it might appear that he is levitating only a few inches off of the ground . When it is aired on television , the shot cuts away to an edited shot of him being lifted off of the ground by wires much higher than that , making it seem much more impressive .
rejected: The levitation illusion is a type of magic trick in which it appears that a person is floating in the air without any visible means of support. There are many different ways that magicians can create the illusion of levitation, and the specific method used by David Blaine may vary depending on the specific trick he is performing. However, in general, magicians use a variety of techniques to create the illusion of levitation, including the use of hidden wires, support structures, and misdirection. For example, a magician might use a hidden wire or support structure to lift themselves off the ground, while simultaneously distracting the audience with movement or other distractions to draw attention away from the support. Alternatively, a magician might use a concealed platform or other hidden support to create the illusion of floating in the air. It is important to note that the levitation illusion is just that - an illusion. It is not real magic, and the person performing the trick is not actually floating in the air. Instead, the magician is using a combination of skill, misdirection, and deception to create the appearance of levitation.

query: Is there some sort of YKK zipper monopoly ? 99.99 % of zippers I 've seen in my life have the stamp YKK on them . Is there a simple reason for this ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Not a monopoly , many high - end and low - end clothing manufacturers have their own zipper production companies . YKK just makes a ton of zippers and have made them for a long time . Same thing as WD40 or GE lightbulbs , they are n't the only company who is allowed to or able to make these products , they just make the most .
YKK is a Japanese company . Their zippers are widely regarded as the best , so a huge chunk of the fashion industry uses them . They even fabricate their own equipment to manufacture the zippers !
A bit , and they were fined over a $ 100 M by a European court 7 years ago for being part of a price fixing cartel , although that was partially overturned . They control about 90 % of the zipper market . They not only make zippers but the machines that make zippers and the raw material needed for zippers . Due to this vertical integration it 's more of a natural monopoly due to economies of scale .
rejected: YKK is a brand name for zippers, and it is very popular. But it is not a monopoly, which means they do not have complete control over all the zippers in the world. There are other brands of zippers out there, but YKK is known for making high-quality zippers that are used in a lot of different products, like jackets, backpacks, and even jeans. Because they are a well-known and trusted brand, many companies choose to use YKK zippers in their products. That's why you see the YKK stamp on so many zippers.

query: Why is it that soda can become flat , even though the can should be sealed and no CO2 should escape ? My assumption is that the can may not be ' truly sealed ' but it seems unlikely . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I 've never experienced soda in a sealed can come out flat .
I know that we 're not supposed to blatantly speculate , but it does n't seem unreasonable to assume that it has something to do with the way the can is packaged . Cans are packaged with an internal pressure difference from outside ( the pressure inside is greater or smaller than the pressure outside the can ) , which is why you can hear the air move when you open them . If you ever open a bottle / can of non - carbonated drink you 'll notice it does n't do this . Obviously , if you re - seal the container , the CO2 ca n't escape . When it goes flat , it simply separates from the liquid part of the soda , and floats above the surface of the liquid . If you ever get a 2 liter , drink most of it , then leave the rest , if you inhale any of the gas inside you 'll notice it 's not air . So , my " educated speculation " is that because of the decrease in pressure , the CO2 can separate from the liquid around it , and go flat . It does n't actually leave the container , but it does n't make soda fizzy if it 's not mixed in .
I 'm going to get down - voted to hell for not providing a direct answer , but look into a book called [ What Einstein Did n't Know ] ( URL_0 ) . The title probably is n't accurate , but it explains this and dozens of other common real life physics problems in * laymen 's terms * . The net reason has to do with the capacity of a gas to absorb more of another gas . In this case it 's a mixture called air which then absorbs as much CO2 from the liquid as it can . This is n't much because the soda is hyper carbonated to start with to account for this . When you release the seal the highly carbonated air is replaced with normal air . To compound this , the soda which contains an un - natural amount of C02 , begins to bleed off C02 as fast as it can into the air around you . tl;dr : Read the book I linked . The C02 in pop keeps it fizzy . The C02 tries to leave the pop in the same fashion that shaken pop leaves the bottle when opened . Same idea , but slightly slower release time for carbonation .
rejected: Soda can become flat because carbon dioxide, which is what gives soda its fizz, can escape from the can even though it is sealed. This can happen because the carbon dioxide is dissolved in the liquid, and it will tend to come out of the solution and into the air if given the chance. When you open a can of soda, the pressure inside the can decreases, which allows some of the dissolved carbon dioxide to come out of the solution and form bubbles. If you leave the can open for a long time, more and more of the carbon dioxide will come out of the solution and escape into the air, which is why the soda becomes flat.

query: Why the Euro would crash if Greece exited it I still do n't understand why Eurozone countries would rather bail them out ( at significant costs ) . Is the cost of them quitting the Euro worse ? I do n't get why . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
To a five year old : You and a bunch of friends are sitting at a lunch room table at school . You are all real close , share a lot of history together , and everybody sees you as a group rather than a bunch of individuals . One day , your friend Winston accidentally craps himself while giving a history presentation . Now the entire school will no longer talk to you , or your friends because you are all associated with Winston craps - his - briefs . Your group of friends is Europe , Winston is Greece , crapping his briefs is defaulting , and nobody talking to you is the world refusing to loan you money because one of the Countries in your association could n't pay back its debts .
Alright , so the five - year - old explanation has already been given , so I wo n't try to compete with that . I 'll do the more involved answer -- say for the high school senior who gets how lending works -- hopefully that does n't piss off the EIL5 - purists . The only reason I 'm doing it this way is because the 5 - year - old explanation ( which was [ fucking amazing ] ( URL_0 ) ) did n't explain why the Eurozone would rather bail Greece out than let them leave . So back up to 10 years ago . The Euro was formed a few years ago and Greece was just admitted . That means that there was this handful of countries that had effectively pooled their GDPs together to produce a single currency . This was a great deal for countries like Greece , whose drachma would fluctuate in value year after year . And it was even great for countries like Germany , whose deutsche marks were part of an economy that was so uniformly wealthy that it was actually really hard for them to do business with anybody -- their currency was TOO valuable . But together , they made the Euro , which had an established value that was designed to be viable in everybody 's country who joined in with it . But it turned out that Greece went a little crazy with the sudden power of their currency , and the cheap debt they could take out and they had some political problems that essentially led the country into economic ruin . Enter the debt crisis , yada yada . So why would the Eurozone countries bail Greece out ? Because if Greece exits , it throws the whole balance of value they designed out of whack . Sure , the Euro probably would n't become uncompetitive just because Greece leaves , but it 's important to remember that the political and economic decisions of a single Euro country do n't occur in a vacuum . Right now , there is no precedent for a country to leave the Euro -- if Greece leaves , that leaves the door open for the other debt - saddled nations ( Ireland , Spain , Portugal , and Italy ) to leave too . And that just leaves the rich countries -- but especially France and Germany -- in the Euro , which means that they 'll be left with a currency that is WAY less viable than the franks and the marks that they made the Euro to replace . So just as the franks and the marks were difficult to trade with because they were too expensive , the new rich - only Euro would be CRAZY expensive . So it 's not clear that the Eurozone benefits by pushing Greece out . Plus , it 's a huge practical problem for a country to take back currency in circulation and replace it with a new currency . Greece does n't have a huge mountain of paper drachma sitting around -- and even if it did , all of its people would rather have Euros because once Greece leaves the Euro , the value of the Euro would shoot up . Greeks would move all their money over into Euros the second there was any hint that Greece was going to exit the Euro . Arguably , this is happening already : Greeks are moving their money to German or French banks so it is n't transformed into drachma overnight . This is a textbook bank run -- the people themselves would devalue their own currency by setting off a panic -- so it should be pretty clear why Greece might not want out either . Plus , the costs of bailing out Greece is a bit like preventative medicine . Sure , it costs money when you could be spending nothing , but by backing up the value of Greek debt now , the Euro would be saying to investors down the road " look , even if you 're not thrilled about investing in Greece in the future , we want to be clear that there are 16 other countries in our union who would receive similar protections . " It sets a precedent that investors would interpret as " The Eurozone is safe for investment . " And so yeah , it will cost a couple hundred billion dollars to bail out Greece over the next couple of years -- but it could leave investors with enough confidence to invest many * trillions * in the rest of the Eurozone over the next couple of decades .
If they do n't bail Greece out , nobody will trust them any more . To get more countries to join the EU , they have to show that they stand by their members . If you do n't mind a little cynicism with that : There 's a lot of profit in it for private companies to see Greece bailed out . Much less in leaving it to collapse further .
rejected:
The Euro is a currency used by many countries in Europe, and Greece is one of those countries. If Greece were to leave the Euro and go back to using its own currency, it could potentially cause problems for both Greece and the other countries that use the Euro.

One potential problem is that it could lead to a loss of confidence in the Euro. If people think that Greece might leave the Euro and go back to using its own currency, they might start to worry that other countries could also leave the Euro. This could lead to people losing faith in the Euro and choosing to use other currencies instead. This could cause the value of the Euro to go down, which would be bad for the countries that use it.

Another potential problem is that it could lead to more economic instability in Greece. If Greece were to leave the Euro, it might be harder for the country to borrow money from other countries or international organizations. This could make it harder for Greece to pay its debts or fund important projects, which could lead to more economic problems.

Finally, if Greece were to leave the Euro, it could lead to other countries wanting to leave as well. This could lead to a breakdown of the Eurozone, which could be very costly and disruptive for the countries that use the Euro.

Overall, while it might seem like bailing out Greece is expensive, the cost of Greece leaving the Euro could be much higher in the long run. That's why Eurozone countries have chosen to help Greece, even though it has been costly.

query: Why ca n't the FBI shut down all of the child pornography websites when everyone knows where to find them ? Maybe I just do n't understand how the deep web works . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
For a better primer you should really read a few ELI5 about tor and the deep web . The short answer is , we do nt REALLY know where to find them . The deep web is much more secure then the normal internet . Normally , its pretty easy to get information about who owns a website , where they 're located , etc . that info is kept private on the deep web . Hosting companies do n't want to know it , website owners do nt want to share it , if they do share it , they re probably lying . Even with minimal security measures , you 're well protected on tor . I live on the east coast of the United States . If i connect to a website through tor though , my connection will be repeatedly bounced around to disguise it , and by the time it reaches a point where its identifiable , I could appear to be in spain .
There was an amazing article / open letter I read a couple of years ago written by someone who helped build CP rings . His whole point was that the level of encryption , connection security , and access methods are so beyond law enforcement and lawmakers level of understanding that catching them is basically a pipe dream .
As an aside to everything else , let 's say they do find one , it 's not just as simple as phoning someone and shutting it down in a few hours . You find a site , great . Now you 've got to prove that the videos and images on it are illegal . Great . How do you do that ? Even once you 've done that , you 've got proof they are hosting illegal pornography the next step is proving you have the authority to shut it down . Most times , courts have to be contacted . You have to get the right paper work . Even once you 've done all that , you 've got proof its illegal , you 've got the authority to do this , it 's time to contact the hosting company . What if it 's a obscure hosting company who s details just go into a black hole ? Phone numbers do n't work , address and name is fake . What do you do ? Even once you do get to the hosting company , it 's now down to them to sort out . The person will be contacted and then the hosting company will pull the plug . All the time , the person behind the site may know they have been caught and just set up another one . It takes time . So even if we did know where " all these sites were " , it would not be an easy thing to deal with anyway .
rejected: The FBI and other law enforcement agencies work to combat child pornography and other illegal activities on the internet, including on the "deep web," which is a part of the internet that is not indexed by search engines and is not easily accessible to the general public. However, shutting down websites that distribute child pornography is a complex and resource-intensive process that requires following legal procedures and working with other countries and agencies. It's also important to understand that the internet is a global network, and websites can be hosted in any country. This means that even if the FBI is able to identify and shut down a website in the United States, it might be hosted in a country with different laws and regulations, making it more difficult to take down. Additionally, the people who operate these websites often use various techniques to try to hide their identities and locations, making it harder to track them down and prosecute them. Ultimately, it's a difficult and ongoing battle to try to eliminate illegal activities on the internet, but law enforcement agencies are working hard to try to make it as difficult as possible for these activities to take place and to hold those responsible accountable for their actions.

query: Why does bright light ( i.e. a flashlight ) cause your eyes to hurt when shined in them ? I understand why it would stop us from being able to see , but why does it hurt ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The iris controls the amount of light that enters your eyeball ( and eventually hit your optic nerve ) by making the hole the light enters smaller or bigger . It 's the black bit in the center of your eye . The pain you feel when you suddenly look at a bright light is your iris literally contracting to prevent overloading your eye with light . Edit : corrected cornea to iris . Thanks /u / jerimiahf Edit2 : the black bit ! = iris . I 'm dumb . But it 's okay . /u / jerimiahf got my back .
I guess it 's conceivable there could be an organism that could see without feeling pain from bright light . But does that organism also have its eyes damaged by bright light ? If it does , and if it has no incentive NOT to look at bright things , then the organisms as a species wo n't do very well because they would all tend to go blind . The entire reason pleasure and pain exist is because the things that cause those sensations generally would make an animal more likely to spread its genes ( eating , mating = pleasure ) or less likely ( breaking your leg , staring into the sun = pain ) . It 's only natural that even if there existed no preexisting reason for staring into the sun to be painful , evolutionarily speaking , one would definitely arise . ---- Also worth notice is that the times when you 're using a flashlight , you 're generally surrounded by little ambient light , so your eyes are adapted to low light conditions ( by exposing their less hardy low - light receptors ) , making it worse to suddenly get light in your eyes .
When your nerves are overloaded from all the light , the brain interprets the nerve signals as pain rather than as sight . A similar thing happens when one of your limbs falls asleep due to compressing a nerve . When that nerve is uncompressed , a huge amount of signal enters it again and the brain interprets it as tingling pain . If you increase the signal by touching the limb that was asleep , you feel pain rather than pressure !
rejected: When you shine a bright light in your eyes, it can be painful because the light is so strong that it overwhelms the cells in your eyes that are responsible for detecting light. These cells, called photoreceptors, are located in the retina at the back of your eye. When they are overstimulated by bright light, it can cause them to become "tired" or damaged. This can lead to discomfort or even pain in some cases. It's kind of like when you look at something really bright, like the sun, and then look away - you might see spots or have a temporary blind spot for a few seconds. This is because your photoreceptors have been overstimulated and need a little time to recover.

query: Please explain what is "Web usage mining"
chosen:
Data mining is the process of extracting and discovering patterns in large data sets involving methods at the intersection of machine learning, statistics, and database systems. Data mining is an interdisciplinary subfield of computer science and statistics with an overall goal of extracting information (with intelligent methods) from a data set and transforming the information into a comprehensible structure for further use. Data mining is the analysis step of the "knowledge discovery in databases" process, or KDD. Aside from the raw analysis step, it also involves database and data management aspects, data pre-processing, model and inference considerations, interestingness metrics, complexity considerations, post-processing of discovered structures, visualization, and online updating.
The term "data mining" is a misnomer because the goal is the extraction of patterns and knowledge from large amounts of data, not the extraction (mining) of data itself. It also is a buzzword and is frequently applied to any form of large-scale data or information processing (collection, extraction, warehousing, analysis, and statistics) as well as any application of computer decision support system, including artificial intelligence (e.g., machine learning) and business intelligence. The book Data mining: Practical machine learning tools and techniques with Java (which covers mostly machine learning material) was originally to be named Practical machine learning, and the term data mining was only added for marketing reasons. Often the more general terms (large scale) data analysis and analytics—or, when referring to actual methods, artificial intelligence and machine learning—are more appropriate.
The actual data mining task is the semi-automatic or automatic analysis of large quantities of data to extract previously unknown, interesting patterns such as groups of data records (cluster analysis), unusual records (anomaly detection), and dependencies (association rule mining, sequential pattern mining). This usually involves using database techniques such as spatial indices. These patterns can then be seen as a kind of summary of the input data, and may be used in further analysis or, for example, in machine learning and predictive analytics. For example, the data mining step might identify multiple groups in the data, which can then be used to obtain more accurate prediction results by a decision support system. Neither the data collection, data preparation, nor result interpretation and reporting is part of the data mining step, although they do belong to the overall KDD process as additional steps.
The difference between data analysis and data mining is that data analysis is used to test models and hypotheses on the dataset, e.g., analyzing the effectiveness of a marketing campaign, regardless of the amount of data. In contrast, data mining uses machine learning and statistical models to uncover clandestine or hidden patterns in a large volume of data.[10]
The related terms data dredging, data fishing, and data snooping refer to the use of data mining methods to sample parts of a larger population data set that are (or may be) too small for reliable statistical inferences to be made about the validity of any patterns discovered. These methods can, however, be used in creating new hypotheses to test against the larger data populations.
rejected:
Web usage mining is the process of using data mining techniques to automatically discover and extract useful information from web log data. Web log data is a record of user interactions with a website, such as the pages that were accessed, the time of the access, and the duration of the visit. Web usage mining can be used to identify patterns in web usage and to understand how users interact with a website.



There are several different approaches to web usage mining, including:



Preprocessing: This involves cleaning and preparing the web log data for analysis.



Pattern discovery: This involves using data mining techniques to identify patterns in the web log data. These patterns may include the most commonly visited pages, the paths that users take through the website, and the time of day when the website is most popular.



Web usage summarization: This involves creating summary statistics and visualizations of the web log data to help understand user behavior.



Web usage prediction: This involves using machine learning techniques to predict future user behavior based on past web log data.



Web usage mining can be used to improve website design and usability, to optimize website content and marketing efforts, and to understand customer behavior.

query: can your hair really turn grey from stress ? and how ? iv'e always heard that your hair will turn grey if you are under too much stress . but Iv'e never really understood how that happens , any explanations ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Anecdotally , while I was in the military ( aged 20 - 24 ) - I developed some pretty large grey spots all around my head . Since I 've gotten out ( aged 25 - 30 ) - I 've had the grey spots gradually disappear , and currently there 's only one left . I think I 'm stuck with it until natural graying from aging kicks in . A few people I 've stayed in touch with since then have pretty similar stories . People react different to stress , balding was [ i]very[/i ] common , and did n't really reverse . One guy had acne all over his face the whole time I knew him ( well into his mid - late twenties ) , and nowadays he 's out and has a clear face . The most likely biological reason behind it is that stress seems to trigger a lot of the same biological processes that aging does .
It ca n't happen overnight like some people claim . Stress is more likely to cause hair loss , and losing some hair can make the grey more obvious . Additionally , things that are traditionally considered stressful , such as having children , happen about the same time as aging would turn your hair grey anyway . The only direct link between stress and hair turning grey that has been found is not stress like you 're thinking about . Hair may turn grey with DNA damage from things that are stressful to your cells , like chemicals and UV rays
When I was 22 I lost my 3 year old son , and a gray patch appeared within 10 days . Still got the skunk patch to this day . I 'm 34 now .
rejected: Yes, it is possible for your hair to turn gray due to stress. When you are under a lot of stress, your body produces chemicals that can affect the cells that produce pigment in your hair. These pigment cells, called melanocytes, can become damaged or die, causing your hair to lose its color. This process happens gradually over time, which is why people's hair usually turns gray as they get older. So, while stress may not cause your hair to turn gray overnight, it can contribute to the graying process. Does that make sense?

query: Why did Japan have shoguns if they already had an emperor ? I do n't understand why a shogun would have control over all of Japan if there was already an emperor that could do that . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Why do we have generals when we have a President ? Why did Europe have Lords when they had a King ? The Emperor was the head of state , but during the Shogunate periods ... he was really just a figurehead . The Shogun held real power by basically being the strongest warlord in the country . Feudal Japan ( the Sengoku Jidai , or Warring States Period ) was basically a bunch of feudal lords ( Daimyos ) fighting over who would be Shogun and trying to manipulate the Emperor to make them Shogun . During this time the Emperor was basically powerless .
The Emperor was chosen by god , he is a divine being . He is infallible and can not be wrong . So how do you make sure he is always right ? The Shogunate was the next step down from the Emperor , and were supposed to interpret what the emperor said . If the country was ruled badly , then they must have been interpreting the emperor wrong . When the country revolted , they would overthrow the shogunate and set up another that would be better at " interpreting " the emperor 's wishes . When your religion says the main guy ca n't be wrong , you need a layer of government to blame for when he is wrong . Because of this , Japan has only ever had one dynasty throughout its existence , even though the " government " has switched hands many times .
Basically a power vacuum was created because the emperor and other government officials became more interested in producing and developing art then protecting their citizens . This ' renaissance ' occurred after zen buddhism was introduced into the country . So private security ( samurai ) stepped in to fill this niche and became very rich in the process . Eventually samurai became so rich and powerful they needed to establish their own military capital ( Shogunate ) . [ This video ] ( URL_0 ) is funny and educational .
rejected: Japan has had an emperor as a symbolic leader for many centuries, but the shoguns were the actual rulers of the country for much of its history. The shoguns were military leaders who controlled Japan on behalf of the emperor. The shoguns ruled Japan from the 12th to the 19th century. Even though the emperor was the highest authority in Japan, the shoguns had more power and control. It was similar to how a queen or king might be the head of state in a country, but the prime minister might be the one who makes most of the decisions and runs the government.

query: Do insects get food poisoning ? For example if a fly eats salmonella contaminated raw chicken does it affect them ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
One reason insects react so differently to many toxins is because their digestive tract is alkaline in contrast to the acidic environment of the vertebrate intestines . A different pH may render some toxins less harmful and others more so . A common " food contaminant " for insects is * Bacillus thuringiensis * , a species of bacteria which lives in the soil ( and on leaves ) and basically produces a crystalline toxin that can only be dissolved in the alkaline digestive tract of insects . However , it 's non - toxic to humans because the crystals pass our digestive system undissolved . This is why * B. thuringiensis * is sprayed on crops to prevent damage caused by larvae . TL;DR : Their digestive system makes insects vulnerable to different bacterial toxins . Yes , they can suffer from certain food poisonings .
From what I understand from my microbiology class it would depend . It depends mainly on what species of bacteria is in that chicken . If the species of salmonella is able to infect the fly and cause a disease then yes the fly would be sick , but if the species of salmonella present is not adapted to attack the digestive tract of the fly then it would be as if nothing is happened to it . These adaptations would include ways to disturb the physiology and ways to counteract the inmune system . Beyond the species there is also the strain which is one of the reasons some people get infected with the same specie yet get more severe symptoms of the same disease . TL : DR it depends of the specie of the salmonella Edit : spelling
Yes , but often from different factors than the ones that affect humans . Salmonella is unusual " food poisoning " where bacteria actually * infect * the victim . Most cases of food poisoning are from toxins contained in bacteria or left behind when they die . That 's why fully cooking rotten meat , for example , does n't make it safe to eat , even though the cooking process would kill whatever bacteria might be present . A notable example is the botulism toxin which can kill , and did so often before improvements in food preservation / canning . If affects a particular neurotransmitter used for transmitting signals between nerves . It affects the nerves humans use for muscle control causing paralysis . But other types of nerves are unaffected . Obviously an insect may have different nerves than that specific type in the human body , and likely would n't be affected .
rejected: Insects can get sick from eating certain types of food, just like humans can. For example, if a fly eats raw chicken that is contaminated with bacteria like salmonella, the fly could get sick. However, insects are different from humans in many ways, including how they process and digest food. They have different types of digestive systems and may be able to tolerate certain types of bacteria that would make a human sick. In general, insects are more resistant to disease and infection than humans are, but they can still get sick if they eat contaminated food or come into contact with harmful substances.

query: Live in Oregon and work in Washington: Do I need to file Oregon state taxes?

rejected: If you live in Oregon and work in Washington, you may need to file taxes in both states. It depends on the tax laws of each state and the amount of income you earned in each state.Generally, if you earn income in a state where you do not reside, that state may require you to file a nonresident tax return in order to report the income you earned there. This is because each state has the right to tax the income of nonresidents who earn income within its borders.However, there are some exceptions and potential credits that may apply. For example, Oregon has a reciprocal agreement with several other states, including Washington, under which residents of one state who work in the other state are not required to pay taxes on the income they earn in the other state. This means that if you are an Oregon resident working in Washington, you may not have to pay Washington state taxes on your income, as long as you file an exemption certificate with your employer.It's important to note that even if you are not required to pay taxes on your income in both states, you may still need to file tax returns in both states in order to claim any credits or deductions you may be eligible for.To determine whether you need to file taxes in both Oregon and Washington, you should review the tax laws of both states and consult with a tax professional or refer to the instructions for the tax forms for each state.

query: What would Happen if a Body Part was Exposed in Space As in , what if your space suit was sealed at the shoulder . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Some theories suggest you will explode or your blood vaporised . [ This is n't the case ] ( URL_0 ) . Our muscles , skin and bones provide a surprisingly strong resistance against outside pressure . Granted , your lungs would collapse , expelling any air remaining . This then leaves you with approximately 3 minutes before permanent brain damage sets in . You do n't freeze instantly either since there 's no atmosphere to transfer heat into . All heat loss is through infrared radiation which is pretty slow . Provided you return to normal conditions fairly quickly ( ~1 minute maybe ) , you may suffer no permanent damage from being exposed in space . In the case of having a suit sealed at the arm , you 'd still be able to breathe and retain consciousness , and at most would suffer from a fair amount of pain in your arm , due to some swelling effects . As before though , you 'd suffer no permanent damage if you re - pressurised quickly enough . Your arm certainly would n't explode .
This happened to Joseph Kittinger in one upper atmosphere trial . It was n't quite space , it was only 30 km or so up , but it was a pretty good example given that at that height the air pressure is about 1 % of sea level . His glove lost pressurization as he was going up , and he decided to continue the test anyway . His hand swelled up to twice its size , he lost circulation , and it was a painful , unusable mess , but it also recovered full functionality with no permanent damage by the time 3 hours had passed on the ground after landing . It honestly depends on how long you are exposed . The temperature really is n't an issue , because there is n't much in space objects take a long , long time to cool down . If you are exposed long enough to radiate away enough heat to be in danger of hypothermia you would have other problems . The biggest issue , like in that story , would be the physical swelling . That could cause tissue and nerve damage and , if exposed long enough , it could be permanent . query: Why are birds and flying insects so sensitive ? How do they react so fast when something is around them ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Well , the bird thing is mostly due to their vision The eye is an extremely complex bunch of cells , and due to it 's complexity , it needs a constant blood flow . No blood flow , dead eye cells . Dead eye cells = = loss of vision . So ... the human eye is almost completely covered in capillaries ( the tiny , tiny blood vessels that carry oxygen rich blood to the smallest places in the body ) . There is a small part of the human eye that is n't covered in blood vessels , the iris . We have a small field of focus We have binocular vision , which is great for judging distances , but when we need to focus on one thing , everything else gets out of focus . A bird 's eye is n't covered in blood vessels , so they can keep many things in focus at once . There is a little organ at the back of the bird 's eye ( the name of which I will tell you , without typing once , not checking URL_0 and know the answer instantly ) which is called the pecten , which holds all the blood required for the eye to ' live ' Birds will ' shake ' their eyes occationally to distribute this blood all over the eye . Because there are no blood vessels covering they eye , they do n't have one point of focus , but their whole vision is in focus . Furthermore , a bird 's eye will tend to be oval shaped , rather than spherical , giving them more peripheral vision . Most birds have eyes on the sides of their heads as well . \ * So eyes that look kinda to the sides , all of their vision in focus at once , and oval shaped eyes gives a bird an amazing vision of the world around them ( almost no colour , not much depth perception , but amazing detail ) will allow them to navigate impossibly small spaces without crashing into anything . \*Raptors ( eagles , falcons , owls etc . ) will have eyes on the front of their heads . These birds have evolved to hunt prey , so they need binocular vision so as to judge where their prey is , and swoop in for the kill . These birds will , on the whole , not fly through tight spaces , rather fly high above the trees , scanning for their quarry .
Partially due to the very short distances that their nerve signals must travel . But I 'd like to talk a bit about something more fundamental : the same reason an oil tanker is so slow to turn . There 's this fundamental thing called the square - cube law : imagine a cube with side length of one meter ( or yard ) wide . Now imagine another cube with twice the side length of the first one . How much greater is its surface area and volume compared to the smaller one ? The surface area will be four times as large , and the volume will be * eight * times the smaller cube 's volume ! The latter also means that , with equal density , the larger cube will also be eight times more massive . As we continue our thought experiment with larger and larger cubes , we quickly notice that the surface area grows quite a bit faster than the side length , and the volume / mass grows even faster . Similarly , if we instead consider smaller cubes , we find that their surface area and especially mass and volume shrink very rapidly . So how is this relevant ? The strength of some load - bearing structure such as bone , an insect 's exoskeleton , or the hull of an oil tanker , is proportional to the surface area of the structure 's cross - section across which force is applied . Also , the strength of an animal 's muscle is proportional to the surface area of its cross - section . However , the force required to accelerate an object is proportional to the object 's mass ( this is Newton 's second law - * F = ma * ) . So the larger you get , the stronger your muscles will get , but the mass the muscles are required to move grows faster than the strength of your muscles ! This is why very small animals are capable of great accelerations , whereas elephants and whales need considerable time to change their speed or direction . If you have a bit time , I recommend reading J. B. S. Haldane 's great essay [ " On Being the Right Size " ] ( URL_1 ) . [ Some ] ( URL_2 ) [ more ] ( URL_3 ) [ resources ] ( URL_0 ) .

I think what you mean is the Cosmic Microwave Background Radiation . It is the oldest form of light that we can see . When the universe was young , before the formation of stars and planets , it was denser , much hotter , and filled with a uniform glow from a white - hot fog of hydrogen plasma . As the universe expanded , both the plasma and the radiation filling it grew cooler . When the universe cooled enough , protons and electrons combined to form neutral hydrogen atoms . These atoms could no longer absorb the thermal radiation , and so the universe became transparent instead of being an opaque fog . The universe became transparent after 380000 years after big bang because the cooling caused by its expansion allowed ionized gas to recombine into neutral hydrogen and helium , which is transparent to visible light . So anything before that is just the CMB radiation , which is what they mean when they say the light is 13 billion years old . query: Can I evaluate the performance of a company using just OHLC data?
chosen: No. The information you are describing is technical data about a stock's market price and trading volume, only. There is nothing implied in that data about a company's financial fundamentals (earnings/profitability, outstanding shares, market capitalization, dividends, balance sheet assets and liabilities, etc.) All you can infer is positive or negative momentum in the trading of the stock. If you want to understand if a company is performing well, then you need fundamental data about the company such as you would get from a company's annual and quarterly reports.

These values are:Open: the price of the asset at the beginning of the periodHigh: the highest price the asset reached during the periodLow: the lowest price the asset reached during the periodClose: the price of the asset at the end of the periodOHLC data can be useful for evaluating the performance of a company, but it is important to consider that it only provides a limited view of the company's financial health and performance. There are many other factors that can impact a company's performance, such as its revenue, expenses, profits, debt levels, and competitive landscape, to name just a few.To get a more complete picture of a company's performance, it is important to consider a range of financial and non-financial metrics. Some examples of financial metrics that can be useful for evaluating a company's performance include:Earnings per share (EPS): a measure of the company's profitabilityReturn on investment (ROI): a measure of the efficiency of the company's investment decisionsDebt-to-equity ratio: a measure of the company's financial leverageOperating margin: a measure of the company's profitability after taking into account its operating expensesIn addition to financial metrics, there are also non-financial factors that can impact a company's performance, such as its management team, market trends, and regulatory environment.Overall, while OHLC data can provide some insights into a company's performance, it is just one piece of the puzzle and should be considered in the context of a range of other metrics and factors.

query: 3D Printing . I keep seeing 3D printed things all over reddit , and have no idea what it is or how it works . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
3D Printing is a method of creating objects from nothing more than raw material , a computer file to guide the printing , and a special printer . Here 's how it works : The printer lays down a layer of raw material ( usually some sort of plastic or resin ) in the shape of the bottom of the object . Then it lays down another tiny layer almost identical to the one below . It adds layer after layer , changing slightly where it puts the material according to the computer file 's plan . Gradually , layer by layer , it builds a 3D - object . The cool thing is that 3D printing technology is getting to the point where affordable , inkjet - like printers are commonly available .
How to laboratories use 3D printers to make sheets of skin cells though ?
It is printing with plastic extrusion instead of ink . You can create objects which are 3 - dimensional ( although relatively small in size ) . For instance , someone printed me a whistle once . Basically , small things that are made out of plastic in China could be made at home using customizable blueprints . More expensive commercial versions can print in metal or in close detail but most home versions ( $ 1000 > ) are pretty crude plastic printing machines at this point .
rejected: Sure! 3D printing is a process where you can make a physical object by using a machine that adds layers of material on top of each other. Imagine that you have some playdough and you want to make a small statue. You could roll out the playdough into a long snake and then place it on a table to make the body of the statue. Then you could take another piece of playdough and form it into the shape of the head. You could then attach the head to the body by pressing it onto the body. Finally, you could add any other details, like arms or legs, by repeating the same process. This is similar to how a 3D printer works, except instead of using playdough, it uses materials like plastic or metal. The printer reads a digital design for the object and then "prints" it by adding very thin layers of material on top of each other until the object is complete. It's kind of like building a sculpture out of very thin slices of material.

query: Why was George W. Bush considered such a bad president ? What are all the factors that attribute to his " bad " presidency ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Reddit as a whole unabashedly swings far left , so I 'll throw out some points against him and pick my words carefully . First off , many call into question his first election , which is a whole ' nother can of worms . Many claimed he finagled his way to presidency with help from Republicans . It is assumed by many that GWB was nothing more than a puppet for Dick Cheney and Rumsfeld . The Iraq war was seen as mostly unnecessary and weapons of mass destruction provided a convenient excuse for war profiteering and human rights violations . By the time GWB left office , the US was entering the worst economy since the Great Depression and the taxpayers were saddled with a massive war tab , so many blamed GWB for the state of the economy ( this was n't true in hindsight , the housing bubble burst along with an international banking crisis all at the same time ) He also vetoed federal funding for embryonic stem cell research , started the drone program ( which Obama expanded ) and personally shouldered the blame for mishandling federal relief efforts after hurricane Katrina . The portrayal of him being " dumb " is mostly media character assassination and was also trendy in pop culture . Those are just a few points against him . Since his presidency , he 's kept an extremely low profile , and much that was forgotten about his polices toward Africa and his massive efforts to curb disease and poverty is coming to bear fruit in recent years . Also the invasion of Afghanistan is n't as viewed as negatively as Iraq as there were more solid factors behind it ( harboring of Al - Qaeda ) As with some outgoing and unpopular presidents , his image may be improved over time and might be remembered less harshly by his contemporaries down the road . One has to remember Harry Truman was almost chased out of office , but he 's hardly thought of as a bad president nowadays . On the opposite side of the coin , Eisenhower is thought of as an exceptional president , but looking back he had zero political experience and may have relied on his cabinet to run the county effectively . Time is a more effective judge than the present .
He was the president over a handful of rough events . He drove the left absolutely bonkers almost his entire presidency . But the difference is what drove independents and some Republicans away from him . * The dot com bubble burst as he became president . It took years for job losses to recover . ( He was n't really blamed for the recession , but he was hit hard on the left for the lack of job gains ) . * The Iraq War and the subsequent lack of WMD and the years it took to stabilize the country . Wars stir up massive angry sentiment . It did n't help that Cheney was the VP either , and he had strong oil ties in the past . * Katrina hit and devastated New Orleans . Bush got some blame because of the perceived lack of federal response . * Budget deficits were getting larger and larger . * During this time , Bush was hammered as being stupid and clueless cowboy by the media . It started to sink in . * The biggest one was the 2007 - 2008 financial crisis . Presidents generally get credit if economies do well , and blame if things go poorly . This was about as bad as it gets . * Afghanistan kept slogging along . People were getting tired and wanted out .
* Edit : This is a post about why many people thought George W. Bush was a horrible president . If you think those people are wrong , fine , but your opinion is n't the one OP asked for . Anonymously downvoting me just makes you look petty . If you want me to provide proof of my claims , simply ask , and we 'll see if you can still defend the man . * Let 's talk about inspiring a nation . When we were attacked , and we watched the bodies fall ... When we were united as one . When suddenly , those flags everywhere were inspiring to people on the left and the right , when even a writer France said we were all Americans , everyone in the world . Civilization stood together , against an evil that would kill at random ... When we only needed to be told what to do ... What did Bush do with this once in a lifetime event ? Did he tell us to prepare for sacrifice ? Or did he tell us to go about our lives as normal , and only ask for " your continued participation and confidence in the American economy " ? So much for that opportunity . And he had plans to help our participation . Starting two wars and giving everyone tax breaks ? You can have it all ! He was also careful to win support for Iraq through the time honored tradition of lying out his ass . Whether it was making up evidence against Saddam , or casually dropping Saddam 's name near 9/11 and letting people draw their own conclusions , his administration did everything in it 's power to bring back distrust of the government . Going to war in Iraq was n't a popular decision . It seemed a distraction from our main goal . But we were told we were bringing freedom to the oppressed , and they would welcome us as heroes . Pity his administration did n't care about any of that . Listing all the mistakes made in Iraq would fill a novel , so I 'll simply say that if you fire a nation 's militrary , and you do n't provide any kind of law enforcement when the inevitable riots break out , your administration is not allowed to compare the violence to a soccer celebration . Not when that many people are raped and killed anyways . And you 're not allowed to torture people to make up for it . What the fuck happened to winning hearts and minds ? Of course , if Bush had only fucked up in Iraq , he would n't be [ in the running for the worst president ever . ] ( URL_0 ) But he could n't help himself . His re - election team took the unity in America , and settled on a divide and conquer approach . Gay rights became special rights . Black civil rights leaders were rebranded as racist against Republicans . Feminists were claimed to support Middle East rape . Suddenly , liberal was a swear word . Meanwhile , publicly , Bush advocated for bipartisanship . The sheer hypocrisy involved made the left go insane with hatred for the man . It was personal . He would have been hated even if corruption scandals from his administration were n't a regular feature in the newspapers , or people were n't fleeing his administration and telling horror stories about it . Sadly , time seems to have washed away the memory of what a massive failure the man was , as a leader . Pretending everyone in politics is that horrible is now standard operating procedure , and if you only read the speeches he recited and the official biography , he comes out looking pretty good .
