query: Mind Uploading Could we achieve it in a near future ? What limits us from achieving it ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
This sort of thing comes under the broad umbrella of [ transhumanism ] ( URL_0 ) . The better our technology gets , the more we 're able to do to improve people . Transhumanism is basically the idea that technology is a really great way to help people overcome their natural ( or unnatural ) limitations . This ranges from stuff like prosthetic limbs and organs grown from stem - cells to eliminating the aging process and using computers to enhance our mental capabilities . Transhumanism also considers the ethical and moral implications of this kind of thing . The ' mind uploading ' idea basically goes like this . Brains are really complex things but we 're getting better at understanding how they work . By comparison , computers are kind of simple but they 're rapidly becoming more complex and more powerful . In theory , at some point , we 'll understand the brain completely and have computers powerful enough to * model * or * simulate * a brain . If our computers can simulate brains then we could use them to simulate a specific person 's brain . This includes storing all of that person 's memories in digital form rather than in organic form . This idea raises a lot of questions . Would the computer - mind be a sentient person ? Would the computer be the * same * person as the one who was copied ? What kind of rights should it have ? What about the rights of the person who owns the computer ? What about if you made a computer mind and let it become it 's own person ? What exactly does it take to make a person anyway ? Luckily we have time to discuss this stuff in detail right now because actually doing this is quite a long way off . There are a number of fields in computer science dedicated to algorithms that enable computers to make clever decisions but even the most sophisticated algorithm comes nowhere near the human brain in terms of complexity , capacity , bandwidth or adaptability . I can think of three main limits ; hardware , software and society . Clearly our computers and the programs which run on them are going to need to be far , far more complex and powerful than they are now . Society is probably the biggest hurdle , not only because people are resistant to change but because this sort of idea challenges everything most people think they know about humanity .
No . We do n't know how the brain works yet .
Philosophers have been arguing about this for some time , and whether or not this is possible depends on who 's right . Can we achieve it ? We 're not sure , and depending on who you ask you 'll get a different answer . The same people that say " Yes , true AI is possible " will say " yes , this is possible . " But there are also a lot of people who think true AI is not achievable who will say " No , this will never be possible . " One of the big limits is whether or not " the mind " is even the kind of thing that really can be described using software . It 's possible that it 's not . We have a long way to go before we can even answer this question with confidence .
rejected:
Mind uploading, also known as whole brain emulation or brain-computer interface, is the idea of transferring a person's consciousness from their physical brain to a computer or another medium. There is currently no scientific evidence to suggest that this is possible, and many experts believe it may never be possible.

One of the main challenges is that the human brain is extremely complex and we still do not fully understand how it works. The brain contains billions of cells called neurons, which communicate with each other through electrical and chemical signals. It is these signals that allow us to think, feel, and perceive the world around us.

Another challenge is that the brain is constantly changing and adapting to new experiences. It is not a static structure, but rather a dynamic system that is constantly learning and adapting. This means that even if we were able to transfer all of the information from a person's brain to a computer, we might not be able to capture all of the changes and adaptations that have occurred over a person's lifetime.

There are also many ethical concerns surrounding the idea of mind uploading. For example, if a person's consciousness were transferred to a computer, would they still be considered a person, or would they be treated as a machine? Would they have the same rights as a person, or would they be subject to different rules and regulations? These are just a few of the many questions that need to be considered before we can even begin to think about the feasibility of mind uploading.

query: Problems with Enterprise Value and better valuation techniques
chosen: This is an example from another field, real estate. Suppose you buy a $100,000 house with a 20 percent down payment, or $20,000, and borrow the other $80,000. In this example, your "equity" or "market cap" is $20,000. But the total value, or "enterprise value" of the house, is actually $100,000, counting the $80,000 mortgage.  "Enterprise value" is what a buyer would have to pay to own the company or the house "free and clear," counting the debt.
rejected: Enterprise value (EV) is a measure of a company's value that takes into account its debt, minority interests, and preferred stock in addition to its market capitalization. It is often used as an alternative to market capitalization for valuing a company because it provides a more comprehensive picture of the company's financial position.However, there are a few problems with using EV for valuation:It does not consider the company's cash and cash equivalents: EV includes the value of a company's debt, but it does not take into account the company's cash and cash equivalents, which can be significant in some cases. This means that a company with a high EV may actually have a lower net debt position than a company with a lower EV.It does not consider the cost of capital: EV does not consider the cost of capital, which is the required rate of return that investors expect to receive on their investment. This means that a company with a high EV may not be as attractive as a company with a lower EV if the cost of capital is higher for the former.It does not consider the company's growth prospects: EV does not consider the company's growth prospects, which can have a significant impact on its value. A company with strong growth prospects may be more valuable than a company with a similar EV but weaker growth prospects.There are other valuation techniques that take into account some or all of these factors. For example, the discounted cash flow (DCF) method is a widely used technique that considers the company's future cash flows and the cost of capital. The net asset value (NAV) method is another technique that considers the company's net assets, including its cash, investments, and tangible assets.It's important to note that no single valuation technique is perfect and it's generally a good idea to use multiple techniques to get a more complete picture of a company's value.

relevant example : you do n't qualify for a job because it requires 3 - 5 years experience , and you can never get the 3 - 5 years experience because no one will give you a job .

rejected:
A Catch-22 is a situation in which a person is trapped by contradictory rules or conditions. The term comes from the novel "Catch-22" by Joseph Heller, in which the main character, Yossarian, is an American soldier in World War II.

Here's an example of a Catch-22:

Imagine you want to get a driver's license, but you need to have a car to practice driving. However, you can't buy a car until you have a driver's license. This is a Catch-22 because you need a driver's license to buy a car, but you need a car to get a driver's license.

Here's another example:

Imagine you are sick and need to go to the doctor, but you can't get an appointment unless you have a referral from another doctor. However, you can't see another doctor unless you have an appointment. This is a Catch-22 because you need to see a doctor to get a referral, but you need a referral to see a doctor.

In both of these examples, there is no way to solve the problem because the rules or conditions are contradictory. Do you understand?

query: Why is there such a huge gap in between the elements in the first three rows of the periodic table ? Such as between hydrogen and helium , berelium and boron , or magnesium and aluminum . Why are n't they immediatly after one another on the table ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The periodic table you know has actually been squished to fit on the page better . [ It more correctly looks like this ] ( URL_0 ) , so if you thought hydrogen and helium were far apart before , well , hold on to your hat !
Short version - the table lines them up by behavior , and the edge behaviors start out frequent but get farther apart . Longer Version - One of the biggest ways elements behave is in how they interact with others . The primary way they interact is by sharing electrons . Elements have layers of electrons they like to fill , so if they have one electron all by itself in a layer , they will be very eager to donate that electron to fill someone elses outer layer . If they have a full outer layer , they wo n't want to interact with anything to avoid messing up their pretty outer layer . Now , the reason for the gap - the layers are not the same size . Like an onion , the inner layers are small , and the outer layers are big . So the very first row goes from only one outer electron to a full outer layer in just 2 elements , because the inner layer only has 2 " spaces " to fill . Further down the chart the larger layers take more electrons to fill , so there are more elements between " only one " and " totally full " . More accurate version - take a chemistry class Really accurate version - Many researchers still make livings figuring out more fine details and coming up with better ways to understand why these things do what they do .
Elements are placed on the table according to the number of valence electrons they have . Generally , nonmetals are on the right side of the table , while metals are on the left . The metals in the middle are the transition metals ; they can have different numbers of valence electrons in their elemental state . The placement of hydrogen is actually pseudo - controversial ; it is n't a metal , but it 's placed in the same family as the alkali metals . Some tables place it next to helium , above flourine , since it also has one valence electron .

query: Why does Fox News have such a terrible reputation ? I understand they run sensationalist , bias - driven stories , but so does every news outlet . What is it about Fox News that makes everyone call " bull$#!t " ? Explain like I'm five.

query: Why is it impossible to exhale into a bottle ? Opening the bottle cap and putting your mouth over , blocking for air to get inside is easy , but when you try to exhale it is almost impossible , why is that ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The pressure of the air in your lungs is about the same as the pressure in the bottle . Since the bottle is rigid and does n't get bigger if you blow into it , any increase in the amount of air in the bottle results in higher air pressure in the bottle . The increased air pressure very quickly gets stronger than the force you can exert with the muscles that push the air out of your lungs and you ca n't exhale any more after that . Think about blowing up a balloon , bike tire , or other inflatable object . It 's pretty easy to blow or pump air into at first because there 's very little air pressure inside . The last few blows or pumps are hard to make though because the air pressure increases as you fill up the object . It 's the same thing here , only the bottle is already full of air from the atmosphere .
Because the bottle is already filled with air at the same pressure as the rest of the room / atmosphere you are standing in . When you try to exhale into the bottle , the existing air in the bottle has no place to go because your mouth is covering the only exit . As you exhale , you 're increasing the pressure inside the bottle , and you 're feeling this pressure as resistance when you try to exhale ... TL;DR : You 're trying to add air to a bottle that is already completely full of air .
The bottle has a limited volume and is already filled with air . Your lungs are not strong enough to compress the air and force more air into the bottle .
rejected: When you exhale, you are trying to push air out of your body and into the bottle. The air inside your body is under higher pressure than the air outside of the bottle, so when you try to exhale into the bottle, the air inside your body is trying to push against the air inside the bottle. The bottle is not able to expand to accommodate the extra air that you are trying to put into it, so it becomes difficult to exhale into the bottle. It's kind of like trying to squeeze a sponge that is already completely full of water. The sponge can't hold any more water, so it becomes difficult to squeeze more in.\n\nAnother way to think about it is to imagine that the bottle is like a balloon. When you try to blow up a balloon, it becomes easier to blow as the balloon expands. But if you try to blow up a balloon that is already completely full, it becomes much harder to blow because the balloon can't expand any more. It's the same with exhaling into a bottle. If the bottle is already full of air, it is much harder to exhale into it because the bottle can't expand any more to accommodate the extra air that you are trying to put into it.

query: If the Nazis were ultranationalists in favour of tradition , why did n't they reinstate the German monarchy ? Seems like . Natural thing to do for party so supportive of nationalistic symbols and tradition . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The German Emperors were n't really a long - established tradition like other monarchies . They were the Kings of Prussia , the last three of whom adopted the title of German Emperor . Also it 's likely that Hitler partly blamed Wilhelm II for Germany 's defeat in the First World War , so bringing in his grandson to restore the monarchy would n't appeal .
There was a rather long discussion about the relationship of nationalism , monarchy and the whole question of whether the Nazis where left or right - wing recently on /r / badhistory . For some deeper stuff , I therefore recommend this [ post of mine ] ( URL_0 ) . The easy version , for ELI5 : Yes , the Nazis were , by modern definition , right wing , ultra - nationalist , traditionalist ; although they personally pretended to reject this left to right spectrum . What we have to consider is that European , and especially German parliaments and parties have always been very different from the classic American two party system . Meaning that parties with every so slightly differing opinions might easily split up , while it would , for example , be crazy for the Tea party in the US to try to form an independent party from the Republicans . Now , the difference between the Nazis and the National conservatives ( who were monarchists ) was n't actually that small though . That 's what so special both about fascism and communism . Both were essentially an alternative interpretation of the left and right wing at this time . The right wing up until then meant being supportive of the recently disposed monarchy . And the Nazis did actually embrace that narrative of the monarchy , or at least the empire , more than most people grant them here in this thread . In the post I linked , there 's a fairly well known postcard from that time showing Frederick II . " The Great " ; Bismarck , chancellor of Prussia and the Empire ; Hindenburg , Chief of Staff at the end of World War II and finally , Hitler . The well known pompous marches , evocative of the Romans ; and the whole term of the " Third Reich " show that connection as well . The problem was , that , as others have pointed out , Germans made the Kaiser , Wilhelm II . , responsible for losing World War II - besides Jews and Socialists , due to Nazi propaganda . They also despised the Elitist nature that was even inherent to the late Imperial army , with Nobles taking all the important positions of power and the common men dying in the trenches . They still liked the idea of a powerful Empire though ; and thought that more conservative mores were needed , making greater social freedom responsible for the fall of the republic . ( The same narrative we see with modern right wingers : Multiculturalism and tolerance towards alternative lifestyles weaken the nation . ) Essentially , Hitler made conservatism for the masses . The Nazis never really abandoned their silent admiration for the monarchy though - see the pompous marches and the whole " Reich"-narrative . The dethroned Kaiser actually resided in Belgium for the rest of his life and thought that Hitler would eventually reinstate him . When he died , Hitler gave him a pompous funeral ; with Swastikas all around , despite for the Kaiser previously having said that he did not want them to be displayed there . The army was also still heavily influenced by former nobles . VON Stauffenberg and his accomplices , who tried and failed to kill Hitler , were all disgruntled monarchists , but had collaborated with the Nazis so far because there evidently was still a lot of ideological overlap .
The were National Socialists ( Nazi is short for this ) - not National Roaylists .
rejected:
The Nazi Party, which ruled Germany from 1933 to 1945, was indeed an ultranationalist party that sought to promote the interests of the German nation and people. However, the party was also authoritarian and dictatorial, and it did not believe in allowing for the existence of any power centers outside of its own control. 
The German monarchy, which had been abolished in 1918 at the end of World War I, represented a potential rival power center that could potentially challenge the authority of the Nazi Party. Therefore, the Nazi leadership decided not to reinstate the monarchy, even though it may have seemed like a natural thing to do given the party's emphasis on tradition and national symbols. 
Instead, the Nazi Party established itself as the supreme authority in Germany, and it sought to eliminate any potential rivals or opposition to its rule. This included suppressing and eliminating political parties, organizations, and individuals who opposed the party's goals or policies.

query: How does a company provide faster internet ? Is there an internet speed knob they turn ? When a company decides to provide faster internet , what are they actually doing ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Two different reason , either the medium changes or the process changes . To make that more concrete : * Medium : the physical connection changes . Copper , coaxial , fiber . Fatter wires , more wires . You now have more physical capacity through which to send a signal . * Process : The equipment at each end of the physical connection changes . For copper ( regular telephone lines ) , this went from ADSL- > VDSL- > bonding . For cable / coaxial , it 's DOCSIS1,2,3 . For fiber there 's still lots of room for room for growth in this area . An analogy : USB1- > USB2- > USB3 , generally there 's no change in the medium . It 's still a regular old USB cable . The controller and software for the end devices changes . This is what I called a process change . For VGA to HDMI , the physical connection type changes ( which also entails a different process ) , as can be seen by the different type of wire that 's needed . In general , changing the physical medium is a lot more costly than changing the process ( which for a telco / cableco usually means changing some of the equipment / nodes in the field and not actually laying new wire connections going to the home )
They change a bunch of scheduler policies and bandwidth configurations in various devices on their network , then they coordinate with third party transport providers like Verizon or TWC who provide fiber or ethernet to your house , they make those same changes in their network , resulting in faster network speed .
Source : I 'm an intern at a major ISP in the US . You are correct in saying that they " turn a knob " to an extent . For this particular ISP , each customer has a " profile " ( for lack of a better term ) for all the services they have . For example : I might have standard internet , phone and tv . Then I might have an HBO or Showtime package with the tv , etc , etc . Behind the scenes , when you call to upgrade , all someone is doing is changing that little profile . So if you wanted 50MBPS , all I have to do is go in and flip the switch that says " Enable 50MBPS " , or whatever it may be called . Now , as for how that is possible : 1 ) It depends on the connections to your modem . 2 ) It depends on the modem itself . If all the infrastructure in the area can support 1GBPS , and your modem can , then I just need to flip the switch and give you that speed . This is where things tend to get hairy . This is the fundamental part of it . ISP 's need to know how much they can provide without having to tear up old infrastructure . If you call and ask for 100MBPS service , and you only get 50MBPS ( assuming the 100 is turned on ) , then something with the infrastructure is wrong . It could be that you have an old modem that is not able to allow more than 50MBPS . It could also be that the connection to your house is outdated . So : Yes , they do " flip a switch " to give you service . That being said , the infrastructure needs to be in place in order for them to actually provide you with that service .
rejected: There are a few things that a company can do to provide faster internet to its customers. One way is to upgrade their network infrastructure, such as the cables that carry the internet signal and the routers that direct traffic. This can allow more data to be transmitted at a faster rate. Another way is to invest in newer, more advanced technologies that can increase the speed and capacity of their internet service. Think of it like a highway. If you want to increase the speed at which cars can travel on the highway, you can widen the lanes or add more lanes to accommodate more cars. You can also improve the quality of the road surface to allow for smoother travel. Similarly, a company can upgrade their internet infrastructure to allow for faster speeds and better performance.

query: Why is there so much smoke produced after a fire goes out ? Even if the material seems to burn clean before it goes out . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
When something burns hotly , it burns cleanly . When you take away the flame , it has enough embers and residual heat to continue smoldering , but without the heat of the flame the result is incomplete combustion resulting in smoke and soot . Details : What is smoke ? It 's soot , and carbon compounds , tar , aerosols , un burned fuel , and other components of incomplete combustion . When a fire burns brightly and hotly , it efficiently combines oxygen with carbon to make carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide , and with hydrogen to make water vapor . If all of the hydrocarbons and organic material being burned are done so cleanly , then there is very little smoke and instead an almost pure reaction resulting in carbon dioxide and water which are odorless and transparent gases . IE not smoke . When a flame is extinguished but there is still a lot of latent heat , the process of burning is snuffed out resulting in incomplete transformation into the water and carbon dioxide , leading to incomplete compounds which create the soot .
Smoke is fine particulate matter suspended in a gaseous stream . Air currents can stir up these fine particles just as easily as outgassing from a fire . Every drop of water stirs it up . Fire requires 3 pieces to exist : heat , oxygen , & fuel . Water reduces the heat of the fire . Halogen removes the oxygen . The fire may be reduced in its ability to reignite but the fine particles of ash already produced can still become airborne , hence smoke long after the fire is out .
I do n't know that this expands to other fires , but candles seem to only produce smoke when they 've just been put out because that " smoke " is actually evaporating wax that is most of the fuel . There are videos of someone blowing out a candle then holding a lit match in the " smoke " above the candle to relight it .
rejected:
When a fire is burning, it is consuming oxygen and producing smoke as a byproduct of the chemical reactions that are taking place. When the fire goes out, it is usually because it has run out of fuel or because it has been deprived of oxygen. When this happens, the chemical reactions stop and the smoke production stops as well.

However, even after the fire is out, there may still be materials that are smoldering or slowly burning. These materials can continue to produce smoke until they are completely consumed or until they are extinguished. The smoke produced by smoldering materials is often thicker and more difficult to see through than the smoke produced by a fire that is burning brightly.

In addition, the smoke produced after a fire goes out may contain particles of burnt materials, such as ash, soot, and other residues. These particles can be carried along with the smoke as it rises and can make the smoke appear denser and more opaque.

I hope this helps to explain why there may be smoke produced after a fire goes out. Let me know if you have any other questions.

query: why ' night lights ' map of the US is divided in the middle [ Here is an example ] ( URL_0 ) Right in the middle there is a ' division line ' and the west side is significantly darker than east side . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
That line is approximately 100 West longitude . That line roughly corresponds with the end of the " Rocky Mountain rain shadow " , which is the zone of North America that has reduced rainfall because the air dried out in its west - to - east trip over the Rocky Mountains . East of that line , the air is wetter in the summer because moisture gets pulled south - to - north from the Gulf of Mexico . What does rain have to do with it ? Well , east of 100W , there 's enough rain to grow corn ( maize ) and soyabeans . Because those crops are so productive per acre , this means that you can have much smaller farms run profitably over a long period of time , so when the area was settled by ( white ) Americans in the first half of the 19th Century , the rural areas were relatively thickly settled . The food they grew was enough to support lots of small towns and midsized cities , both in terms of food and dollars . Later , when people left the farms and moved to cities ( and their suburbs ) , they tended to stay near the places where they grew up . Meanwhile , west of 100W , with less rainfall , the main crop is wheat . It 's still productive , but to really make a go of it , you need more mechanization and irrigation than you do in the east , and the economies of scale are much larger . So that area did n't settle until the late 19th Century ( when mechanization and irrigation were available ) , and when it did , fewer people lived there .
It 's because Utah sucks the life out of any state in contact with it .
URL_0 First off , America gained that territory in 1803 after the French had made very little use of it , so while the East Coast was getting peopled by British emigrants ( Britain had a strong pro - emigration policy , France 's was much weaker ) , the French were sitting on their hands . Further , before infrastructure lines were built like railroads and canals , the crops that were grown west of the 100th meridian could n't effectively make it to population centers , making farming in the region unprofitable . Even more than that , the land west of the 100th meridian is extremely dry , so farming is almost impossible without heavy irrigation anyway . EDIT : More info : When the railroads were laid , they allowed for boomtowns to crop up along rail lines , but civilization stopped a mile past either side of the track . The railroads created a national market for crops , thus depressing prices , so even when railroads made it possible to get to the midwest , it also killed the already low potential for profits in the region . The great plains were , however , great for cattle ranching because cattle could be driven to rail lines , unlike crops which must be carried somehow ( and the arid climate lends itself better to ranching ) . Cattle ranching is a high - investment , high - risk enterprise , so most Americans could n't afford to set up a ranch even with credit , so the money was held by ranchers and not farmers who wanted huge tracts of land . The farmers had no real chance out there . And with the popularization of the RR , access to California and Oregon became possible , so potential farmers moved to CA instead of OK or KS ( can you blame them ? ) .
rejected: The "night lights" map of the United States shows how bright different parts of the country are at night. The map is divided in the middle because it is showing the United States from coast to coast, and the country is very big. The west side of the map is generally darker than the east side because there are fewer people and fewer cities on the west coast. In general, cities are brighter at night because they have more streetlights, buildings, and other sources of light. So the map shows that the east coast, which has more cities, is brighter at night than the west coast.

Over time, the word "libra" was shortened to "lb," and this abbreviation has been used to represent the pound unit of weight in many different countries and languages. So, instead of "pds," we use "LB" as the abbreviation for pounds because it comes from the Latin word for this unit of weight.

query: Does a larger down payment make an offer stronger?
chosen: There is a lot of your financial information that the selling agent handles in the course of a real estate transaction, including but not limited to your pre-approval letter which states what maximum purchase price might be. Closing costs and interest rate are not details they would know unless you shared that with them, given that that is done after you go binding.  I agree with xiaomy in that, while in absolute monetary terms the higher amount should always be more attractive, the selling agent wants to ensure the transaction goes as smoothly as possible. With contracts falling through due to first-time buyers not making it through mortgage underwriting, it is in the seller's interest - and thus the seller's agent's concern - that the buyer not present such hurdles. Insofar as a higher down payment is a signal for that, then I can understand why it would be more attractive.
rejected: In general, a larger down payment may make an offer more attractive to a seller, because it demonstrates the buyer's financial stability and commitment to the purchase. A down payment is the amount of money that the buyer puts towards the purchase price of the home at the time of the offer, with the balance of the purchase price being financed through a mortgage loan.A larger down payment can also potentially result in a lower mortgage interest rate, as lenders may view a larger down payment as a sign of the borrower's ability to make their monthly mortgage payments. In addition, a larger down payment can result in a lower loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, which is the ratio of the amount of the mortgage loan to the value of the property. A lower LTV ratio may be seen as less risky to the lender and may result in more favorable loan terms.It's important to note that a larger down payment is not always necessary or possible for everyone. There are programs available that allow for smaller down payments, such as FHA loans, which may be an option for some buyers. Ultimately, the decision about how much to put down as a down payment should be based on the buyer's financial situation and goals.

query: What do Brita water filters do to make " cleaner " water ? I see filters have those black specs ... what are they ? how do the filters " clean " and filter water . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Activated Carbon . It has microscopic pores that catch other small " things " in the water . Does n't really make it much cleaner and stops working very soon after .
The filter is basically a [ chromatography column ] ( URL_0 ) . Activated carbon is one of the materials used in the filter , and it has very high surface area for adsorption of impurities . Like all other chromatography techniques , it relies on the impurities moving down the filter at a much slower rate than water does . That 's why it 's recommended you change it after a couple of months - after a certain time you 'll start eluting whatever that was adsorbed onto the filter before .
Personally , I have a Brita pitcher and it does n't make the water taste any better , so I tend to only use it for refrigerating the water .
rejected:
Brita water filters are designed to remove impurities from tap water. They work by using a process called filtration. Filtration is a way to separate solid particles from a liquid by passing the liquid through a filter.

The black specs in a Brita water filter are made of a material called activated carbon. Activated carbon is a type of carbon that has been treated with oxygen to make it very porous. This means that it has a lot of small holes in it, which can absorb impurities in the water as it passes through the filter.

When you pour water into the top of a Brita water filter pitcher, the water flows through the filter and passes through the activated carbon. As it passes through, the activated carbon absorbs impurities like chlorine, bacteria, and other contaminants. This helps to make the water cleaner and more pure.

After the water has passed through the filter, it comes out of the bottom of the pitcher, ready to be poured and enjoyed. So, in short, Brita water filters use activated carbon to remove impurities from tap water, making it cleaner and better tasting.

query: Why does eating spicy food make me sweat ? That 's about all there is to it . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Ok I 've got this one , basically the active chemical in chilies is capsaicin , and one of the main ways it makes its chilli sensation is by activating some nerves by binding to a specific channel in the nerve that makes the nerve fire off , it just so happens that this same channel is important in regulating body temperature . Activation of it in the skin has a cooling affect , it is usually left on , to stop your body from overheating . so when you have capsaicin it overactivates the channel , so your body decides that it is too hot and needs to cool down so as a reaction you sweat . I 'm sorry I do n't think i was very ELI5 but i can expand and/or simplify if you 'd like
Fun fact ! Cultures closer to the equator generally have spicier foods than cultures further away from the equator because of this ! They eat the spicy food , start sweating , and find it easier to keep cool in their hot / humid climate . :)
The spicy compound capsaicin triggers the same receptors in the mouth as heat does , which is why we describe spicy foods as " burning " . The body 's natural response to increase in temperature is to sweat , and since your autonomic system ca n't tell the difference between genuine heat and the fake spicy heat , you sweat .
rejected:
Spicy food makes you sweat because it contains a chemical called capsaicin, which is found in hot peppers. When you eat spicy food, capsaicin activates special receptors in your mouth and throat that are sensitive to heat. These receptors send a message to your brain that says, "This food is hot!"

Your brain responds to this message by turning up your body's natural cooling system. One way your body cools itself down is by making you sweat. The sweat on your skin helps to cool you down, like water on a hot car radiator. So when you eat spicy food and start to sweat, it's your body's way of trying to cool you down and get rid of the heat from the capsaicin.

query: How could disappear over 4 trilion USD during the financial crisis from 2007 ? I mean how can money get lost , so no one has it anymore ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Here 's a way to think about it . You own a painting . You think that the painting is an original by Da Vinci , worth $ 10 million . You have the value of that painting as part of your net worth , not cause you have the actual cash on hand , but because the painting has worth and adds value . However , something horrible happens . On an examination , it turns out the painting is n't an original Da Vinci , but rather a clever forgery some art student made in the 60s , and it 's only worth $ 100,000 . You 've effectively lost $ 9.9 million . You did n't actually lose any money , but something you owned that you thought was worth $ 10 million , is worth only a tiny fraction of that . In reality , the painting was stocks , this happened on a massive scale .
Do n't think of it necessarily is money lost but wealth lost . For example , if a company has a share price of $ 50 and is valued at $ 1B and the share price falls to $ 25 $ 500 million was just erased from that company 's evaluation and so is worth that much less . If you go to the grocery store and buy milk for $ 3 a gallon and next week it 's $ 6 a gallon your wealth as it pertains to your ability to buy milk was just cut in half even though no money disappeared .
Say you buy a car from me . You paid $ 10,000 for it two years ago , and now it 's worth $ 7K. Where did the money go ? Well , the * * money * * did n't go anyplace -- it 's still in my desk drawer , all $ 10,000 of it . But what you * * * bought * * * with it * * lost value * * . Now just scale that up to everybody in the northern hemisphere :-)
rejected:
During a financial crisis, like the one that happened in 2007, lots of people and companies can lose money at the same time. When this happens, it can be hard for them to get the money back, and it can seem like it has "disappeared".

One way that people and companies can lose money is if they invest in things that go down in value. For example, if someone buys a house, and then the value of houses goes down, they might not be able to sell the house for as much as they paid for it. This would mean that they have lost some of the money they invested in the house.

Another way that people and companies can lose money is if they lend it to other people or organizations that are not able to pay it back. This is called a "default", and it can happen if the person or organization that borrowed the money runs into financial problems.

Finally, during a financial crisis, people and companies might also lose money because they are not able to do as much business as usual. This could be because people are worried about the economy and are not spending as much money, or because it is harder to get loans or other financial help.

All of these things can contribute to a financial crisis, and they can make it seem like a lot of money has disappeared.

In many children 's books , adult figures serve this function , either by validating or denying the children 's sometimes tangential take on reality . Think about it this way . In the cartoon strip " Calvin and Hobbs , " the long - suffering parental figures are the ones who enable his childhood . By providing that brief base in reality , they enable Calvin 's flights of fancy to relate much better to the audience than they would if , for example , the strip were solely a fantasy strip about a boy and a talking tiger .
Because they 're children 's books and having anthropomorphic engines is what interests the children . If it were just a series of conversations between the director of the railway and his engineers it would n't have nearly the same appeal to children . Conversely , the author of the books , Rev. W. Awdry , was a steam enthusiast who wanted to portray the running and functioning of a railway as realistic as possible while not sacrificing the element that interests children , the engines as characters . This is why in the first couple of books Sir Topham Hatt was known as ' The Fat Director ' because the first books were written before 1948 when British railways were independent businesses . Shortly after he became known as ' The Fat Controller ' because after 1948 all British railways were nationalised , owned by the state , but administered by a local controller . Also , a number of episodes / books featured ' Rule 55 ' , or the breaking of it , where engines must alert the signal box if they are waiting on a line .
Ah , but who 's operating who ? When an engine like Thomas decides it 's time to switch tracks and uncouple the passenger cars so he can play hide and seek , it 's the engineers that do that because the engines lack hands to do it themselves .
rejected:
In the world of Thomas and Friends, Sir Topham Hatt is the "Fat Controller," which means he is in charge of all the trains on the island of Sodor. The trains in Thomas and Friends are animated characters, and they are able to understand and communicate with Sir Topham Hatt and with each other.

Even though there are real people who operate and maintain the trains in the real world, in the world of Thomas and Friends, the trains can think and talk on their own. Sir Topham Hatt talks to the trains to give them instructions, praise them for their hard work, or scold them if they have done something wrong.

It might seem strange to us that Sir Topham Hatt is talking to trains, but in the world of Thomas and Friends, this is just how things work. It's like how in a cartoon, animals can talk and behave like humans, even though that's not how things work in the real world. Does that help to understand why Sir Topham Hatt talks to the engines?

query: Why Australia is so dry despite being surrounded by ocean ? What 's different with , say , NZ ? I know that the south - east coast is not desert , but what 's the explanation for the rest of the country ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Basically , the earth 's atmosphere has interesting air currents that flow from sea level up to the upper atmosphere and back down again . These currents are essentially locked in place . When air is lifted up into the atmosphere , clouds form from water vapor in the air chilling which forms clouds . Over a certain latitude in Australia , cool dry air from the upper atmosphere is falling over the continent which results in warm dry air on the ground . Helpful infograph : URL_0
For one , the Great Dividing Range , which runs down most of the east coast , soaks up the rain coming in from the pacific .
While I am not an expert on climatology and geography , I believe it is because of the way the trade winds circulate around that section of the globe . The trade winds of that region runs smack into the mountains on the eastern portion of the continent and releases all the moisture at once and therefore there is none left to rain upon the rest of the land . Also the prevalence of exceedingly hot temperatures in the interior also limit the amount of rain that can fall based upon the adiabatic circumstances of how much moisture can be held in warm / hot air versus colder air . That last sentence would be a worthy subject of a EL5 thread itself .
rejected: Australia is a very large country with a wide variety of climates, including both wet and dry areas. The reason that much of Australia is dry is because it is located in the subtropics, which means that it gets less rainfall than other parts of the world. This is due to the fact that the subtropical high pressure system, which is a large area of high pressure in the Earth's atmosphere, tends to bring dry and stable air to much of Australia. New Zealand, on the other hand, is located in the temperate zone, which means that it gets more rainfall than Australia. This is because the temperate zone has more variable weather patterns, with cold fronts and low pressure systems bringing rain to the region. So, in short, Australia is dry because it is located in a part of the world that gets less rainfall, while New Zealand is wetter because it is located in a part of the world that gets more rainfall.

query: h.264 It 's the top post on /r / technology right now , but I ca n't seem to find a simple explanation of its function , and why it 's such a big deal . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Without getting too much into things , H264 is a general purpose video compression codec that is incredibly good at what it does . A video codec is a way of compressing raw video so that it 's a much smaller file while still maintaining the quality of the video . H264 videos are quite small compared to other codecs at the same visual quality . What this means is that H264 is a great format for high quality , low bandwidth streams . It 's also not very CPU intensive to encode and decode , which means it does n't take super powerful computers to deal with it . It getting natively built into Firefox is important because it means content providers can use H264 video for HTML5 video streams and it will be supported natively , by Firefox , on any platform without the need for additional system codecs or browser plugins like Flash . This is huge for linux folks . Also by having it native in - browser it allows for better performance of any video streams , including video conferences , which is a pretty big win for global businesses who often need to rely on expensive and proprietary subscription services for high quality video conferencing . At least that 's my read .
I could go into a little more detail , if anyone 's interested . * * A quick primer on video coding * * : " raw " video is a sequence of raw images . A raw image is what the camera sensor generates . Typically , a camera sensor would use between 1 - 4 bytes to store / transmit a single pixel . So a 1080p image would e.g. take 1920x1080x2 bytes . At 30 frames ( images ) per second , that would be a _ bit _ rate of 1920x1080x2x30x8 bits per second . That 's ~1 Gbps . No one has that kind of bandwidth ( yet ) . Video coding works to reduce the space it takes to store video . So our example sensor would still generate ~1 Gbps , but I would then _ encode _ the video and reduce it to something a lot lesser . This is transmitted / stored , _ decoded _ and then displayed . This is what ALL codecs do . However , different codecs have different features . Almost ALL popular consumer codecs make use of so - called " lossy " coding techniques . What this means is that the decoded video _ loses _ some information relative to the original video . Often times the loss is perceivable ( URL_0 ) , but a good codec attempts to minimize the loss . ( NB : lossless codecs do exist , and are often used in applications where there is a legal need to NOT have losses . e.g. medical , law enforcement , etc ) * * History / Examples * * * MJPEG - as the name would suggest , is motion JPEG . It compresses each image in the sequence independently as a JPEG image . These are sometimes called " Intra frames " . * MPEG1 - designed way back in the late 80s / early 90s . It was one of the first to standardize " inter - frame " encoding . This is key and helps save a lot of space . In a typical video there 's not too much difference from frame to frame ( think about it- there 's not much movement in 1/30th of a second unless you 're recording The Flash ) . Inter - frame coding is a technique where encoders need to store " just " the difference between frames * H263 - The predecessor to H264 and designed in the mid 90s . Also maintained by the same organization as H264 ( ITU - T ) , added features like a deblocking filter ( where the " blockiness " due to compression losses could be reduced ) , larger resolutions , etc . Standardization is important to allow interoperability . However , video standards do n't really standardize _ how _ encoding should be done . They just standardize how the output of encoding should look . This leaves a lot of leeway to implementers of encoding algorithms to come up with better algorithms , while still letting everyone play the video . In effect , organizations like the ITU are gatekeepers of the larger interest of interoperability , but they are NOT deciding quality . * * H264 * * H264 , ratified in the early ' 00s was essentially an umbrella standard that took all the advances in video encoding / decoding sigproc research since the past iteration and standardized them . It added a WHOLE bunch of really amazing features . Some examples : * multiple reference frames - you could use multiple past frames as reference to describe the present frame , thus allowing better compression * more accurate " sub - pixel " motion compensation - so movement could be encoded better * entropy coding - optimizes pixels down to " bits " by taking advantage of statistical models of the video Some of this stuff is really involved and really seriously cool . However , stuff like this involves big money , and so some of the algorithmic bits of the H264 standard are patented by various companies . For example , Cisco has a patent that involves the entropy coding bit above . So , in countries that respect software / algorithm patents , large parts of H264 are patented . A company called the MPEG LA ( no real relation to MPEG ) administers the pool . MPEG LA actively litigates people who implement H264 without licensing the patents . They charge royalties based on the number of users of a particular implementation . * * WebRTC , Mozilla , Cisco , et al * * Thanks to all the funky stuff above , H264 is a really awesome codec for peer - to - peer video communications ( high quality , low bandwidth , etc ) . Industry standard H264 based communication stacks can cost millions . As media consumption moved away from standalone apps into the web and browsers , browser support for video codecs became critical . In 2011 ( ? ) Google released a Free and open source browser based video conferencing stack called WebRTC " into the wild " . ( Google 's aim is always to increase web usage in any way possible , so they can sell ads and make money ) However WebRTC could not use H264 because web codecs had to be open , and no one wanted to take the hit of paying for H264 . This is exactly what Cisco and Mozilla have done . Cisco has agreed to license H264 from MPEGLA and release it into the wild while paying MPEGLA for it . Mozilla has agreed to include H264 into WebRTC now that it 's open source . To tie all this together , getting H264 into WebRTC would mean that A LOT more people can use high quality video in the browser . Thus the hullabaloo .
Why is it being linked with real time communication . Are we talking video - calling upgrades ?
rejected:
H.264 is a video compression format that is used to reduce the size of video files while maintaining good quality. It works by using algorithms to remove redundancies and inefficiencies in the data of a video file. This allows the file to be stored and transmitted more efficiently, without taking up as much space or requiring as much bandwidth.

H.264 is a big deal because it has become one of the most widely used video compression formats in the world. It is used to encode video for a variety of applications, including streaming video online, storing video on devices like DVDs and Blu-ray discs, and transmitting video over the internet or other networks. It is also used in a variety of industries, including entertainment, healthcare, education, and more.

In short, H.264 is important because it allows us to store and transmit video more efficiently, making it easier to watch and share video online.

query: What is sound ? So , yeah , what is it ? EDIT : Thanks everyone for the explanations , I feel a little smarter now . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's a wave Go disturb some water . See the ripples moving away in all directions ? Sound is exactly that , only with air instead of water
Sound is just compression waves in the air . Actually , it is both compression and de - compression ( properly called rarefaction ) . For example , a speaker makes sound by rapidly moving in and out . When it moves out , it pushes the air molecules , compressing them . When it moves back in , the opposite happens ... the air molecules become less compressed together than normally . This pushing / pulling of the air will then move and spread out , gradually losing intensity as it moves both due to resistance of the air and the fact that it is spreading , making the energy that is present fill a larger volume . Eventually , the sound becomes no more than background noise . In effect , the energy that was in the sound wave has turned into heat , slightly warming the air that it has traveled through . Very slightly .
The easiest way to figure out sound is to take apart a speaker . You can feel and see how the speaker moves back and forward very quickly to make the sound . That movement makes waves in the air just like ripples in the water . When those ripples in the air get to your ears , they make your ear drum move back and forward just like the speaker does . When your brain picks up this movement of your ear drum , it has a corresponding feeling which is how your experience the sound . Just like how you have a feeling for pain , or a feeling for seeing colours . Microphones and speakers are basically the same thing in reverse . Microphones are made to be really good at detecting sound , speakers at creating sound . But they both work by moving air and moving surfaces .
rejected: Sound is a type of energy that travels through the air (or through other substances, like water or metal) as a vibration. When something vibrates, it moves back and forth very quickly. These vibrations create waves of pressure that move through the air, and our ears are able to pick up on these waves and turn them into something we can hear.For example, if you bang a drum, the drum skin vibrates, creating sound waves that travel through the air. When these sound waves reach your ears, your ears pick up on the vibrations and send a signal to your brain, which interprets the signal as sound.Sound is all around us, and we use it to communicate with each other, to listen to music, and to learn about the world around us.

query: How comes warm air comes out cool from a fan and not warmer ? I just thought anything ( particles ) that gets " moved " around became warmer . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The temperature is no different . The cooling effect is simply air movement over the skin drawing heat away . If the ambient temperature is high enough , the cooling effect is lost .
[ Physics ] ( URL_0 ) shows us that the average speed of molecules at room temperature is around 1100 mph , much much faster than the fan can pump them out . At these speeds , adding 10 - 15 mph is n't heating the air much at all , and most of the time , is n't increasing the speeds of the individual molecules themselves . The fan just changes the average speed of the molecules . Most of the time , air molecules are bouncing off of each other , flying around the room like crazy , but without any noticeable trends ; when there is a measurable average speed of the air molecules , we call that wind . Still , the fan _ is _ increasing the temperature of the air ever so slightly , because all of the energy used to power the fan is escaping from the fan as heat . However , the moving air over your body subjects you to what is called the wind chill effect : this is the same effect that makes a cold day colder when it is windy outside . This effect takes place at all temperatures , like the temperatures inside your room when you turn a fan on . The wind chill effect is much greater than the small amount of heat given off by the fan , so a fan will cool you off even while making the air itself warmer . There 's a rather interesting physics - esque explanation for how sweating ( and moving air ) cools your body , but that 's less ELI5 and more [ ELIStudent ( physics link , not real subreddit ) ] ( URL_1 ) . It involves the heat of vaporization ( energy needed to change the state of a liquid to a gas ) . Because molecules in a gas are moving faster than molecules in a liquid , the liquid needs to absorb energy from its surroundings in order to make that change and evaporate . When this happens to the sweat on your body , that energy comes from the heat of your body . When your body loses this heat , you feel cooler , and the sweat evaporates . When the sweat evaporates , the air around your body becomes more saturated with sweat , making it harder for more sweat to evaporate , and slowing the rate that you cool down . The moving air coming from the fan allows that sweaty air to be recycled , keeping the evaporation of your sweat going , and cooling you further .
The air is n't cooler . The ability of the air to reduce your temperature due to evaporation is improved because your skin contacts more air . The ability to radiate heat also improves because the heat can be transferred into a larger volume of air . This is why your PC probably has a fan , even though it does n't rely on evaporative cooling .
rejected: When you use a fan, it doesn't actually make the air warmer. Instead, it makes the air feel warmer by increasing the airflow over your skin. When the air is moving, it can more easily carry away heat from your body, which makes you feel cooler. This is why using a fan can be helpful on a hot day. However, if the air coming from the fan is already cool, it will still feel cool when it reaches your skin, even if the fan is blowing.

query: If the deficit has been cut in half , why does the debt ceiling need to be raised ? . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Deficit = the amount we are borrowing in a particular year . Debt = the cumulative amount of we 've borrowed Even though the deficit is shrinking , debt is still going up . Just a slower pace than previously .
Picture it this way ( rough numbers from Wikipedia for 2012 ) ~US debt : $ 16 Trillion ~Expenditures : $ 3.5 Trillion ~Revenue : $ 2.5 Trillion ~Deficit : $ 1 Trillion So Since the government spent roughly $ 1 Trillion more than it brought in through taxes and stuff , the debt went up by $ 1 Trillion ( So next year we owe ~$17 Trillion ) If next year , the deficit is cut in half , that means we 're spending around $ 3.0 Trillion and bringing in $ 2.5 Trillion , so the Deficit that year is " only " $ 0.5 Trillion . The total debt still goes up to $ 17.5 Trillion . If the Debt ceiling is $ 17.4 Trillion , you 're in trouble .
A lower deficit is still a deficit , and money is still being borrowed to cover that budget shortfall . There 's really a lot more to it , of course , and that 's just a first approximation . Even if the deficit were reduced to zero , there 'd still be a lot of borrowing going on .

query: why does plastic degradation take so long ? As far as I know , plastic consists of hydrogen and carbon elements , which are very common in organic chemistry and living things in general . So how come it is so bad for the environment ? Thanks . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
A quick google : URL_0 Things organically break down because organisms break them down , stuff like bacteria or fungi . These have evolved to break down certain things and ignore certain other things - just like how people have evolved to eat fruits and meats , not wood or metal . We evolved this way because we chose what was abundant , but also what was easy to digest . You eat to gain energy , so you do n't want to * spend * energy just to get it . Plastics are made of extremely strong molecular bonds . These are extremely rare in nature ( so evolution would rarely know of it ) and extremely tough to break ( so organisms would prefer other things ) TL;DR : Because plastics are man - made and there is n't anything that eats plastic .
Before a material ( like plastic , wood , rust , protein , etc . ) can turn into something else , it must acquire the energy to do so . This is called " activation energy " . Transformations that occur quickly ( like how vinegar and baking soda become water and carbon dioxide ) have low activation energies ; basically , all the energy that is needed is already in the molecules . Transformations that occur slowly ( like the degradation of plastic ) have high activation energies . This , of course , then provokes a question about what causes different materials to have different activation energies . The answer to that is that the atoms in different materials have bonds that are stronger or weaker . The bonds between two carbon atoms and between a carbon atom and a hydrogen atom are extremely strong .
Plastic is made from petroleum . The most common petroleum component found in most plastics is propylene ( or propene ) . It 's a molecule made of 3 carbon atoms and 6 hydrogen atoms . When heated up , propylene forms bonds with other propylene and turns into long chains called polypropylene . The reason things decompose is that micro - organisms like bacteria have evolved naturally to eat certain things . Food , clothing , stuff like that . Simple stuff made of easy to break down molecular chains . Polymer chains ? Not so much . Bacteria have no idea what to do with it . They did n't evolve to eat it because it takes more energy to do so than it does " natural " organic compounds and there are plenty of other , easier things to eat and thrive on . In recent times , bacteria have been discovered that eat some petroleum products and plastics .
rejected:
Plastic is made from long chains of molecules called polymers, which are made up of hydrogen, carbon, and sometimes other elements like oxygen, nitrogen, or chlorine. These polymers are very stable, which means they don't break down easily. That's why plastic doesn't rot or spoil like food does.

But the fact that plastic doesn't break down easily is also what makes it a problem for the environment. When we throw away plastic, it can end up in landfills or the ocean, where it can take hundreds or even thousands of years to break down. During that time, the plastic can cause problems for animals and plants. For example, animals can get caught in plastic bags or mistake plastic for food and eat it, which can make them sick or even kill them.

There are some ways to make plastic break down faster, such as by adding special chemicals or by breaking it down with heat or light. But these methods are not always effective, and they can also have their own negative effects on the environment. That's why it's important to try to reduce our use of plastic and recycle it when we can, so that it doesn't end up in landfills or the ocean where it can cause problems.

query: How you can make an SOS call without phone service ? If you have no service on your phone how are you still able to call emergency numbers ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Just because you do not have service with your particular carrier , it does not mean that you are not in the coverage of another service network . By law , all cell phone carriers are required to allow emergency calls to be made on their networks regardless of the phone 's primary carrier . Edit : Gold for this ? Seriously ? This is n't even rocket science and somebody thinks this is gold worthy ? Goddammit , this is not the sort of comment I wanted to lose my Gilded - Virginity with .
When you turn your phone on , it attempts to connect to a carrier ( cell phone company ) . Which carrier it chooses is based on a list on the SIM card . If you do n't have a SIM card , the phone chooses the strongest carrier and electronically says " hello , I 'm here " . All carriers keep track of the phones that try to connect to it . Some will allow certain numbers through , like 611 . Others will redirect every call you make to their customer service department . But every one , as mandated by law in North America , must allow a 911 call to go through to the 911 operator in that area . This is why a lot of phones will show " SOS " or " Emergency calls only " instead of No Service .
Also , every BS - base station ( cell tower / antenna ) has a limited number of channels . Every cellphone has to simultaneously send and receive voice / data . That is called full - duplex communication . Now , for example if every single channel is busy you would have no way of placing a call or sending sms / data , because there is no physical possibility . That is why emergency calls have priority and when the BS receives an emergency call , it immediately drops any other calls / data to make your call possible . Source - telecom engineer p.s . I could go for days on awesome stuff in telecommunications
rejected: An SOS call is a type of emergency call that is used to signal that you are in a life-threatening situation and need immediate help. If you have no phone service, there are still a few ways you can make an SOS call: 1. Use a satellite phone: A satellite phone is a special type of phone that can make calls using satellite signals, rather than relying on phone service from a cell tower. Satellite phones can be used in areas where there is no phone service, and can be used to make SOS calls. 2. Use a two-way radio: A two-way radio is a device that uses radio waves to communicate with other radios. Some two-way radios have the ability to call emergency services, and can be used to make an SOS call if you have no phone service. 3. Use a landline phone: If you are in a building or other location that has a landline phone, you can use that phone to make an SOS call. Landline phones do not rely on cell phone service, and can be used to call emergency services even if you have no phone service on your cell phone. 4. Use a public phone: Many public places, such as gas stations and airports, have public phones that you can use to make an SOS call. These phones do not require phone service and can be used to call emergency services. 5. Use a medical alert device: Some medical alert devices, such as those worn as a bracelet or pendant, have the ability to call for help in an emergency. These devices are often equipped with a button that can be pressed to call for help, and can be used to make an SOS call if you have no phone service.

query: The relationship between HTML , CSS , PHP , Javascript etc I know all of these are involved in websites , but have no idea how , or what they do . There 's probably some other important stuff I 've missed out too . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I do n't want to pretend that you are 5 years old , but I will try to explain it on very basic level . There is a computer somewhere that is connected to the internet all the time . The programmer writes * * PHP * * code that will generate * * HTML * * code(it can generate all kind of output actually , but most of the time it is used to generate HTML ) and puts it on that computer . Your browser download that generated HTML code along with CSS and JavaScript code . HTML is basically definition of how the page will look . It has descriptions like " Put a box with the following text on the far left corner and put a picture next to it " . It also tells your browser which CSS and Javascript files to download . * * CSS * * is the description about the look of these things described in the HTML code . For an example you can tell that box on the left corner should be 120px wide and have yellow background . the * * JavaScript * * is responsible for the actions of these things described in the HTML . For an example , using JavaScript you can make that box described previously to move to the right corner when clicked . So in a nutshell , PHP generates HTML , HTML describes what you will have on the page and how they are organized , CSS describes how these things will look like and JavaScript controls how they will act .
Okay , for a five - year - old : Your web browser requests a page from a web server . The server runs PHP code ( or any other similar language ) , which returns other code to your browser . Your browser then reads this code : - HTML describes a web page 's information - what text , images , etc . are on the page - CSS describes how the information should look on the page - color , size , etc . - Javascript controls actions on the page , and anything else dynamic that your browser can do ; this can include things like tracking time , doing something when a button / link is clicked , updating the page 's code or style , or a lot of other things .
HTML is the wood frame of a house . CSS is the curtains , carpet , and other styled elements . PHP is the landlord that you rent the house from . JavaScript is what you do in the house .
rejected: Sure! Here's a brief overview of the role that each of these technologies plays in building websites: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard markup language for creating web pages. It's used to structure and format the content of a website, and to define how the content is structured. CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is a style sheet language used for describing the look and formatting of a document written in HTML. It's used to control the appearance of web pages, including colors, fonts, and layout. PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor) is a programming language that is often used to build dynamic websites. It allows developers to create web pages that can interact with a database, and can generate content on the fly based on user input or other factors. JavaScript is a programming language that is commonly used to build interactive elements on websites. It allows developers to create things like drop-down menus, image sliders, and form validation. There are many other technologies that are commonly used to build websites, such as databases, content management systems, and frameworks. But these are the main ones that you'll encounter most frequently. I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.
