query: What does the conductor of an orchestra actually do ? Would a live orchestra sound noticeably different to an untrained ear without a conductor ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I 've been in several band programs throughout the years . The conductor 's job in its most basic form is to keep time . However , a good conductor does much more than that . They can help with dynamic ( volume ) changes , and many other things that attribute to the overall emotion of the piece .
Before the conductor comes to rehearse , they study the work to be performed , in its context of other pieces written around the same time , or by the same composer . They form a mental image of how they imagine this piece should sound . They then inspire , work with , and lead the orchestra into performing the music to get as close as possible to this . The conductor makes many decisions during preparation and rehearsals , for example , the speed ( tempo ) that a piece is played at , and whether that should fluctuate or change at various points during the piece . They will listen to the orchestra to make sure that the balance ( respective volume ) of the different instruments matches what he or she wants . Occasionally there may be notes that are misprints in the music , or players just play the wrong notes for whatever reason , but the conductor needs to be able to hear these mistakes and fix them . Conductors also indicate to players when to play . This is called cueing , and players do n't necessarily need it , as they have the music in front of them , but by and large orchestral players appreciate cues from the conductor as it confirms that they are playing in the right place , or in the right manner . The conductor also tries to describe at rehearsal ( either by words or by gestures ) the mood or feeling or phrasing of each part of the piece , so that the musicians understand what he or she is trying to achieve . For example if any notes are being played too loudly or too softly , or too aggressively , or too fast or slow , and so on . Depending on the orchestra in question , the conductor may have to make judgement calls on what to rehearse and what to leave . Many orchestras , even large professional ones , have limited rehearsal time , so the conductor has to make the best use of what time he or she has . The undefinable factor is the way each conductor inspires and leads the musicians of an orchestra ( the " beast with 80 or 100 heads " as one conductor put it ) and to get them to play the piece of music in * * the * * way which the conductor feels best communicates the emotion and beauty of the music to the audience . It is as much psychology and dealing with people ( all the members of the orchestra have their * own * ideas of how a piece should be played ) as it is about musical skill or talent . As throwawaythefourth said , an orchestra which had rehearsed with a conductor but played in performance without one , would sound a lot different than one which never had a conductor to begin with . As an aspiring conductor , I find I learn a lot by watching conductors rehearse . There are things which you ca n't pick up just from watching performances . This is just off the top of my head , feel free to ask more detailed questions if you want . * * tl , dr :* * its complicated . ( edited for clarity )
Orchestra musician here . The conductor of an orchestra usually stands at the front of the orchestra and keeps the time of the piece which the orchestra is performing by waving their hand with the tempo of the piece . That way , the musicians can watch the conductor and always know what beat of the measure they are on , this is n't always necessary , but on tricky pieces it becomes very helpful . The conductor also stands to conduct fermatas . A fermata is when a note is held out as long as the conductor likes it to be . ( Think of the ending of a loud orchestral soundtrack where the last note is held out a really long time . That is the conductor telling the orchestra to hold the note until they out their hands down . ) the conductor also tells the orchestra what to do with dynamics ; loud and quiet , soft or impactful . Also , it is kind of a visual thing to see a conductor in front of a performing orchestra . Not completely necessary , but it looks better . And to answer your other question , depending on the skill level of the orchestra and the difficulty of their piece of music , a good orchestra would not sound significantly different without a conductor except on fermatas where every musician would have to listen to when the other musicians ended their held out notes . Hope this answers your question ! Edit : words .
rejected: The conductor of an orchestra is the leader of the musicians. They stand in front of the orchestra and use their hands and arms to give the musicians cues about when to start and stop playing, and how fast or slow to play the music. The conductor also helps the musicians to play together in time and in tune with each other. An orchestra without a conductor might still be able to play the music, but it might not sound as good because the musicians would not have a clear leader to follow. The music might not start and stop at the right times, and the different instruments might not play together as smoothly. A conductor helps to keep the orchestra sounding polished and professional.

query: As a parent of a high school student, what should my short-term cash policy be to optimize my college costs?
chosen: There is no simple answer to your question.  It depends on many things, perhaps most notably what college your daughter ends up going to and what kind of aid you hope to receive. Your daughter will probably fill out the FAFSA as part of her financial aid application.  Here is one discussion of what parental assets "count" towards the Expected Family Contribution on the FAFSA.  You can find many similar pages by googling.  Retirement accounts and primary residence are notable categories that do not count.  So, if you were looking to reduce your "apparent" assets for aid purposes, dumping money into your mortgage or retirement account is a possibility. However, you should be cautious when doing this type of gaming, because it's not always clear exactly how it will affect financial aid.  For one thing, "financial aid" includes both grants and loans.  Everyone wants grants, but sometimes increasing your "eligibility" may just make you (or your daughter) eligible for larger loans, which may not be so great. Also, each college has its own system for allocating financial aid.  Individual schools may ask for more detailed information (such as the CSS Profile).  So strategies for minimizing your apparent assets that work for one school may not work for others.  Some elite schools with large endowments have generous aid policies that allow even families with sizable incomes to pay little or nothing (e.g., Stanford waives tuition for most families with incomes under $125,000). You should probably research the financial aid policies of schools your daughter is interested in.  It can be helpful to talk to financial aid advisors at colleges, as well as high school counselors, not to mention general financial advisors if you really want to start getting technical about what assets to move around.  Needless to say, it all begins with talking with your daughter about her thoughts on where to go.
rejected:
There are a few things you can do to try to optimize the costs of college for your student:Start saving as early as possible: The earlier you start saving for college, the more time you have for your savings to grow through compound interest. This can help you to save more overall, which can reduce the amount of money you need to borrow or pay out of pocket.Research financial aid options: Many colleges and universities offer financial aid to help students pay for their education. This can include grants, scholarships, and loans. Research the financial aid options available at the colleges your student is interested in attending, and consider applying for as many as possible.Consider attending a community college: Community colleges often have significantly lower tuition costs than four-year colleges and universities. Your student could attend a community college for the first two years and then transfer to a four-year school to complete their degree, which could save you a significant amount of money.Look into work-study programs: Work-study programs allow students to work part-time on campus in exchange for a reduction in their tuition costs. This can be a good way for your student to help offset the cost of their education while also gaining valuable work experience.Consider taking out loans: If you don't have enough savings to cover the full cost of college, you may need to borrow money to help pay for it. There are a variety of loans available to help students pay for college, including federal student loans, private student loans, and parent loans. Be sure to research the different types of loans available and compare the terms and conditions before deciding which one is right for you.
As a parent of a high school student, it's important to start planning and saving for college costs as early as possible. While it's not possible to predict exactly how much college will cost in the future, there are some steps you can take to help manage the costs:Start saving early: The earlier you start saving for college, the more time you have for your savings to grow. Consider opening a 529 savings plan or another tax-advantaged college savings account.Research financial aid options: There are many different types of financial aid available to help cover the cost of college, including grants, scholarships, and loans. Research the options available to you and your student to help cover the cost of tuition, fees, and other expenses.Consider attending a community college for the first two years: Attending a community college for the first two years of college can be a more affordable option, as community colleges typically have lower tuition costs than four-year colleges and universities. After completing an associate degree, your student can transfer to a four-year college or university to complete their bachelor's degree.Look into tuition reimbursement programs: Some employers offer tuition reimbursement programs for employees who are pursuing a degree related to their job. If you or your spouse work for a company that offers this benefit, it could help offset the cost of tuition.Shop around: It's important to compare the costs of different colleges and universities to find the best value for your money. Consider factors such as tuition costs, fees, and the availability of financial aid when making your decision.By following these steps and being proactive about saving and planning for college costs, you can help manage the expenses and make the most of your short-term cash policy.

query: List of Investments from safest to riskiest?
chosen: I think your premise is slightly flawed. Every investment can add or reduce risk, depending on how it's used. If your ordering above is intended to represent the probability you will lose your principal, then it's roughly right, with caveats. If you buy a long-term government bond and interest rates increase while you're holding it, its value will decrease on the secondary markets. If you need/want to sell it before maturity, you may not recover your principal, and if you hold it, you will probably be subject to erosion of value due to inflation (inflation and interest rates are correlated). Over the short-term, the stock market can be very volatile, and you can suffer large paper losses. But over the long-term (decades), the stock market has beaten inflation. But this is true in aggregate, so, if you want to decrease equity risk, you need to invest in a very diversified portfolio (index mutual funds) and hold the portfolio for a long time. With a strategy like this, the stock market is not that risky over time. Derivatives, if used for their original purpose, can actually reduce volatility (and therefore risk) by reducing both the upside and downside of your other investments. For example, if you sell covered calls on your equity investments, you get an income stream as long as the underlying equities have a value that stays below the strike price. The cost to you is that you are forced to sell the equity at the strike price if its value increases above that. The person on the other side of that transaction loses the price of the call if the equity price doesn't go up, but gets a benefit if it does. In the commodity markets, Southwest Airlines used derivatives (options to buy at a fixed price in the future) on fuel to hedge against increases in fuel prices for years. This way, they added predictability to their cost structure and were able to beat the competition when fuel prices rose. Even had fuel prices dropped to zero, their exposure was limited to the pre-negotiated price of the fuel, which they'd already planned for. On the other hand, if you start doing things like selling uncovered calls, you expose yourself to potentially infinite losses, since there are no caps on how high the price of a stock can go. So it's not possible to say that derivatives as a class of investment are risky per se, because they can be used to reduce risk. I would take hedge funds, as a class, out of your list. You can't generally invest in those unless you have quite a lot of money, and they use strategies that vary widely, many of which are quite risky.
rejected: Here is a list of investments from safest to riskiest:Savings accountsCertificates of deposit (CDs)Money market accountsTreasury bondsCorporate bondsMutual fundsIndex fundsExchange-traded funds (ETFs)Municipal bondsReal estatePrecious metalsStocksOptionsCryptocurrenciesThis is just a general list and the actual risk of an investment will depend on a variety of factors such as the stability of the issuer, the market conditions, and your personal risk tolerance. It's important to diversify your portfolio and consider seeking professional financial advice before making any investment decisions.

query: How can someone invent a new math ? What does the math do ? Coming from someone who struggled with maths at school , is n't maths numbers and equations ? How can you invent a new number ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Mathematics is all about representing the world around us . Here 's an example of how a whole new branch of math was invented : Study the symmetry of objects . Then figure out all the different possible ways you can achieve symmetry , rotational symmetry , reflections , etc . Then you turn those symmetries into elements which comprise groups . That 's the basis for group theory . So you 're not inventing new numbers , you are inventing new ways of thinking about certain concepts . For example , we can model the trajectory of a projectile using a parabola , but we are n't born with that knowledge . Someone had to look at the world around us and imagine that motion could be described with numbers and symbols . And someone else imagined that symmetry can be described with numbers and symbols . And so forth .
What is a " number " ? No , really . That 's not a rhetorical question . In the ancient world , a ' number ' would have just been a counting number : 1 , 2 , 3 ... and so on . The Greeks added fractions : 1/2 , 2/3 , 7/4 . It was n't until later that they realized that was n't enough - you need things like the square root of two . And even later , it was found that even * that * is n't enough - not even operations like roots will give you the entire number line . But math is n't just about things we can point to on a number line . We 're interested in behaviors ( " how big a population do you need for X species not to go extinct ? " ) , patterns ( " how many colors do you need to color a map ? " ) , strategies ( " what 's the least moves you can take to solve a Rubik 's Cube from any position ? " ) , and so on . Each of these have fields of math associated to them , and anytime we find a new kind of object , we either ( a ) figure out that it fits into an existing field or ( b ) create a new field to deal with it .
That 's like asking how can anyone write a new story , is n't it all just words ? The arithmetic you did in school is only a tiny piece of math . Most of the research these days is in areas like Number Theory , which form the foundation of cryptography on the Internet , not in your basic arithmetic or algebra .
rejected: Inventing new math is a very creative process that involves coming up with new ideas and concepts, and then finding ways to express those ideas using mathematical language. This can involve coming up with new symbols and rules for manipulating those symbols, as well as new ways of thinking about and understanding mathematical concepts. One way to invent new math is to start with a problem or question that you want to solve, and then try to find a mathematical way to solve it. For example, if you wanted to figure out how to measure the area of a circle, you might start by drawing a circle and trying to come up with a formula for calculating its area. Another way to invent new math is to start with a set of ideas or concepts that you find interesting or useful, and then try to find ways to express those ideas using math. For example, if you were interested in the concept of infinity, you might try to come up with a mathematical way to describe and understand it. So, to answer your question, math is not just numbers and equations, it is also a way of thinking and a way of understanding the world around us. And just like people can invent new words or ideas in other fields, they can also invent new math to help them better understand and describe the world we live in.

query: Tax treatment of dividends paid on short positions
chosen: In the USA there are two ways this situation can be treated. First, if your short position was held less than 45 days. You have to (when preparing the taxes) add the amount of dividend back to the purchase price of the stock. That's called adjusting the basis. Example: short at $10, covered at $8, but during this time stock paid a $1 dividend. It is beneficial for you to add that $1 back to $8 so your stock purchase basis is $9 and your profit is also $1. Inside software (depending what you use) there are options to click on "adjust the basis" or if not, than do it manually specifically for those shares and add a note for tax reviewer. Second option is to have that "dividednd payment in lieu paid" deducted as investment expence. But that option is only available if you hold the shorts for more than 45 days and itemize your deductions. Hope that helps!
rejected: In general, dividends paid on short positions are treated as ordinary income for tax purposes and must be reported on your tax return. If you are a trader in securities and you elect to be taxed as a trader, you may be able to claim certain deductions related to your trading activity, including any dividends you receive on short positions. However, if you are not a trader, you may not be eligible for these deductions.It's important to note that the tax treatment of dividends paid on short positions may vary depending on the specific circumstances and the laws of the jurisdiction in which you reside. You may wish to consult with a tax professional or refer to the tax laws and regulations in your jurisdiction for more information on the tax treatment of dividends paid on short positions.

query: When does a ticket become a misdemeanor , and a misdemeanor become a felony ? I 've never known the criteria to see how a crime fits into a specific category . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Only criminal offenses are misdemeanors or felonies . That 's why you hear people say things like pot has been decriminalized . It 's still illegal , but not a criminal offense . We will get back to this as soon as I take care of the easy ones . A criminal offense , according to the Constitution , gives the accused a lot of rights . Among these are the right to an attorney , the right to a judge and jury , and the right to know what you are accused of . When you are accused of a criminal offense , you will usually hear your rights read to you . You will also have a mug shot taken and your fingerprints ran . All of your rights according to the Constitution are provided to you . The difference between a misdemeanor and a felony is nothing more than the maximum sentence you can get for the crime . Generally , a misdemeanor can get you a sentence of no more than one year in jail and a maximum fine . A lot of jurisdictions have some variances on the maximum amount of a fine and the maximum amount of jail time . But the thing to take from this is you ca n't get a life sentence for a misdemeanor . A felony , on the other hand , has no limits . If you commit a felony , you could get one or even multiple life sentences . You could even get the death penalty . The fine can be in the millions of dollars if the court finds the penalty appropriate . The thing to take from this is a felony has unlimited punishment within the limits of the law . Back to non - criminal offenses . The courts figured out a long time ago that there are a lot of things that people should n't be doing , but the courts did n't want to take a lot of time to clear these things up . You do n't get the rights specified in the Constitution ( though most courts will still respect your rights ) , and you do n't have to be proven guilty beyond a reasonable doubt . All that has to be shown is that a reasonable person would believe you did what you were accused of . Generally , the maximum penalty is a fine . Non - criminal offenses are a compromise , granted , but imagine if you had to sit on a jury for a bunch of speeding tickets .
In the US , there are three types of offenses : civil , misdemeanor , and felony . Note that " ticket " is n't a type of offense , it is just a means of documenting an alleged offense took place . Tickets are common in civil and misdemeanor offenses , and it is conceivable you could even be ticketed for a felony , thought it would be very rare . The level of offense determines which penalties are allowed and what rights you have . For example , you do n't have a right to a jury trial or an attorney for many civil offenses , where as to be tried on a felony , you must first be indicted . It costs more to prosecute and punish more serious crimes , to lawmakers have to consider the consequences when deciding which classification to use .
It 's decided by the legislature when they create the law making something a crime . You 'll find that the relevant codes ( like the United States Code , which defines federal crimes ) will be full of phrases like " Any person found to have eaten ice cream on horseback within the limits of Bumble Township shall be guilty of a misdemeanor of the fourth degree " , and then elsewhere a set of sentencing guidelines .
rejected:
In the United States, a crime is generally considered a misdemeanor if it is punishable by a fine or imprisonment in a county or local jail, rather than in a state or federal prison. Misdemeanors are typically less serious crimes than felonies, and they often carry a maximum sentence of up to one year in jail. Examples of misdemeanors include petty theft, simple assault, and driving under the influence (DUI).

On the other hand, a crime is generally considered a felony if it is punishable by imprisonment in a state or federal prison. Felonies are typically more serious crimes than misdemeanors, and they often carry a maximum sentence of more than one year in prison. Examples of felonies include murder, rape, and aggravated assault.

In some cases, the severity of a crime may be determined by the circumstances surrounding it. For example, a simple assault may be charged as a misdemeanor if it involves a minor injury, but it may be charged as a felony if it involves a serious injury or the use of a deadly weapon.

In general, the criteria for determining whether a crime is a misdemeanor or a felony depend on the laws of the state where the crime was committed and the nature of the crime itself.

query: What is Monsanto is and why its so bad . Ok Edit .rewording . Not sure how to change the title . What is Monsanto & what are the pros/ cons of what they do . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Monsanto is a company that develops pesticide resistant crops , and many similar types of products . It 'd be inappropriate for ELI5 to tell you " why they 're bad " , because that would be introducing our own bias . If others comment here as well I 'd encourage them to describe the things Monsanto does , without saying if those particular things are good or bad . OP can decide if those actions make Monsanto good or bad . Edit - meta - discussion removed below .
I 'll rephrase OP 's question : What are the reasons people think Monsanto is bad ?
Monsanto is a company that develops genetically modified / enhanced crops . This means these plants - like soybeans and corn - do special things that " normal " plants do n't . For instance , Monsanto is in the news a lot the past couple days for having soybeans which are are resistant to a weed killer ( putting weed killer down might unintentionally also kill off your soybean crop ) . I 'm going to try to not say whether the company is " good " or " bad , " but just clarify the issues at hand that has made them contentious during recent events . These special plants do n't come cheap , though . A soybean farmer , for instance , needs to pay three times the price for these special , weed killer - resistant seeds than if they bought just regular seeds . This cost largely gets transferred to the consumer , who has seen prices on soy - based products rising something like 325 % in the past few years . Moreover , Monsanto almost monopolizes the market on this sort of product - they own something like 90 % of market share . These are some reasons people find them bad . However , some people argue that a farmer can afford these more expensive seeds because it pays for itself . Using these more specialty seeds , the logic goes , you can expect higher yields than regular seeds . For instance , you could buy three lots of regular soybean seeds or , for the same price , buy one lot of these special soybean seeds ; the claim is that you 'll end up making more product with the special seeds from Monsanto . The Supreme Court recently made a unanimous decision regarding Monsanto 's soybean seed . See , Monsanto owns a patent for its technology ( plant patents are actually a " special " type of patent within the US Patent system ) . However , a plant is a " self - replicating technology " - a plant will produce more seeds , obviously , and those seeds , in all likelihood , will have the same properties as the original ones you bought from Monsanto . Thus when Monsanto sells seeds to farmers , they tell them that the contract prohibits them from using seeds that develop , forcing the farmer to come back the next year to buy more seeds from Monsanto , because otherwise a farmer would only ever need to do business with Monsanto once and that 'd put them out of business . Some people think this is akin to an " enslavement " to this company , while others recognize it as a need for Monsanto to stay in business . Without going into too much detail , the Supreme Court case involved a farmer using these " new " seeds ( he thought he found a loophole , basically , and would be able to use these special seeds without having to repurchase them from Monsanto ) . Typically , when you buy a product , you 're free to do with it as you wish . This is what 's known as " patent exhaustion . " The Court ruled that such patent exhaustion does n't quite exist in the case of these self - replicating technologies ( plants , specifically - they were careful to be very narrow in this ruling ) because while the technology itself may have replicated , it did so as part of the original , patented design , and thus the " new generation product " falls within the same bounds of that original patent . It is n't exactly the consumer using the patented product in the way he wants ; it 's part of the design . This means that Monsanto can remain profitable . Opponents to this ruling , which favors Monsanto , say that this violates one of the big goals of patents , which is to encourage further innovation . One generation is not exactly like its previous generation , even if that difference is slight . And this is one of the grounds for creating a patent - improving " prior art . "
rejected:
Monsanto was a company that produced agricultural products, including seeds for crops like corn, soybeans, and cotton. The company was known for using genetically modified organisms (GMOs) in its seeds, which are plants that have had their DNA altered in a laboratory in order to give them certain desired traits, like being resistant to pests or herbicides.

One of the main pros of using GMOs is that they can help farmers grow more food and increase crop yields, which can be especially important in areas where there is a lot of poverty and hunger. GMOs can also be helpful in reducing the amount of pesticides and herbicides that farmers need to use, which can be harmful to the environment.

query: Why are farmers poor or considered poor ? Should n't they be rich , as there are n't a lot of farmers but a lot of hungry people ? Explain like I'm five.

Right now the poorest people in the world are probably sustenance farmers , people who farm just enough ( if they 're lucky ) food for themselves . When industrialization took off people started leaving farms and their low output ( and thus low pay ) for jobs in factories . You can see this in the developing world now with massive migration from rural to urban areas ( all developed countries around around 75 - 90 % urban , in very poor countries it can be more like 20 % ) . At the sametime farming got better equipment , today , in the developed world , a small number of farmers can produce huge amounts of food using modern equipment . Thus we have the modern , more wealthy , farmer . In poorer regions , like much of sub - Saharan Africa ? Most people are still farming very inefficiently .

Another reason is that farming is often a labor-intensive job that requires a lot of hard work. Farmers may not have a lot of time to rest or take breaks because they have to tend to their crops and animals every day. This can make it difficult for farmers to make a lot of money, especially if they are only selling their products locally.

Finally, the prices that farmers receive for their products can sometimes be very low. This can make it difficult for farmers to make a profit, especially if they have a lot of expenses, such as the cost of seeds, fertilizers, and equipment.

Overall, while there may not be a lot of farmers, farming is not always a profitable or easy job, which is why some farmers may be considered poor.

query: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles question
chosen: You recognize expense when you sell the hot dog. When you pay for the buns you have inventory, which is an asset. When you sell the hot dog - you have cost of goods sold, which is the expense. Expense principle says that you recognize expense when you use the product. You use the buns when you actually sell the hot dog, not before. The matching principle is also honored because you recognize expense of the buns at the time of recognizing revenue of the hot dog.
rejected: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) are a set of rules, standards, and guidelines that companies and organizations use to prepare and present financial statements. These principles are based on the belief that financial statements should provide a fair and accurate representation of a company's financial position, performance, and cash flows.GAAP consists of a set of rules and procedures that have been developed over time by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and other professional organizations. These principles are designed to ensure that financial statements are consistent, transparent, and comparable, which helps users of financial statements, such as investors, creditors, and regulatory agencies, to understand and evaluate a company's financial performance and position.Some of the key principles of GAAP include:Relevance: Financial information should be useful to users in making decisions.Reliability: Financial information should be reliable and free from material misstatements.Comparability: Financial information should be comparable across companies and over time.Consistency: Companies should use the same accounting methods and principles from one period to the next.Materiality: Financial information should be presented in a way that is material to the decision being made by the user.Full disclosure: Companies should disclose all relevant information in their financial statements.GAAP is used to prepare financial statements for companies in the United States, although many other countries have similar sets of accounting principles. In the U.S., GAAP is considered the standard for financial reporting, and companies are required to follow these principles when preparing their financial statements.

query: Torque ... I know it allows vehicles to climb hills better , but what determines it and how does it actually work ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Think of torque as leverage . Lets say we have a stubborn nut and you have a wrench to loosen it , but the wrench is to short so you ca n't turn the nut . Your first instinct is to get a longer wrench and now you can turn the nut with applying the same force . This is because torque is a function of both the force you apply and how far away from the rotational axis ( The nut ) . Also the direction of the force is also important . To understand the direction think of a door . Lets say the door is open and you push the door on the edge ( so you are looking at the lock mechanism and such ) The door will not move , however if you are to push ( or pull in this example ) on the door knob you will rotate the door . So now we have 3 important aspects which define torque . The force applied , the direction the force is applied and how far the you are away from the rotational axis . Going back to the door , if you were to push on the hinges of the door , the door would n't open , but if you push on the door knob the door will rotate . So now Torque is a measurement of all three of these aspects , mathematically is it the cross product between the force and the distance from the rotational axis , The more force you apply the more torque , or the more distance between the force and the rotational axis , the more torque . * * TL;DR Torque is a measurement of rotational force via the force applied and distance , think of a door , you must apply your push or pull force away from the hinges to open it . The more force the faster the door opens , the larger the door the easier it is to open * *
Think of torque as strength , as in the power needed to move an object . To pick a 1 lb object off the ground one foot you will need 1 ft lb of torque . To pick a 10 lb object one foot off the ground you will need 10 ft lbs of torque . Horsepower is a way to describe how fast you can move that object . In engines it is a function of torque , defined as : ( Torque X RPM ) / 5252 Most car engine specs give you the bore and stroke of an engine , with bore being the diameter of the piston and stroke being how far the piston travels . In simple terms a longer stroke will develop more toque and lower horsepower at a lower rpm while a shorter stroke will develop more horsepower and lower torque at a higher rpm .
Easy ELI5 answer : Torque is basically the amount of leverage you have to rotate something . Physics answer : Torque = force X displacement ( force vector cross the displacement vector ) Everyday example : Open a door in your house . It is much easier to push near the handle opposite the hinge than it is to push near the hinge . However , if you push near the handle you have to move your hand further to move the door . If you push near the hinge , you only need to push the door a little to get the door to move very far , but you have to push harder . ( distance from the hinge is the displacement vector , the push on the door is the force vector )
rejected:
Torque is a measure of how much force is being applied to an object to make it rotate. It is usually measured in units of force times distance, such as Newton-meters (Nm).

In the context of a vehicle, torque is important because it determines how easily the vehicle can accelerate, or how well it can climb a hill. The engine of a vehicle produces torque, which is then transmitted through the transmission and drivetrain to the wheels. The wheels use this torque to rotate and push the vehicle forward.

Imagine you are trying to open a jar with a tight lid. If you use a long, thin wrench, you will have to apply a lot of force to the wrench to get the lid to turn. But if you use a shorter, thicker wrench, you will be able to apply the same amount of force with less effort, and the lid will turn more easily. This is because the shorter, thicker wrench has more torque than the long, thin wrench.

In a vehicle, the engine produces torque and sends it to the wheels through the transmission and drivetrain. The transmission changes the gears of the vehicle to adjust the amount of torque being sent to the wheels. For example, when driving up a steep hill, you might need to shift into a lower gear to increase the torque being sent to the wheels, which will help the vehicle climb the hill more easily.

This idea that we call " continuity " is important to those in favour of the monarchy . In the old days of course the King or Queen had was often a tyrant , but now we see them as a guard against tyranny . In the armed forces there is a big deal made of their loyalty being to the Queen . She or other royals always appear at a lot of military functions . The idea is that anyone wanting too much power in Britain would at some point have to confront the Queen , since she embodies our laws and democracy , and it would be that much harder to turn the army and the people away from her . This is also part of the reason all new laws are read out by the Queen in a special ceremony . Another job of the Queen is to provide a sense of identity , especially now that the British identity is quite up for debate with so many changes and new arrivals over the last decades . She appears at a lot of big cultural events as well as being the reason for others . I 've noticed visiting foreigners are always excited about the Queen , she must be a big part of our perceived national personality . The Royal Family is good for tourism . If Buckingham palace was just home to some President or Windsor Castle just a disused relic it would not have the same appeal . They are a direct link to the past . This idea is called " heritage , " and is part of continuity . Tourism is one of the reasons given for the Royals being economically justified . There is a fair bit of debate about whether they make money for Britain or not . There is a lot spent on security and ceremonies . Without getting too involved in the finances , they do not really need to " earn , " they have a lot of land and wealth and it is easy to make more money once you have a lot to begin with .
Taking the British monarchy , cos I know that one . Queenies ' does n't do much , but technically is able to . So , there 's the knighting , which she does herself , but the people chosen are done by a team of advisers and the like . Equally , whilst an old monarch ( Henry 8th style ) controlled the laws of the land , that is now done through politicians in the House of Commons , and those laws and things are passed onto the House of Lords and then at the top , the queen signs it off to go ahead . She does that for pretty much everything . It 's like when you get a small child to think he is controlling things , but you 're actually making him do what you want . Not that she is being used , but she is just redundant , and we get a lot of tourist money from her .
rejected:

query: What is a citizen 's arrest and how / when to perform one ? When should you and how would you go about it ? Thanks ! Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
When should you ? Basically never . When * can * you ? Varies from state to state , but generally only when you actually witness someone committing a felony , or are instructed to do so by a lawfully appointed police officer . See , the thing is , cops have a bunch of laws that protect them if they screw up . If they arrest someone improperly , * at most * it 's a slap on the wrist . If you do it , it 's kidnapping . Just call the cops unless your life or someone else 's * depends * on you stopping this person before they get away .
Let 's say someone is robbing a store and you catch them , you have the right to detain them under citizens arrest . So you CAN do it when you see a crime in progress . NEVER DO IT . Here 's why : they will turn around and sue you for wrongful imprisonment , not to mention you may hurt the actual investigation into the crimes by the authorities or worse put others in danger .
I 've actually made a citizens arrest . I work security for a large business hotel chain . One night while walking the parking garage , I see a guy go to exit in his SUV . He went into the entrance lane . He stopped , backed up , and I turned when I heard his reverse beeper things going off . I saw him back into a car . He was going to drive off . I flashed him with my light , walked over and asked him if he knew he hit a car . He said no , and with his answer , I was able to figure out he was intoxicated . So I called my dispatch office and told them to call PD . I basically stalled the guy with some bullshit like " just hold on a sec , we 're checking to see if we can find the owner " ... The cops showed up , did a field sobriety test and breathalyzer , and arrested him . I signed a form saying I conducted a citizens arrest since PD did n't witness it . They had him drunk inside a vehicle tho , and CCTV video of him operating it , so it was a legit arrest . So I kept a guy from possibly hurting someone on the street that night . TLDR : I made a citizens arrest for DUI . Edit : proper word usaging
rejected: A citizen's arrest is when a person who is not a law enforcement officer arrests someone they believe has committed a crime. In some countries, it is legal for a citizen to make an arrest if they witness a crime being committed, or if they have reasonable grounds to believe that a crime has been committed and that the person they are arresting is responsible for it. To perform a citizen's arrest, you should first try to find a law enforcement officer and tell them what you have witnessed or what you suspect has happened. If a law enforcement officer is not available, you can try to detain the person yourself, but you should be very careful and try to stay safe. Here are some things to keep in mind if you decide to make a citizen's arrest: Make sure you are confident that a crime has actually been committed and that the person you are arresting is responsible for it. If you are not sure, it is best to let the police handle the situation. Do not use more force than is necessary to detain the person. You should not physically harm the person or use weapons. Stay calm and try to keep the situation under control. Do not get into an argument or confrontation with the person you are arresting. Try to get other people to help you, if possible. It is safer to have more than one person present when making a citizen's arrest. Call the police as soon as you can and let them know what is happening. They will be able to take over and handle the situation properly. Overall, it is generally best to leave the job of arresting people to the police. They are trained to handle these situations and are better equipped to do so safely. If you do decide to make a citizen's arrest, make sure you are confident that a crime has been committed and that the person you are arresting is responsible for it, and use only the amount of force necessary to detain the person safely.

query: Why do newspaper and magazine articles often use [ square ] bracketed words in quotes . I see sometimes , when a quote is published in an article , that square brackets are sometimes used on seemingly random words in the quote , what do they signify ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The brackets signify words that were n't part of the original quote , but which the editor added in for clarity . Like let 's say that they 're interviewing a baseball player about a game , and the player says " This was a great game , he was on fire today , " while pointing at his teammate . Well it might not be apparent to the reader who " he " is , so the quote might show in the newspaper as : " This was a great game , [ Rodriguez ] was on fire today . " That way they are n't misquoting anybody , but they 're making the quote clearer to the reader .
They are used to add a word that the author have added that was not actually said in the interview but that would make the quote easier to understand for the reader . Example : " I saw him perform in 1983 " does n't realy say anything unless you know the context it was said in so instead of printing the entire question asked : " Did you ever see Michael Jackson perform ? " , you can simply add [ Michael Jackson ] into the quote : " I saw him [ Michael Jackson ] perform in 1983 " .
They 're usually used in place of a word that , if used , would make the sentence harder to read , or the bracketed word is to help the sentence follow proper grammar rules . Let 's say I said " Maggie is a good girl , " then a newspaper might read " When asked about Maggie , he said " [ she ] is a good girl . " The bracket replaces " Maggie " with " She " to avoid repeating her name .
rejected: Square brackets are used in quotes to add information that was not originally present in the quote. For example, if someone is quoting someone else saying "I went to the store," but the person being quoted forgot to specify which store they went to, the journalist could add the name of the store in square brackets to give more context to the quote, like this: "I went to the [local grocery] store."Square brackets are used to show that the information inside them was not originally present in the quote, but was added by the person writing the article to provide more context or clarity. It helps the reader understand the quote better and know what the person being quoted was trying to say.

query: What happens if futures contract seller defaults?
chosen: MD-Tech answered: The answer is in your question: derivatives are contracts so are enforced in the same way as any other contract.  If the counterparty refuses to pay immediately they will, in the first instance be billed by any intermediary (Prime Broker etc.) that facilitated the contract. If they still refuse to pay the contract may stipulate that a broker can "net off" any outstanding payments against it or pay out using deposited cash or posted margins. The contract will usually include the broker as an interested party and so they can, but don't need to, report a default (such that this is) to credit agencies (in some jurisdictions they are required to by law). Any parties to the trade and the courts may use a debt collection agency to collect payments or seize assets to cover payment. If there is no broker or the counterparty still has not paid the bill then the parties involved (the party to the trade and any intermediaries) can sue for breach of contract. If they win (which would be expected) the counterparty will be made to pay by the legal system including, but not limited to, seizure of assets, enforced bankruptcy, and prison terms for any contempts of court rulings. All of this holds for governments who refuse to pay derivatives losses (as Argentina did in the early 20th century) but in that case it may escalate as far as war. It has never done so for derivatives contracts as far as I know but other breaches of contract between countries have resulted in armed conflict. As well as the "hard" results of failing to pay there are soft implications including a guaranteed fall in credit ratings that will result in parties refusing to do business with the counterparty and a separate loss of reputation that will reduce business even further. Potential employees and funders will be unwilling to become involved with such a party and suppliers will be unwilling to supply on credit. The end result in almost every way would be bankruptcy and prison sentences for the party or their senior employees. Most jurisdictions allow for board members at companies in material breach of contract to be banned from running any company for a set period as well. edit: netting off cash flows netting off is a process whereby all of a party's cash flows, positive and negative, are used to pay each other off so that only the net change is reflected in account balances, for example: company 1 cash flows netting off the total outgoings are 3M + 500k = 3.5M and total incomings are 1.2M + 1.1M + 1.2M = 3.5M so the incoming cash flows can be used to pay the outgoing cash flows leaving a net payment into company1's account of 0.
rejected: If a futures contract seller defaults, it means they are unable to fulfill their obligation to deliver the underlying asset to the buyer at the agreed upon date and price. This can occur if the seller does not have sufficient inventory of the underlying asset to meet the contract, or if they are otherwise unable to deliver the asset as required by the contract.When a seller defaults, the buyer can choose to either accept delivery of the asset at a later date or seek damages from the seller for any losses they incur as a result of the default. The buyer may also choose to close out their position in the contract by entering into an offsetting transaction with another party. This involves buying or selling a futures contract with the same terms as the original contract, but in the opposite direction, effectively canceling out the original contract.The consequences of a seller defaulting can be significant, as it can lead to financial losses for the buyer and damage to the seller's reputation. In some cases, the default may also trigger the termination of the contract and the liquidation of any related positions.

query: Why has technology advanced so much in the last 100 years compared to the previous 1000 ? What 's caused tech to advance so rapidly in recent times ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
A ton of reasons , but here are a few of the big ones : * Society began rewarding innovation . If you made carts in the 12 century and found a way to make wheels a bit faster , you could n't , as you can today , market that idea , spread it around , and make money off of it . You would likely just have continued making carts and the innovation would have died with you . * Certain innovations , like the ability to use chemical energy stored in coal , opened up a * ton * of avenues for technological advancement . Being able to use a steam engine to power things simply makes a lot of things possible that previously would be infeasible if they had to be powered by muscles . * Increased communication . A lot of these early innovations in the industrial revolution were organizational and centered around communication . When people get to cooperate more , the chance that they come up with new ideas greatly increases . * Many fewer people tied to the land and thus free to innovate . When 80 % of the population has to grow food ( and thus do n't have much time to innovate ) it puts restraints on technological growth .
Part of it is technology begets technology . By that , I mean as we develop better tools we can use those tools to make even better tools and so on . For example , even if we had the knowledge to make modern computer chips in the 1950s , we would n't have had the machines capable of working on the microscopic scale that they do now . We had to have rudimentary computers that could help make those machines first . Another part of it is the amount of people who are alive and able to do research . Right now there are about 7 billion people on earth . In 1750 there were under a billion . That means there are 7 times as many people able to do research now than there were about 250 years ago . Additionally , Before modern times only a small percentage of people in most civilizations were educated and an even smaller percentage of them pursued science and engineering . Nowadays a huge number of people from all sorts of backgrounds can get degrees and many go into the sciences .
We got better at the technologies that help us make new technologies . * Food and shelter got cheap and plentiful , allowing more people to be scientists , engineers , and scholars . * More and more of what we knew was written down and assembled into libraries , and was accessible to an increasingly literate public . * Better means of communication and transportation emerged , allowing new ideas to spread across the globe . * Legal protections and global markets meant more and more people could get rich off of good ideas .
rejected:
Technology has advanced a lot in the last 100 years because people have been working hard to create new things and make old things better. One of the main reasons technology has been able to advance so quickly in recent times is because of the internet. The internet is a huge network of computers that are all connected together and can share information with each other. This has made it easier for people to communicate and share ideas, which has helped drive the development of new technologies.

In addition to the internet, there have also been a lot of other important technological advancements in recent times. For example, people have been working on developing new materials, such as plastics and advanced alloys, which have allowed for the creation of new products and have made many things stronger and more durable. There have also been significant advances in fields such as medicine and renewable energy, which have helped improve people's lives in many ways.

Overall, the rapid advancement of technology in recent times is due to a combination of many different factors, including hard work, creativity, and the use of new tools and resources like the internet.

query: Money Laundering What is it , why is it illegal and what advantages to criminals gain from practicing the act ? Thanks ! Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Suppose you want to hire someone to do something illegal ( murder someone , steal something for you , whatever ) , but they want to charge you an obscene amount of money for it , say 50 grand . So , you go to your bank and withdraw 50,000 from your life savings . If the police or the IRS ever take a look at your bank activity , you 're going to have a very hard time explaining where that 50 K went , since you do n't exactly get a receipt from a hitman . Or , let 's say you offer a service for something illegal ( eg . you 're a hitman ) . When one of your customers pays you 50 K to kill someone , you ca n't just deposit that money in your bank account without raising a few eyebrows , and if someone ever asks you where you got that money from , you 're going to have a hard time explaining it . Either way , you 've got a fair amount of money either coming out of or going into your bank account , and no legal way to explain it . This is where a " front " comes in - a front is a business that is n't really what it appears to be . Let 's say you open an art store selling crappy homemade works of " art " for incredible amounts of money . One of your " customers " comes to you and asks you to kill someone for 50 K , so you sell them a work of " art " for $ 50,000 . This looks like a legitimate transaction , and both the customer and the front owner can claim it legally , but the 50 K was n't really for the artwork , it was for the hit job . The term money laundering comes from the fact that a lot of the early front companies were laundromats , charging money for " laundry " but actually performing other ( illegal ) services as well . The money goes in dirty , and comes out clean ( i.e. perfectly legal as far as the IRS can see ) .
Say you 're a high school chemistry teacher and made $ 10 Million in cash by cooking meth . Awesome ! Now what are you going to do with that $ 10 Million ? You could go eat an expensive meal , go out and buy some new clothes , maybe even go buy a new LCD TV ! Life is good ! But wait . You have $ 10 Million ... and all that stuff does n't cost a whole lot . So , you think big ... really big . You want to buy a new car and that $ 5 Million mansion across the street . You also want to put a couple million in the bank and invest it so that you can make even more money through your investments . After all , keeping $ 10 Million in cold hard cash up in your attic probably is n't a smart thing to do . But to do all this , you need to go to a bank and make a $ 10 Million deposit . After all , no one simply buys a house with a big bag full of cash . So you go up to the teller , hand her your big duffle bag of $ 10 Million and say that you 'd like to make a deposit . Easy peasy right ? Wrong ! A day later , the cops kick down your door and arrest you . They ask you where you got all that money . You say to them " I saved it up from working as a high school chemistry teacher all those years . " . " Prove it ! " they say . You ca n't . Now you 're in jail . What you could do instead , is launder the money . You could run a car cleaning business , and funnel your money through your business . Who cares if no goes to your car cleaning service , because on the record books , you could write down that you make $ 10k a day in business . Everyone just thinks your some rich guy cause your car cleaning business is doing well . So this time , when you go to the bank and deposit your cash , nothing is out of the ordinary . You 're just a businessman who is making a deposit from money earned from his business . Sure , you end up paying taxes and lose a little money from running the car cleaning business , but you now have laundered legitimate money . Money that you can now use to buy yourself a new car and a fancy mansion , and not have to worry about the cops asking where you got it from .
Money laundering is cleaning up dirty money . Dirty money , in this case , is money that you get for doing something wrong , or against the law . Let 's say that you managed to steel a bunch of candy bars from the vending machine guy at your grocery store . Somehow , you manage to take them , get away with it , and hide them from your parents . Oh no ! You got the ones with stupid peanuts in them . You ca n't stand peanuts so you decide to sell them to your friends , because you 're a clever business person . Situation one ( no laundering ): You sell the bars for a dollar a piece , and after they 're gone , you buy yourself a new toy that you 've wanted . Your parents notice that you 've gotten that toy , and they wonder where you got the money for it , or if you stole the toy . They ask around , and call your friends parents and bam . You were caught stealing either candy , money , or the toy . Situtation two ( with laundering ): You sell the bars for a dollar a piece but , ask your friends to only give you quarters . When you 're around your parents , you tell them that you 've been helping people with homework for a quarter and that the one day , you helped four people . You keep your quarters in a jar in the kitchen . The next day , you only helped two kids so you only put two quarters in ( and hide the other two until you can put them in ) . Eventually , you 'll have " helped " enough others that all of the money you actually made from stolen peanut candy will look like it came from your efforts to benefit your peers . Then you can buy your toy , and no one will suspect a thing . ---- Money laundering is the practice of making money obtained via illegal methods ( sale of drugs , sex , people , murder , etc ) look like it was obtained legally through a legitimate method , and to pay the taxes on the income , to avoid suspicion of the government . Once the government notices that you 've been buying Corvette 's and mansions but only reporting that you 're making $ 30,000 a year salary , you 're pretty quickly found out .
rejected:
Money laundering is the process of taking money that was earned through illegal activities, and making it seem like it was earned through legal means. It is illegal because it helps criminals hide the fact that they have earned money through illegal activities, and it allows them to use that money without being caught.

For example, let's say a person sells drugs and makes a lot of money from it. If they just put all of that cash in the bank, it would be obvious that they earned it through illegal activities. So instead, they might try to "clean" the money by buying something with it, like a house or a car. Now, if anyone asks where they got the money to buy those things, the person can say they saved up the money from their job or from investing. This makes it seem like the money was earned legally, even though it was actually earned through illegal activities.

Criminals gain several advantages from practicing money laundering. First, it helps them hide the fact that they have earned money through illegal activities, which makes it harder for law enforcement to catch them. Second, it allows them to use the money without attracting attention, since it seems like the money was earned legally. Finally, it allows criminals to keep their illegal profits instead of having to give them up to the government.

query: What is a “convertible note”?
chosen: Source, see if you have access to it Convertible notes are often used by angel investors who wish to fund businesses without establishing an explicit valuation of the company in which they are investing.    When an investor purchases equity in a startup, the purchase price of the equity implies a company valuation.  For example, if an investor purchases a 10 per cent ownership stake in a company, and pay $1m for that stake, this implies that the company is worth $10m. Some early stage investors may wish to avoid placing a value on the company in this way, because this in turn will affect the terms under which later-stage investors will invest in the company.  Convertible notes are structured as loans at the time the investment is made.  The outstanding balance of the loan is automatically converted to equity when a later equity investor appears, under terms that are governed by the terms set by the later-stage equity investor.  An equity investor is someone who purchases equity in a company. Example:- Suppose an angel investor invests $100,000 using a convertible note.  Later, an equity investor invests $1m and receives 10% of the company's shares.  In the simplest possible case, the initial angel investor's convertible note would convert to 1/10th of the equity investor's claim.  Depending on the exact structure of the convertible note, however, the angel investor may also receive extra shares to compensate them for the additional risk associated with being an earlier investor The worst-case scenario would be if the issuing company initially performed well, meaning that the debt would be converted into shares, and subsequently went bankrupt. The converted shares would become worthless, but the holder of the note would no longer have any recourse. Will twitter have to sell their offices and liquidate staff to close this debt? This depends on the seniority(priority) of the debt. Debt is serviced according to seniority. The higher seniority debts will be paid off first and then only the lower seniority debts be serviced. This will all be in the agreements when you enter into a transaction. When you say liquidate staff you mean sell off their assets and not sell their staff into slavery.
rejected: A convertible note is a type of debt instrument that can be converted into equity at a later date. Convertible notes are often used by startups to raise funding from investors. They are similar to traditional debt instruments in that they require the borrower to pay back the principal amount plus any interest that has accrued over time. However, unlike traditional debt, convertible notes do not have a fixed repayment schedule or a set maturity date. Instead, the repayment of the principal and interest is typically tied to the occurrence of a specific event, such as the company achieving a certain level of revenue or completing an initial public offering (IPO).Convertible notes also have an option for the investor to convert their debt into equity in the company at a predetermined conversion price. The conversion price is typically set at a discount to the company's valuation at the time of conversion, which gives the investor the opportunity to participate in the company's future growth.Convertible notes are attractive to investors because they offer the potential for a higher return on investment compared to traditional debt instruments. They are also attractive to startups because they can be easier to raise than equity financing, as they do not dilute the founders' ownership stakes in the company as much. However, there are also risks associated with convertible notes, as the value of the equity received upon conversion may be lower than the value of the principal and interest owed on the debt.

query: Why exactly are people shy ? I 'm guessing it 's because of a lot of things , but could be because of anxiety , which leads them into thinking / caring of what others think of them ? Please explain like I'm five.

There is many different types of " shyness " and many different reasons for why people may be shy in different ways . We as a society tend to think of shy people as X or Y. I 'm a fairly extroverted person , but I imagine that if I was introverted , I 'd feel fairly fed up with the generalizing perceptions of introverted / shy people . I do n't like idle conservation either . Talking about nothing is boring for both parties , I 'd rather sit in silence . People can be shy for a variety of reasons. Some people may be shy because they are naturally introverted, which means that they tend to feel more comfortable being alone or with a small group of close friends, rather than being in large groups or around new people. Others may be shy because they are anxious about social situations, and may worry about what others think of them or about making mistakes. 
Still others may be shy because of past negative experiences, such as being teased or bullied, which can make them feel self-conscious or afraid of being rejected. Some people may also be shy because of cultural or social norms that discourage them from speaking up or expressing themselves. 
Overall, shyness is a normal and common human emotion, and it's something that many people experience at some point in their lives. It's important to remember that everyone is different and that it's okay to be shy. With time and practice, many people are able to overcome their shyness and become more confident in social situations.

query: Why do dogs sleep so much I 've been spending more time at home , and all my dogs seem to do is sleep . Does their body need that much rest ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
In my view , the way to approach this is : why do they * not * sleep ? Or , when speaking of humans , why do * we * not sleep ? We do not sleep when we need to eat , work , go out , do things , maintain relationships , friendships etcetera . When we are not busy doing things we need to do , the best way for our bodies to preserve energy is sleeping . For humans , the things we * need * to do are now things like working so that we can buy things , but also social and/or entertaining activities . For dogs and cats , there is not that much they feel they need to do - everything is taken care of . And so they preserve their bodies and their energy in the way they best know how to : by sleeping .
I always felt that dogs tend to sleep a lot because when they are active they seem to be giving 100 % of their effort to whatever they are doing and that 's pretty tiring . When I play with my dogs they run as fast as possible , pull on the rope toy as hard as they can and in general use up a lot of energy . When they do n't want to play anymore they normally go to sleep for a little bit only to wake up and want to play again .
My dog only sleeps when I 'm on my computer ( or when I 'm sleeping ) , which I assume is because I 'm not active ( compared to chatting with family in the living room , working out , or playing games ) . Do you spend a lot of time on the computer or in other ' quiet ' activities ? They might not have anything to do but sleep during that time , unless you give them a toy or something .
rejected: Dogs sleep a lot because their bodies need rest just like ours do. When we sleep, our bodies and minds get a chance to rest and repair themselves, and the same is true for dogs. Dogs also sleep more than humans do, and this is because they are more active when they are awake. They use a lot of energy running, playing, and exploring, so they need more sleep to help them recover and get ready for the next day. So if you see your dogs sleeping a lot, it's because their bodies are working hard and need the rest to keep going!

query: Why are there so many personal injury lawyers ? Is it that profitable of a business ? Also , what happens when the clients lose the case ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Because a lot of people sustain personal injuries . They all need representation . In addition , it is much easier to get clients that way , because clients are not required to pay anything out of pocket . Most personal injury cases are handled on contingency basis making legal representation affordable even for poorest people .
Lawyer here ! It is incredibly profitable , * if you 're good at it . * There are plenty of grinders out there who honestly do n't do thrillingly well by attorney standards . But there are many who do quite well . In the U.S. , 90%+ of all personal injury ( PI ) cases are taken on a " contingency fee " basis , meaning that the attorney only takes a fee if you win . That fee is usually 33 % of the total recovery , plus expenses . In a * lot * of PI cases and related proceedings , the injured party often has the ability to recover attorney 's fees . This puts PI attorneys in a very enviable position — they can take big and small cases and make out like a bandit . If they win a big case , they take a massive cut . If they win a small case , they may be able to recover a " reasonable " fee for their time . ( Think $ 300-$400 / hour . ) And the whole time , they 're pursuing deep - pocket or insured defendants . By way of example , I recently settled a product liability case for $ 1.9M. ( Horrible fluke accident ; a woman lost her husband and suffered nasty burns herself , so I hardly begrudge the money . ) That 's $ 600k in his firm 's pocket right there , and the whole ordeal went on for about a year . And we were only one part of the puzzle — in products liability cases , you can sue the retailer of the product , the distributor , and all the way up the chain to the manufacturer / designer of the product . And each one of these parties has insurance money behind them . By the end of the day , I estimate that this one accident brought between $ 2.5 M and $ 3 M to that guy 's firm . On the other side of the coin , I lost a small - fries $ 10k case as a young pup . But the other side got fees of $ 24k or so . In the aggregate , it was probably 2 weeks worth of solid work for him .
Yes , yes it is that profitable . For the Ken Nugents and Jackson Masseys of the world , they have a small army of underlings who work for an injured person , and most times the case is settled out of court and the injured person is charged about 33 % of their recovery . In this case , the underling may spend a few hours of work and bring in 10k or more . Then you have the case where someone is hit by a commercial truck or something , think Coca Cola , Sysco Foods , some very large company , they sue , go to court and plaintiff gets awarded 3 Million dollars . Lawyer gets $ 1M. Plaintiff pays taxes on $ 3 M , plus $ 1 M to the lawyer , and is left with less than $ 1M. If I 'd have known how much personal injury attorneys made , I 'd have been one .
rejected: There are many personal injury lawyers because people get injured in accidents and need legal help to get compensation for their injuries. Personal injury lawyers help these people by representing them in court and negotiating with the other side to try and get a settlement. It can be a profitable business because if a lawyer is successful in getting a settlement for their client, they will usually receive a percentage of the settlement as their fee. If a client loses their case, it means that the court has decided that they are not entitled to receive any money. The lawyer will still get paid for their work, but the client will not get any money to compensate them for their injuries.

query: Why do cameras have circular lenses but produce rectangular images ? I have always wondered this and never seen this question asked before . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Lenses produce circular images . Film and sensors capture only a rectangular ( or sometimes square ) portion of it .
The CCD ( the device which captures the picture ) is rectangular .
Lenses pretty much have to be circular to avoid distorting the image . So why are n't the images circular ? It is simply because making things is easier when dealing with rectangular shapes . Back when each image was captured on a photographic plate , the unexposed plates were slid into a holder , and that 's easier to make if things are rectangular . Later , film was made in rolls , so dividing the rolls up into segments was the simple thing to do , and that makes rectangular image segments . Dividing them up into circular shapes would waste a lot of some of the most expensive parts of the process ... the film and photo paper . Now a lot of things are imaged digitally with special semiconductor sensors . Those are also made rectangular in shape , because that shape results in the least waste when manufacturing an Integrated Circuit . ICs are made many at a time on a disc of silicon called a wafer , and then later " diced " into individual components , with hundreds or thousands coming from one wafer . Cutting them into rectangles results in the least amount of waste . Plus it is difficult to cut silicon into round shapes efficiently .
rejected:
Cameras have circular lenses because it helps to capture light more efficiently. The circular shape of the lens allows it to gather light from all around and focus it onto the sensor, which is what captures the image. The reason why the resulting image is rectangular is because the sensor is rectangular in shape. The lens focuses the light onto the rectangular sensor, and the sensor captures the image in this rectangular shape.

Imagine shining a flashlight onto a wall. The beam of light is circular, but if you put a piece of paper in front of the beam, the shadow of the beam on the paper will be rectangular. It's the same concept with a camera - the circular lens gathers light and focuses it onto the rectangular sensor, which captures the image in a rectangular shape.

query: Should I pay off my car loan within the year?
chosen: First, don't owe (much) money on a car that's out of warranty.  If you have an engine blow up and repairs will cost the lion's share of the car's bluebook value, the entire car loan immediately comes due because the collateral is now worthless.  This puts you in a very miserable situation because you must pay off the car suddenly while also securing other transportation!  Second, watch for possible early-payment penalties.  They are srill lokely cheaper than paying interest, but run the numbers.  Their purpose is to repay the lender the amount of money they already paid out to the dealer in sales commission or kickback for referring the loan.  The positive effects you want for your credit report only require an open loan; owing more money doesn't help, it hurts.  However, interest is proportional to principal owed, so a $10,000 car loan is 10 times the interest cost of a $1000 car loan.  That means paying most of it off early can fulfill your purpose. As the car is nearer payoff, you can reduce costs further (assuming you cna handle the hit) by increasing the deductible on collision and comprehensive (fire and theft) auto insurance.  It's not just you paying more co-pay, it also means the insurance company doesn't have to deal with smaller claims at all, e.g. Nodody with a $1000 deductivle files a claim on an $800 repair.  If the amount you owe is small compared to its bluebook value, and within $1000-2000 of paid off, the lender may be OK with you dropping collision and comprehensive coverage altogether (assuming you are).  All of this adds up to paying most of it off, but not all, may be the way to go. You could also talk to your lender about paying say, 3/4 of it off, and refinancing the rest as a 12-month deal.
rejected: Paying off your car loan within a year can be a good financial decision, but it depends on your individual circumstances. Here are a few things to consider:Interest: If you have a high interest rate on your car loan, paying it off as quickly as possible may save you money in the long run. The longer you take to pay off the loan, the more you will pay in interest.Financial stability: Before you decide to pay off your car loan within a year, make sure you have the financial stability to do so. Paying off a car loan may mean you have less money available for other expenses or emergencies. It's important to have an emergency fund and to be able to cover your living expenses before paying off a loan.Other debts: If you have other debts, such as credit card debt or student loans, it may be more beneficial to focus on paying those off first. It's generally recommended to pay off high-interest debt before low-interest debt.Opportunity cost: Consider the opportunity cost of paying off your car loan within a year. If you have the money to pay off the loan, you may be able to earn a higher return by investing it instead.Ultimately, the decision to pay off your car loan within a year should be based on your own financial situation and priorities. It's a good idea to carefully consider the pros and cons before making a decision.
