rejected: Homosexuality is not necessarily "genetic" in the sense that there is a specific gene that determines whether someone is gay or straight. However, research has shown that sexual orientation, including homosexuality, can be influenced by a combination of genetic, environmental, and social factors. One way that scientists have studied the potential genetic influences on sexual orientation is by looking at twins. Twins are an interesting group to study because they share many of the same genetic characteristics. If one twin is gay, researchers can study whether the other twin is also more likely to be gay, compared to people who are not twins. Studies of twins have found that if one identical twin (who have the same genes) is gay, the other twin is more likely to also be gay than if the twins are fraternal (who have different genes like any other siblings). This suggests that there may be some genetic factors that can influence sexual orientation. However, it's important to remember that genetics is just one factor that can influence someone's sexual orientation. There are also many social and environmental factors that can play a role, such as the culture and community a person grows up in, their experiences and relationships, and their own personal identity and feelings. So, while there may be some genetic factors that can influence sexual orientation, it's not as simple as saying that homosexuality is "genetic" in the way that some physical characteristics, like eye color or height, are determined by specific genes.

query: What is the rate of return for a security when there is no risk-free rate (CAPM)?
chosen: For starters, the risk-free rate has nothing to do with stocks.  It would be independent of anything.  It pays out the same return in all states of nature.  The definition of a risk-free asset is that regardless of how the universe turns out, including a meteor striking the Earth killing everyone but the recipient, then the payout would happen exactly as planned.  One could imagine a computer still being on, connected to a power supply and printing a check.  Most people use the 90-day t-bill as the risk-free rate.  A beta greater than one implies it is more volatile than the market, not that it moves more perfectly. The CAPM should not be used for this.  Cryptocurrencies should not be used with this model because they have valuation dynamics related to the new issue of coins.  In other words, they have non-market price movements as well as market price movements. In general, you should not use the CAPM because it doesn't work empirically.  It is famous, but it is also wrong.  A scientific hypothesis that is not supported by the data is a bad idea.  My strong recommendation is that you read "The Intelligent Investor," by Benjamin Graham.  It was last published in 1972, and it is still being printed.  I believe Warren Buffett wrote the current forward for it.  Always go where the data supports you and never anywhere else, no matter how elegant. Finally, unless you are doing this like a trip to Vegas, for fun and willing to take the losses, I would avoid cryptocurrencies because you don't know what you are doing yet.  It is obvious from the posting.  I have multiple decades working in every type of financial institution and at every level, bottom to top.  I also have a doctorate, and I am an incredible researcher.  I am professionally qualified in three different disciplines. If you want to learn how to do this, start with the "Intelligent Investor."  Get a basic book on accounting and learn basic accounting.  Pick up economics textbooks at least through "Intermediate" for both microeconomics and macroeconomics.  Get William Bolstad's book "Introduction to Bayesian Statistics."  You will need them for reasons that go very far beyond this post.  Trust me; you want to master that book.  Find a statistician and ask them to teach it to you as a special topics course.  It will help you as both either a Marine officer or a Naval officer.  Then after that pick up a copy of "Security Analysis."  Either the 1943 copy (yes it is in print) by Benjamin Graham if you feel good about accounting, or the 1987 copy by Cottle under the Graham/Dodd imprimatur.  Then, if you are still interested in cryptocurrencies and they will be blasé by then, then pick up an economics textbook on money.  If I were you, I would learn about Yap money, commodity money, and prison money first, then you might understand why a cryptocurrency may not be an investment for you.
rejected: The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) is a model used to determine the required rate of return on an investment, given the level of risk associated with that investment. The required rate of return is the minimum return that an investor should expect to receive in order to compensate for the level of risk taken on by investing in a particular asset.The CAPM model is based on the premise that investors require a higher rate of return to compensate for the additional risk associated with an investment. This additional risk is measured by the asset's beta, which is a measure of the asset's volatility relative to the overall market.In order to use the CAPM model, it is necessary to have a risk-free rate, which is the rate of return that can be earned on an investment with zero risk. This rate is usually represented by the yield on a long-term, risk-free security, such as a U.S. Treasury bond.If there is no risk-free rate, it is not possible to use the CAPM model to determine the required rate of return on an investment. In this case, investors may need to rely on other methods to estimate the required rate of return, such as the discounted cash flow (DCF) model or the dividend discount model (DDM).

query: What to do with old company's 401k? [duplicate]
chosen: Your best bets are a Roth IRA or traditional IRA.  If you roll it to a Roth, you will have to pay taxes on the amount you roll over (unless it was a Roth 401k), however what is in the Roth will grow tax free and it will be tax free when you withdraw.  With a traditional IRA, you won't owe taxes on the money now but will pay taxes when you withdraw. You won't be able to withdraw this money until 59 1/2 years of age without paying a penalty, the same goes for your current 401k.  If you take the money (for mortgage, other investment, etc.) and don't roll it over to a qualified account, you will owe taxes on it plus a 10% penalty.  So you will only get between 60% and 70% of its value.
rejected:
If you have an old 401(k) account from a previous employer, you have several options for what to do with it:Leave the money in the old account: You can leave the money in the old 401(k) account if you are happy with the investment options and fees. However, you will not be able to contribute to the account or take out loans from it.Roll the money over into a new employer's 401(k) plan: If you are starting a new job and your new employer offers a 401(k) plan, you can roll over the money from your old 401(k) into the new plan. This allows you to continue saving for retirement and take advantage of any employer matching contributions.Roll the money over into an individual retirement account (IRA): You can also roll the money from your old 401(k) into an IRA. This gives you more control over your investment options and may offer lower fees than your old 401(k) plan.Cash out the account: You can cash out the account, but this is generally not a good idea as you will have to pay taxes on the money and may also be subject to an early withdrawal penalty if you are under age 59 1/2. Cashing out a 401(k) can also significantly reduce the amount of money you have saved for retirement.It's important to carefully consider your options and consult with a financial professional before making a decision about what to do with your old 401(k).
If you have left your former employer and have an old 401(k) account with that company, you have several options for what to do with the money in the account. Here are some common options:Leave the money in the old 401(k) account: If you are happy with the investments in the account and the fees are reasonable, you may choose to leave the money in the account. Keep in mind that you may not be able to contribute to the account anymore, and the account may not offer the same level of investment options as a new employer's plan.Roll the money over into a new employer's 401(k) plan: If you have started a new job and your new employer offers a 401(k) plan, you may be able to roll the money from your old 401(k) into the new plan. This can simplify your retirement savings and make it easier to manage your investments.Roll the money over into an Individual Retirement Account (IRA): If you do not have a new employer with a 401(k) plan, or if you prefer more investment options, you may choose to roll the money into an IRA. You can open an IRA at most financial institutions, and it offers a wide range of investment options.Cash out the account: If you need the money for other purposes, you can cash out the account. However, keep in mind that if you are under age 59 1/2, you will likely face a 10% early withdrawal penalty, in addition to any income taxes that may be due on the withdrawal. Cashing out an account is generally not a good idea, as it can reduce your retirement savings and result in a significant tax bill.Before making a decision, it is a good idea to consider the potential tax implications and fees associated with each option, as well as your long-term retirement savings goals. You may also want to consult a financial advisor for guidance on the best course of action for your particular situation.

chosen:
These would have to be really big asteroids to have any significant effect on the economy . In that case i d think the world would be a bit more concerned about getting slammed by some big asteroids , cause you know millions of deaths and ecological disasters and such .

It would n't matter because any meteor large enough to increase the known and speculative amount of gold on earth by 100x would be large enough to cause a mass extinction event .
rejected:
If several asteroids made of solid gold were to crash into Earth, it would likely have a significant impact on the global economy. The value of gold is largely based on its rarity, so if there were suddenly a lot more gold available, the value of gold would likely decrease. This would likely cause the price of gold-based investments, such as gold bars and coins, to fall.

Overall, the impact of such a large increase in the available gold supply would be complex and multifaceted, and would likely have both positive and negative effects on the global economy.

query: Why do Netflix and Amazon Prime / Instant Video have almost identical movie libraries ? Are n't they competitors ? Would n't they want to mix up the selection and have some exclusives , aside from TV shows ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
First mover disadvantage . Say you 're Netflix and you ' * invent * ' streaming commercial video . For your business to work you 've got to put in the leg work and sell all the movie / TV companies on the idea , figure out what kind of contracts they 're happy with and so on . I come along as Amazon Instant Video . I do n't need to do any of that , I just hire one of your employees who knows how it all works and I sweep on in behind you signing all the same companies you did without any of the effort . They already understand the concept and they 're willing to jump on board .. * you * made sure of that . So I quickly catch up and we 're equal in terms of library . I can either try and surpass you by putting more effort into getting all the as - yet - unconvinced companies ( Who you 'll mop up behind me ) * * or * * I can remain a little behind you and let you keep doing the convincing . Chances are I 'm not going to be magically 100 % better at convincing those not - yet - onboard than you are so whichever option I choose we 're * both * going to be moving forward at the same pace , the pace of convincing new people . There will be outliers here and there , obviously both will try things to differentiate themselves ( Netflix with Orange is the New Black , Amazon connecting with their existing services ) but the main thrust of the business is an obvious and finite resource . Get all the media companies on board . They both know exactly who 's left to join the party , neither is going to be pulling any surprises and getting 1,000 surprise hit movies nobody has heard of from some magical place .
Exclusives cost a LOT of money . It 's easier just to pay the minimum to get the movies / TV shows on their service .
I think that the people who own the content only give out rights to streaming certain movies . I guess Netflix gets exclusive rights to a couple of movies that Amazon does n't and vice versa . The rest can be streamed by anyone . Same goes for Vimeo , Crackle , Yahoo , Hulu , etc .
rejected: Netflix and Amazon Prime are both streaming video on demand (SVOD) services that offer movies and TV shows to subscribers. While they are competitors, they often have similar movie libraries because they both license content from the same studios and production companies. This means that they have the right to stream many of the same movies and TV shows. However, they do also have some exclusive content, which means that it is only available on their platform and not on any other platform. Both Netflix and Amazon Prime produce their own original content, which is exclusive to their platforms. This means that you can only watch these movies and TV shows on Netflix or Amazon Prime, respectively. Overall, both Netflix and Amazon Prime offer a mix of licensed content and exclusive original content to attract and retain subscribers. They are constantly looking for new content to offer their subscribers in order to keep them interested and coming back for more.

query: Why ca n't magnets be used to create a motor that never stops ? Several videos allegedly show how magnets can be used to create perpetual motion , but all comments say they 're hoaxes . Why is n't this possible ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Because a motor has to drive something else to move also , and in doing so the energy input that began the perpetual motion you describe gets dissipated . This causes that motion to slow and eventually stop . Edit : Just to add , most perpetual motion displays ( often toys ) use an object that does n't appear to stop spinning once it starts . What 's happening here is that the magnets are used to reduce the resistance to the objects turning as much as possible , so nothing slows it down . This does n't work for a motor because a motor that is not being resisted is essentially doing nothing .
So you 've got a wheel with magnets . And you have another , standing magnet that attracts a magnet in the wheel . Awesome , now your wheel starts to turn . Then the rotating magnet passes . What happens now ? It does n't just stop being magnetized . Now your magnets are trying to pull apart . The force required to do this is equal to the force that attracted them in the first place . Your system would simply reach an equilibrium point and stop . If you actually wanted to draw energy from the system to do * work * of any sort , it would reach that point faster . To get it away from the equilibrium point , you 'd have to adjust the magnets . That is to say , put energy back into the system through some other means . The reason these videos are hoaxes are because this final portion is always being hidden , or obfuscated in some way . They 're using blown air to move the device , or powering it with batteries , or turning something by hand , and so on . I suppose it 's not inconceivable that an honest person could also simply be ignorant enough of physics to be adding energy to the system unwittingly , and so not performing an intentional hoax .
A motor uses changing magnetic fields to move magnets around . However the magnetic fields of a static magnet does not change . So eventually it is going to stop in a certain orientation where its energy levels are the lowest .
rejected:
Magnets can't be used to create a motor that never stops because it would violate the law of conservation of energy. This law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.

In order for a motor to work, it needs to have a source of energy to power it. This energy can come from burning fuel, like in a car engine, or from electricity, like in a fan.

If you try to use magnets to create a perpetual motion machine, you would need to find a way to keep the motor running without any energy input. But this is impossible because energy cannot be created out of nothing.

Think of it like this: if you have a toy car that runs on a battery, the battery provides the energy that makes the car move. When the battery runs out of energy, the car stops. You can't make the car keep moving without replacing the battery or finding a new source of energy. It's the same with a perpetual motion machine – it can't keep going without a constant source of energy.

So, while it might be fun to watch videos of magnets being used to make things move, it's important to remember that these machines can't run forever without some kind of energy input.

query: What causes people ( recently highlighted young Muslims ) to become radicalized ? What is it that is so appealing that would cause them to leave what they grew up with and devote themselves to a life of such evil ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I do n't believe that it actually has much to do with religion when you look deep enough . These young men join these groups ultimately for the same reason poor young men here in the US join street gangs . Most of them ( I realize there are exceptions ) are quite poor and uneducated , with little to no prospects for a future . So they start to gravitate towards people who claim to offer something more . What is offered varies , but it 's mostly a sense of belonging to a particular group . Human beings are tribal creatures and most of us seek to belong to one " tribe " or another . The only differences come from what " tribes " are readily available . If this is too simplistic or just plain wrong , I apologize . I 'd love to hear from someone who has grown up beside young Muslims like this . Angry young men who are dissatisfied with the hand they 've been dealt are easily manipulated .
Well , to give you the ELI5 answer it would be education . There is a lack of education in the Middle East , which in turn affects the income of the average person . Eventually , when you 're stuck in poverty and your government ( Iraq ) , which is created by some other Western Democracy ( The U.S. ) , does nothing , you tend to blame the U.S. because they created your government , so you create a group of like - minded individuals , such as the Muhajadeen , you fight off the government , and you choose to fight in the name of your God . That 's how I view it .
[ Once again , time to post this ] ( URL_0 )
rejected: Radicalization is a complex process, and there is no single answer to what causes people, including young Muslims, to become radicalized. There are many different factors that can contribute to radicalization, and these factors can vary from person to person. Some people may be drawn to radical ideologies because they feel a sense of injustice or frustration with the world around them. They may believe that their religion or ideology offers a solution to these problems, and they may feel a sense of belonging and purpose by joining a group that shares their beliefs. Other people may be influenced by the propaganda and messaging of extremist groups, which can be very persuasive and manipulate people's emotions and beliefs. These groups may use social media, internet forums, and other online platforms to spread their ideology and recruit new members. It's important to remember that radicalization is not the same thing as religion. Most people of all religions are peaceful and do not advocate for violence or terrorism. It is a small minority of people who become radicalized and use their religious beliefs to justify violent actions.

query: Electronics In water with no power ? Do electronics stop working if you put them in water even if they are n't turned on / no battery Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
if you dry them thoroughly before you put the battery back or connect them to power , they will most likely be fine , so long as they were not in the water long enough for contacts / connections to oxidize . We used to wash electronics ( circuit boards ) in an ultrasonic cleaner using mostly distilled water and a small amount of detergent all the time as a means to FIX things , in fact . With a pretty good success rate , i might add .
Most likely , no . The reason why electronics break in water is due to short - circuit , or when water makes a bridge connection between places that are not suppose to touch . Say there 's line running 10V and another line running 1V , these lines are separate and will never touch . But when water makes a bridge from 10V to 1V line , it will blow up and ruin the 1V line . Imagine one of those high - voltage power line tower 's electricity going straight into your iPhone charger and you get the picture . So as long as this short circuiting does not occur , it wo n't break the electronics . But , it could also break if there are capacitive charges left as RhynoD suggested to provide that electricity for shorting or if there are some water soluble parts or something . Edit : An extra story added in the response
Pure water is n't a good conductor . Water with minerals dissolved in it ( salt ) which aid in the conductivity .
rejected: Electronics are made up of many small parts that are sensitive to water. When water gets inside the parts, it can cause them to short circuit, which means that the electrical current flowing through them takes an unexpected path and causes the device to stop working. Even if an electronic device is not turned on or does not have a battery, the water can still get inside and cause problems. So it's generally not a good idea to put electronics in water, even if they are not turned on.

query: How do military aircraft know they are being locked on ? When does the pilot know to try and evade any incoming attack , and how does the aircraft it self detect this and warn the pilot ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Former aircrew here , and have had missiles fired at my aircraft before . /u / GhostfromTexas is correct , but I may be able to offer some detail . There are a few types of " lock " that are possible , and each missile is typically focused on one . Each airframe 's specific countermeasures are different , but they all start with determining threat / danger . First , heat - seeking . This type has the missile look for thermal energy . Once the missile can ID a target above threshold and within range , it is considered " attempting to create lock " or " locking " . " Lock " is achieved when the missile has above X% chance of success , which is determined by software and differs based on mechanical characteristics . Some missiles at this point are still " passive " , meaning they are only receiving energy instead of broadcasting to find their target . The first step for " lock - prevention " is reducing heat signature . It 's very noticeable with the F-22 and F-35 . Achieving / maintaining " lock " for these requires you to be basically right behind the exhaust , and you can lose " lock " easily if they turn sharp enough to block line - of - sight . The second step is flares . These work by providing a bigger heat signature , i.e. a bigger target . The missile " bites off " on the flare , it ignores the airframe and follows the flare away , and it either explodes or runs out of fuel . The second major type used is " active radar " . This is where the missile sends out a " ping " that bounces off the enemy and back . " Locking " starts with the first " ping " received and becomes " lock " after a predetermined amount ( and above X% chance of success - all missiles base " lock " on probabilities ) . It follows the target by continually " pinging " and adjusting flight . " Lock - prevention " starts with stealth characteristics , which involve ensuring the " ping " travels anywhere except back to the source . Second , the " ping " activates a system that automatically processes threat / danger . Depending on the system , it may automatically deploy countermeasures or it may simply alert you . The last common method is " anti - radar " . Every airframe is broadcasting a signal of location / identification , commonly referred to as " Identify Friend / Foe " , or IFF ( Note : it may be called something else in civilian planes ) . For most civilian planes it is used for avoiding collisions and ensuring location . Military have an extra " Mode " used to denote " Blue Force " or " Red Force " , and is used to prevent friendly fire . " Anti - radar " missiles will " lock " onto the energy broadcast outward . They continually adjust as they receive more " pings " , and attempt to follow the " pings " back to you . For these , the first step in countering is stopping your broadcast if possible . No signal means no " lock " . A decoy is possible , but may be too expensive ( weight , money , material , etc . ) for aerial use . For all missiles , there are common limitations imposed : the specific frequencies are usually designed to not conflict with ATC radar , the " Area - Of - Responsibility " ( AOR ) is usually well removed from ATC radar locations , they have short range , and missiles are expensive . Relevant side note : ATC is n't tracking planes only by radar . They have two parts : tower and radar . Tower focuses on approach / departure / taxi and uses visual control , whereas radar focuses on planes mid - flight between departure and approach . Many places do n't have radar coverage , and deconfliction is determined on paper , then instructions are relayed to the pilot . Sorry if I missed something , but feel free to ask anything . EDIT : Here 's a previous post of mine about countermeasures . Another is after the reply , and has more detail . Hopefully it helps . URL_0 EDIT ( 2 ): Thank you to whoever gilded me . I appreciate it more than I can express , and I will pay it forward .
Basic answer is that the missile emits radar to track the target , and the plane has sensors to detect when radar is being blasted toward itself to alert the pilot
Radar quality more than anything . Normal garden variety traffic control radar is pretty low power and generally a rotating array ( the line sweeping in a circle you always see on tv ) . Think of it like a 30w light bulb in a lamp in a living room ... Fire control radars are usually fixed and higher power . A wide beam searchlight . Considerably brighter . The ones that shoot missiles at you very often have a secondary track ability , like a second spotlight as an auxilliary , with a narrow beam , to get a better " look " at you . Very advanced radars have TVM . Track via missile . A radar ( usually a fire control radar ) illuminates a target with a very intense beam , the laser of the radar world . The missile heads for that spot , just like a smart bomb locking on a spottered ground target . Some missiles have passive seekers , and just head for the dot . Some have no seeker at all and are guided by the radar using a steering uplink . Others have active seekers , and use their own on board mini radar to find a target and intercept . Really evil ones do all three at once . All of these different types of tracking have specific profiles , some " lock on " warnings are simply based on intensity and go off when they register a TVM or missile seeker signal aimed at them , others look for frequencies , intensity , TVM signal , etc . And determine status with an onboard computer . All of that gets WAY more involved of course :)
rejected: Military aircraft have systems on board called radar warning receivers (RWR) or electronic support measures (ESM) that can detect when another aircraft or surface-to-air missile (SAM) is using radar to track or target the aircraft. When the RWR or ESM system detects a radar signal, it can alert the pilot and display the direction and strength of the signal on a display in the cockpit. The pilot can then use this information to determine if they are being targeted and take appropriate evasive action if necessary. In addition to the RWR or ESM system, military aircraft also have other sensors and systems that can detect threats and provide information to the pilot, such as infrared sensors that can detect the heat signature of a missile launch, or a laser warning system that can detect when the aircraft is being illuminated by a laser. When the pilot is alerted to a potential threat, they may take evasive action such as changing the altitude, speed, or direction of the aircraft to try and break the lock or make it more difficult for the enemy to track and target them. The pilot may also use countermeasures such as flares or chaff to try and deceive the enemy's radar or missiles.

query: Simplifying money management
chosen: Personally, I have a little checkbook program that I use to keep track of my spending and balance. Like you -- and I presume like most people -- I have certain recurring bills: the mortgage, insurance payments, car payment, etc. I simply enter these into the checkbook program about a month before the bill is due. Then I can run a transaction list that shows the date, amount, and remaining balance after each transaction. So if I want to know how much money I really have available to spend, I just look for the last transaction before my next payday, and see what the balance will be on that day. Personally, I always keep a certain amount of pad in my account so if I made a mistake and entered an incorrect amount for a check, or forgot to enter one completely, I don't overdraw the account. (I like to keep $1000 in such padding but that's way more than really necessary, it's very rare that I make a mistake of more than $100.) In my case, I don't enter electric bills or heating bills because I don't know the amount until I get the bill, and the amounts fall well within my padding, and for just two bills I can factor them in in my head. BTW I wrote this program myself but I'm sure there are similar products on the market. I used to use a spreadsheet and that worked pretty well. (Mainly I wrote the program because I have a tiny side business that I have to keep tax records for even though it makes almost no money.) You could in principle do it on paper, but the catch to that is that when you write payments on your paper ledger in advance of actually writing the check, you will often be writing down payments out of order, and so it becomes difficult to see what your balance is or was or will be on any given date. But a computer system can easily accept transactions out of order and then sort them and re-do the balance calculations in a fraction of a second.
rejected: There are several ways you can simplify your money management:Create a budget: A budget helps you keep track of your income and expenses, and helps you make a plan for how to allocate your money. You can use a spreadsheet or budgeting app to create a budget.Automate your savings: Set up automatic transfers from your checking account to your savings account so you can save money without having to think about it.Use financial tools: There are many financial tools available, such as personal finance software, mobile apps, and online banking, that can help you keep track of your money and make financial decisions.Keep your financial documents organized: Keep all of your financial documents, such as bills, receipts, and statements, in one place so you can easily access them when you need them.Seek professional advice: If you need help with your money management, consider seeking the advice of a financial planner or advisor. They can help you create a financial plan and provide guidance on how to meet your financial goals.

query: Planes on fire ? If a plane is flying and caught fire would the wind smother the fire or make it larger ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Potentially yes , if the fire starts on a wing where high wind velocity could blow the fire away . But given that fuel , especially airplane fuel , even at 30,000 feet at 700 mph , could potentially burn very efficiently in a small amount of space compared to regular ol' wood , more than likely the fire would remain internal within the panelling of the wing , or , God forbid , the fuselage ( where the passengers sit ) .
So , just as a note , airliners always have at least a few fire extinguishers inside the cabin . There is also a fire extinguisher in the cockhouse . The engines and APU also have explosively - fired fire bottles that can usually extinguish those fires . So that pretty much covers anything on a plane that has any real chance of catching fire .
Depending on where and what is on fire it could be either .
rejected:
If a plane is flying and caught fire, the wind would not have much of an effect on the fire. The fire would be fueled by the plane's own fuel and other materials on board, and the wind would not be strong enough to significantly change the size or intensity of the fire.

The fire on a plane could potentially be extinguished by the crew using fire extinguishers or other equipment on board. However, if the fire is too large or too difficult to control, the pilots may need to make an emergency landing to try and get the plane and passengers to safety.

query: How does a rocket engine work ? Upon liftoff the rockets are providing thrust against the ground which forces the object into the air . What is the rocket " pushing " against once it is in the atmosphere ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
A rocket is never really providing thrust against the ground . It 's providing thrust against the exhaust gases . By propelling the exhaust backwards , the rocket is propelled forwards . Think of the recoil when you fire a gun - the bullet is pushed one way , and the gun is pushed in the opposite direction .
The rocket is never pushing against the ground nor the air . Rockets work by ejecting large amounts of exhaust gasses out the back at high speeds ( several times the speed of sound ) . Because that gas has had force exerted on it to accelerate it to those speeds , that force must also act on the rocket in the opposite direction . That 's Newton 's Third Law : For every action , there is an equal and opposite reaction .
The rocket is not pushing against the ground . It 's pushing against the rocket itself . Think of a rocket engine as a controlled explosion that creates pressure . By letting the pressure out of one end , the resulting pressure on the inside of the rocket chamber provides thrust against the rocket itself . This is why they continue to work in near vacuum conditions once in orbit and beyond .
rejected:
A rocket engine works by using fuel to create hot gases that are expelled out of the back of the engine at a high speed. These hot gases create a reaction force, also known as thrust, that pushes the rocket in the opposite direction. 
Imagine you are holding a balloon filled with air. If you let go of the balloon, the air inside will rush out and the balloon will fly away. The same thing happens with a rocket. The hot gases are like the air inside the balloon and they rush out of the back of the rocket, pushing the rocket forward. 
Once a rocket is in the atmosphere, it is still pushing against the air around it as it flies through the sky. The rocket's engines continue to burn fuel and produce hot gases, which creates the thrust needed to lift the rocket off the ground and into space. 
So, to sum it up, a rocket engine works by burning fuel to create hot gases, which are expelled out of the back of the engine and create a reaction force that pushes the rocket forward.

query: Can I pay a loan under someone else's name? (assume the dispenser of the loan is malicious)
chosen: I don't think there's anything to worry about.  TFS doesn't really care who's paying, as long as the loan is being paid as agreed.  Of course you're helping your dad's credit history and not your own, but I doubt TFS would give back money just because it came from your bank account. A business may claim a payment wasn't made against the loan, but you'd have the records that you did in fact pay (keep those bank statements).  In theory they could sue you, in practice you'd send them the proof and they'd investigate and find the misplaced money.  THAT does happen sometimes; the wrong account is credited.  If it did end up in court, again you'd win because you have proof you sent payments.  Even if you put the wrong loan account number to pay to, you'd have proof you in fact sent the money.   If you're talking about something like a loan shark... they can do whatever they like.  They won't sue you though, because again you'd have proof.  That's why they'd use violence.  But probably a loan shark wouldn't falsely claim you didn't pay if you did, as word would get out and the loan shark would lose business.  And again, as long as they get what's agreed to, they don't care how they get it or who they get it from.
rejected: It is generally not legal to pay a loan under someone else's name without their knowledge and consent. This is because loans involve a legal agreement between the lender and the borrower, and only the borrower is responsible for repaying the loan. Paying a loan under someone else's name without their knowledge or consent could be considered fraud, which is a criminal offense.Additionally, borrowing money under false pretenses is not only unethical, but it could also have serious consequences for the person whose name is being used. For example, if the loan is not repaid, the lender may try to collect the debt from the person whose name was used, even if they had nothing to do with the loan. This could damage their credit and financial reputation.If you are facing financial difficulty and are considering borrowing money, it is important to be honest and upfront about your circumstances. There may be options available to help you manage your debt, such as negotiating with your lender for more favorable terms or seeking the assistance of a financial advisor or credit counselor. It is always better to explore legal and ethical options for managing your debts rather than resorting to fraudulent or deceptive practices.

query: Why do people say that a giant creature like Godzilla would n't be able to support itself ? Would n't a giant creature also have Giant muscles and bones to support it 's huge size ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Yes , but any material , including bones , has a maximum capacity beyond which it will break , shatter , or collapse in on itself . This is due in part to the [ square - cube law ] ( URL_0 ) , which says that size and weight do not scale linearly as they increase .
[ The Science of Godzilla ] ( URL_0 )
Think of a bone . Now the bone material has a certain strength . That bone 's total carrying ability can roughly be summed up as : Material Strength X Material Cross section = how much total you can carry on it . Now if you doubled the size of a monster , it gets twice as wide ( great , now have 4 ( 2 X 2 ) times as much cross section ) . but it also gets twice as tall . Thus all the stuff about that bone is 8 times as heavy ( 2 wide X 2long X 2 high ) . Since this happens , as you get larger and larger you need to make things wider at the base . query: How are betting odds calculated ? Is it genuine insider knowledge from the betting companies , a fancy algorithm or something else ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
1 : From historical trends and past performance , take the population of contenders and figure out the likeliest performance of each against the other . This is easy in a sports team oriented game or boxing match where there 's a published record and only two contending teams / players . It 's more complex when you have a field of racing horses . 2 : Convert the results into a percentage . So for horse X , it 20 % chance they 'll take the prize ; for horse Y , it 's a long shot that 's likely a 2 % chance . Divide each number into one . 1 divided by 20 % = 5 , so the odds on that horse are " 5 to 1 " if a perfect payout is desired . And it would be 50 to 1 for horse Y. 3 : Now mark up your payout so you are going to earn something out of it . Instead of paying 5 to 1 , you make your payout 3 to 1 , meaning if that horse wins you keep 40 % of the overall bet pool . The more illegitimate your betting business , the greater the markup you 'll put on it . 4 : Next , for each contender , estimate how many people are going to bet on it compared to the others . In a race where one horse is clearly clearly superior , most people will bet on that one . So adjust the winnings on that one way , way down ( 1.2 to one , for example ) to encourage people not to bet on that sure thing . 5 : Finally , if you can , repeat 4 by analyzing how many people are betting on each horse or team as the bets come in . Almost everyone is betting on horse # 3 ? Cut its odds from 4 to 1 down to 3 to 1 . ( This is why bet payouts shift at racetracks ) .
Worked for a bookies for 4 years , its all of the above . 1 . Deterministic models : A sport is modeled to a point were every outcome you want to offer of a game can be statistically predicted . Feed the model the starting probabilities ( the odds of win - draw - win ) and it 'll calculate the rest . This model is a little weak because it can only predict things you 've build the model to predict , for example , you can ask it to be predict the odds of X and Y where they are n't dependent on each other , but you ca nt predict the odds of X and Y were Y is directly effected by X. A trader can then tweak or lock the prices in any direction they like if they think the info they have is better than the model . You can then hook your model up to a feed of information based on the game , the state of the game updates in real time , so do the odds . 2 . Monte carlo model : A game is simulated from the current point on to calculate the probability off all possible outcomes of a game . This improves on the deterministic model by allowing outcomes to be chained together . This option is pretty processing intensive as you need to do a lot of simulations . Apart from that , very similar to the deterministic model . 3 . Manual : A trader enters the odds for every outcome in a market directly . You do n't get a lot of mileage out of this option , it does nt scale well . This would be used for the likes of novelty bets or something like formula 1 . 4 . Customers bets : This can be applied to any of the 3 above . If customers are betting significant amounts of money on an outcome , there is a system that can nudge the price based on the idea that the customers know something we do n't . This option is applied to horse racing quite a lot . 5 . Scraping : Scrape the competitions website to see what prices they 're offering , if ours are significantly different , nudge ours to be closer . Ok , that gives you your odds . Then , based on any combination of sport , tournament , venue , market , a margin will be taken off the top . This is how you go from odds - > prices , and how you make your money . The margin varies from about 0 - 50 % . There is an important point to note about your question . A customer never gets ' odds ' from a bookie , they get a ' price ' . Odds implies a relation to the probability of an event occurring . Sometimes they 're miles off .
It is a mix of everything . Usually a person with deep knowledge of particular sport makes initial betting line . Note that line might have a significant margin , for example an event with two possible outcomes has probability of 50 % , so without margin decimal odds would look like 2.0 : 2.0 . But in reality they might look like 1:85 : 1.85 . Also , you are not allowed to put very large sum on particular outcome , so bookmaker can balance line and before start of event the line might look like 1.65 : 2.10 -- that means many bets were put on the first outcome .

They then use this information to set the odds for each outcome.For example, if two football teams are playing each other and one team is much stronger than the other, the betting company may set the odds so that the stronger team is more likely to win. If you bet on the stronger team, you may not win as much money as if you bet on the weaker team, because the outcome is more likely to happen.The odds are not based on any inside knowledge or insider information, but rather on a careful analysis of all available information and a calculation of the likelihood of different outcomes.

query: Amps , Volts , and Watts ! I know they 're all different but they always confuse me . Grace me with your knowledge reddit . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Think of it like the flow of water . Volts = water pressure Amps = volume of water that 's moving If you have high pressure , but low volume ( high voltage , low amperage ) that 's like a dentist 's water pik . If you have high volume , but low pressure ( high amperage but low voltage ) that 's like your basement flooding through the walls or drains If you have high volume and high pressure ( high amperage and high voltage ) that 's like a fire - hose hitting you in the chest at 3 feet and blowing you back across the room . Watts is a measurement of how much force is produced , in other words , how big an effect that water flow ( electrical flow ) produces .
Amps are a measure of electric current ( a measure of how much electricity is flowing through the wire ) , Volts are measure of Voltage or ' electric potential difference ' , and Watts are a measure of power . Power is calculated by Voltage ` x ` Current . So a device that uses 2 Amps when plugged into a 120 Volt electrical outlet uses 240 Watts of power . You can reorganize the equation to work out the other values . For example , you can calculate that a standard 60 Watt incandescent light - bulb must use 0.5 amps if it 's connected to a standard ( North American ) 120 Volt power system ( Current = Power / Voltage ) . When it comes to safety , both voltage and amperage are important . The human body can generally survive high voltage electric shocks so long as the amperage is low . For example , a ' taser ' may produce a 50,000 Volts shock that temporarily incapacitates someone , but the electric current is so low that the victim of the shock is unlikely to die . At the same time , an electrical outlet ( which has a much lower voltage ) definitely has the potential to electrocute you to death if you are not careful because of the much higher current .
Voltage is the difference in electrical potential . It 's the " pressure " that causes current to flow . Amperes is the unit for current . It 's the " amount " of electrons that flow . Watts = effect . An appliance that is 50 watts will consume 50 Joules of energy every second .

Similarly, the larger the electric current, the more electrical charge can flow through a wire in a certain amount of time. Now let's talk about volts. Volts are a unit of measurement for electric potential difference, or electric tension. Imagine a water tank on the top of a hill. The higher the tank is above the ground, the more potential energy the water has. Similarly, the higher the voltage, the more potential energy there is to move electric charge from one place to another. Finally, let's talk about watts. Watts are a unit of measurement for power. Power is the rate at which energy is transferred or used. Imagine a water wheel that is turned by the flow of water. The faster the water flows, the more power the water wheel will have. query: What causes people 's personal preferences for what races they find attractive ? There are people who find all races attractive but there are many who prefer certain race 's faces and find certain race 's features unnattractive . What causes this ? Please explain like I'm five.

This is one of those areas that 's going to be clouded by bias and ideology , so keep that in mind when reviewing answers . Now , as far as mate selection goes , the instinct to mate with similar individuals is logical from a basic biological perspective ; members of the same species tend to resemble each other in ways that members of other species do not , and only mating attempts within the same species will be successful , so those organisms with this instinct will pass it on to their offspring , and it will be reinforced in subsequent generations . Taken to its extreme , however , this attraction to similar organisms will result in inbreeding , and any genetic defects will be magnified until the species good extinct . Obviously , extinct species do not reproduce , but those individuals with a preference for novelty , within certain constraints , will produce more viable offspring , and the interplay between these two instincts will tune mating behaviour to fairly optimal levels , all things being equal . Now , when we discuss humans , it gets much more complicated , because instinct is no longer entirely in control ; vast volumes of information are exchanged between individuals and groups that has goals other than mating , but also has an influence on mate selection . So for humans , the short version is that instinct is less important , but it 's still a factor . When mating opportunities are few , and resources are scarce , instinct will draw an individual toward more similar mates , in order to maximise the probability of reproductive success , at the expense of less genetic robustness . When mating opportunities are abundant , and resources are not constrained , instinct will favour novelty , to maximise the overall health of an individual 's offspring . Or maybe , on a particularly good day during your childhood , the young woman at the ice cream stand , who gave you extra rainbow sprinkles and smiled when you said thank you , maybe she had curly hair and brown eyes , so you associate curly hair and brown eyes with happiness , and you look for those traits in a partner without ever knowing exactly why . Humans are complicated , and frequently dumb . They 're also endlessly fascinating , and totally worth more than the colour of their skin or the shape of their eyes . I hope that helps .

query: Please explain what is "Image processing"
chosen: Digital image processing is the use of a digital computer to process digital images through an algorithm. As a subcategory or field of digital signal processing, digital image processing has many advantages over analog image processing. It allows a much wider range of algorithms to be applied to the input data and can avoid problems such as the build-up of noise and distortion during processing. Since images are defined over two dimensions (perhaps more) digital image processing may be modeled in the form of multidimensional systems. The generation and development of digital image processing are mainly affected by three factors: first, the development of computers; second, the development of mathematics (especially the creation and improvement of discrete mathematics theory); third, the demand for a wide range of applications in environment, agriculture, military, industry and medical science has increased.
rejected:
Image processing is a technique for manipulating digital images by applying mathematical operations to them. It involves analyzing and modifying images to extract useful information, enhance visual quality, or reduce noise and other distortions. Image processing techniques are used in a variety of fields, including computer vision, medicine, robotics, and machine learning, among others.



There are many different types of image processing techniques, including image enhancement, image restoration, image segmentation, and image recognition. Image enhancement techniques are used to improve the visual quality of an image by increasing contrast, sharpness, or color accuracy. Image restoration techniques are used to remove noise, blur, or other distortions from an image. Image segmentation techniques are used to divide an image into multiple regions or segments, each of which represents a different object or background in the image. Image recognition techniques are used to identify objects, people, or other features in an image.



Image processing can be done using a variety of tools and techniques, including software algorithms, hardware systems, and specialized image processing hardware. Some common tools and techniques used in image processing include image filters, convolutional neural networks, and machine learning algorithms.

A liquid oxygen plant typically consists of several main components:

Oxygen production unit: This is the main component of the plant where oxygen is produced. It typically includes an air separation unit, which separates oxygen from other gases in the air using a process called cryogenic distillation. The air separation unit may use a variety of techniques, such as low-temperature distillation or pressure swing adsorption, to separate the oxygen from the other gases.

Oxygen storage tanks: Once the oxygen has been produced, it is typically stored in high-pressure tanks for later use. These tanks are typically made of stainless steel or other corrosion-resistant materials and are designed to withstand the high pressures required to store liquid oxygen.

Pumps and piping: The liquid oxygen is transported from the storage tanks to the end user through a network of pumps and piping. The pumps and piping must be designed to handle the extremely low temperatures of liquid oxygen and must be properly insulated to prevent heat loss.

Safety systems: A liquid oxygen plant typically includes a number of safety systems to prevent accidents and protect workers. These may include emergency shutdown systems, fire suppression systems, and alarms to alert workers to potential hazards.

Overall, a liquid oxygen plant is a complex and specialized facility that requires careful design and maintenance to operate safely and efficiently. It may be housed in a large, industrial-style building or in a series of smaller buildings or structures, depending on the size and scope of the operation.
A liquid oxygen plant is a facility that produces liquid oxygen through the process of cryogenically separating it from the air. The plant typically consists of several main components:

Air separation unit (ASU): This unit separates the oxygen from the air using a series of filters and cooling processes. The ASU consists of a compressor, heat exchangers, and a distillation column.

Cryogenic storage tanks: These tanks store the liquid oxygen produced by the ASU. The tanks are typically large, insulated vessels that are designed to maintain the low temperature of the liquid oxygen.

Control room: This is the central control center for the plant, where operators monitor and control the various processes and equipment.

Utilities: The plant will also include various utilities, such as electrical and mechanical systems, to support the operation of the plant.

Overall, a liquid oxygen plant is a large industrial facility that is designed to produce and store liquid oxygen. It may have a variety of different structures and buildings depending on the specific design and layout of the plant.
A liquid oxygen plant typically consists of several main components:

An air separation unit (ASU) - This is the primary component of a liquid oxygen plant. It separates oxygen from the air using a process called cryogenic distillation. The ASU consists of one or more columns filled with adsorbent materials that selectively adsorb the different gases present in air. The air is cooled and compressed before being fed into the ASU, and the resulting gases are then separated based on their boiling points.

Compressors - These are used to compress the air before it is fed into the ASU. The compressors may be reciprocating or screw-type, and they are typically driven by electric motors.

Cryogenic heat exchangers - These are used to cool the air and the gases produced by the ASU to the required temperatures for cryogenic distillation. They may be shell and tube, spiral, or plate heat exchangers.

Storage tanks - These are used to store the liquid oxygen produced by the plant. They are typically cylindrical in shape and are made of stainless steel or other corrosion-resistant materials.

Pumps - These are used to transfer the liquid oxygen from the storage tanks to the end-use points, such as hospitals, industrial plants, or rocket launch pads.

Control and monitoring systems - These consist of various sensors, controllers, and other equipment that are used to monitor and control the various processes and systems in the plant.

In addition to these main components, a liquid oxygen plant may also include various auxiliary systems and equipment, such as cooling towers, boilers, and emergency shutdown systems. The layout and design of the plant may vary depending on the specific process technologies and equipment used, as well as the size and capacity of the plant.

query: How to condition myself to sleep properly What time should one sleep at , how many hours and the benefits of a good sleep pattern . Thanks Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Do n't eat at least 2 hours before bed time . Do n't use digital screens at least 30 minutes before bed time . Go to bed at the same time every night . Wake up at a consistent time approx . 8 hours later . A ritual can be very helpful . Do n't do stuff outside of this ritual in your bed . Sex , read a book before bed , sleep . Do n't dick around in bed for hours every day on your laptop or phone . Associate your bed with sleep and night . I like to have a cup caffeine free tea , my GF and read a book together ( we do voices ) , and then crash . The benefits of good sleep are basically infinite , more alert , less lethargic , faster metabolism , better mood , etc . The time one should sleep at obviously depends on when you need to wake up in the morning . If you have to be at work at 5 am you should probably be going to bed at like 8 pm .
Some other things that slash178 did n't mention that might help : * Sleep in a cool , quiet , dark room with no lights * Avoid having your phone on at night , turn it off ( or airplane mode ) so that texts and calls do n't disturb you at night * Avoid multiple alarms in the morning that go off over the course of an hour . Have one alarm and a back up 10 minutes later to get you up . * Place the alarm on the other side of the room so it forces you to get out of bed * Avoid coffee right away , try other methods of waking up ( e.g. cool glass of water , shower ) so that you do n't become dependent on the caffeine to perk you up . Have your morning cup later .

rejected: Sure! Here are some tips for getting good sleep:Set a bedtime and stick to it: It's important to go to bed at the same time every night, even on weekends. This helps your body get into a routine.Make your bedroom a comfortable place to sleep: Keep the room cool, dark, and quiet, and use a comfortable mattress and pillows.Avoid screens before bedtime: The blue light from screens like phones, tablets, and TVs can make it harder to fall asleep. query: What led game designers away from Mac as a viable platform for their content ? I do love the five games I can play on Steam , but REALLY . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The most likely reason is market share . If you have a set amount of resources , do you design for the 95 % or the 5 % .
I think the market share answer people are giving is only half the answer . DirectX is a graphics / sound / etc . library Microsoft developed for Windows , and were able to optimize better than OpenGL . Game developers chose to use DirectX for the better performance , at the expense of portability . It was n't that hard of a decision in the 90s when 95 % of people had Windows PCs . If developers used OpenGL , which is supported on Windows , Mac and Linux , porting to those other OSes would have been a lot easier . It 's also worth noting that Apple did n't place much importance on optimizing the performance of their graphics drivers until recently , however I 'm not sure if that 's a contributing factor towards game developers moving away from Apple , or if it 's the other way around and Apple adopted that attitude because gaming was n't much of a thing on Macs .
Old guy here ( and an old Mac guy from back in the day , if not so much any more ): One mostly - forgotten factor is that once John Sculley took over , Apple threatened to pull their ads ( and thus a big chunk of the revenue ) from the weeklies like MacWeek and MacWorld if they did n't cut down their coverage on games and charge game companies extra for ads . Why would they do that ? Because Mac had acquired a bit of a reputation for having all the best games , and people inside PC - centric companies who were asking for Macs were getting accused of only wanting them for games . In the meantime , though , over on the other side of the fence , IBM and Microsoft had learned that productivity software mostly did n't need much hardware , so people who had PCs were n't buying new PCs , which was also holding down new OS sales . But 3D games use hardware that goes obsolete every couple of years . So Microsoft embraced game developers just at the point in time where Apple was shitting all over them , creating the DirectX suite of UIs and drivers .
rejected: There are a few reasons why some game designers may have chosen not to develop games for the Mac platform. One reason is market share. The Mac has a smaller market share than Windows, so it may not be as financially viable for game developers to create games specifically for the Mac. This means that they may choose to focus their resources on creating games for the larger Windows market instead. Another reason is technical differences between the two platforms. Macs use a different type of processor (called a "CPU") than most Windows computers, which can make it more difficult to port games from one platform to the other. This can increase the time and cost of developing games for the Mac. Finally, there may be other business considerations at play, such as partnerships or contracts with other companies that make it more attractive for game developers to focus on other platforms. Overall, the decision to develop games for a particular platform is a complex one that involves many different factors. While some game developers may choose not to create games for the Mac, there are still many great games available for the platform, and the situation may change in the future as the market evolves.

query: Why in America is it called TJ Maxx and in the UK it is called TK Maxx ? Same situation , Opel , the car company , is called Vauxhall in the UK . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The first European T.J Maxx store opened in Bristol in 1994 . The company modified the name to T.K. Maxx to avoid " confusion with the established British retail chain TJ Hughes ( which is not affiliated with TJX ) " - URL_0
Regarding your car question specifically : Vauxhall was an established and well - known brand in the UK before the US company General Motors took it over . Therefore even though they aligned the company with their main European marque Opel in terms of the models produced , it made sense to stick with a well - known brandname . For a similar scenario you can look to when the US supermarket chain Walmart took over the UK supermarket chain Asda in the early 2000s . Because Asda was a well - known brand in the UK with significant market share it would have made no sense for them to lose all that brand association with the public , so today we still have Asda ( although a few of the larger stores are now branded " Asda Walmart " with the Walmart in smaller writing ) .

Maxx to avoid " confusion with the established British retail chain TJ Hughes ( which is not affiliated with TJX ) " . Regarding your second question : Opel and Vauxhall are both owned by the American company General Motors , so they pretty much have the same cars . query: Why do birds never shut up ? There 's an enormous tree outside my window where the birds are making noise day and night . It never stops . Does n't it take a lot of energy ? Does n't it reveal their location to predators ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Birds are often brightly colored and noisy . They do n't avoid predators by hiding - they avoid them by simply being able to * fly away * at a moment 's notice . The noise is helpful actually , as birds warn each other if there are predators nearby . Those birds tryin ' to get laid , man . They squawk so females come to them .
Mockingbirds are notorious for singing 24/7 until they get a mate . Do n't hate on them , they 're horny and desperate .
You know the Reddit - ism , * does n't matter , had sex * ? That 's a big part of it ; bird songs are often a mating display . They 're also not all that costly : they do n't take a lot of energy , and as for revealing a bird 's location , well , it reveals that there are songbirds * present , * but that 's a risk the birds just have to take . You ca n't announce your presence to potential mates without also announcing your presence to potential enemies . That sanguine attitude has its limits , though : when there 's a more immediate danger- like a cat or a hawk seen nearby , you 'll notice the birds tend to shut up .
rejected:
Birds make noise for a variety of reasons. Some birds sing to attract mates, while others sing to defend their territory or to communicate with other members of their flock. Making noise also helps birds to stay in contact with each other, especially if they are flying in a group. Some birds, like parrots, can also make noise for fun or to express emotions.

As for the energy required to make noise, it really depends on the type of noise and how long the bird is singing for. Singing can take a lot of energy, but many birds are able to conserve energy by resting in between songs.

As for revealing their location to predators, this is definitely a risk that birds face. However, most birds have developed ways to minimize this risk, such as singing from high up in a tree where they are less likely to be seen, or singing at times when predators are less active.

query: Blog income taxes?
chosen: If thinking about it like a business you normally only pay taxes on Net income, not gross.  So Gross being all the money that comes in. People giving you cash, checks, whatever get deposited into your account. You then pay that out to other people for services, advertisement. At the end of the day what is left would be your 'profit' and you would be expected to pay income tax on that.  If you are just an individual and don't have an LLC set up or any business structure you would usually just have an extra page to fill out on your taxes with this info. I think it's a schedule C but not 100%
rejected: Income from blogging can be subject to income taxes in the United States. The specific tax treatment of income from blogging will depend on whether the activity is considered a hobby or a business.If the blog is considered a hobby, any income from the blog will be treated as hobby income and will be reported on a tax return as other miscellaneous income. Hobby income is taxed at the individual's marginal tax rate.If the blog is considered a business, the income from the blog will be treated as business income and will be reported on a tax return as self-employment income. Self-employment income is subject to self-employment tax, which includes both the employee and employer portion of Social Security and Medicare taxes.In order for the income from a blog to be considered business income rather than hobby income, the blog must be conducted with the intention of making a profit. This means that the blog must be operated in a businesslike manner and the blogger must regularly and continuously pursue the activity with the reasonable expectation of making a profit.If you have questions about the tax treatment of income from your blog, it's a good idea to consult with a tax professional or the Internal Revenue Service (IRS).
