However, there are a few reasons why bison are not raised as widely as cows for meat production. One reason is that bison are generally slower to mature than cows, which means that it takes longer for them to reach slaughter weight. This means that it takes longer for farmers to raise bison for meat compared to cows. 
Another reason is that bison can be more challenging to raise than cows. They are generally more independent and can be more difficult to handle, which can make it harder for farmers to manage them. In addition, bison require more space to roam and graze, which can be a challenge for farmers who have limited land available. 
Finally, bison can also consume more feed than cows, which can be more expensive for farmers. Overall, while bison have some advantages as a source of meat, they are generally not as practical to raise as cows for a variety of reasons.

query: Why is the price of my investment only updated once per day?
chosen: There is no fundamental, good reason, I think; "that's just how it's done" (which is what all the other answers seem to be saying, w/o coming out and admitting it). Just guessing, but I'll bet most of the reason is historical: Before up-to-the-moment quotes were readily available, that was a bit tedious to calculate/update the fund's value, so enacted-laws let it be done just once per day.  (@NL7 quotes the security act of 1940, which certainly has been updated, but also still might contain the results of crufty rationales, like this.) There are genuinely different issues between funds and stocks, though: One share of a fund is fundamentally different from one share of stock: There is a finite supply of Company-X-stock, and people are trading that piece of ownership around, and barter to find an mutually-agreeable-price.  But when you buy into a mutual-fund, the mutual-fund "suddenly has more shares" -- it takes your money and uses it to buy shares of the underlying stocks (in a ratio equal to its current holdings).  As a consequence: the mutual fund's price isn't determined by two people bartering and agreeing on a price (like stock); there is exactly one sane way to price a mutual fund, and that's the weighted total of its underlying stock.  If you wanted to sell your ownership-of-Mutual-Fund-Z to a friend at 2:34pm, there wouldn't be any bartering, you'd just calculate the value based on the stated-value of the underlying stock at that exact moment. So: there's no inherent reason you can't instantaneously price a mutual fund.  BUT people don't really buy/sell funds to each other -- they go to the fund-manager and essentially make a deposit-or-withdraw.  The fund-manager is only required by law to do it once a day (and perhaps even forbidden from doing it more often?), so that's all they do. [Disclaimer: I know very little about markets and finance. But I recognize answers that are 'just because'.]
rejected:
There are several reasons why the price of an investment may only be updated once per day. One reason is that the market for the investment closes for a period of time each day, and the price is only updated when the market reopens. For example, stock markets typically close at 4:00pm Eastern Time and do not reopen until the next business day. During this time, the price of a stock will not change.Another reason why the price of an investment may only be updated once per day is because the investment is not traded on a continuous basis. Some investments, such as mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs), are priced at the end of the trading day based on the value of their underlying assets. The price of these investments is typically only updated once per day, after the market closes.It's also possible that the platform or service you are using to track the price of your investment only updates the information once per day. In this case, the investment itself may be traded more frequently, but the platform is only showing the most recent price at the end of the trading day.If you need more up-to-date information on the price of your investment, you may be able to find it on other financial websites or by contacting the company or organization that manages the investment. It's important to keep in mind, however, that the price of an investment can change rapidly and may not always be immediately reflected in the information that is available.
The price of an investment is typically only updated once per day because it reflects the closing price of the investment on a particular day. The closing price is the price at which the investment is traded at the end of the day, after the market has closed.There can be fluctuations in the price of an investment during the trading day, but these fluctuations are not reflected in the closing price until the next trading day. This means that the price of an investment will only be updated once per day, at the end of the trading day.There are some exceptions to this rule. For example, some investments, such as futures contracts and options, may have prices that are updated more frequently. Additionally, some investments may have prices that are updated in real-time, such as certain types of equities that are traded on electronic exchanges. However, these types of investments are relatively rare and are not the norm.

query: How do programs like Google Translate recognize handwriting so accurately ? When I 'm practicing Chinese characters I 've noticed that it will still read my poor handwriting , even when my stroke order is completely wrong . What kind of algorithms play into this ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
I 'm not sure which algorithm(s ) they use , but back in Uni I had an AI class where we did a small program to identify user inputted letters using an neural net algorithm called " Perceptron " . In this small program , there was a grid ( 2 states per cell ) which allowed the user to " draw " the " standard " or " normal " form of some letters . This was used to " teach " the program those letters . The user could then swap some of the grid elements ( activate / deactivate them ) and ask the program to identify the letter , and the neural network would attempt to match the new pattern to the ones it knew , returning the best match . If the user swapped ( or deleted ) enough cells , it would eventually not be able to ( correctly ) identify the letter . I 'm assuming they do something like this , with a large dataset of handwritten letters in order to " teach " their algorithm . Here 's a [ demo applet of a multi - level - Perceptron being used for OCR ] ( URL_0 ) . EDIT : typos and grammar .
Here 's a " simple " way I learned in a class : Basically average the digits down to black and white blocks of " On " or " Off " ( our example had 783 blocks I think ) , write each character 's on / off blocks into a line . In a training file , say " this line of on / off blocks is a ' 1 ' " , " this line is a ' G ' " . Use something like a " Random Forest " where is says " hmm , lines that have these columns set as ' on ' tend to be 4 's , but these rows with these on / offs tend to be G 's " . No idea if that 's how a pro would do it , but it works for some stuff . I 'd never roll my own in reality ; there 's free libraries way better than I could write . More here for the curious : URL_0
Not 100 % sure , but I think it is an average of other people 's writing . They compile native peoples writing and see if any of them are similar to yours .
rejected:
Google Translate uses a combination of machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques to recognize handwriting. Here's a simple explanation of how it works:

First, the program needs to be "trained" on lots of examples of handwriting. This means showing it many different handwritten characters and telling it what they are supposed to spell.

The program uses this training data to learn patterns in the way different characters are written. For example, it might notice that the character "爱" is usually written with a certain stroke order and certain shapes.

When you write a character and show it to the program, it looks at the shapes and strokes you used to write it. It compares these to the patterns it learned from the training data and tries to find the best match.

If the program is confident that it has found the right character, it will translate it for you. If it's not sure, it might ask you to confirm or rewrite the character.

This process can be quite complex, but the basic idea is that the program is using its knowledge of common patterns in handwriting to try to understand what you've written. Even if your handwriting is a bit messy or the stroke order is incorrect, the program might still be able to recognize the character as long as it can see enough of the key features.

query: What performance difference can we see between an athlete that warms up and one that does nt before a race ? I would like to know specifically about cyclists Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
In short , warming up brings the body from its natural state of rest closer to its exercising state during training , performing etc . Essentially , it makes the transition from rest to exercise much more efficient as our heart rate is already substantially increased due to warming up . Our blood circulation is subsequently increased , leading to increased delivery of oxygen and removal of metabolic waste products from working muscles . Warming up allows athletes to jump straight into performance without having to worry about the stage known as oxygen deficit , where the body literally needs to take in a greater amount of oxygen to satisfy the increased demand for oxygen by the muscles . So to answer your question , a cyclist that has warmed up will be able to cycle harder and faster as their heart rate , blood circulation , respiratory rate and more are already substantially increased . A cyclist that has not warmed up will need an increased amount of oxygen delivered to working muscles to satisfy the sudden increase in demand .
The most important reason for doing a warm up is to allow the body to prepare steadily and safely , thus preventing the possibility of an injury during the performance . In case an athlete did n't do the warm up exercise and an injury like a hamstring strain did occur that will obviously affect the performance , otherwise a considerable difference in performance is highly unlikely . Basically it 's a matter of safety , gradually increasing the heart rate and circulation thus loosening the joints and increasing blood flow to the muscles . There is also a psychological factor that helps athlete when doing warm - up because it 's an opportunity for the athlete to prepare mentally for the performance ahead .
Interestingly enough there might not be much of a difference . There was a study done with runners that showed that people who did warm up and stretch before running had the same rate of injuries as those who did n't .
rejected:
Sure! When an athlete warms up before a race, it helps their body to get ready for the physical activity that is coming. Just like when you warm up before playing a sport or running around outside, your body feels more awake and ready to move.

For a cyclist, warming up can be especially important because cycling is a sport that involves using your legs a lot. When you warm up, your legs will feel looser and more flexible, which can help you pedal your bike more smoothly and quickly.

If you don't warm up before a race, your body might feel a little stiff and sluggish, which can make it harder to pedal your bike as fast as you might like. So, overall, warming up can help you perform better in a race because it helps your body feel more ready and able to do its best.

query: Is there a bank account that allows ACH deposits but not ACH withdrawals?
chosen: Nowadays, all checks you write will not be send to your bank anymore, but instead the bank where they get deposited does an ACH from your bank. That implies that not allowing this to happen, your bank would not be able to honor any checks you wrote (without enforcing paper check delivery in the mail, but the Check21 bill does not allow such enforcing anymore). ACH deposits are electronic transfers of funds that are initiated by a payor and are credited to the recipient's bank account. ACH withdrawals, on the other hand, are initiated by the recipient and result in funds being debited from the account.Some banks offer deposit-only accounts specifically for the purpose of receiving ACH deposits, such as payroll or government benefit payments. These accounts may not allow ACH withdrawals, checks, or debit card transactions, and may have other restrictions as well.It is important to carefully review the terms and conditions of any bank account before opening it, to ensure that it meets your needs and fits with your financial plan. query: the arguments for and against fracking . I saw the documentary Gasland and thought that was a pretty powerful statement against fracking but I have n't seen fracknation or any pro - frack media . Can somebody explain the arguments to me ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:

Superfund ) * * due to an amendment in the Energy Policy Act of 2005 pushed through by Vice President Dick Cheney , who was CEO at Halliburton before the 2000 election . While conventional oil and gas wells are permitted and regulated through a variety of programs ( e.g. EPA 's Underground Injection Control program ) , fracking is exempt unless they inject diesel compounds underground ( a rarity ) . Fracking, or hydraulic fracturing, is a process used to extract oil and natural gas from shale rock formations. It involves injecting a mixture of water, sand, and chemicals into the ground at high pressure to create fractures in the rock, which allows the oil and gas to flow more easily and be extracted.

There are several arguments for and against fracking:

ARGUMENTS FOR FRACKING:

Fracking can provide a domestic source of energy: Fracking has helped the United States become less dependent on foreign sources of oil and natural gas. This can help to stabilize energy prices and reduce the need for importing energy from other countries.

Fracking can create jobs: The fracking industry employs a large number of people, including engineers, geologists, and truck drivers.

Fracking can lower energy prices: Fracking has helped to increase the supply of oil and natural gas, which can lower energy prices for consumers.

ARGUMENTS AGAINST FRACKING:

Fracking can pollute the environment: Fracking can potentially contaminate the air and water with chemicals used in the fracking process. There have also been reports of methane leaks, which contribute to climate change.

Fracking can cause earthquakes: There have been instances where fracking has been linked to small earthquakes in certain areas.

Fracking can impact public health: Some people believe that fracking can have negative impacts on public health, including respiratory problems and other health issues.

Overall, the arguments for and against fracking are complex and there are valid points on both sides. It is important to carefully consider the potential risks and benefits of fracking before making a decision about its use.

query: why does our body sometimes twitch uncontrollably when we try to fall asleep ? occasionally when i 'm about to fall asleep , my legs will jerk / spasm and i 'm not sure what causes it Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's the hypnagogic state . Part of your brain is asleep and dreaming . But it has n't shut down the part of your brain that keeps you conscious and the part that controls motor movement .
It 's called a " hypnic jerk " ( " hypnic " as in for example " hypnotic " , meaning " sleep - related " ) and the exact cause is n't actually known . The American Academy of Sleep Medicine ( an organisation that studies sleep ) say that a lot of things might be the potential cause of these hypnic jerk ( the list includes stress , caffeine , etc . ) , but most hypnic jerks just occur randomly . An interesting hypothesis ( or " idea " ) as to why humans experience hypnic jerks , has to do with evolution . I 'll explain with an anecdote ( a short story ) . Imagine an animal that sleeps high up in trees . Seems dangerous , right ? If the animal falls into a deep sleep , it would risk falling out of the tree and being seriously hurt , or even dying . Now imagine that some of these animals wake up , whenever their body feels as though it is falling . Those animals would n't fall to their death , because they would wake up and adjust their sleeping position . Since the animals that experience a " hypnic jerk " live longer than those who do n't , they have a better chance of passing on their " genes " to their children , who will also experience a hypnic jerk . They will likewise live longer , have more children , pass on the trait and so on . This is actually a simple model for how evolution works ! Human ancestors would also have lived in trees ( if you go back far enough ) , and so , the same logic applies to us . The hypnic jerk we experience , might be a " leftover " from a time when we needed it to survive ! Another theory as to why humans experience hypnic jerks , is that your brain " thinks " that your body is dying when you enter sleep ( due to things like your body temperature dropping etc . ) and as a result , makes your muscles jolt to make sure your not dying . This theory however seems a bit strange to me and I have n't been able to find a credible ( trustworthy ) source for this theory . Only things like BuzzFeed articles etc . I hope this makes sense ! And I hope you did n't find my explanation too patronising or oversimplified , I thought the safe route would be explaining everything in the simplest terms possible and assuming no prior knowledge on any of the subjects ( it 's called " Explain it like I 'm 5 " after all ) . tl;dr It 's called a hypnic jerk . It happens at random , some factors can make it more likely to happen . It 's perfectly normal although the exact reason is unknown . It may be an archaic reflex that helped early humans not fall out of trees . Some say it 's the brain making sure the body is n't dying , but I ca n't find a credible source for this .
Hypnic Jerk is not a mean hypnotist , it 's a reflex action that can occur as your body transitions in to sleep . Your motor cortex has a kind of built in safety that gets engaged as you sleep in order to prevent damaging yourself as you dream . When you dream you 're running , this is the process that keeps you from actually running in bed . As you begin to drift off to sleep though if this gradual transition does n't happen smoothly , sometimes the paralysis kicks in before you 're sufficiently unconscious and you jerk awake feeling like you were falling for a moment . It happens more often when you sleep sitting up , or leaning against something , when you 've been doing rigorous physical activity prior to napping , or when you are very tired or under a lot of stress .
rejected:
It's normal for your body to twitch or move slightly as you fall asleep. This is usually due to your muscles relaxing and sometimes twitching as they do so. It's also common for people to experience brief muscle spasms or twitches while they're awake, especially when they're feeling tired or stressed.

However, if you're experiencing frequent or severe muscle twitching or spasms that are disrupting your sleep or making it difficult for you to fall asleep, it could be a sign of a medical condition called periodic limb movement disorder (PLMD). PLMD is a sleep disorder that causes repetitive movements of the legs or arms during sleep. If you're concerned about your muscle twitching or think you may have PLMD, it's a good idea to talk to your doctor. They can help determine the cause of your muscle twitching and recommend the appropriate treatment.

query: Why , at the moment of first contact / enslavement did populations taken as slaves not revolt ? interested to hear opinions / fact . I was originally thinking continental Africa , but am now thinking generally . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
In many cases , it was the result of the subjugation of a people by a much more powerful invading force . The soldiers of those people could n't stop them , and so the common laborers did n't stand much of a chance . Other times , it was a result of contact with societies where slavery was already an accepted practice . People are much less likely to revolt against something that they were brought up to believe is normal .
It 's not like somebody just walks into town one day and says " you 're all slaves , get used to it " and everyone just decides to agree to it . A group of people might get enslaved after they 've lost a major battle . With all the warriors gone , it 's hard to rise up against somebody . Sometimes , it 's only a few people that get captured at a time . During capture , they 've probably had the shit beaten out of them which really reduces their will to try fighting again . Other times , the slaves are kept split up so they ca n't organize a revolt . It 's hard to start revolting when there 's only 5 of you in one place . In the earliest days of American slavery , they tried to keep slaves from the same parts of Africa separated . If the slaves ca n't talk to each other , it 's harder for them to plan a revolt . Even with all that , however - [ slave rebelions ] ( URL_0 ) have taken place in pretty much every society that 's had widespread slavery . They might not have been successful , but people will inevitably try .
- Because they have control of horses and weapons that can kill a man in an instant from far away . - And their vast wealth and technology has ensured that our leaders and neighbors leaders and our enemies leaders are now under their control . - And they have railroads and massive ships that allow them to bring in fresh troops and weapons from far away . - And 500 of their men , killed 12,000 of our men in 5 days and they are still marching towards our main town .
rejected: There are many reasons why people who have been taken as slaves may not have been able to revolt or resist at the moment of first contact or enslavement. One reason is that they may have been outnumbered and outgunned by the people who were enslaving them. Another reason is that they may have been taken by surprise, and may not have had the opportunity to organize and mount a resistance. Additionally, they may have been subjected to violent intimidation and threats of harm if they resisted, which could have made it difficult for them to defy their captors. It's also important to note that slavery has existed in many different societies throughout history, and the specific circumstances surrounding the enslavement of a particular group of people may have been unique and shaped by the culture, history, and power dynamics of the time and place in which it occurred.

query: What is the difference between Marxism and socialsim ? Is Marxism the ideological premise for it ? Then socialism is the actual economic structure ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Marxism is a sociological principle created by Karl Marx . Basically , it 's the idea that there are two classes , the proletariats and the bourgeois . In his theories , it is the bourgeois , who own the means of production , who actively exploit workers and reduce their standard of living . Marx thought that when the proletariats , who greatly outnumber the bourgeois , would awaken a class consciousness and rebel , collectively seizing the means of production and establishing a completely egalitarian society . Meanwhile , socialism is an economic and political system in which the public owns a great deal of property , especially the means of production , and the government has an active role in the economy . Modern European democracies like Denmark are good examples of socialist nations .
I think the Marxism ideology is like socialism only that it involves using force to achieve those goals ( say , taking property from the rich by force and handing it out evenly ) . Though this is probably inaccurate .
Marxism is the theory of transition from capitalism into socialism .
rejected:
Marxism and socialism are two related, but distinct, political and economic ideologies.

Marxism is a political and economic ideology that was developed by Karl Marx, a philosopher and economist who lived in the 19th century. Marxism is based on the idea that society should be organized around the principle of "from each according to their ability, to each according to their need." In other words, under Marxism, the means of production (such as factories and other resources) would be owned and controlled by the workers, rather than by a small group of capitalists (owners of businesses). According to Marx, this would lead to a more equal society, with no class distinctions between workers and capitalists.

Socialism is a broader term that refers to a range of economic and political systems that are based on the idea of collective ownership and control of resources and the means of production. Socialism can take many different forms, ranging from democratic socialism, which combines elements of socialism and democracy, to more authoritarian forms of socialism, in which a single party or group controls the economy. Socialism is often associated with the goal of creating a more equal society, with a greater degree of economic and social justice.

So, in short, Marxism is a specific ideology that was developed by Karl Marx, while socialism is a broader term that refers to a range of economic and political systems that advocate for the collective ownership and control of resources and the means of production. Marxism is often seen as a precursor to or foundation for socialist ideas and movements, but there are many different types of socialism that may not be directly influenced by Marxist ideology.

query: Why are there three credit bureaus ? I do n't get why you have three bureaus , three ratings - instead of one . Would n't it be simpler ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
> Would n't it be simpler ? It would also be simpler if Walmart , Target , and Costco were all the same company but they are n't . Private companies are separate because they developed as separate entities and they either do not wish to merge or are prevented from doing so by government action . It " being simpler " is n't really a motivation for them .
The BIG three Transunion , Equifax , and Experian gradually grew to dominance by buying up smaller regional credit bureaus . They used to divide the nation in three , Transunion in the midwest , Experian the westcoast states , and Equifax out east ... but they all nationwide report now that people tend to move around significantly more than they used to . There are a number of specialty credit bureaus still in operation though ... Dunn and Bradstreet being the most notable ... they do business credit reporting . PRBC , Cortaris , Innovis , and others each have their own niche in the market though .
The Credit Bureaus are private , for - profit companies . They compete with each other , just like McDonalds and Burger King . Having one would create a monopoly , which would make the services they provide more expensive as they would n't have to compete with the other two for price and services .
rejected:
There are three major credit bureaus in the United States: Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion. These companies collect information about people's credit history, including their credit card balances, loan payments, and other financial information. They use this information to create a credit report, which is a summary of a person's credit history.

Credit bureaus create credit reports to help lenders decide whether to give people loans or credit cards. Lenders can use the information in a credit report to decide if someone is likely to pay back their loans on time. A high credit score, which is a number that summarizes a person's creditworthiness, can make it easier to get a loan or credit card. A low credit score can make it harder.

Having three credit bureaus might seem confusing, but it actually provides more choice and competition in the marketplace. Each credit bureau may have slightly different information in their credit reports, so having three different bureaus can give lenders a more complete picture of a person's credit history. It's important to check your credit report from all three bureaus to make sure the information is accurate and complete.

query: Could someone explain to me the difference between socialism and liberalism ? Please :-) Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
This is a really loaded question because the usage of the terms ' liberal ' and ' socialist ' in the United States are n't representative of what the terms have meant historically . Further complicating this question is that both socialism and liberal theories have different component parts , i.e. economic liberalism , social liberalism , and philosphoical liberalism . To put it * very very very very * simply though : liberalism focuses on personal and private property and a laissez - fair approach to economics and society . Socialism on the other hand , is a rejection of this personal and private property and favours communal ownership of property . The reason that socialism and liberalism sometimes get confused in the United States is because social liberalism in the United States is connected to social progressivism and social progressivism ( its more radical components ) are associated with communist and anarchist movements .
ELI5 : What we call ' classical liberalism ' turned into ' libertarian socialism ' when our society changed and a really bad , powerful big government gave way to really bad , powerful big business . Today , we call the off - shoots of classical liberalism by a lot of different names , because the things that it hoped would free people from oppression ( markets and private enterprise ) have turned into the new tools oppression . This forced classical liberals to change and adapt to the new world . Some people kept the principles and changed their focus and some people kept the name and insisted that if anything is to blame , it ca n't be the fundamentals of capitalist markets or private ownership . Now there 's a whole bunch of confusing stuff and most people ca n't agree on exactly what to call anything , probably in part because there 's a lot of stuff involved that people with power do n't really want a lot of people thinking or talking about . ELI13 : [ Libertarian socialism ] ( URL_4 ) is basically a [ direct extension ] ( URL_2 ) of [ classical liberalism ] ( URL_6 ) , adapted to an industrialized world -- and the logical end - point of applying enlightenment ideals to society . Classical liberalism was mostly about maximizing the liberty and autonomy of individuals . Living in a pre - capitalist society , the canonical founders of this ideology usually focused on the tyranny of the state . Socialists oppose private tyranny , which has largely displaced and merged with state tyranny . They want a society where workers own and control the means of production , rather than renting themselves to the holders of capital . Nominal socialism kind of split into two branches . The left , anarchist - leaning side sought to end capitalism by replacing it with worker and/or collectively owned , self - managed production , with the goal of diminishing or even eventually eliminating the state , maximizing individual liberty and autonomy -- see [ Anarchist Spain ] ( URL_0 ) . They focused on creating a society without hierarchy and stratification . The right , authoritarian branch , sought to end capitalism by concentrating power and ownership in the hands of the state , basically arguing that socialism is nothing more than centrally planned state - capitalism -- see [ Leninism ] ( URL_3 ) . What we call liberalism in the US is something a little more like [ this ] ( URL_1 ) or [ this ] ( URL_5 ) though . The first is a kind - of - but - not - really allegiance to classical liberal ideals . The latter is basically a euphemism for a business - run society , where the markets are only ' free ' in sense of being free from the meddlesome public , while ( as always ) actively subsidized and insured by taxpayer dollars . You have to be careful with slippery words . A lot of potentially dangerous words that we 're not really supposed talk about suddenly took an Orwellian turn and became very slippery in the mid to late 20th century . In the USSR , the state did most of the work . In the US , a bunch of words suddenly took on their opposite meaning in the 70s , around when the Charles Koch Foundation / CATO institute spawned , with a pretty successful , privately - funded [ public relations ] ( URL_7 ) effort . So now , you 've got ads on youtube telling everyone that classical liberalism is Ayn Rand and that Adam Smith was head - over - heels in love with division of labor . If you want to understand what words like libertarian , liberal , conservative , anarchist , or anything like that mean in their historically faithful context , a good rule of thumb is to assume their * exact opposite meaning * and then make some small adjustments as you dig into the etymology and history . It saves a lot of headaches . There 's a lot more to this , but that 's gist of it . [ Here 's ] ( URL_8 ) a good speech that explains it much better and connects a lot of the dots .
well thanks for taking the time to help edumacate me . I think I get it now .
rejected:
Sure! Socialism and liberalism are two different ways of thinking about how a society should be organized and how it should take care of its people.

Socialism is a system where the government owns and controls certain resources, like factories and land, and makes decisions about how they should be used. The idea behind socialism is that everyone in society should have equal access to these resources, and that the wealth generated from them should be shared more equally among all people.

Liberalism is a system where people are free to make their own choices and decisions, and where the government is less involved in controlling resources. Instead, the government is responsible for protecting people's rights and ensuring that there is a level playing field for everyone. The idea behind liberalism is that people should be able to pursue their own goals and interests, as long as they do not harm others.

So, in short, socialism is a system that focuses on equal access and sharing resources, while liberalism is a system that focuses on individual freedom and personal responsibility.

These laws apply to companies and individuals in China, just as they do in other countries. If a company in China were to copy a product without permission, they could be sued for infringing on the patents or other intellectual property rights of the original manufacturer.

The World Trade Organization (WTO) is an international organization that helps to regulate international trade. One of the ways it does this is by establishing rules for how countries should protect intellectual property. These rules are designed to ensure that companies and individuals who create new products or inventions are able to benefit from their work, and to encourage innovation.

So, while China is a major player in the global market for manufactured goods, it is not accurate to say that they are able to freely copy products without consequences. Instead, China, like other countries, has laws in place to protect intellectual property and follows international rules established by the WTO to ensure fair competition in the global market.

query: Please explain what is "European Commission"
chosen:
The European Commission (EC) is the executive of the European Union (EU). It operates as a cabinet government, with 27 members of the Commission (informally known as "Commissioners") headed by a President. It includes an administrative body of about 32,000 European civil servants. The Commission is divided into departments known as Directorates-General (DGs) that can be likened to departments or ministries each headed by a Director-General who is responsible to a Commissioner.
There is one member per member state, but members are bound by their oath of office to represent the general interest of the EU as a whole rather than their home state. The Commission President (currently Ursula von der Leyen) is proposed by the European Council (the 27 heads of state/governments) and elected by the European Parliament. The Council of the European Union then nominates the other members of the Commission in agreement with the nominated President, and the 27 members as a team are then subject to a vote of approval by the European Parliament. The current Commission is the Von der Leyen Commission, which took office in December 2019, following the European Parliament elections in May of the same year.
The governmental powers of the Commission have been such that some, including former Belgian Prime Minister Guy Verhofstadt, have suggested changing its name to the "European Government", calling the present name of the Commission "ridiculous", likening it to a misnomer.
rejected:
The European Commission is the executive branch of the European Union (EU), a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe. The European Commission is responsible for proposing legislation, implementing decisions, and enforcing EU laws. It is also responsible for managing the EU's budget and representing the EU in international negotiations.



The European Commission is headquartered in Brussels, Belgium, and is composed of a team of commissioners, each responsible for a specific policy area. The commissioners are appointed by the member states of the EU and are responsible for proposing and implementing EU laws and policies in their respective areas of expertise. The European Commission also has a number of other bodies and agencies that assist it in its work, such as the European Medicines Agency and the European Environment Agency.



Overall, the European Commission plays a key role in shaping the direction and policies of the EU and in ensuring that EU laws and policies are implemented effectively.

query: Is there a lower threshold for new EU VAT changes coming 1 Jan 2015 related to the sale of digital goods?
chosen: Been digging through all the EU VAT directives and have called HMRC as well.. There does not seem to be any lower threshhold for charging VAT into the EU. If you sell £10 of goods/services you have to charge VAT and file a VAT return. Your options are: 1) Register for MOSS and file a single VAT return in your home country for all countries. In the UK this means that you also have to be VAT registered and have to charge VAT locally as well - even if you are below the UK threshold. 2) Register and file a VAT return in every EU country you sell into.  You also have to apply the correct VAT rate for each country (typically 15% to 27%), and you have to keep at least two pieces of evidence for the customer location. eg. billing address, IP address, etc.

query: Why are companies removing the head phone jack ? The only reason I can think of is to sell wireless headphones , but I do n't that 's worth taking something away from the customer . I just want to know why . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Well , luckily it actually is n't very widespread yet but here 's the gist . Prior to computer created sound files or digital sound . Sound was recorded via analog . That means engineers used electronic or mechanical means to capture and transfer sound . These are called analog technology . For example , a tape player is * analog * . It plays back sound through mechanical means . Meanwhile , your computer plays * digital * sound . This is encoded data that the computer reads and translates into sound . Well , almost all the consumer wired headphones are * analog * technology . However , our phones and the music we play is obviously * digital * . That means phones need DACs installed in the phone . These are digital to analog converters . Compared to other cell phone parts , they are typically much larger and I presume there 's a physical limitation to how small they can make them . Also DACs can have a significant effect on sound quality . It 's like having someone interpret spanish to you that is n't very good vs a native speaker . Some things may get lost in translation sound wise with a bad DAC . Well the way to get significant space to make the phone smaller , and possibly conserve battery life would be to eliminate the DAC and headphone jack altogether . In addition , more phones are competing for Water resistant certifications . And the headphone jack presents a challenge because it needs to be able to conduct electricity yet , resist water damage . Lastly , less analog parts and less ports means less maintenance issues . One more port and the DAC means one more chance for something to put the phone out of commission . The downside though , is that digital headphones are n't prevelant and not nearly as cheap . Obviously , having sound go through same port as charging is not ideal . There is n't a standard that all manufacturers can agree on for digital headphones . This leaves Apple to charge a premium price for proprietary technology . Apple has a rich tradition of not clinging to tradition . If they see something that represents holding back future change , they do n't mind dropping it . They also are n't nearly as afraid to require customers to buy proprietary technology because they have a business model that supports this dynamic with their customer . Plus their market segment does not include customers looking for the best bargain . That gives Apple alot of flexibility .
> I do n't that 's worth taking something away from the customer . The mistake you 're making is thinking anyone gives a shit about the customer . You 're 100 % right . Removing the headphone jack forces people to replace the stock headphones with another $ 65 set of stock headphones rather than some $ 5 earbuds .
The headphone jack is fairly large , so it frees up room for other pieces . It 's also apparently relatively susceptible to water damage . But yeah , it also sells new headphones , and Apple has a history of making old accessories obsolete . Consumers also seem to reward their change for changes ' sake , to a degree . I 'm not sure I 'd say " companies " , as it has n't become widespread yet , and there are still some technical challenges . It 's mostly just Apple , and a few companies copying Apple . the headphone jack is probably pretty safe until those tech issues get ironed out .
rejected:
The main reason that some companies are removing the headphone jack from their devices is to make them thinner and more lightweight. This is because the headphone jack takes up a lot of space inside the device, and by removing it, the device can be made smaller and more portable.

Another reason is that some companies believe that wireless technology has improved to the point where it can provide a similar or better listening experience than wired headphones. This means that people can still use headphones with their devices, but they will need to use a wireless connection instead of a wired one.

Finally, some companies may also be removing the headphone jack in order to encourage people to use their own brand of wireless headphones, which they may sell separately. This can be a way for the company to make more money.

Overall, the decision to remove the headphone jack is driven by a combination of factors, including the desire to make devices thinner and lighter, the improvement of wireless technology, and the potential for companies to make more money by selling their own brand of wireless headphones.

query: Please explain what is "Computer network"
chosen:
A computer network is a set of computers sharing resources located on or provided by network nodes. The computers use common communication protocols over digital interconnections to communicate with each other. These interconnections are made up of telecommunication network technologies, based on physically wired, optical, and wireless radio-frequency methods that may be arranged in a variety of network topologies.
The nodes of a computer network can include personal computers, servers, networking hardware, or other specialised or general-purpose hosts. They are identified by network addresses, and may have hostnames. Hostnames serve as memorable labels for the nodes, rarely changed after initial assignment. Network addresses serve for locating and identifying the nodes by communication protocols such as the Internet Protocol.
Computer networks may be classified by many criteria, including the transmission medium used to carry signals, bandwidth, communications protocols to organize network traffic, the network size, the topology, traffic control mechanism, and organizational intent.
Computer networks support many applications and services, such as access to the World Wide Web, digital video, digital audio, shared use of application and storage servers, printers, and fax machines, and use of email and instant messaging applications.
rejected:
A computer network is a group of computers that are connected to each other for the purpose of sharing resources, exchanging files, and allowing communication. The computers in a network can be connected through various methods, such as through cables or wirelessly, and they can be located in the same place or in different locations.



Networks can be classified into different types based on their size, the distance between the computers, and the type of connections used. For example, a local area network (LAN) is a network that connects computers in a small area, such as an office or a home. A wide area network (WAN) is a network that connects computers over a large geographical area, such as across cities or even countries.



Networks can also be classified based on their topology, which refers to the way the computers are connected. Some common network topologies include a star topology, where all the computers are connected to a central hub or switch; a bus topology, where all the computers are connected to a central cable; and a ring topology, where the computers are connected in a circular pattern.



Networks are an important part of modern computing and allow computers to share resources and communicate with each other, enabling the exchange of information and the creation of distributed systems.

query: How did the idea of swear words come to be and why are they considered bad and rude words ? [ Edit ] Wow thanks for all the answers guys , I did NOT flipping expect thing to be on the front page ! Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
In every language I know , the swears are either religion - based , family / ancestry - based , or based on private body functions . It would not work to make - up random words and say " ok , these are offensive now " . Religion and body - functions work because you are taking something very private , personal , and essential to a person and figuratively throwing it in the gutter . In other words , the context of the curse is important . " Jesus " or " fuck " are offensive when used as a curse specifically because they also sacred or special .
I actually did a brief research project on the history of english swearing . The Romans are the ones who globalized the idea of " swears " or reserved , inappropriate language . The latin ' profanus ' , from which ' profane ' derives , literally meant ' outside of the temple ' - Romans would shoot their mouths off before entering the sacred space . In english - speaking societies , it was understood by the Latin speaking clergy that certain words were unfit for print in official church records - and yet mixed Latin - English versions of curses still made it into a few . For most everyone else , they cursed often and there was n't nearly as much of a fuss about it . " Fuck " only referred to the act of copulation , and " cunt " was used strictly to refer to the labia or vagina . This is n't to say that this language was n't considered harsh or offensive , but that it was spoken frequently enough in an indicative context that reduces the likelihood of the existence of any social taboo surrounding the acts or the words . Similarly , before public sanitation takes off , there is nothing profane or insulting about mentioning someone else 's defecation or urination - it 's just " shit . " And it 's everywhere . The very beginning of the conception of swears we see today starts around the invention of the printing press . We know from Chaucer - the most highly reputed English author pre - printing press , whose works were some of the first to be widely printed - that cursing was common , as his novels commonly provided realistic ( read : explicit ) dialogue between characters from different parts of England . As the craft of writing develops in the early Enlightenment we start to see far more euphemism , dirty word play , double entendre , and similar devices ( " making the beast with two backs " ) to allude to these topics more lightly . By Shakespeare 's time , there is definitely social taboo surrounding these curses , as none are explicitly mentioned in any of his plays . An important note : At this point these words are considered inappropriate - but they are not yet insults . If you called someone a " fuck " in the 1600s in England - they would have no idea how to interpret that other than your body language and tone . More on this later . Continuing from Shakespeare - English writing becomes more and more prudish in its choice of words through the Victorian Era . Some new , creative applications of old swears start to appear - " fucked " or " getting fucked " gets the meaning of " being taken advantage of " in the 1700s for the first time . Overall , the taboo of these phrases only increases in polite & distinguished society , though elsewhere people probably cursed as much as always . The next turning point is the Industrial Revolution . Here cursing starts to really take off - popular swears begin to take on different parts of speech and they start being used as direct insults rather than just chatter about inappropriate contexts ( " You fucking fuck ! " ) . Fast forward to the world wars , and swearing has become the most popular thing on the planet . Basically creative measures of cursing explode to the point that we start losing track of them all - " bullshit " was Australian or New Zealand soldiers making fun of the British for their keen intent on shining or " bulling " their boots . Other compound curses - " motherfucker , clusterfuck , etc . " appear around this time of bloodshed and frustration with nationalist governments . Basically these wars are responsible for the " is my child or my boss around ? no ? well fuck this then " attitude , and the multitude of curses we can draw from . Is cursing still taboo ? To an extent ... it 's just that our instantaneous communication makes it so that you see it a lot more than you would , and makes it less shocking . Also , cursing does increase your pain tolerance , and there is some interesting information about aphasia ( loss of speech ) and cursing . Patients who have brain injuries that leave them unable to speak , find it easiest to start speaking again through cursing . This and other evidence that curses are linked with the emotional centers of the brain more so than other words suggests that profanity has a lasting purpose in our society - to reduce stress and pain . There are studies that go into Tourette 's and aphasia more to figure out what the neurobiological triggers of cursing are and what it can tell us about our behavioral predispositions . TLDR : ... - 1400 ~ cursing is understood as disrespect to the gods ( or god ) , people curse a lot 1400 - 1600 ~ more educated elements of society start using euphemism , cursing slows 1600 - 1850 ~ growing middle classes begin to take up the taboos of the wealthy , cursing slows more , though creativity and number of curses grows 1850 - present ~ everyone curses however the fuck they want to and most people do n't give a shit
I can contribute to this one ! It depends on the language . For example the Dutch use diseases with kanker ( Cancer ) being the worst one . Kankerzoot ( I think ) is one of the worst things you can say . You 'd say like like we say " fuck ! " when something bad happens . I think the French use some kind of religious based thing . Anyway , English was a Germanic language at one point , then England was invaded by people who spoke a Latin language . Once that happened the language of the royalty and high society was this Latin language because they were the conquerors and now ruled everything . Over time these two languages began to blend together but we still had two words for most things ; one Germanic , and one Latin . For example we have vagina ( a Latin word ) and cunt ( a Germanic word ) . The Germanic word is the bad one , essentially because using the Germanic word meant you were uneducated and lower class . Do this for long enough , boom : English swear words . Hopefully that 's an a good ELI5 answer . If you want to know who invaded and when , Google is your friend here or maybe go ask /r / history . TL;DR : Two versions of the same word . Germanic is bad ; Latin is good .
rejected:
Swear words, also known as profanity or obscenities, are words that are considered offensive or taboo because they are generally considered to be rude, vulgar, or insulting. The origins of swear words can be traced back to the earliest human societies, where certain words and phrases were used to express strong emotions or to convey disrespect or aggression. 
One theory is that swear words originally evolved as a way for people to vent their anger or frustration without physically harming others. Swearing may have also served as a way to bond with others and build solidarity, as people who swore together may have been seen as belonging to the same social group. 
Over time, certain words and phrases came to be considered inappropriate or offensive in many cultures and societies, and using them in public or in polite conversation became taboo. In some cases, this may be due to the words being associated with particular groups or activities that are considered taboo or shameful. In other cases, the taboo nature of certain words may simply be due to their association with strong emotions or with disrespectful or aggressive behavior. 
In general, it is considered rude to use swear words in public or in polite conversation because they can be offensive or disturbing to others, and because they can reflect poorly on the person using them. However, there is often a great deal of cultural and personal variation in what is considered to be a swear word, and what is considered acceptable language.

query: Waterjet cutting / Abrasive Waterjet I like watching videos from URL_1 , but still have no acctual idea of how it works . P.S. URL_0 did not help :) Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Water has mass , so if you move it quickly it has momentum too . Mix a slurry of small , hard particles into the water and the water will force them into the material it is directed at , causing it to erode . A water jet machine will push water out of a small nozzle at extreme force and speed , carrying very hard particles which take tiny amounts of material off the target at a time . In total though it cuts through most things with ease .
Same as sandblasting , except mixed with water and concentrated into a fine point . Instead of the sandblast spot being several inches in diameter and taking off a few millimeters of thickness with each pass , the waterjet is a few millimeters in diameter and can punch all the way through the material in one pass . If you 've ever used a regular pressure washer , you know that you could use the fine pinpoint tip to gouge into wood at only ~1500 - 3000 PSI . Now raise that up to 50,000 PSI and make the pinpoint even smaller , and boom you have waterjet technology .
If you raise the pressure in a cylinder of water to 50,000psi , then pass it through a tiny hole in a sapphire , it will achieve a speed of Mach 3 , or about 1000 meters per second . It will be loud . This tiny stream of water will be stable for a few inches , meaning after several inches it will turn into a fast moving mist instead of a cylindrical stream . BUT ! If you surround this stream with a cylinder when it first comes out of the tiny hole , it will create a vacuum around the column of water . This vacuum is used to suck tiny bits of garnet from a gravity feed plastic tube , and the garnet cuts through whatever is below it . The garnet surrounds the column of water , rather than mixing with it . This defines the width of the cut , or the kerf . It 's a wonderful tool for cutting hard or abrasive materials , but only up to a certain thickness . If the material to be cut is too thick , the stream will sculpt it instead of cutting it , leaving ragged , wavy edges . SOURCE : I ran a 3 - axis waterjet making space flight hardware for two years in a previous career .
rejected: Sure! Waterjet cutting is a process that uses a high-pressure stream of water mixed with an abrasive material to cut through different materials. This is done by using a machine that shoots out the water at very high speeds, kind of like how a hose shoots out water when you turn it on full blast.The water is mixed with an abrasive material like sand or tiny bits of metal to help it cut through hard materials like metal or stone. The water and abrasive mixture is shot out through a small hole in a nozzle, and it cuts through the material like a saw blade.Waterjet cutting is used to cut through a variety of materials, including metal, plastic, stone, and even food. It's a very precise way to cut materials, and it doesn't create a lot of heat or dust, which makes it a clean and safe process.I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.

query: What causes those pulled neck muscles that happen from doing nothing like yawning or rolling in your sleep ? Those pulled muscles that last for a few days but basically come out of nowhere : Is it a diet thing ? Just a luck of the draw ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Physiotherapist here ! ELI5 : It can be the position our neck is in at night or an injury from earlier in the day . Less ELI5 : 1 ) Because many of our normal pain responses and reflexes are inhibited during sleep , we may find ourselves in a position of stress on the joints in the neck to which the stimulus to move does not exceed the necessary threshold for waking up . Sleeping on your stomach or with too many or too few pillows can cause this ; try to have a pillow large / small enough that your head sits in ' neutral ' or in the middle when you lay on your side . 2 ) More commonly , stress occurred on the neck the day before . Many people who experience whiplash from a motor vehicle accident will have a delayed pain / inflammatory response . This likely is due to the mechanics of the synovial joints in our spine . It may be a harmless move in the wrong direction or hours stressed in one position , but many people attribute this morning stiffness erroneously to ' sleeping wrong ' . We call this an ' acute torticollis ' - although generally this is a misnomer as many people wo n't actually have an altered head position . The neck is a complicated system of joints , muscles , and stability , so there 's no one answer on how to avoid ' sleeping wrong ' , but one good exercise is strengthening the deep neck flexors longus colli & capitus , which pull the occiput ( skull ) into a bit of flexion ( bent position ) and provide tension and stability for the entire neck . URL_0 . Do n't do this if you get pain during !
Another reason can be bruxism . The nightly grinding of the teeth can cause the the neck muscles to tense up . If it is strong enough it is visible on worn down teeth . Bruxism can be caused by e.g. stress or also by other rem sleep disorders .
Most people get it from sleeping sitting up or sleeping in a bad neck position . If you 're getting it just from yawning you might need to get your posture checked and some exercises . It 's not a muscle it 's a nerve pinch .
rejected: Pulled neck muscles can happen when the muscles in your neck are stretched or strained beyond their normal range of motion. This can happen when you move your neck in a way that it is not used to, such as when you yawn or roll over in your sleep. It can also happen if you have poor posture, or if you spend long periods of time in one position, such as when you are working at a computer or driving for an extended period of time.\n\nA pulled neck muscle is not usually caused by diet or luck, but it can be more likely to happen if you are tired or stressed, or if you have weak or tight muscles in your neck. To prevent pulled neck muscles, it is important to maintain good posture, take breaks to stretch and move around if you are sitting for long periods of time, and to engage in regular exercise to strengthen the muscles in your neck and upper back. If you do experience a pulled neck muscle, it is important to rest and apply ice to the affected area to reduce swelling and discomfort.

query: What is a mathematical proof ? Like , is " 2 = 2 " a proof that two equals two ? How can math prove anything ? ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Mathematical proofs usually are n't simply arithmetic . It 's often formalized logic trying to prove a statement about a set of numbers or the behavior of certain aspects of math that requires certain steps to show that the statement is true or not . So , for example : let me prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers . I will show this with a proof by contradiction . I assume the opposite is true , get a contradiction , which ca n't happen in the world of math , meaning my assumption about the opposite being true must be false , making my original proposition true . So , let me start off by assuming the there are actually FINITELY many prime numbers , let 's say n of them . I 'll label them p1 , p2 , p3 , ... , pn . Now , let me construct this new number : p = p1 * p2 * p3 * ... * pn + 1 . Because I added one to the product of all these prime numbers , I know that p1 does n't divide evenly into it ( there 's a remainder of 1 ) . Same thing with p2 , p3 , p4 , ... pn . None of my previous n prime numbers divides evenly into it . That makes this new p , a prime number . But , I just said there are only n prime numbers . This means the statement " there are FINITELY many prime numbers " is false . This is equivalent to " there are an infinite number of prime numbers " . This is how many mathematical proofs work , although they usually involve more Greek letters and fancy notation .
A proof is a sequence of statements that logically follow from each other in order to reach a theorized conclusion . Proofs are based on what we call " axioms " the things that are so fundamental to the mathematical system you 're working in , that you can assume them to be true without proof . Sets of axioms vary depending on what kind of math you 're doing . Sometimes proofs are based instead on the results of other , more basic proofs . Here 's an example of a fairly simple proof using a process we call " induction " : I assert that for any whole number * n * , the sum of all numbers 1 + 2 + 3+ ... +n is equal to n\*(n+1)/2 . You can pick any * n * you want and verify that this is true , but how can we * * prove * * that it is true for * * every * * single number * n * without checking the formula for every single number ? Here 's where induction comes in . Let 's establish a base case with n=1 . The sum is just 1 . The formula n\*(n+1)/2 with 1 plugged in for n turns out to be 1\*(1 + 1)/2=1\*2/2=1 . Cool so our base case is good and true . Now let 's * assume * that the formula holds for some random number * k * . We 're going to show that it has to hold for the next number * k+1 * . Our assumption that the formula holds for * k * means that we assume 1 + 2 + 3+ ... +k = k\*(k+1)/2 . Now lets examine it for * k+1 * . First we 'll look at the sum 1 + 2 + 3+ ... +k+(k+1 ) . We want this to be equal to ( k+1)\*(k+2)/2 as per our formula . Well check out the first part of the sum ( the part in square brackets ): [ 1 + 2 + 3+ ... +k]+(k+1 ) . That 's the part we assumed followed the formula , so we can substitute the square brackets for k\*(k+1)/2 . So 1 + 2 + 3+ ... +k+(k+1)=k\*(k+1)/2 + ( k+1 ) . Let 's write the ( k+1 ) term as 2\*(k+1)/2 so we can add the two fractions with a common denominator . We get ( k\*(k+1 ) + 2\*(k+1))/2 . Or , more simply , ( k+1)\*(k+2)/2 just like we wanted ! So here 's what we did . We showed that something was true in a very simple case . Then we showed that if you assume the result is true for any case , then it follows that the result is true in the next case as well . In other words , if it 's true for n=1 ( which we proved it is ) , then it is true for n=2 . If it 's true for n=2 , it 's true for n=3 etc . A couple of lines of a proof just showed something to be true * * for every single whole number 1 and bigger * * . That 's some powerful stuff . Of course not all proofs are of the induction kind . I just wanted to show an example of proving something big without too much complexity . Source : Minor in mathematics .
A logical proof is an * argument * such that , if its * premises * are true , then its * conclusion * must also be true . Proofs are conducted according to certain rules that mathematicians accept will ensure that the truth of the premises guarantees the truth of the conclusion . Premises and conclusions are what logicians call * propositions * : statements that must be either true or false . So " The Earth is a sphere " is a proposition ( a false one , strictly speaking ) , but " What time is it ? " is n't one , nor is " the dog . " > Like , is " 2 = 2 " a proof that two equals two ? No ... and yes . This is like asking whether " yes " is a sentence . No , because " yes " is a word ... but yes , because there 's also a sentence whose only word is " yes " . In the case of " 2 = 2 " : 1 . " 2 = 2 " is mathematical notation for the proposition that two equals two . 2 . But also , one of the proofs that two equals two consists simply of the proposition " 2 = 2 " . One useful way to explain this topic is to step away from mathematics for a minute , and into philosophy rather . A topic in modern philosophy is * identity propositions * , and one of the classic examples is the following proposition : [ " The Morning Star is the Evening Star . " ] ( URL_0 ) Quoting from the link : > The ancient Greeks and Egyptians thought that Venus was actually two separate objects , a morning star and an evening star . The Greeks called the morning star Phosphoros , “ the bringer of light ” ; and they called the evening star Hesperos , “ the star of the evening ” . A few hundred years later , the Hellenistic Greeks realized that Venus was actually a single object . An equation like " 15,348 + 24,334 = 19,841 × 2 " is similar to the sentence " the Morning Star is the Evening Star"—in both cases , for the proposition to be true , the two * names * must refer to * the same object * . The difference is that in the Venus case we 're talking about objects in space , while in the mathematical example we 're talking about numbers . So " 15,348 + 24,334 = 19,841 × 2 " is true if and only if " 15,348 + 24,334 " and " 19,841 × 2 " are two ways of naming the same number . The way we prove it is by using the rules of arithmetic , which allow us to rewrite these " names " into the same number : 1 . Start with the equation 15,348 + 24,334 = 19,841 × 2 2 . Do the addition , and rewrite the equation to use the result : 39,682 = 19,841 × 2 3 . Do the multiplication , and rewrite the equation to use the result : 39,682 = 39,682 The last equation in that proof is true because the proof rules that mathematicians use for equality accept as true any equation of the form " x = x " . This is what I meant when I said earlier that " 2 = 2 " is both a proposition and a proof ; if the first step of your proof is " 2 = 2 " , then you 're already done , and that 's the only step of your proof ...
rejected:
A mathematical proof is a series of logical steps that shows that a statement is true. For example, a proof that two equals two might start by saying: "We know that 1 plus 1 equals 2. And if we have two of something, that means we have 1 plus 1 of that thing. Therefore, two equals two."

So a proof is like a recipe that shows that if we start with certain things that we know are true (called axioms), and we follow certain steps (called theorems), we can end up with a new statement that we want to prove is also true.

In math, we use proof to show that certain statements are true no matter what. This is different from how we use evidence in other parts of life, where we might use it to show that something is probably true, but we can't be completely sure. In math, we can use proof to be completely sure that something is true.

query: Value of a call option spread
chosen: The Explanation is correct. The Traders buys the 1st call and profits linearly form 40$ onwards. At at 45 the short call kick in and neutralizes any further profit on the first call.
rejected: A call option spread is an options strategy in which a trader buys and sells call options with the same expiration date, but with different strike prices. The value of a call option spread will depend on a number of factors, including the strike prices of the options, the underlying stock price, the time remaining until expiration, and the implied volatility of the options.To calculate the value of a call option spread, you can use the Black-Scholes option pricing model or a similar model. These models take into account the factors mentioned above to determine the fair value of the options. The difference between the value of the call option that you sell and the value of the call option that you buy is the net value of the option spread. If the value of the call option that you sell is greater than the value of the call option that you buy, then the option spread will have a positive value. If the value of the call option that you sell is less than the value of the call option that you buy, then the option spread will have a negative value.

query: Do dreams really have " meanings " behind them ? Can they really represent your behaviours whilst awake ? Any evidence to suggest otherwise ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
No offense or anything , but this is better in /r / askreddit . There is no clear , ELI5 explanation on this topic . If you do n't get traction here , or it gets removed , post it to /r / askreddit . It 'd be interesting to see the discussion .
Not all dreams have meanings . Most of them are throwaways . They 're your brain blowing off steam . Reoccurring themes * can * be your brain trying to cope or somehow make sense of issues in your day - to - day life . Not everybody dreams in the same way , so it is hard to say emphatically that , for example , the appearance of a unicorn in your dream means the same thing as a unicorn appearing in someone else 's dream . If anything , the meaning you should take is that , if you seem to be having reoccurring dreams , it could be an indication of uncertainty in some aspect of your life . But how your mind relates that distress to you is not standardized in any way .
Like most of what goes on in your mind , dreams draw on memories and emotions . As a result , dreams can reflect past life events and feelings . There has been no conclusive evidence that there are any set patterns in dreams that represent certain things in every day life that are consistent across multiple people . For instance , watching the sun get eaten by a giant moose in a dream does n't mean that you are having second feelings about eating that last slice of pie , although it could certainly be a combination of a moose you saw the other day .

query: Why does AAPL trade at such low multiples?
chosen: This is an opinion, but I think it has more to do with the market's uncertainty about the long-term future of the company without Steve Jobs.  Apple hasn't released anything more than incremental upgrades to its existing product lines since Jobs passed, and while some people would argue about the Apple watch, Jobs played a significant role in its development prior to his death, so that doesn't really count. Whether you like or hate Apple, you had to admire Jobs' passion and creativity, and there's real question as to whether the company can sustain its dominance in the market without the Jobs vision over the long haul.  My guess is that the market is leaning slightly toward the "no" column, but only ever so slightly.  The company continues to deliver fantastic results, but how long will that last of their next products don't wow consumers the way previous ones have?  This skepticism manifests itself in a stock that trades at a lower P/E than it deserves to, but this is just my opinion.   I hope this helps. Good luck!
rejected: It is not accurate to say that Apple Inc. (AAPL) trades at low multiples. In fact, Apple has consistently traded at relatively high price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios, which are a common measure of a company's valuation. A P/E ratio is calculated by dividing the current market price of a company's stock by its earnings per share (EPS). A high P/E ratio can indicate that investors are willing to pay a premium for the company's earnings, while a low P/E ratio may suggest that the stock is undervalued.There are a number of factors that can influence a company's P/E ratio, including its financial performance, growth prospects, and risk profile. In the case of Apple, the company has a long track record of strong financial performance, with consistent revenue and earnings growth. It is also a leader in the technology sector, which is known for its high growth potential. These factors may contribute to Apple's relatively high P/E ratio.It is also important to note that P/E ratios can vary significantly over time, and can be influenced by market conditions and investor sentiment. As a result, it is important to consider a variety of factors when evaluating a company's valuation, rather than relying on a single metric such as the P/E ratio.
