query: Why is there a higher incidence of Down syndrome among older mothers ? I understand that women are born with all the eggs they will ever have and that Down syndrome is caused by an extra chromosome . What happens to add a chromosome to older eggs ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Ovaries release the best condition eggs first , although the process is n't perfect . As time progresses the ratio of good to faulty eggs increases , thus the chances of a faulty egg being released increases .
Each time a female produces an egg , it requires a cell division event . During cell division ( meiosis in this case ) chromosomes are divided up between the new cells . As women age , the probability that a mistake ( also called a nondisjunction ) will occur during this event increases . This may be explained by by a decrease in proteins that regulate how chromosomes are divided up during cell division .
They used to think it was all due to the mother 's eggs being faulty . But they 've subsequently found that it 's often due to faulty sperm of older fathers , since older mothers generally are having babies with men of a similar or older age . Sperm is constantly being made , so it 's copies of copies of copies . The more copies , the more errors can creep in .
rejected:
Down syndrome is a condition that occurs when a person has an extra copy of chromosome 21 in their cells. This extra chromosome can cause physical and developmental delays, and people with Down syndrome may have certain facial features and may be shorter than average.

There are several different ways that Down syndrome can occur. One of the ways it can occur is if an egg or sperm cell has an extra copy of chromosome 21 when it combines with the other egg or sperm cell to form an embryo. This can happen by chance, and it is not caused by anything the parents did or didn't do.

It is true that the risk of having a baby with Down syndrome increases as a woman gets older. This is because the eggs in a woman's ovaries get older over time, and as the eggs get older, there is a higher risk that they will have an extra copy of chromosome 21. This is because as eggs get older, they may not divide and replicate their DNA as accurately as younger eggs do, which can lead to errors such as an extra chromosome.

So to explain it in a way that a five-year-old might understand: every person has a certain number of "building blocks" inside their cells called chromosomes. Most people have the right number of chromosomes, but sometimes, a baby can be born with an extra one. When this happens, it's called Down syndrome. It's not something that the parents did or didn't do - it just happens sometimes by chance. And as a woman gets older, the chance of having a baby with Down syndrome gets higher because the eggs inside her body are getting older and might not work as well as they used to.

query: How does time dilation work ? Why should you age slower if you 're moving faster ? I never understood this . Do you actually experience less time , or do you just age slower ? For either of these options , why ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
* They * actually experience less time , from * your * point of view . The important thing to remember is that the speed of light ( in a vacuum ) is always the same . Now imagine a simple type of clock , a beam of light bouncing between to equally spaced mirrors . * * * * * | | * * * * * Since the speed of light is the same , the time it takes to go from the top to the bottom is the same each time , one tick of the clock . Now imagine this clock is moving : * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * -- > \ / \ / \ \ / \ / \ * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * Since light * always * moves at the same speed , and the diagonal lines are longer , it takes longer for light to go from the top to the bottom , it has longer ticks . But , what if you are moving at the same speed as the moving mirror ? Then , the light will look like it 's going straight up and down again , and since light * always * moves at the same speed , the ticks will be back to their original size ! So if I am on the ground with my own clock , watching you run with your clock , I will see that it takes longer for each tick on your clock compared to my clock . Since both clocks are working properly , I must conclude that time itself is moving slower for you . Now for the * really * confusing part . From your point of view , light on your clock is moving straight up and down , but the light on my clock has to go backwards diagonally , so you will similarly conclude that time is moving more slowly for me ! And we would both be right ! This is why it is called the theory of * relativity * , because the relative point of view you are talking about makes a * huge * difference .
[ Al 's Relativistic Adventures ] ( URL_0 ) Fits perfectly with the theme of this subreddit . How I finally came to understand compression of space and time . Also , it 's interactive . Do n't forget to print your Diploma at the end of it .
I 've always thought of it as the speed of light is constant and can never change but all of us are moving at the speed of light through the 4 dimensions of our universe ( the three dimensions that we have and the 4th , time ) Since we ca n't break the speed of light , the faster we move through the 3 dimensions of space , the slower we move through the 4th dimension , time . That 's why it 's called space / time and is all related to that . Also , they 've found that particles of light ( because they are moving at the speed of light duh haha ) do n't age at all and have n't aged since they 've been created because of this constant movement at the speed of light . Gravity itself is just a bending of space / time through by a massive body ( technically , everything with mass produces gravity because we all have to occupy space / time ) Basically , imagine a sheet suspended in the air by its four corners . You introduce a bowling ball to that sheet , it will warp the sheet due to its mass which is what planets do to space / time . This is also something that Einstein discovered through relativity .
rejected: Time dilation is a phenomenon that occurs when two objects are in relative motion to each other. It means that time appears to pass at different rates for different objects, depending on their relative speeds. To understand this, let's imagine that you are standing on a platform and your friend is standing on a train that is moving very fast past the platform. If you look at your friend, it will seem to you that they are moving very quickly, and that time is passing more slowly for them than it is for you. This is because, from your perspective, your friend's movements are slowed down, and so it appears to you that time is passing more slowly for them. But from your friend's perspective, it is you who are moving, and it is your movements that are slowed down. From their perspective, it is you who are aging more slowly, because time appears to be passing more quickly for them. So, to sum up, time dilation is all about how time appears to pass at different rates for different objects, depending on their relative speeds. And this is due to the fact that, from different perspectives, different objects will appear to be moving, and it is the movement of these objects that affects how quickly time appears to pass.

query: Algo Trading I 've always wondered how being 5 microseconds quicker to market can make companies and hedge fund managers literally billions of dollars . Is this gaming the system somehow ? Please explain like I 'm five . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Imagine Alice has a toy she wants to sell for $ 10 . Bob wants to buy the same toy , and is willing to spend $ 11 on it . If Alice and Bob find each other and talk , Bob will end up buying the toy for $ 10.50 and they 'll both be better off . Now , imagine that Hector the hedge fund manager is running around really quickly and talks to Alice and Bob before they talk to each other . Hector quickly buys the toy from Alice for $ 10 , then runs and sells it to Bob for $ 11 . Hector just made $ 1 . The next day , Hector 's sister Harriet starts her own hedge fund and tries to do the same thing . Now Hector does n't just need to be faster than Alice and Bob , he also needs to be faster than Harriet .
Well assuming your transaction costs are negligible , which means it does nt cost you anything to buy or sell a stock , it 's pretty easy . Here 's a very simplified example : let 's say you work for a hedge fund and you have a program that scans news reports looking for a company 's name and the words " good " or " bad " . If you find the company and the word " good " , you buy 10,000 shares of stock . Same thing with " bad " , except you sell . Now , there 's also 20 other hedge funds doing the same exact thing . Since in the short term ( very short ) a stock 's price is based on supply and demand , if there are suddenly 21 orders , each for 10,000 shares of a stock , it 's going to make the price go up . So , if you 're the first one to buy shares , you get to ride the wave as all the others come in and drive the stock up . If you 're late , it probably does n't make a whole lot of sense to buy as the price has already gone up about all it can support . You were hoping to get in quick , watch it go up , and then sell it and take a quick profit . We 're talking buying and selling within minutes or less .
Hedge funds and algo trading are basically entirely separate type of businesses . Algo trading takes advantages of mis - pricings of securities ; think of stocks for now . The thing about the stock market is there are multiple ways you can order . Let 's try to think of something really simple . One type of order is a " market order " ; this just says " I want to buy 10,000 shares , give me 10,000 shares at the best price in the market . " Now , in the market , maybe the best offer someone is giving is 30.50 , but they only have 1,000 at this price . But maybe someone else has another 800 at 30.55 , another person has 750 at 30.60 , and so on . So you come in and buy up 10,000 all at once , and you sweep up through a bunch of different prices , taking up all the shares these people are offering at these shares . Now , usually what will happen is someone will come in , sweep up all these orders , and the best offer might be something something 31.20 , even though the best bid someone has out there is still 30.45 ( A bid is someone wanting to buy a stock , an offer is someone wanting to sell a stock ; so if you come in and want to buy up a bunch immediately , you have to buy it from someone offering it , and the reverse if you want to sell ) . One approach of an algorithm might be : Look , this stock averages about 30.50 , and does n't move that much . If all of a sudden the price shoots up to 30.65 or more , I know this is just temporary spike because there 's a bunch of people trying to buy and not enough people offering in the market , so I can sell to those people trying to buy at 30.65 , undercutting all those other people who were at 30.70 or more that would 've been hit by the 10k order earlier , and once the price settles , I can buy back at 30.50 and make 15 cents a share . This is n't that much , but small little spikes like this happen all the time , and you make small bits of money on millions of shares , since you are n't quite as exposed to as much risk as someone who holds stock for months or years is , because you are holding the stocks for seconds . And this is beneficial for the people buying , too ! Now , the guy who used to come in with 10k and had to pay an average price of 30.80 after all was said and done , he never pays more than 30.65 , since my algorithm picks up his activity as soon as he enters and is willing to keep selling to him at 30.65 .
rejected: Being just a few microseconds faster than other traders can give a company or hedge fund a significant advantage in the financial markets. This is because financial markets move very quickly, and even a small difference in speed can mean the difference between making a profitable trade or missing out on an opportunity. To understand why this is important, let's imagine that you and a friend are playing a game where you both have to race to grab a toy from a shelf. If you are just a little bit faster than your friend, you will be able to grab the toy before they do. This means that you will get to keep the toy, while your friend will have to look for another one. In the financial markets, the "toy" is often a trade or investment opportunity. If a company or hedge fund is able to execute a trade just a few microseconds faster than its competitors, they will be able to get a better price for the trade and make more money. This is because they are able to buy or sell the asset before the price changes, which can be influenced by other traders entering the market. So, being just a few microseconds faster can give a company or hedge fund a significant advantage in the financial markets and allow them to make more money. It is not necessarily "gaming the system," but it is using technology and speed to gain an advantage in the market.

query: How does a HSA ( Health Savings Account ) work and compare to traditional insurance ? The other answers were n't great and are possibly outdated . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
HSA is a bank account . You put your own money in it pre - tax and it can be used for medical expenses . Your employer can also put money in it . It 's not insurance by itself , but usually a part of a coverage plan .
essentially when you contribute money into a HSA and use it for medical , you 're getting a 25 - 30 % discount on prices , depending on how much tax rate you pay .
HSAs are savings accounts . They usually are partnered with a high - deductible health plan , and your employer may contribute to the account as well . Your pre - tax deductions go into the account , and you can use the funds for your copays , coinsurance , glasses or contacts , some over - the - counter medicines , and such that your insurance does n't cover and you 'd usually pay for out of pocket . Since most of the money is yours anyway , the big benefit comes from its pre - tax status : the deduction is made from your gross pay before you are taxed on the remainder . So you 're saving off the top , and getting your employer 's contribution , as well . It 's a step up from the old Health Reimbursement Account , because those funds HAD to be used by the end of the year or you 'd simply lose them . The " newfangled " HSAs are different because you can keep those funds and roll them over from year to year , as a savings plan in case you have big medical expenses at some point in the future . You may be expected to provide documentation to the IRS as to your expenditures from that account , though , so save your receipts ! I only use the debit card at pharmacies or medical providers , so that all the debits are clearly identifiable as medical - related expenses . The HSAs are also accounts that you may be able to open wherever you like , so you need to choose wisely . Some banks , like Chase , can charge a monthly fee for these accounts . I use a credit union for mine , so not only do I get a little bit of interest on my balance , I also do n't get charged a fee for the account . I hope that helps !
rejected: A Health Savings Account (HSA) is a type of account that you can use to pay for medical expenses. It is often paired with a high-deductible health plan (HDHP), which is a type of health insurance that has a higher deductible than traditional health insurance plans. The deductible is the amount of money you have to pay for your medical expenses before your insurance starts to cover them. With an HSA, you can contribute money to the account on a tax-free basis. You can use this money to pay for qualifying medical expenses, such as doctor's visits, prescription drugs, and certain types of medical equipment. You can also use the money to pay for your deductible and other out-of-pocket costs associated with your HDHP. One of the main advantages of an HSA is that the money in the account can be used to pay for medical expenses tax-free. This means that you can use the money in your HSA to pay for medical expenses without having to pay taxes on it. This can help you save money on your medical expenses. Another advantage of an HSA is that the money in the account belongs to you. This means that you can keep the money in your HSA even if you change jobs or insurance plans. Overall, an HSA can be a good option for people who want to have more control over their healthcare expenses and save money on taxes. However, it is important to keep in mind that an HSA is paired with an HDHP, which may have a higher deductible than traditional insurance plans. This means that you may have to pay more out of pocket for your medical expenses before your insurance starts to cover them.

query: Which doctors and nurses work in the Intensive Care Unit of a hospital ? Do they all have to be experts in everything ? If the most high risk cases come through the ICU , are there specialists in each area of medicine working there ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:

query: Why gravity pulls in objects By that , I mean , why do objects of greater mass have more gravity ? Why does gravity attract objects in the first place ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Imagine you have a ten pound bowling ball in the center of a trampoline . The bowling ball is heavy enough to make the surface of the trampoline bend down due to it weight . if you were to place tennis ball along the edge of the trampoline where the bend is n't that bad then the tennis ball probably wo n't move , or move pretty slowly towards the center at the start . Now if you placed the tennis ball closer to the bowling ball it will move faster to the bowling ball because of the bend in the trampoline . If you changed the ten pound bowling ball with a hundred pound ball the bend will be deeper and larger . Now imagine the bend in the trampoline is gravity , heavier objects make larger bends in it and can pull in more objects from farther distances closer . The trampoline in this example is space - time .
Think about a suspended rubber sheet . Placing a small marble on it . Now place a smaller marble near it . The two will roll to each other and the speed will depend on the ' heaviness ' of the marble ( weight is not strictly correct here but for the analogy , whatever ) . Now replace the marble with a bowling ball . Place the marble near the vicinity of the bowling ball and one will expect the marble to roll much faster toward the bowling ball because it creates a larger depression . Now , I think the rubber sheet represents space - time or fabric of the universe or some other crap like that not sure that 's way beyond me .
Your question is circular . You 've essentially posed the question " Why are heavier objects heavier ? " why not ask " Why are ducks ducks ? " We , by definition , call objects that are heavier ( more massive ) heavier ..
rejected:
Gravity is the force that pulls objects towards each other. It is what makes things fall down to the ground when you drop them.

The reason why objects with greater mass have more gravity is because they have more mass, which means they have more matter packed into them. The more matter an object has, the more gravity it has. This is why the Earth, which has a lot of mass, has a lot of gravity and can pull smaller things towards it.

But why does gravity attract objects in the first place? Well, it all has to do with the way that matter is made up. Matter is made up of tiny particles called atoms, and these atoms have a lot of mass. When two objects are near each other, their atoms are attracted to each other because of their mass. This is what causes gravity.

So to sum it up, gravity is the force that pulls objects towards each other because their atoms are attracted to each other due to their mass. Objects with more mass have more gravity because they have more atoms packed into them, which means they have more mass and more gravity.

query: How can enemy AI in video games have difficulty settings . How is it possible for enemy AI to be programmed to miss on an easy difficulty but still have pin point accuracy on harder difficulties . Please explain like I'm five.

query: Why does my car loan interest go up despite making payments on-time?
chosen: The interest probably accrues daily, regardless of whether your payments are on time.
rejected: There are several reasons why the interest on your car loan may go up despite making payments on time. Here are some possible explanations:Adjustable interest rates: If your car loan has an adjustable interest rate, it may go up if market interest rates rise. This means that the lender can adjust the interest rate on your loan based on changes in the economy.Late payments: If you make a late payment, the lender may charge you a late fee and may also increase the interest rate on your loan. This is because the lender sees you as a higher risk borrower, and therefore may charge a higher interest rate to compensate for the increased risk.Credit score: Your credit score can also affect the interest rate on your car loan. If your credit score goes down, the lender may increase the interest rate on your loan to compensate for the increased risk.Fees: There may be fees associated with your car loan that can increase the overall cost of the loan. For example, if you pay off the loan early, you may be charged a prepayment penalty.It's important to carefully review the terms of your car loan and to ask your lender for an explanation if you have any questions about the interest rate or other terms of the loan.

query: If China is a communist state , why are there so many multi billion dollar companies and free markets ? How communist can a country be when they are so wealthy , including many of its citizens ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Modern China was founded with communist principles in mind . But after Deng Xiaoping took power , he decided to move China toward a more capitalistic economy . But the Communist Party still rules China and therefore they still are labelled " communist " . Similarly , the US is n't completely capitalistic because there are government regulations and also government - owned businesses ( such as the post office ) . It 's really a label more than anything else . EDIT : Another thing to point out is that a lot of those " billion dollar companies " are actually owned / operated by the Chinese government . For example : Industrial and Commercial Bank of China . They have branches in the US now too .
Westerner living in China / studied modern Chinese history here . When the Communist Party came to power in 1949 , they did implement Communist policies like land redistribution , communal living , " Iron Rice Bowl " jobs ( guaranteed job security ) , and such ( and they became very popular in the 20s and 30s in rural areas because they espoused ideas like class struggle , the supremacy of the working proletariat , and equality between men and women ) . Some of these policies were rather disastrous ( the Great Leap Forward and the Cultural Revolution , for starters ) , and in the 1970s and 80s , the government began experimenting with turning some cities into Special Economic Zones , many of which were close to Hong Kong , which was still a British possession at the time ( it became a Special Administrative Region of China in 1997 ) and had a booming capitalist economy . Cities like Shenzhen ( which is right on the border with Hong Kong ) became wealthy really , really fast when they were allowed to experiment with capitalism . When Deng Xiaoping took power , he further liberalized the economy in order to promote national stability . He called it " Socialism with Chinese Characteristics " , because it tried to marry the old Maoist / Marxist ways of thinking with capitalism . The most famous quote you 'll hear about Deng Xiaoping 's theories is " It does n't matter if the cat is black or white , as long as it catches mice . " ( Translation : it does n't matter if the policy fits Marxist or capitalist ideals as long as it promotes economic stability ) . Of course , capitalism made a lot of Chinese people very rich ( especially a lot of government types ) , so the economy is quite liberalized now . Anyone can open up a store or a restaurant or start a business . There are still a lot of State - Owned Enterprises ( SEOs ) and some holdover policies from pre-1970s Communism still around and a lot of government meddling in business ( though not enough safety regulation , IMO ) , but in my own opinion and from my own observations , China is not a Communist country at all anymore .
They say it is but it 's not . I do n't think they even say they are communist anymore .
rejected: China is a country that is run by the Communist Party, but it does not have a traditional communist economic system like the one that was used in the Soviet Union. Instead, China has what is called a "socialist market economy." This means that the government owns some of the businesses and industries in the country, but there are also private companies and free markets. So, while the government has some control over the economy, there is still a lot of competition and people are able to start their own businesses and make a lot of money. Does that make sense?

query: Why does a TV screen look normal when I see it with my own eyes , but when I take a picture of it there are horizontal lines across it in the photo ? Example : URL_0 Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Pictures make this work much better ! What you 're seeing is a Moire pattern . You probably noticed that this happens when the camera is very sharply in focus , and mysteriously goes away when the image gets very slightly blurred . The reason is your camera 's sensor does n't capture a grid of tightly packed squares . It captures a grid of tiny dots that actually have a fair amount of space between them . Your TV 's display surface is similar ; there are tiny sources of light surrounded by dark lines where either there are n't any phosphors , or where there are edges of opaque material between the LCDs . When the image of the TV screen is in perfect focus on your image sensor , sometimes the lines go between the tiny sensor dots , and that pixel gets the full bright color it should . But sometimes the lines go right over the sensor dots , so that dot picks up the dark line or the area between phosphors , and that whole pixel gets a darker color even though only a tiny part of that region was dark . Try it . Find a way to deliberately defocus the camera a little bit , and the dots will go away . Also , as you move the camera you 'll see the pattern shifts and changes as you move . That 's a telltale sign of moire patterns .
because your eyes are too slow to ' catch ' it updating ... everyones are ... the pixels do n't all change at once ... b / c the computer controlling them can only do a couple of things at a time ... it updates them one or two at a time , in rows , usually top to bottom , and left to right ... sometimes in different patterns to ease the transition , but you 're camera is catching it in - between updating frames .
Your eyes take in a nearly constant stream of information . Cameras , however , only open their shutter for a tiny fraction of a second . TV screens flash images ( or more often parts of images ) many times per second . Your eyes take in these flashes of images and perceive a constant stream of video . Your camera captures only the part that flashes while it 's shutter is open , making it look strange .
rejected: When you look at a TV screen with your own eyes, you are seeing the picture being displayed on the screen in real time. Your brain is able to process this information and make sense of it, so the picture looks normal to you. When you take a picture of the TV screen, the camera is taking a still image of the screen at a specific moment in time. The camera is not able to process the information on the screen in the same way that your brain does, so the picture may look different than what you see with your own eyes. The horizontal lines that you see in the photo are called "scan lines." TVs display pictures by rapidly drawing each line of the picture on the screen, from top to bottom. When the camera takes a picture of the screen, it may capture only a portion of these lines, which can cause the picture to appear distorted. I hope that helps! Is there anything else you would like to know?

query: Black is possibly the last colour you want to be wearing on a sunny day , so why is the burqa often this colour in such hot climates ? White reflects , black absorbs etc etc . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
TL;DR : loose black clothing lets heat radiate away from the body . > Black clothing absorbs sunlight and the heat radiating from your body , but if it is loose - fitting , and there is wind , the wind convects the heat away faster than it is absorbed . White clothing reflects sunlight , but also reflects internal heat back towards your body , so the net effect under identical conditions is less cooling than if you wore black . ... Desert - dwelling nomadic people such as the Tuaregs wear loose - fitting black clothing , and have been doing so for a very , very long time . If there were an advantage to wearing white clothes , you 'd certainly expect they 'd have figured that out by now . Source : [ The Straight Dope ] ( URL_0 )
Black absorbs more light , which is the reason why black stuff heats up more quickly then white stuff . But there is a reason people in very sunny countries have darker pigmentation : Some of the light can actually harm you . A sunburn is an example of that , but there are also types of light coming from the sun that can destroy DNA in some of your cells , causing stuff like skin cancer . Darker materials heat up more quickly because more of the light is transformed into heat , meaning it is no longer this dangerous light . Also , only the speed of the temperature increase changes . A black rock and a white rock left in the sun indefinitely will reach the same maximum temperature , unless the environment is significantly colder and allows heat to radiate away from the respective rock . So if you are exposed to the sun for extended amounts of time , dark clothing offers more protection . TL;DR : Wearing black clothing makes you heat up faster , but you wo n't get a sunburn as quickly or take other radiation related damage . Also after a while in the sun , you will have about the same temperature regardless of the color of your clothing .
I live in UAE , near the Oman border . I have Arab friends and have asked them about this . Black abayas are not a universal among Arabs- in Oman the women wear bright colors . It seems to be something that migrated over from Saudi . Why ? It 's a variation on why women grow long fingernails . It used to be that a woman with long nails was assumed to be of gentle birth , as a woman who worked with her hands would not be able to maintain a perfect manicure- so long nails are a signal that a woman is used to luxury , and that she is married to a wealthy man . Similarly , a woman in the Gulf region would not be going out in the sunlight- that is what servants do . So she can stay indoors and be protected from the sun because her husband is rich and can afford servants . Similarly , the men wear spotless white kandouras to show that they never have to do any dirty work- they have underlings for that . Funny thing is , vitamin D deficiency is often a problem among the women now .
rejected: The color of the burqa (also spelled burka or burqua) can vary, but it is often black. The reason for this is that black is a conservative color and is often associated with modesty and religious piety. In some cultures, wearing black is also a way to show respect for God or for the deceased. In hot climates, the color black may absorb more heat than lighter colors, but the burqa is typically made of lightweight materials to help keep the wearer cool. Additionally, the burqa often covers the entire body, including the head and face, which can provide shade and protect the wearer from the sun's rays.

query: Why do humans find " nature " so beautiful ? We find waterfalls , lakes , sunsets , landscapes , trees and flower aesthetically pleasing and awe - inspiring . Why is this ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:

We get so used to it , so used it becomes bland and boring . In nature , everything is so colorful and massive , amazing . Its all so uncommon and new that its so much to take in and it looks to much better than a gray office . Our ancestors did nt wear uncomfortable suits and have to sit all day .

Nature is calming . It makes people reflect on there everyday lives . Or it lets them forget there everyday lives and enjoy what really matters .
rejected: There are many different theories about why humans find nature so beautiful. One theory is that humans have an innate preference for nature because it helped our ancestors to survive. For example, the beauty of a clear, clean stream might have indicated that it was a good source of water, while the beauty of a lush, green forest might have indicated that it was a good place to find food and shelter. Another theory is that humans find nature beautiful because it provides a sense of peace and relaxation. Overall, it seems that humans find nature beautiful for a combination of these reasons, and the experience of beauty in nature is something that is deeply personal and unique to each individual.

query: How does Google have a searchable version of the internet that is n't the same size as the internet ? I just what how can this machine even work what is this a school for ants ? Explain like I'm five.

First of all the Google Search Database does contain a large portion of the text of the internet but is less concerned with the video , and pictures which is where most of the actual size comes from . It then creates a index of the data allowing the search engine to quickly search the internet , and then compresses the crap out of the webpage making it small and manageable enough .

They also do n't they only index about 5 % of the web , or so i was told in my comp sci class .
rejected: Google has a searchable version of the internet because they have special computers called "servers" that are always connected to the internet. These servers are really powerful and can do a lot of things really quickly. When you search for something on Google, the servers go and look for websites that might have the answer you're looking for. The servers are also really good at remembering things. They can save a copy of almost every website on the internet so that they can look at it again later if they need to. This way, when you search for something on Google, the servers can look at the copy of the website they saved instead of having to go out onto the internet and find the website all over again. This is how Google can have a searchable version of the internet that isn't the same size as the internet. It's kind of like how you might have a bunch of books in your room that you can read anytime you want, instead of having to go to the library every time you want to read something.

query: Why is n't Latin spoken anymore ? The wikipedia page says it gave rise to the Romance languages , spread by the Roman empire throughout Europe . So why did the parent language die out ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
This is covered in detail in [ this thread ] ( URL_1 ) from /r / AskHistorians . [ This answer ] ( URL_0 ) by /u / dangerbird2 is accurate and yet simple enough for ELI5 : > It is important to recognize that for most of its history , the Latin language existed in two primary registers , the Classical Latin used in Roman politics , literature , and law , and the Vulgar Latin spoken in daily life . Vulgar Latin never died out , it simply evolved into the modern Romance languages around the years 600 to 800 . Although Classical Latin sees very little use today , the literary register of Latin based off Classical Latin survived for a very long time , evolving independently of the Romance languages . In Western Europe , during Late Antiquity and the Middle Ages , " Classical " Latin remained the primary language of literature , philosophy , the court , and the church . Even after authors like Chaucer and Dante popularized the use of vernacular languages in the late middle ages , and after the Protestant Reformation supported the abandonment of Latin as the liturgical language in their denominations , Latin remained the primary language of diplomacy until the seventeenth century , and of academia until the late nineteenth century . And of course , Church Latin remained the liturgical language of Roman Catholicism until the Second Vatican Council of 1962 - 1965 , which allowed the use of vernacular in mass .
The Roman empire spread Latin all over Western and Southern Europe . In most parts of the empire , Romans did not make up the majority of the local population . There were Gauls , Celts , German tribes , ... who through Roman occupation developped an own kind of Latin , a dialect . Fastforward a few centuries and all these Latin dialects have evolved . Spanish , French , Portuguese , Italian and Romanian are all languages who have evolved from Latin , you can see a lot of similarities between them . That 's why we call them Roman languages .
It ( kind of ) still is ! Over time , language changes . Its why old English sounds very different from the English we speak . When travel is rare and literacy is low , languages change much more quickly . After Rome fell , different areas of Europe continued to speak Latin . However , Latin began to change and it changed differently in different places . In what is now France , Latin began to slowly turn into old French . In Spain , Latin began to slowly turn into Spanish . In Italy , Latin became Italian . No one speaks Latin the way the Romen Empire spoke it . But no one speaks English the way Chaser spoke it either . You probably have a better chance understanding modern German than old English ( try to read the [ original Beowulf ] ( URL_0 ) to see what I mean ) . Today most European languages are a descendants ( or part descendant in the case of English ) of Latin , so in a way many people are still speaking Latin .
rejected:
Latin was the official language of the Roman Empire and was spoken by people living in the Roman Empire and in other parts of Europe. However, as the Roman Empire fell, the Latin language also began to decline in usage.

One reason for this is that Latin was a highly formal language, used mainly for official documents, laws, and other written works. It was not a language that was commonly spoken by ordinary people in their daily lives.

As time passed, the Latin language evolved and changed into other languages, such as Spanish, French, Italian, and Portuguese. These languages are known as the Romance languages because they are derived from Latin.

So even though Latin is not spoken as a daily language anymore, it has had a lasting influence on the languages that are spoken today.

query: Why is it considered necessary for a planet to have an atmosphere to sustain life ? Could n't life have evolved to survive without respiration ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Of course ; it 's not really strictly necessary . But having an atmosphere does more than provide metabolic fuel , it also shields the planet 's surface from some types of radiation , smooths out the night / day temperature gradient , and keeps liquids on the surface from boiling away into space . All of these things make it much more likely that we 'll find * recognizable * life on planets with atmospheres than without , but as usual with the search for life on exoplanets , " recognizable " is the key word .
You are walking down the street and you drop your wallet . Do you look for it on the sidewalk , or down a sewer grate , where it is too dark to see ? Sure , your wallet * might * be down the grate , but if it is , it does n't matter , you ca n't find it , so why waste the effort ? The search for extraterrestrial life is like that . We know a * lot * about carbon / water based oxygen breathing lifeforms , and have some pretty good ideas how to find them from very far away . But beyond that , we have virtually nothing about what other kinds of life are possible , and even less about what to look for .
My grandfather explained it to me like this ( probably when I was about 5 years old ): Basically for all we know there could be giant gold whales floating around in gas giants or huge sentient clouds of gas floating around in space . But creating life is apparently very difficult because we have n't seen any of it around us . So instead of looking for life , we look for life as we know it . And life as we know it requires water and air and all the other things that make our planet unique
rejected: An atmosphere is important for life on a planet because it helps to regulate the temperature and protect living things from harmful solar radiation. Without an atmosphere, the surface of a planet would be too hot during the day and too cold at night, which would make it difficult for life to survive. An atmosphere is also important because it provides the air that living things need to breathe. All living things need to exchange gases with their environment in order to survive. For example, humans and other animals need to breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide. Plants, on the other hand, use a process called photosynthesis to take in carbon dioxide and produce oxygen. So, while it is possible that life could evolve to survive without an atmosphere or without the need to breathe, it is much more likely that life would evolve to take advantage of the benefits that an atmosphere provides.

query: Acceleration is ( m / s^2 ) ... what the hell is s^2 ? So ... what is a second squared ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
what is distance ? Meters ( m ) What is speed ? Meters per second ( number of meters moved per unit time m / s ) What is accelleration ? Meters per second per second ( number of meters per second moved per second or change in the ' speed ' per unit time ) This is m / s / s which simplifies to m / s^2 . For the simplification : The fraction can be re written as ( m / s)/(s/1 ) remembering how to work with fractions to divide you ' invert ' one and multiply numerators and denominators . So ( m / s)/(s/1 ) = ( m x 1)/(s x s ) = m / s^2
When you measure the rate of change of your position , you measure your velocity in meters travelled per second , right ? So when you want to measure the rate of change of your * velocity * ( your acceleration ) , you measure your meters per second change , per second . Or m / s^2 .
Acceleration is the rate of change in velocity per time interval . Let 's say we 're measuring velocity in meters per second . To measure acceleration we 're looking at the change in velocity ( meters per second ) every second . So the unit becomes meters per second per second . Or m / s^2 .
rejected: In physics, "acceleration" is a measure of how quickly an object's speed or velocity changes over time. It is usually measured in units of meters per second squared, which means the speed of the object is changing at a rate of so many meters per second for every second that passes. A "second" is a unit of time. It is the duration of 9,192,631,770 periods of the radiation corresponding to the transition between the two hyperfine levels of the ground state of the caesium 133 atom. In other words, it is a very small unit of time that is used as a standard to measure other, longer periods of time. Squaring a unit of time means multiplying it by itself. So "second squared" means "one second multiplied by one second," or "one second times one second." It is a way of expressing a change in speed or velocity over a period of time. For example, if you are driving in a car and your speed increases by 10 meters per second every second, your acceleration would be 10 meters per second squared. This means that your speed is increasing by 10 meters per second for every second that passes.

query: Please explain what is "Ordinary least squares"
chosen:
In statistics, ordinary least squares (OLS) is a type of linear least squares method for choosing the unknown parameters in a linear regression model (with fixed level-one effects of a linear function of a set of explanatory variables) by the principle of least squares: minimizing the sum of the squares of the differences between the observed dependent variable (values of the variable being observed) in the input dataset and the output of the (linear) function of the independent variable.
Geometrically, this is seen as the sum of the squared distances, parallel to the axis of the dependent variable, between each data point in the set and the corresponding point on the regression surface—the smaller the differences, the better the model fits the data. The resulting estimator can be expressed by a simple formula, especially in the case of a simple linear regression, in which there is a single regressor on the right side of the regression equation.
The OLS estimator is consistent for the level-one fixed effects when the regressors are exogenous and forms perfect colinearity (rank condition), consistent for the variance estimate of the residuals when regressors have finite fourth moments  and—by the Gauss–Markov theorem—optimal in the class of linear unbiased estimators when the errors are homoscedastic and serially uncorrelated. Under these conditions, the method of OLS provides minimum-variance mean-unbiased estimation when the errors have finite variances. Under the additional assumption that the errors are normally distributed with zero mean, OLS is the maximum likelihood estimator that outperforms any non-linear unbiased estimator.
rejected:
Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) is a method used to estimate the parameters of a linear regression model. Linear regression is a statistical model that describes the linear relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables.



In OLS, the goal is to find the values of the parameters (also called coefficients) that best fit the data. This is done by minimizing the sum of the squared differences between the predicted values and the actual values of the dependent variable. The difference between the predicted value and the actual value is called the residual.



To find the best fit, OLS uses an optimization algorithm to find the values of the parameters that minimize the sum of the squared residuals. The resulting model is called the OLS model.



OLS is widely used in statistical modeling and data analysis because it is relatively simple to implement and has nice statistical properties. It is also easy to interpret, as the parameters of the model can be directly related to the relationships between the variables in the model.

query: " Magic eye " images Magic is still my only explenation :( Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
One of the depth cues your brain uses to determine where things are located is called binocular disparity . Binocular disparity works by comparing the image of the world in the left eye to the image of the world in the right eye . Depending on how different the two images of an object are in the two eyes ( the binocular disparity ) , that can be used by your brain to calculate exactly how far away an object is . When you see a magic eye picture , it contains many elements that repeat over and over again . To see the magic eye picture , you have to focus behind the page in order to create a double image . Then , you slowly bring the two images back together . The magic eye image has been constructed such that when you do this , it will match up each spot on the image in the left eye with a < < different > > spot on the image in the right eye . The magic eye image has been constructed so that the binocular disparities between all the points in the image create a new picture that usually is recessed into or pops out of depth . Notice , because of the way it works , you can make it go away by tilting the page . The magic eye image only works when it is horizontal .
One of the ways we see 3D is because our left and right eyes see the world from a slightly different angle : things that are closer to you appear more to the left for your right eye , and more to the right for your left eye . These " magic eye " pictures , or stereograms , work by providing a different image to both eyes . It 's a repeating pattern , so that you can look at one repetition with one eye , but instead of looking at the same repetition with the other eye , you 're looking at the next or previous one . Since the repetitions are similar , your brain is tricked into thinking that both eyes are looking at the same thing . But the repetitions are not exactly the same , and by making the appropriate variation , you can give the illusion that part of the image is closer or farther than the rest . Here is a crude stereogram : . + ------------+ + ------------+ |ghdopnhlmoph| |ghdopnhlmoph| |rewljobfrjdc| |rewljobfrjdc| |gvshellowciu| |gvsfhellociu| |jacworldminz| |jacsworldinz| |lovszjlgvsdh| |lovszjlgvsdh| |gbnkiolgdgtj| |gbnkiolgdgtj| + ------------+ + ------------+ . It is made for eye crossing . That is , you 're supposed to cross your eyes so that you look at the left square with your right eye and the right square with your left eye , and try to superimpose both images . If you prefer , here 's a version where you have to focus far away : you look at the left square with the left eye , and the right square with your right eye . It 's actually the same as above , except both squares switched places . . + ------------+ + ------------+ |ghdopnhlmoph| |ghdopnhlmoph| |rewljobfrjdc| |rewljobfrjdc| |gvsfhellociu| |gvshellowciu| |jacsworldinz| |jacworldminz| |lovszjlgvsdh| |lovszjlgvsdh| |gbnkiolgdgtj| |gbnkiolgdgtj| + ------------+ + ------------+ . If you look closely , the squares are identical except for a bit of text in the middle : there 's " hello world " in there , but it 's not at the same position in both squares . By positioning it more to the right in the image for the left eye and more to the left in the image for the right eye , it gives the illusion that this part is closer to you , so it makes it pop .
Have you ever looked at a 3D movie without glasses on ? You can see two overlapping pictures of the same thing ? Magic Eye pictures ( or autostereograms ) work the same way , it 's just that you do n't recognise there are two images there because they 're drawn on that messy , fuzzy background that breaks up the lines . When you focus your eyes correctly , tricking your brain into thinking the picture is further away than it is , you end up having one eye see one picture and the other eye see the other picture , and your brain pieces the two together to form the 3D image .
rejected:
Sure! "Magic Eye" images are pictures that contain hidden 3D images. When you look at them, you might see a bunch of colorful shapes and patterns, but if you look at them in a special way, you can see a 3D image hidden inside.

Here's how to do it: First, hold the picture about 10 inches away from your face and focus on a point in the distance behind the picture. Then, slowly bring the picture closer to your face while still looking at that point in the distance. As you do this, you might notice that the shapes and patterns in the picture start to shift and change. Keep looking and try to relax your eyes. Eventually, you should see a 3D image appear in the picture. It might take a little practice to get the hang of it, but it's a fun trick!

query: Why after downloading some applications , do I need to restart but others work straight away ? ^ Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Because some applications use low - level resources which are already loaded into memory and used continuously by other applications and the operating system as well . Since reloading that particular resource in to memory is way too hard without crashing other applications or the OS itself , the best is to make the user reload the full operating system , hence the need to restart .
Related Eli5 : why then has Linux never once told me I need to restart to finish installing anything including the proprietary Nvidia xserver ?
Actually , most programs still work perfectly without restarting the computer . It used to be a big thing to having to restart the computer when installing something , it it kinda just stuck as a precedent . Even changes to the registry or low - level resources can be changed without restarting . This does n't mean that * all * programs do n't need a restart to work . Some programs hook into the OS to do certain tasks , and that has to be loaded during boot .
rejected:
When you download and install a new application on your computer, it usually needs to make changes to your system in order to work properly. Some of these changes might involve modifying certain system files or settings, or adding new files to your system. 
In order to make these changes, the application needs to have special privileges that allow it to access and modify these system resources. These privileges are typically granted by your operating system, but they are only effective while the operating system is running. 
That's why some applications require you to restart your computer after installation: so that the changes made by the application can take effect and the application can start using the privileges it needs to run. Other applications might not require a restart because they don't need to make any changes to the system, or because the changes they need to make can be done while the system is still running.

query: What happens after a counterparty defaults on a derivative trade?
chosen: The answer is in your question: derivatives are contracts so are enforced in the same way as any other contract.  If the counterparty refuses to pay immediately they will, in the first instance be billed by any intermediary (Prime Broker etc.) that facilitated the contract. If they still refuse to pay the contract may stipulate that a broker can "net off" any outstanding payments against it or pay out using deposited cash or posted margins. The contract will usually include the broker as an interested party and so they can, but don't need to, report a default (such that this is) to credit agencies (in some jurisdictions they are required to by law). Any parties to the trade and the courts may use a debt collection agency to collect payments or seize assets to cover payment. If there is no broker or the counterparty still has not paid the bill then the parties involved (the party to the trade and any intermediaries) can sue for breach of contract. If they win (which would be expected) the counterparty will be made to pay by the legal system including, but not limited to, seizure of assets, enforced bankruptcy, and prison terms for any contempts of court rulings. All of this holds for governments who refuse to pay derivatives losses (as Argentina did in the early 20th century) but in that case it may escalate as far as war. It has never done so for derivatives contracts as far as I know but other breaches of contract between countries have resulted in armed conflict. As well as the "hard" results of failing to pay there are soft implications including a guaranteed fall in credit ratings that will result in parties refusing to do business with the counterparty and a separate loss of reputation that will reduce business even further. Potential employees and funders will be unwilling to become involved with such a party and suppliers will be unwilling to supply on credit. The end result in almost every way would be bankruptcy and prison sentences for the party or their senior employees. Most jurisdictions allow for board members at companies in material breach of contract to be banned from running any company for a set period as well. edit: netting off cash flows netting off is a process whereby all of a party's cash flows, positive and negative, are used to pay each other off so that only the net change is reflected in account balances, for example: company 1 cash flows netting off the total outgoings are 3M + 500k = 3.5M and total incomings are 1.2M + 1.1M + 1.2M = 3.5M so the incoming cash flows can be used to pay the outgoing cash flows leaving a net payment into company1's account of 0.
rejected: If one party (the "defaulting party") to a derivative trade defaults on its obligations under the trade, it means that the defaulting party has failed to perform its obligations as agreed to in the trade. This can occur for a variety of reasons, such as financial difficulty, bankruptcy, or other events.When a counterparty defaults on a derivative trade, the non-defaulting party (also known as the "non-defaulting counterparty" or "protective party") may have a number of options, depending on the specific terms of the trade and the legal and regulatory framework in which the trade is governed.One option available to the non-defaulting party is to close out the trade and receive any outstanding amounts due from the defaulting party. This typically involves selling the defaulting party's positions and using the proceeds to offset any amounts owed to the defaulting party.Another option is for the non-defaulting party to exercise any rights it has under the trade, such as the right to terminate the trade or the right to require the defaulting party to post additional collateral.In some cases, the non-defaulting party may also have the right to pursue legal action against the defaulting party to recover any losses or damages resulting from the default.It is important for parties to carefully review the terms of their derivative trades and understand their rights and obligations in the event of a default by the counterparty. It is also advisable for parties to consider entering into a credit support agreement or other risk management measures to mitigate the risk of counterparty default.

query: Does re-financing an FHA-insured mortgage incur the UFMIP again?
chosen: When you got your original HUD backed mortgage there were three options: monthly, annual and upfront payments. The plan is designed to insure the lender of the mortgage against your default. The plan is not expected to cover the mortgage for 30 years.  If you are in the early years of the mortgage, you may be owed a refund for the unused years. HUD has a Fact sheet discussing this, and a page to help you determine if they owe you a refund. If you are refinancing back into a HUD/FHA mortgage they will not give you a refund, but will roll the refund back into your new loan. FHA to FHA Refinances: When an FHA loan is refinanced, the refund from the old premium may be applied toward the up-front premium required for the new loan. Note: Depending on the year of the original loan the government has different lengths they used for coverage and refunds. I suggest you use the webpage to determine if you are due a refund, or a roll over.
rejected: If you refinance an FHA-insured mortgage, you will not be required to pay the Upfront Mortgage Insurance Premium (UFMIP) again. The UFMIP is a one-time fee that is paid at closing when you first get an FHA loan. It is a percentage of the loan amount and is typically financed into the loan.However, you will still be required to pay the annual mortgage insurance premium (MIP) on your new loan. The MIP is a ongoing insurance premium that is paid in monthly installments as part of your mortgage payment. The MIP is required on all FHA loans and helps to protect the lender in case the borrower defaults on the loan.It is important to note that refinancing an FHA loan may not always be the best option, even if you are able to avoid paying the UFMIP again. You should consider factors such as the costs of refinancing, the terms of the new loan, and how long you plan to stay in your home before deciding whether refinancing is the right choice for you.

query: Why do girl scout cookies have a cult following ? Every damn year Please explain like I'm five.

Because they are delicious and typically only accessible during a certain time of the year . Same reason any seasonal flavor ( pumpkin spice during fall ) has a " cult " following .

They are popular because they are made with high-quality ingredients and have a variety of different flavors, including classic options like Thin Mints and Samoas. Additionally, the Girl Scouts organization is well-respected and has a strong reputation, so people may be more likely to support their fundraising efforts by purchasing the cookies. Overall, the combination of their delicious flavors, limited availability, and support for a good cause contribute to their popularity and cult following.

query: Trickle down economics Can someone explain the theory behind trickle down economics ? what are the benefits and drawbacks of trickle down economics ? And does it actually work ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The theory Trickle Down ( also called Supply Side ) behind it ? Sure . On paper it makes sense . The wealthy buy big ticket items such as cars , planes , boats , etc . This creates demand and people are employed to create these products . They then use that payment to pay mortgages , cable bill , new shoes , etc . Or the rich start businesses . Employing many people who receive a wage so they can in turn provide for themselves and others . Or the rich invest in stocks . Which gives businesses capital . Allowing them to expand , hire , etc etc . And while this does happen , it 's never in a large enough quantities to affect the economy like the creators envisioned . Most just horde the money in real estate or money market accounts . It works on paper , and its a good theory , but it does n't work in reality . Keynesian Economics ( also called Demand Side ) has consistently proven to be a more effective method of jump starting an economy .
Let 's say the government ( who collects a lot of taxes , and has a lot of money to spend ) decides to give you , Joe Taxpayer , $ 50 a month to spend on food . ( well , you and let 's say , 99 other taxpayers in your town ) You and the other 99 go to the grocery store , buy more food , which helps the grocery store pay it 's bills , including wages for its employees and buying groceries from suppliers . Some of that $ 5000 goes to the employees , some go to the farmers and bakers and suppliers , and some goes to the grocery store owner . The employees have a little more money so they can buy more things for themselves . The farmers and bakers and whatnot have more money , so they grow more crops and bake more bread to sell to those employees who have more money . This is a really simple version of " demand - side " economics , and the idea was originally expressed by a guy named Keynes . More money to people means more spending , means more demand , and the economy grows . Trickle - down economics looks at the other side of the equation . It suggests we should give $ 5000 to * the grocery store owner * . With that extra money , the owner can afford to expand the store . Maybe he decides the store can be open for a few more hours a day , and needs to hire more people to cover those hours . Maybe he decides to buy more groceries to get a discount , and lowers his prices . Maybe he just decides to give his employees raises , so they 're more motivated and productive on the job . Basically , he can use the money to grow his business , or make his business more efficient so his employees or his customers ( or both ) get a benefit . The theory behind trickle - down economics comes in large part from something that happened in the 1970 's . For a long time , our government used demand - side economics , like in the first example . Demand - side economics has a known drawback : by increasing demand and having more money in circulation , you create inflation . You can fight inflation by having less money in circulation , but that leads to higher unemployment rates . For a very long time , this was believed to be an absolute truth of economics : if inflation was high , unemployment was low , and vice - versa . But when the oil crisis and the price shocks happened in the 70 's , something unexpected happened : inflation went way up , * * and * * the unemployment rate went up . This happened because the * supply - side * of the economic decreased , something that really had n't happened in a very long time before or since . But it got some people thinking : if its possible to shrink the * supply * and create high inflation * * and * * high unemployment , then should n't it be possible to grow the supply - side , getting both low inflation and low unemployment ? The answer to that turns out to be " it might be possible in theory , but in the real world , no we ca n't reliably grow the Supply part of the economy the way we can consistently stimulate the Demand part of the economy " .
Here 's what I posted a few hours ago when somebody asked about Reaganomics ... Reagan popularized the idea of " trickle down " economics ... the idea that cutting taxes for the rich would cause them to spend more , invest more , and result in creating jobs for others . 40 years on , and the rich have gotten way richer while the lower and middle classes have not benefited in the ways the theory suggested . This is why the income gap has grown over the past 3 - 4 decades rather than closed . The reality is that the rich have less marginal demand and that they do n't invest money when there is n't demand to justify investment . A tax cut does n't necessarily mean they 'll buy more cars or homes or restaurant meals . They wo n't hire workers or build a factory just because they paid less in taxes , if the business itself does n't have demand for its good or services to justify the investment in more people or equipment . Sure , some of that money might get spent or invested , but not immediately and not all of it . Meanwhile , more money in the hands of lower income workers would very likely get spent quickly . There is pent up demand for new cars , better housing , newer clothing , furniture , more meals out , etc . from those with limited means . This increased demand would boost businesses and then promote investment in growing the businesses to meet rising demand . So after 40 years of practice , it 's safe to say that Reaganomics was not effective ... even thought the GOP continues to push it as the main part of their economic policy . EDIT : fixed tickle to trickle
rejected:
Trickle-down economics is an economic theory that suggests that tax cuts and other benefits given to wealthy people and businesses will eventually "trickle down" to the rest of society in the form of increased economic activity, jobs, and prosperity. The idea behind trickle-down economics is that by giving more money to the people who have the most money, they will be more likely to spend it or invest it, which will stimulate the economy and create wealth for everyone.

One of the main benefits of trickle-down economics is that it can potentially encourage economic growth and development by providing incentives for businesses to invest and expand. However, there are also several drawbacks to this theory. Some people argue that trickle-down economics does not actually work, and that it benefits only a small group of wealthy individuals and corporations, rather than the general population. There is also evidence that trickle-down economics can lead to increased income inequality, as the benefits of tax cuts and other policies are often disproportionately received by the wealthy.

Overall, the effectiveness of trickle-down economics is a subject of debate among economists and policymakers. Some believe that it can be an effective way to stimulate economic growth, while others argue that it is not a reliable or fair way to distribute the benefits of economic policy.

query: Please explain what is "Turing Award"
chosen:
The ACM A. M. Turing Award is an annual prize given by the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) for contributions of lasting and major technical importance to computer science. It is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science and is colloquially known as or often referred to as the "Nobel Prize of Computing".
The award is named after Alan Turing, who was a British mathematician and reader in mathematics at the University of Manchester. Turing is often credited as being the key founder of theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence. From 2007 to 2013, the award was accompanied by an additional prize of US$250,000, with financial support provided by Intel and Google. Since 2014, the award has been accompanied by a prize of US$1 million, with financial support provided by Google.
The first recipient, in 1966, was Alan Perlis, of Carnegie Mellon University. The first female recipient was Frances E. Allen of IBM in 2006.
The latest recipient, in 2021, is Jack Dongarra, of the University of Tennessee.
rejected:
The Turing Award is a prestigious award given annually to individuals who have made significant contributions to the field of computer science. It is named after Alan Turing, a pioneer in the field of computer science and artificial intelligence, and is often referred to as the "Nobel Prize" of computer science. The award is presented by the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM), a professional society for computer scientists, and is accompanied by a prize of $1 million.



The Turing Award is considered one of the highest honors in the field of computer science, and recipients are chosen based on their outstanding technical or theoretical contributions to the field. Some of the most notable recipients of the award include John Hopcroft, Vint Cerf, Barbara Liskov, and Tim Berners-Lee, among others. The award is given to individuals who have made significant contributions to the development of computer science theory, practice, and application, and who have had a lasting impact on the field.

query: Dreams How does my brain decide what I 'm going to experience in my dreams ? And why ca n't I always remember them ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Since the first question is still up for debate , but the second question is more answerable - I will start there . Sleep itself is broken into 5 stages and they do nt have real names so I 'll just paraphrase them a bit - 1 ) Light Sleep 2 ) Moderate Sleep 3 ) Deep Sleep 4 ) Deep Sleep Part 2 5 ) REM Sleep . Now the one we want to talk about is REM Sleep . REM stands for Rapid Eye Movement . Now while your in deep sleep part 2 you eventually get into REM and your heartbeat starts to race , breathing can become irregular , but most importantly you begin to dream . Once REM is over its back to stage 1 - and preferrably if you wake here , you should remember everything that happened from the previous REM stage . However , if you continue to sleep past stage 1 and into stage 2 - your mind sort of forgets the REM as it begins to prepare the new cycle . If you wake up in stage 2,3,or 4 , your memory of REM is most likely gone .
First , nobody really quite understands dreams , but there are lots of guesses . So what follows are just some of my favorites of the theories I 've heard . The most convincing seems is that it is a way for your brain to reinforce important memories and discard unimportant ones . It 's not clear how they do this . One theory is that the brains sends a flood of random signals through the brain , so that only the strongest memories survive . ( Otherwise , the important ones would be mixed in with remember ever street address you saw on the way to work . ) If this latter theory is true , then your dreams are just some part of your brain interpreting random noise in your brain , with a touch of " here 's what struck me as interesting during the day " .

Dreams are a natural part of the sleep cycle and they occur during the rapid eye movement (REM) stage of sleep. During this time, your brain is more active and your body is paralyzed, which helps you stay asleep.

Your brain creates dreams by pulling together bits and pieces of your memories, thoughts, and feelings from the day and weaving them into a story. Sometimes these dreams can be strange or confusing because they are not based in reality.

Not all dreams are remembered, and it is normal to forget most of your dreams. This is because the brain's memory system is not as active during sleep as it is when you are awake, so it is harder to store the dream in your long-term memory. However, some people are able to remember their dreams more easily than others.

In summary, your brain creates dreams by using bits and pieces of your memories and thoughts and combining them into a story. You may not always remember your dreams because the brain's memory system is not as active during sleep as it is when you are awake.

query: why are insects important ? I 'm actually trying to teach some students and I ca nt explain it . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Because they are part of the ecosystem . for example : Hawks could not survive without bugs because they eat smaller birds , the bird at the bottom of that chain must eat something smaller right ? So they could eat berries , or bugs . So that is the first part . Some plants could not live without Bugs . Bees for example pollinate flowers , so that the flowers can reproduce . Cows depend on plants for food , and therefore also on bugs . Nearly all plants and and animals depend on insects for these reasons .
1 . Insects are low on the aerial and land food chain . Many small animals have a diet composed either entirely of insects or mostly composed of insects . This makes them vital for smaller carniverous or omnivorous life , such as smaller species of birds . 2 . Insects pollenate . Because a majority of plants are stationary , they are unable to find mates themselves . So , they attract insects with scents and colors , the insects come to the plants and extract sugars and saps ( for nourishment ) , then fly to a different plant . While they 're on one plant , pollen attatches to the insect , usually on the leg , and when they visit new plants , the pollen is dropped off and exchanged , allowing for fertilization and pollenation . ( There 's a great Magic School Bus episode about pollenation that works for all ages . Show it to your class ) 3 . Many insects have been known to show rudimentary intelligence . Ants in particular organize themselves into highly efficient colony structures that at times have been known to organize resources centrally , wage wars , and even mine minerals . Bees also demonstrate communication with each other by dancing to tell the rest of their colony where there 's flowers for making honey . ( Again , a great Magic School Bus episode on bees ) 4 . There are more insects in the world than any other life , and their total mass ( biomass ) is also greater than any other animal .

Insects are important for many reasons! Here are a few:

Insects help pollinate plants. This means that they help spread pollen from one plant to another, which helps the plants make seeds and reproduce. Without insects, many plants would not be able to grow and thrive.

Insects help to control other pest populations. Some insects eat other insects that can be harmful to plants or humans, such as aphids or mosquitoes. This helps to keep these pest populations in check and prevent them from causing damage.

Insects provide food for other animals. Many birds, reptiles, and other animals rely on insects as a source of food. Without insects, these animals might not have enough to eat.

Insects are important for scientific research and understanding the natural world. Scientists study insects to learn more about how they behave and how they interact with their environments. This helps us to better understand the world around us and how different species depend on each other.

Overall, insects play a vital role in many different ecosystems and are an important part of the natural world.
