query: Understanding Put Options
chosen: Options, both puts and calls, are typically written/sold at different strike prices. For example, even though the stock of XYZ is currently trading at $12.50, there could be put options for prices ranging from $0.50 to $30.00, just as an example. There are several factors that go into determining the strike prices at which people are willing to write options. The writer/seller of an option is the person on the other side of the trade that has the opposite opinion of you. If you are interested in purchasing a put on a stock to hedge your downside, that means the writer/seller of the put is betting that you are wrong and that the stock price will rise instead.
rejected: A put option is a financial contract that gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to sell a certain quantity of an underlying asset at a predetermined price (also known as the strike price) on or before a specified date (the expiration date). The person who sells the put option is known as the writer.The buyer of a put option is betting that the price of the underlying asset will decrease below the strike price before the expiration date. If the price does indeed fall below the strike price, the buyer can exercise the option, sell the underlying asset at the strike price, and make a profit.For example, suppose you buy a put option on a stock with a strike price of $50 and an expiration date three months from now. If the stock price falls below $50 within the next three months, you can exercise the option and sell the stock at $50, even if the current market price is lower. If the stock price stays above $50, however, you will not exercise the option and will lose the premium you paid for the put option.The writer of the put option, on the other hand, is betting that the price of the underlying asset will stay above the strike price. If the price stays above the strike price, the option will expire worthless and the writer will keep the premium as profit. If the price falls below the strike price, however, the writer will have to buy the underlying asset at the higher market price and sell it to the option holder at the lower strike price, resulting in a loss.

query: Agnosticism Does agnostics believe in god or not ? What do they believe ? P.s . Sorry for my poor english . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Agnosticism is the idea that it 's impossible to know whether or not God ( or any higher power ) exists . Agnostics believe that humans are incapable of providing a rational argument for or against the existance of God , because if God exists , he would be so far beyond our plane of experience that we could n't possibly comprehend him . It 's like asking an ant to describe a human . We 're so far above the ant 's point of reference that he could n't begin to describe us , because nothing he knows would be capable of fully describing what he sees when he looks up at the giant standing over his anthill . Do n't take agnosticism as a complete denial of god , though . You can be an agnostic theist , which would mean you * believe * there is a god , but you do n't claim to * know * there is a god . Agnostic atheists ( which I describe myself as , for the record ) believe that there is no god , but generally admit that there 's no way for humans to know for sure .
Agnostics believe that you can never know whether or not God exists . You can be an agnostic and believe in God ( * I can not know there is God , but I believe in him * ) . You can be an agnostic and not believe in God ( * I can not know there is a God and I do n't believe in him * ) . You can be an agnostic and suspend judgement on religious matters altogether ( * I do n't give a shit * ) . Most atheists are agnostic because you can never say with 100 % certainty that the probability of God existing is 0 . There might be something out there that resembles our criteria of a * God * , but the likelihood of it existing is so tiny , so minuscule , that we might as well say no God exists .
The word agnostic does n't actually have anything to do with belief . Atheism is the lack of a belief in a god , while Theism is the belief in one . Gnosticism is to " know " , which is slightly but importantly different than " believe " . A Gnostic about God , knows that God exists . An Agnostic does n't know that God exists . It is that simple . So a person can very much believe in a god but not for certain know that it exists , and that person would be an Agnostic Theist . I hope that helps .
rejected:
Agnostics are people who are unsure about the existence of God or a higher power. They may believe that it is impossible to know for certain whether or not God exists, or they may believe that the existence of God is unknowable or unknowable by humans.

Agnostics do not necessarily have a specific belief about God or the existence of a higher power. They may believe that it is possible that God exists, but they do not have any proof or evidence to support this belief.

In general, agnostics tend to be open to the possibility of God or a higher power, but they do not feel certain about it. They may believe that it is important to be open to different beliefs and ideas, and to be willing to consider the possibility of God or a higher power without being certain about it.

In simple terms, agnostics are people who are not sure if God exists or not. They may believe it is possible, but they do not have enough evidence to say for sure.

query: Why is all glass not safety glass ? Just dropped a beaker at work and it got me thinking why all glass is nt configured the way lets say a windshield or window is . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
for general use , cost . car windshield glass is laminated . it 's actually two pieces of glass with a clear glue in the middle . it does n't shatter into million pieces when it breaks . windshield glass also needs to be mostly distortion free . you 'll note that windshields are bent , but when you look thru them , the image is not bent . car side window glass is not laminated , but it 's still special glass . it 's tempered . it 's stronger than just " normal " glass . but it shatters into many pieces when it breaks . then there 's glass made for ever other specialty application . optical glass does n't need to be super strong or not shatter . it needs to be very clear and very distortion free . gorilla glass is very scratch and shatter resistant . and very pricey . UV protection glass filters out UV light . residential window glass is treated for filtering our both UV and IR light .
Most probable reason ? Price . Plain glass is much cheaper than shock resistant glass , or even scratch resistant glass or so . All these improvements make the product a tad more expensive and sometimes this difference gets bit enough to matter . We could also say that in some situations a heat tempered glass piece is just plain stupid . Imagine if a window in you house got hit by a sharp rock . If it was a heat tempered piece it would shatter in a million pieces and cleaning that would be a pain in the butt . Now , if we use plain glass it just breaks into big chunks near the impact . See ? Its much easier to clean and sometimes can be " fixed " with a piece of tape till the next day , while if it was HT glass you woudn't have a window anymore , just a hole in your wall .
Toughened or tempered safety glass is very difficult to work with . It ca n't be cut to size or drilled , it has to be made to order the specific shape needed . The plastic layer in laminated safety glass affects the optics and working with it . And in general , you ca n't really get everything in one kind of glass . For lab glassware heat resistance and chemical inertness are usually most important , borosilicate glass ( ' pyrex ' ) is good for that . Some uses need it to be shaped when heated though , for example to make ' glass welds ' , so ordinary soda - lime glass is used for that . For cars , impact resistance is important . For camera lenses , optical properties are paramount . And so on .
rejected: Safety glass is made in a way that makes it less likely to break into sharp pieces when it is hit or broken. This is especially important for things like car windows and shower doors, where people might walk or bump into the glass. However, not all types of glass need to be made with safety in mind. For example, the beaker you dropped at work might not have needed to be made with safety glass because it is not being used in a place where people are likely to walk or bump into it. Instead, the beaker is used for holding liquids, and as long as it doesn't leak, it doesn't need to be made with safety features.

Every time I felt the jellybeans I got was close enough can be thought of as within a standard deviation and when I did n't get close enough jellybeans , it was outside a standard deviation . * * TL;DR Do n't fuck around with a Sith 's jellybeans . * *
Standard deviation is a number used to tell how measurements for a group are spread out from the average ( mean ) , or expected value . A low standard deviation means that most of the numbers are very close to the average . A high standard deviation means that the numbers are spread out . [ :) ] ( URL_0 )
* * Motivation / basic idea * * Everything random varies- some guys have huge dicks , some have little dicks- and a lot of people get insecure about it . There was once a guy , who got particularly insecure about the length of his manhood , and compared it to the average- at five inches it turned out to be half an inch below the average of five and a half . For several days he cried alone in his room . " Below average ! " he thought , " who would want a man below average ? ! " ... It was n't until nearly a week later ( he was * very * insecure ) that a thought ocurred to him : " Hold the fucking phone ! " he said " like half of everyone is below average- being below average does n't mean shit ! " But he realised that there was still such a thing as having a small wang- he just had to find a way of doing the math . " So , " he thought " I will work out how far people 's dicks are from the average- and then- I will check out the average of these . I will work out * * the average distance from the average * * ! If I 'm within this , then to all intents and purposes I have a normal johnson ! " * * Extra in depth bonus :* * And so he did- him and his three friends got round a ruler and found the lengths in inches : 4.5 " , 5 " , 6 " , 6.5 " . He worked out the average : 5.5 " , and subtracted it from each of the lengths to get * the distance to the average * , giving : -1 " , -0.5 " , 0.5 " , 1 " . Now , in order to get the average , he just needed to add these up and divide by 4 . Except he got an answer of zero . And that did n't seem right . He tried it with other numbers- his neighbours ' even some made up ones- but every time he did it he got zero . Soon he realised that it was n't a coincidence- and that in fact the negatives were cancelling out the positives every time . All he had to do then was get rid of the negatives . As a mathematician , he knew that you could get rid of minus signs by squaring the numbers so that 's what he did- giving 1 , 0.25 , 0.25 , 1 . Taking the average of these gave 0.625 . Which seemed pretty cool , except he rememebered he 'd squared all the numbers- so to counteract this , he square rooted his answer giving 0.729 " as the standard deviation . * * End * * This meant that on average people were 0.729 " either side of the five and a half inch average- which meant that our hero 's purple - headed - spunk - python was well within the bounds of normal . And he ran into the sunset clutching his newly normalised member . His dick may have been fogettable- but his method of working out whether something random is within the bounds of normality was not . And they all lived happily ever after .
rejected:
Sure! Standard deviation is a way to measure how spread out a group of numbers is. Imagine you have a bunch of numbers, like the heights of a group of kids in your class. Some of the kids might be really tall, and some of them might be really short. The standard deviation is a way to measure how much those numbers are spread out.

To find the standard deviation, you first have to find the average (also called the mean) of all the numbers. This is just the sum of all the numbers divided by the total number of numbers. Then, for each number, you subtract the average and square the result. Finally, you add up all those squared differences and divide by the total number of numbers. This gives you the standard deviation.

So, if all the kids in your class are about the same height, the standard deviation will be small. But if some kids are really tall and some kids are really short, the standard deviation will be bigger. Standard deviation is often used to describe how much a group of numbers is spread out, because it tells you how far, on average, each number is from the average of the whole group.

query: The term dialectic used by Marx As in dialectically related for example ! Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
So you 've got Socrates ' idea of how arguments work through thesis , antitheses and synthesis as /u / ameba spells out above . Basically you talk , you find common ground , and the new common ground forms the basis of your future understanding . Socrates thought you did that until you got to the " right " answer , then stopped . Then Hegel took it to the next step and basically said all thought , all words , and all understanding of everything , but particularly in terms of philosophical concepts , is the consequence of dialectic . We talk , we argue , we come to a shared understanding . That understanding = what we think is the correct answer at that point . Until someone else builds on it by arguing more . So there is no right answer , there 's just the evolution of understanding through time . Then Marx takes it one step further with his concept of * dialectical materialism * . That basically means that if you apply the idea of the dialectic to history and to politics you realise that reality itself is what we decide it is , and so we can change it through argument . Take any assumption you make about the way the world is and Marx will say that that assumption is historical : it is correct now but it might not be correct in future because we are constantly changing our conception of reality through conversation and debate . So take the idea that " Communism is a good idea in theory but it would never work in practice because people are selfish and money oriented " . Dialectical materialism says well that might be true now , but through argument we can slowly shift our idea of reality over time and at some future point people wo n't be selfish or greedy because selfishness and greedyness are products of our society and culture which can be altered by sustained debate shifting the cultural and historical norms . I 'd need to see the context but I think the phrase " dialectically related " I think just uses the old fashioned Socratic sense of the term dialectic . So " x is dialectically related to y " means " arguments about x , eventually led us to think y "
It comes from Hegel 's philosophy : URL_0 The basic idea is that ideas & society progresses through 3 phases : 1 . You 've got a popular idea ( the * thesis * ) . 2 . Eventually , the opposite of that idea will become popular ( the * antithesis * ) 3 . Finally , people figure out a compromise between the two ( the * synthesis * ) . Marx made some significant changes to the idea but I ca n't remember what they were off the top of my head .
Around the 19th century when Marx was around , philosophy was being turned on it 's head . Since the ancient Greek philosophers discussed the Land of the Forms , and the idea that all things on earth are imperfect reflections of a perfect idea , and the subsequent inclusion of Christian moral ideas into philosophy , Europe developed what Nietzsche would call a Genealogy of Morals , a way in which previous philosophies and ideas were the foundations for the next , and how that caused growth in a particular direction but disfavored others . One of these directions was called ' metaphysicalism ' , like the Land of the Forms . When examining an idea philosophically , you 'd examine it in isolation from everything else and from reality itself , developing a model for how the perfect version worked in isolation from surrounding flaws . Another , opposing direction , which lost popularity briefly in the middle ages but can be found both in Greece and later , was the dialectic argument . It 's been restated a bunch of times , but it 's based on a dialogue between two parties who disagree and who try to undermine each other 's argument for what is through finding imperfections , flaws , and ultimately contradictions in what appear to be metaphysically pure ideas . Socrates was famous for forcing people to engage in these ' dialogues ' with him . One of his most famous examples of this was to consider a heap of sand . In a metaphysical worldview this concept is clear , but in a dialectic , suppose a light breeze comes along and starts to lift sand grains one at a time , at what point would it cease to be a heap ? In Engel 's view , this speaks to the need to examine the actual numbers , rather than the metaphysical ideas , and consider how quantitative changes can effect qualitative ones . Marx never actually used the words ' dialectic materialism ' ; they were coined by others to describe the mode of his work . Many of our arguments in the 19th century for how societies worked were metaphysical , and to an extent you can still see that today . People think about , say , a factory worker , and they imagine a physically healthy and fit man who is raising a family on his salary , and takes the best work he can find , and we examine the capitalist investor who owns the factory and paid to build it , and how he picks the best projects , and pays those willing to work at the lowest rate , but when we start to examine those dialectically and from a materialist standpoint we start to reveal the flaws and contradictions inherent in those ideas . Our conception of the factory owner is incomplete if we do n't consider this dialectic relationship to the workers at his factory , and the imperfections in that relationship and how they create the imperfections in the greater whole . The most famous example concerns private property . He argues that the collapse of private property is inevitable , on the following basis : Capitalists use property to acquire profit . Profit is an increase in the amount of property you have . That acquisition concentrates private property in the hands of the few , until most do not own property anymore . While in principle , you expect good ideas to be rewarded with value , the reality of the power relationship created by the owner / employee relationship does not allow a fair exchange , and inevitably the factory worker who augments production will not be rewarded the full value of his invention . The concept that anyone , not just the government / nobility may own land , with the rules of a capitalist free market , inevitably leads to a new nobility arising who themselves have the exclusive privilege of owning property . You ca n't get this from examining just the factory owner or just the laborer , but only from examining the nature of their relationship to one another , by looking for the ways in which in reality they will differ from their ' perfect ' forms , and so on . Read the book if you want his full argument in detail . The dialectic approach is essentially a rejection of metaphysicalism , the idea that phenomena can be understood in isolation from one another or in a perfect , ' textbook ' form . It can be applied to history , economics , sociology , everything . When examining Marx , what they mean to say when there is a dialectic relationship is that there is a common misconception that arises from a metaphysical worldview that examines concepts , ideas , movements , classes , or people in their perfect or ideal form , without considering a third party relationship ( the relationship between the factory worker and the grocery store , for example ) , common flaw ( a lack of education in the workforce to know what their options are for selling a money - saving idea and the common willingness to exploit that through disinformation and threats ) , or simply slow , quantitative changes ( how the ability of the free market to operate in a community changes when the ability to own property is in fewer and fewer hands , and more and more people have to try to outbid each other downwards for less and less reward ) . TL;DR : when they say something is ' dialectically related ' , they really mean " put down your beer , I 'm about to blow your mind , because there 's something you would n't normally think about that 'll completely change how you see this topic . "
rejected:
The term "dialectic" refers to a way of thinking about ideas and problems that involves examining different points of view and looking for a synthesis or resolution. In the context of Marxist theory, dialectics refers to the idea that social and historical change occurs through the conflict and resolution of opposing ideas or forces.

For example, Marx believed that the conflict between different classes in society, such as the bourgeoisie (owners of the means of production) and the proletariat (workers), was a key driver of social and economic change. He believed that this conflict could eventually lead to a resolution, such as the overthrow of the capitalist system and the establishment of a socialist society.

So, when something is described as being "dialectically related," it means that it is connected to or influenced by this process of conflict and resolution. It might be helpful to think of dialectics as a kind of "back and forth" between different ideas or forces, with the ultimate goal of finding a solution or resolution.

query: What are Computable Numbers ? I need to write an essay on computable numbers , but it seems to me that basically every number is a computable number . Either that , or I clearly do not understand what a computable number is . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The set of Real numbers is _ uncountable _ . That is , you ca n't assign a natural number to every Real number . However you _ can _ assign a natural number to every Turing machine : the set of Computable Numbers is _ countable _ . Therefore the set of Computable Numbers is a subset of Real numbers . To take a specific case , for a given non - infinite Turing machine there will be Real numbers that it ca n't compute , because there will be numbers that it can not represent or output .
You know how there exist algorithms for computing pi and the like ? The ones people use to get the 100000 - zillionth digit of whatever ? Well , almost every number we normally use is like pi , in that there is a program that can calculate it to any accuracy you want . Even if the digits of pi never end , you can calculate pi up to any digit with no problems . Using mathematics , people have been able to show that there exist other numbers that do n't have this feature . By that I mean , there is no program that can compute them like pi can be computed . There is no program that will give you every digit of the number if you run if for long enough . It can not exist . These numbers are practically inaccessible . I could never give you a way to enumerate the digits of one , because that would mean the number is computable . This is proven by basically showing that there exist * more * numbers ( real numbers , including numbers like 0.34234451231 .... that never terminate or repeat and go on to infinity ) than there exist programs . It 's true that there is an infinite amount of programs , but it turns out the way numbers are infinite is bigger than the way programs are infinite . The argument for this is a very clever one called a diagnolization argument . There is an infinite number of computable numbers , just like there is an infinite number of programs , but that infinity is smaller than the infinity of all real numbers , so we say that almost every real number is NOT computable . It is ironic that even though non - computable numbers outnumber the computable kind , we 'll rarely if ever talk about ones that are n't computable . Some clever mathematicians have found ways of describing specific numbers that are n't computable . This is usually done by finding a feature that only they possess . Then you can say , " look , the number that has property P is called X " . You can do this even if you ca n't compute the number .
This one is a bit of a problem for me to explain so hopefully there is someone with a stronger mathematics / CS background that can do it easier . Any number that can be explained , whether plainly or by algorithm , through a program is a computable number . This includes almost every number , however there are exceptions ; basically numbers which will halt a program before completion . The only non - computable number I am aware of is Chaitin ’s Constant / Construct . Basically it claims that each program has a normal and transcendental number that is not computable . Which as a group are referred to as CC ’s Omega . The wiki should provide a pretty good explanation .
rejected:
Computable numbers are numbers that can be calculated by a computer or other mechanical process. Not every number is a computable number.

Imagine that you have a calculator. This calculator can perform basic arithmetic operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. It can also handle more complex operations like finding the square root of a number or calculating the sine of an angle.

The calculator can only work with numbers that it is programmed to understand. For example, it may not be able to handle numbers with an infinite number of decimal places, or numbers that are too large or too small to fit in its memory. These numbers are not computable by the calculator.

On the other hand, there are many numbers that the calculator can work with. For example, it can handle whole numbers like 1, 2, 3, and 4. It can also handle decimal numbers like 3.14 or 0.01. These numbers are computable by the calculator.

So, to sum up, a computable number is a number that can be calculated by a computer or other mechanical process. Not every number is a computable number, but there are many numbers that are computable.

query: Will someone explain why the election in France is so globally important ? Title says it . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
As I understand it , there was a candidate in the running who wanted to have France pull a Brexit . Since France is a large economic force in the EU , a lot of countries would possibly be hurt if they left . And since the EU is such a large economic force in the world , if the Euro suffers , the rest of the world will , as well .
I 'll try to give as balanced of an answer as possible , as responses will vary depending on what side of the political spectrum you reside in . Essentially , the importance of the French election comes down to Marine Le Pen , the leader of the National Front party , a far - right political party that is against Muslim immigration and is strongly nationalist and anti - EU . Under a National Front presidency , Le Pen has stated that she will push for a referendum for France to leave the EU ( similar to Brexit ) . If France does leave the EU , the entire union will likely dissolve . The effects of this are largely unknown , but there are some certainties about what would happen in a post - EU world . Each country would have to establish a new currency , which would likely be devalued in order to boost exports . Many people , especially on the political left , believe this could have disastrous economic consequences . I highly recommend listening to Le Pen speak to understand her views on populism , immigration , and maintaining the identity of a cultural France . [ Watch here ] ( URL_0 )
France is a large , populous country with a very long democratic history . Like great Britain , it has ruled large sections of the planet in it 's time , is still a world player , is a nuclear nation and has a history for left - wing political leanings with a strong socialist tradition . It is currently being tempted by the ultra right - wing nationalist parties exemplified by Ms. Le Pen and her cohorts . It is one of the founding members of the European Union and the European Ideal . It is a significant indicator of European direction , opinion and likely change ( if at all ) . Recall if you will , that the recent Low Countries ( Holland et al ) elections threw out it 's right - wing apologists for a centre - left candidate , so the breath of many nations around the world is being held to see how the French will swing . Will they remain reasonable or have matters degenerated so far that the right - wingers seem the only way forward ?
rejected:
The election in France is important because France is a large and influential country in Europe and around the world. It is a member of the European Union and the United Nations, and it has a strong economy and military.

The person who is elected as the leader of France, known as the president, has a lot of power and responsibility. They make decisions about how the country is run, including its policies on things like the economy, education, and foreign affairs.

The election in France is also important because it can affect other countries. For example, if the president of France has different ideas about how to solve problems or handle certain issues, it could affect how France works with other countries.

So, the election in France is important because the person who is elected as president will have a big impact on France and on the world.

query: ETF vs Mutual Fund: How to decide which to use for investing in a popular index?
chosen: If you just want to track an index, then ETFs are, generally speaking, the better way.
rejected: Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and mutual funds are both types of investment vehicles that can be used to invest in a popular index. Both offer diversification and professional management, but there are some key differences to consider when deciding which is best for you.One main difference is that ETFs are traded on stock exchanges, while mutual funds are not. This means that ETFs can be bought and sold throughout the day, while mutual fund transactions are only processed once per day at the end of the trading day. ETFs also tend to have lower fees than mutual funds, because they have a more passive investment strategy and do not have to pay the same level of commissions and other expenses as mutual funds.Another difference is in the way that they are structured. ETFs are typically structured as a basket of securities that track a specific index, such as the S&P 500. Mutual funds, on the other hand, can invest in a wide range of securities and can have a variety of investment objectives, such as growth, income, or a mix of both.Ultimately, the choice between an ETF and a mutual fund will depend on your investment goals and your tolerance for risk. Both can be effective tools for investing in a popular index, but it's important to carefully consider the differences and determine which one is the best fit for your needs.

query: When a stock price rises, does the company get more money?
chosen: When a stock price rises, the company's assets are worth more. This doesn't mean it gets more cash directly, but it can liquidate (= sell) some of its stocks for a higher return than before.
rejected: When the price of a stock rises, it means that the market value of the company has increased. This can be due to a variety of factors, such as improved financial performance, positive news about the company, or increased demand for the stock. However, the company itself does not directly receive any additional money as a result of the stock price rising. Instead, the value of the company's stock is a reflection of the market's perception of the company's worth. If an individual or entity owns shares of the stock and the price goes up, they may be able to sell their shares for a profit. However, the company itself does not receive any additional funds as a result of the stock price increase.

query: How exactly would a vaccine cause neurological defects in a person ? Strictly speaking as if this is something that could happen . How would it ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The theory was that the vaccine could alter your own immune system leading to attack on various systems including the central nervous system . Some vaccines were implicated in causing diseases like Guillain Barre Syndrome ( GBS ) , a demyelinating disorder that can lead to death from respiratory failure and autonomic instability , as well as other conditions like autism . The idea of the flu shot causing GBS was debunked recently where it was found that the incidence of GBS in the non vaccinated population was no different than the vaccinated population . The claims of vaccines causing disease like autism were also retracted when it was discovered that the researcher who published the initial paper in the Lancet in 1998 had falsified results .
It wo n't directly do so , and it 's very rare for it to happen indirectly . But it 's possible for a person to have an allergic reaction to an ingredient in the vaccine , with the usual symptoms and dangers of a systemic allergic reaction . And if a person 's immune system overreacts to the weakened " invader , " s / he can develop a fever high enough to cause brain damage . This is n't more likely to lead to autistic - like symptoms than any other dangerously high fever .
They do n't so there 's that ....
rejected: Vaccines are designed to help protect people from getting sick by giving them a small, safe dose of a virus or bacteria. They do this by teaching the body's immune system to recognize and fight off the virus or bacteria. Most of the time, vaccines are very safe and do not cause any problems. However, in rare cases, vaccines can cause side effects, just like any medicine can. These side effects can be mild, such as a sore arm or feeling tired, or they can be more serious. One rare but serious side effect that has been reported after some vaccines is called a neurological disorder. This is a problem with the nerves or brain that can cause problems with things like movement, learning, or behavior. It is not fully understood why this happens or how often it occurs, but it is thought to be very rare.

query: Does the Fed keeping interest rates low stimulate investment in the stock market and other investments?
chosen: Investopedia has this note where you'd want the contrapositive point: The interest rate, commonly bandied about by the media, has a wide and   varied impact upon the economy. When it is raised, the general effect   is a lessening of the amount of money in circulation, which works to   keep inflation low. It also makes borrowing money more expensive,   which affects how consumers and businesses spend their money; this   increases expenses for companies, lowering earnings somewhat for those   with debt to pay. Finally, it tends to make the stock market a   slightly less attractive place to investment. As for evidence, I'd question that anyone could really take out all the other possible economic influences to prove a direct co-relation between the Federal Funds rate and the stock market returns.  For example, of the dozens of indices that are stock related, which ones would you want that evidence: Total market, large-cap, small-cap, value stocks, growth stocks, industrials, tech, utilities, REITs, etc.  This is without considering other possible investment choices such as direct Real Estate holdings, compared to REITs that is, precious metals and collectibles that could also be used.
rejected: Low interest rates can stimulate investment in the stock market and other investments by making it cheaper for businesses to borrow money. This can encourage companies to invest in new projects and expand their operations, which can lead to increased profits and higher stock prices. Low interest rates can also make other investments, such as bonds, less attractive relative to stocks, which can encourage investors to put more money into the stock market.However, it's important to note that the relationship between interest rates and the stock market is complex and can vary over time. Factors such as economic growth, corporate earnings, and investor sentiment can also have a significant impact on the stock market. In addition, low interest rates alone do not guarantee increased investment or higher stock prices.

query: Why do baby car seats expire after 5 years ? If it 's due to the plastic deteriorating over time , I would think that someone would have created an affordable replacement part by now . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Plastic does deteriorate over time , especially when exposed to heat or cold , which a vehicle can easily reach 100 + degrees or below freezing . Also , when a vehicle is in an accident , the impact can create stress on the plastic that creates small fractures that are n't noticeable but the integrity is compromised . For manufacturers , that 's just a lot of reliability so by limiting it to a certain time period , it reduces that liability .
Just liability . They do n't want it resold and reused then have to deal with any problems the second hand consumer has .
Reminds me of bike helmets . Those expire as soon as you have a significant accident even If no damage is showing
rejected: Baby car seats are designed to be very safe, and they have a number of features that help protect babies and young children in case of an accident. These features are designed to hold up well over time, but they can become less effective as the car seat gets older.One of the main reasons baby car seats expire is because the materials that they are made of can break down over time, especially when they are exposed to heat and sunlight. This can make the car seat less safe to use, because it may not be able to provide the same level of protection as it did when it was new.Another reason baby car seats expire is because safety standards can change over time. Manufacturers are always working to improve the safety of their products, and they may come out with new car seats that have different features or are made with different materials that are safer for babies and young children. When this happens, the old car seats may no longer meet the new safety standards, and they may expire.Overall, baby car seats expire to help make sure that babies and young children are as safe as possible when they are riding in a car.

query: how exactly does medicine work ? Like what in your body does it tamper with ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The most common route is taking medication by mouth - the oral route . From here the medication has a journey to make that starts from our mouths to the target area . * * Drugs In The Digestive System :* * Once medicines reach the stomach they may start to dissolve . However , some pills that are in capsule form will remain intact until they reach the small intestine . This is because if the capsule was to disintegrate in the stomach the contents would be destroyed by the hydrochloric acid . Therefore some medications , are sealed in special capsules to survive the stomach . They are then absorbed through the lining of the small intestine . Medications are then carried into the blood stream - moving from a high concentration fluid in the intestine to a lower concentration fluid - the blood . * * The Circulatory System :* * No matter what form a medicine takes they will be dissolved and enter the blood stream at some point . In this form the medicines are circulated around the body and so delivered to organs and tissues . This process is very fast . The heart can pump a full cycle of blood in about one minute . During this process the medicine molecules are being delivered throughout the body . The brain will receive the largest portion - about 16 % . The effects of the medicine will continue to be active until metabolism begins . The metabolism for each medicine is different . In other words some are broken down and prepared for elimination more quickly than others . That is why some medicines will be given frequently , for example 2 - 4 hourly , others less so . In fact some medicines may already be breaking down when they passed through the liver for the first time - this is called ' first - pass effect ' . Some form of tablets and capsules may have to go through the liver several times before they can be eliminated from the body . * * Within the body tissues and cells :* * Within the body tissues we eventually get down to the cellular level . The cells of the body can absorb fluids and so will soak up the molecules of medicine within blood fluid that surrounds them . Not all medicines will start to work at the same time . This is because different medicines all have their own specific molecules that will dissolve either quite quickly or more slowly than others . In addition some medicines find it easier to dissolve in human tissue than others . Human cell membranes have a high content of fats . Therefore if you have a medicine that can dissolve in fat then they will begin to work more quickly than those that ca n't . This ability to dissolve in fat is a very important point on how fast a drug will begin to act in the body . Once the dissolved medications are within the cell this is where most of them do their work through biochemical reactions . They then re - enter the blood stream where they will once again enter the liver . Here they will be broken down further and prepared to be eliminated by the body . * * Elimination :* * The main organs associated with elimination of medicines is the liver and kidneys . Basically these organs sieve out toxic substances . The liver breaks them down through a series of complex metabolic reactions . These toxins / waste products are stored by the liver until it is ready to send them to the kidneys . In the kidneys the prepared substances are further processed and then eliminated from the body through the urine .
You 're going to have to specify which medicine you want to know about . There are a lot of different medicines and they work in very different ways .
To put it as simply as possible , most drugs work to either enhance or block chemicals interacting with receptors in the body , depending on the desired outcome . If you 're trying to prevent something , they work to block specific receptors . If you 're trying to boost something , they work to stimulate or enhance targeted receptors . They are picked due to their chemical structures . Depending on the medication in question , it 's actually very specific . But in general , medicine works due to it 's chemical structure and the relationship of that structure to specific receptors in the human body .
rejected:
Medicine is a way that doctors and other healthcare professionals use to help people feel better when they are sick or hurt. There are many different kinds of medicine, and they work in different ways.

For example, some medicine helps to kill bacteria that can make you sick. This type of medicine is called an antibiotic. When you have an infection, like a cold or the flu, your body has bacteria inside it that are making you feel sick. An antibiotic helps to kill those bacteria and make you feel better.

Other medicine helps to reduce pain or inflammation. If you have a headache, for example, you might take a pain medicine to help the pain go away. Or if you have a sore muscle, you might take a medicine that helps to reduce inflammation, which is when your body's tissues become swollen or tender.

Medicine can also help to control your body's functions, like your blood pressure or your blood sugar. If you have high blood pressure, for example, you might take a medicine that helps to lower it.

In general, medicine works by interacting with your body's cells and tissues in different ways. Some medicine works by blocking certain chemicals or signaling pathways in your body, while others work by stimulating certain processes or activating certain proteins.

It's important to remember that medicine can be very helpful, but it can also have side effects. That's why it's important to always follow your doctor's instructions when taking medicine and to talk to your doctor if you have any concerns.

query: How were movies edited before the invention of the computer ? Especially movies like King Kong . My mind is boggled at people able to edit king holding a person in his hand without a computer . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Movies used to be shot on 35 mm film . Directors & editors would view everything that was shot . They would cut the films apart and splice together what they wanted . The rest was discarded , or saved for later . This is where the expression '' Left on the cutting room floor '' came from .
They would edit by hand . Special effects like King Kong could be done with models and things . They could also shoot part of a scene with a blocker preventing part of the film from being exposed and then use the same film to shoot the other part of the scene with the other part exposed . Like filming the left half of the screen separate from filming the right half of the screen so that you have a character playing their own twin on screen at the same time .
A lot of those old effects are very large props ( or conversely miniature models ) shot to give the perspective of a giant ape . As for editing and adding post effects , everything was done directly with the physical film .
rejected: Before the invention of computers, movies were edited using physical film reels. This meant that editors had to physically cut the film into pieces and splice them back together in the desired order using tape or cement. This process was called "film cutting" or "film editing." To edit a scene where King Kong is holding a person in his hand, the editor would have to find the footage of King Kong holding the person and the footage of the background or other elements that should be in the scene. They would then cut these pieces of film into smaller sections and rearrange them so that they were in the correct order. For example, if the editor wanted to show King Kong holding the person for a few seconds, they might cut out a section of film from the middle of the scene and splice it back into the film before or after the King Kong footage. They would repeat this process for each shot or scene in the movie, until the entire film was edited. Editing film this way was a very time-consuming and labor-intensive process, but it was the only way to edit movies before computers. Today, most movies are edited using computer software, which makes the process much faster and easier.

query: How can new phones like the Nokia Lumia 1020 have a 41 MP camera when DSLR 's are ~20 MP and cost way more ? I 'm baffled by this , someone please explain D : Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The number of megapixels only tells part of the story ( a relatively small part , too ) . The quality of the lens , and the size of the CCD ( the part that records the light ) are much bigger deals . A 41 MP camera with cheap optics and a small CCD will produce images that look quite a bit worse than an 8 MP with good optics and a large CCD ( which means less noise in the resulting image ) . The optics in particular are what tends to add the most cost to a good camera .
The actual sensor size in the phone camera is much , much smaller . Sure it may be " 41 MP " but it will have a lot more noise per pixel than a 20 MP DSLR sensor , which is overall much higher quality . Also , a DSLR has a lot more going into it . Physical autofocus , dedicated photo processors , and more .
MP are only 1 measurement of camera quality . It 's like asking why your car with a 12 gear transmission is n't the fastest racer . It still needs the rest of the drive train to be of the same quality to succeed .
rejected: The main difference between a phone camera and a DSLR camera is the size of the image sensor. The image sensor is a piece of hardware inside the camera that captures light and turns it into an image. DSLR cameras typically have much larger image sensors than phone cameras, which allows them to capture more detailed images, especially in low light conditions. However, just because a phone camera has a higher megapixel count doesn't necessarily mean that it will take better pictures than a DSLR with a lower megapixel count. Megapixels refer to the number of pixels in an image, but they don't necessarily determine the quality of the image. Factors like the size and quality of the image sensor, the lens, and the image processing software all play a role in the overall quality of the photos a camera can take. So, while it may seem strange that a phone camera can have a higher megapixel count than a DSLR, it's important to remember that there are many other factors that contribute to the overall quality of a camera.

query: why do people of all ages , races , languages , ethnical persuasion laugh the exact same way ? From a 1 year old American baby to a 50 year old Aboriginal tribe chief , all humans laugh the same way . Why ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
My best guess would be that laughter is sort of built in to the human brain , almost like instinctual , like crying when you 're sad or screaming when you are in pain , and is n't taught to you . Basically because it 's built in it is n't affected nearly as much by a persons ethnicity or location as their language is . For example , every cat I 've ever observed , regardless of breed or location , hissed when it felt threatened and purred / rubbed up against something when it was pleased . * * TL;DR laughter is part of the human 's natural language in a way words are not . * *
I believe its the same as coughing , screaming , sniffing or sneezing . Its a biological response to a stimulus , not a language response to a conversation in a society Apes laugh too , so it 's not even species specific , its just something generations of mammals have passed down
Laughter is biological , not cultural . Of course things that trigger laughter are cultural , but the physical act of laughing is biological . Many basic emotions are universally experienced , and are physically expressed the same way across all cultures . Many facial expressions are " hard wired " to look the same everywhere . Expressions of joy , sadness , agression , disgust , etc , always use the same facial muscles to be communicated . ( For example there is n't a culture anywhere that expresses happiness by frowning . ) Laughter is the same thing ... babies are hard wired to know how to do it ... it 's universal and it is n't " learned " . For more about this I 'd suggest reading studies conducted by Paul Ekman , a professor at UCSF . He studied facial expression and emotion back in the 70s in Papua New Guinea . Although not every emotion is encoded universally in facial expressions , many are . The facial expressions that he found to be universal are anger , disgust , fear , happiness , sadness , surprise , amusement , contempt , contentment , embarrassment , excitement , guilt , pride in achievement , relief , satisfaction , sensory pleasure , and shame .
rejected:
Laughter is a natural response that humans and some other animals have when they find something funny or enjoyable. It's a way of expressing happiness and can help us feel good and bond with other people.

Laughter is produced by a series of sounds and movements that are controlled by the muscles in our face and chest. When we laugh, we make a "ha ha ha" sound and our face changes by scrunching up our nose and mouth. Our chest may also shake or vibrate.

People of all ages, races, and cultures can laugh in the same way because we all have the same muscles in our face and chest that allow us to make the same sounds and movements when we laugh. Laughter is a universal language that everyone can understand and enjoy.

query: How are there naturally - flavored fruit drinks that contain no juice ? My strawberry - flavored sparkling water says that it has only natural flavors , but contains no juice . How is this possible ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
1 : you do not need to have the flavor come from the source in order for it to be natural . One example of this is ( if I recall ) vanilla . Vanilla flavor can be naturally replicated with excretions from glands near a beavers ass for example . It 's still 100 % natural but not from the actual plant . 2 : extracts and the like are commonly used to flavor things naturally , and is usually made from soaking the plant in alcohol for a long period . This means it 's not juice but concentrated flavor .
Flavor is generally just a few chemical compounds . For example vanilla flavor can be extracted from vanilla beans . Or can be artificially made , or can extracted from trees ( I think oak ? ) So something can be naturally vanilla flavored ( depending on your countries labeling laws ) and contain no actual vanilla bean extract . In your example , strawberry flavor is often [ Ethyl methylphenylglycidate ] ( URL_0 ) , which may be made artificially or extracted from a natural source which may or might not be actual strawberries .
Someone figured out exactly what molecule or combination of molecules causes strawberry flavor , and extracted this from a strawberry . But they did n't juice a strawberry and put the juice in the water , they just put the chemical that tastes strawberry - like in the water .
rejected:
Some fruit-flavored drinks that contain no juice are made by adding natural flavors to water. These natural flavors are made from essential oils, extracts, or concentrates that are derived from real fruits. These flavors are used to give the drink a fruity taste, just like how adding a drop of vanilla extract can give a dessert a vanilla flavor.

So even though the drink doesn't have any actual fruit juice in it, it can still taste like a certain fruit because it has the same flavors as the fruit. It's kind of like how you can have a toy that looks like a banana, even though it's made of plastic and not a real banana. The toy looks and feels like a banana, but it's not actually made from a real banana. Similarly, a fruit-flavored drink that contains no juice can still taste like a certain fruit because it has the same flavors as the fruit, even though it doesn't contain any actual fruit juice.

query: Do I need multiple credit monitoring services?
chosen: Monitoring all three is good practice. That way, you will be notified as soon as there is a hard pull on any of your reports. Most financial institutions only pull one of your three reports to open new credit. If you're only monitoring one, you won't be alerted to new accounts until about a month passes and they are reported to all three. By this time, restoration will be much, much more difficult than if you called the financial institution immediately to say "that's not me!"
rejected: It is generally not necessary to use multiple credit monitoring services. Credit monitoring is a service that alerts you to changes to your credit reports, such as when a new account is opened in your name or when there is a change to your personal information. It can be helpful in detecting fraudulent activity and protecting your credit score.There are many credit monitoring services available, and you can choose one that meets your needs and budget. Some credit monitoring services offer additional features, such as credit scores, credit reports, and identity theft protection, so you may want to consider these options when selecting a service.If you are concerned about your credit and want to monitor it closely, you may choose to use a credit monitoring service. However, you do not need to use multiple services, as one service should be sufficient to meet your needs.

query: how does a candle burn so long ? Like it 's just a string that would normally burn up in 5 seconds but put wax on it and now it lasts for hours ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
The fire melts the wax which is drawn up into the wick which then evaporates under the even more intense heat of the center of the flame which then ignites , producing more flame , and cooling down the wick enough to keep it from burning . Basically , evaporative cooling . The wick does not burn because the wax evaporates off it fast enough to cool it down enough to not burn . If you start a candle then blow it out , and do this over and over , you can burn up the wick to the point where it 's unusuable , in which case you have to melt down the candle to the point enough extra wick comes out . This is because the wick burns a little before enough wax melts to start the cycle .
It 's actually the wax that 's burning , mainly . The wick is just there to convey the molten wax upward by capillary action to a point where the flame will heat it enough to burn . Theoretically , you could make a candle with a wick made of some other material , not string , that would n't burn at all .
There are two parts that work together , fuel ( made out of some sort of wax ) and the wick ( which should be naturally absorbent ) the candle wax is melted by the heat of the flame , the liquid wax is drawn back up the wick and released as a gas .
rejected:
A candle burns because the heat of the flame melts the wax near the wick. The melted wax then moves up the wick and is drawn into the flame, where it is vaporized and burned. The heat of the flame also breaks down the molecules in the wax, causing them to react with oxygen from the air to create heat, light, and water vapor.

The reason a candle can burn for a long time is because the wax acts as a fuel source for the flame. As the wax burns, the heat of the flame melts more wax, which is drawn up the wick and into the flame. This process can continue for a while as long as there is enough wax to keep the flame going.

So, to sum it up, a candle burns because the heat of the flame melts the wax, which is drawn up the wick and into the flame. The wax then reacts with oxygen to create heat, light, and water vapor. As long as there is enough wax, the candle can continue to burn.

query: How do multi-currency bank accounts work? What is the advantage?
chosen: Today typically a Business needs to hold accounts in more than one currency. Banks in certain countries are offering what is called a dual currency account. It is essentially 2 accounts with same account number but different currency. So One can have an account number say 123456 and have it in say AUD and USD. So the balance will always show as X AUD and Y USD. If you deposit funds [electronic, check or cash] in USD; your USD balance goes up. Likewise at the time of withdrawal you have to specify what currency you are withdrawing. Interest rates are calculated at different percentage for different currencies.  So in a nutshell it would like operating 2 accounts, with the advantage of remembering only one account number. Designate a particular currency as default currency. So if you don't quote a currency along with the account number, it would be treated as default currency. Otherwise you always quote the account number and currency. Of-course bundled with other services like free Fx Advice etc it makes the entire proposition very attractive. Edit: If you have AUD 100 and USD 100, if you try and withdraw USD 110, it will not be allowed; Unless you also sign up for a auto sweep conversion.  If you deposit a GBP check into the account, by default it would get converted into AUD [assuming AUD is the default currency]
rejected: A multi-currency bank account is a type of bank account that allows you to hold and manage balances in multiple currencies. This can be useful if you frequently make or receive payments in different currencies, or if you frequently travel or do business internationally.One advantage of a multi-currency bank account is that it can help you save on foreign exchange fees. When you receive a payment in a foreign currency, it is typically converted into your local currency before it is deposited into your bank account. This conversion process usually involves a fee, which can add up over time if you frequently receive payments in different currencies. With a multi-currency bank account, you can hold balances in the original foreign currencies, which means you can avoid these conversion fees.Another advantage of a multi-currency bank account is that it can provide you with greater flexibility when it comes to managing your finances. For example, if you have a multi-currency bank account, you can hold balances in multiple currencies and transfer funds between them as needed, which can be useful if you need to make payments in different currencies or if you want to take advantage of fluctuations in exchange rates.It is important to note that multi-currency bank accounts may have different fees and requirements than traditional bank accounts, so it is a good idea to compare the features and terms of different accounts before deciding which one is right for you.

query: How do ETF fees get applied?
chosen: The ETF price quoted on the stock exchange is in principle not referenced to NAV. The fund administrator will calculate and publish the NAV net of all fees, but the ETF price you see is determined by the market just like for any other security.  Having said that, the market will not normally deviate greatly from the NAV of the fund, so you can safely assume that ETF quoted price is net of relevant fees.
rejected:
Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are investment vehicles that allow you to buy or sell shares in a basket of securities. ETFs are similar to mutual funds in that they both offer investors exposure to a diverse range of assets, but they trade on stock exchanges and can be bought and sold throughout the day like individual stocks.One of the key features of ETFs is that they typically have low fees compared to other types of investment vehicles. These fees are often referred to as the "expense ratio," which is the annual fee that the ETF charges its shareholders for its management and other expenses. The expense ratio is expressed as a percentage of the value of the assets in the ETF and is deducted from the returns earned by the ETF.For example, if an ETF has an expense ratio of 0.5% and you have invested $10,000 in the ETF, the annual fee that you would pay would be $50 ($10,000 x 0.5%). This fee would be deducted from the returns earned by the ETF, so the actual return that you receive would be lower than the return earned by the ETF before the fees were applied.In addition to the expense ratio, ETFs may also charge other fees, such as trading fees when you buy or sell shares in the ETF. These fees are generally much smaller than the expense ratio and may be waived by some brokers.It's important to carefully consider the fees that an ETF charges before you invest in it, as they can have a significant impact on your returns over the long term. You can typically find information about the fees that an ETF charges in its prospectus or on the website of the company that manages the ETF.
Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are investment vehicles that allow investors to purchase a diversified portfolio of assets in a single security. These fees are generally paid out of the assets of the fund, and they are reflected in the ETF's expense ratio.The expense ratio is the percentage of the fund's assets that are used to pay the fund's expenses. For example, if an ETF has an expense ratio of 0.50%, it means that 0.50% of the assets in the fund are used to pay the fund's expenses. This percentage is then deducted from the fund's returns each year.There are also other fees that may be associated with investing in ETFs. These can include brokerage commissions and other transaction fees that are charged when you buy or sell an ETF. These fees will vary depending on the broker you are using and the specific ETF you are trading.It's important to carefully consider the fees associated with any investment, as they can have a significant impact on your returns over time. It's a good idea to compare the fees of different ETFs before making a decision about which one to invest in.

query: Why do 3d printers aim to heat the filament only in the nozzle instead of keeping a reservoir of fluid filament ? It seems to me that temperature control would be easier that way . Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
What happens if you need to change colors ? You now have to cool down a large reservoir of fluid plastic before you can switch . The energy used to heat up the reservoir is lost . If the reservoir is within the machine , you have to remove the solid cooled plastic and then clean out the reservoir so that the previous color does n't mix with the new color . If the reservoir was in the color cartridge , then each cartridge would be more expensive because it has to have a reservoir that can the heat instead of a simple spindle . You also have to clean the tubing that leads from the cartridge to the nozzle when changing colors .
If I remember right , some polymers degrade if kept at high temperatures for too long . Best to heat only as needed
Temperature control of the reservoir is n't the issue . It 's controlling the temperature as the plastic transforms from a liquid / viscous state into something hard enough to self - support . The challenge with running a liquid reservoir is accurate throughput / pressure coming out of the nozzle that matches the printing parameters . The pressure placed on the unmelted > melted plastic is constant and easily modified in a traditional FDM printer . If you have a cheap , reliable , and easy to implement solution to flowing and then cooling / curing a thin thermoplastic filament out of a large liquid reservoir you should get yourself rich , quick .
rejected: 3D printers typically heat the filament only in the nozzle because it is more efficient and easier to control the temperature that way. If the printer tried to heat a whole reservoir of filament, it would take a lot longer and use a lot more energy to get the filament up to the right temperature. Additionally, it would be more difficult to maintain a consistent temperature throughout the entire reservoir of filament. By heating only the filament that is about to be extruded through the nozzle, the printer can more easily control the temperature and ensure that the filament is the right temperature when it is being used to build the 3D object.

query: Why after a few days do cold - cuts get " slimy " feeling ? Sometimes its only just 1 or 2 days in the fridge . Is it because they are are bad ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Few years working a company chain - Deli counter , simple answer is that is bacteria that is forming on the meat . Indicating that is indeed spoiling . Why you may be encountering that is . Meat generally lasts a little more than a few days . 5 I 'd say is a good average . Sliming can occur earlier if 1 . ) Bad refrigeration on either you or the Deli when they sliced it . 2 . ) The Deli may of used an already opened , day or few old piece of meat . Yes , Delis use few day old meat . It 's not uncommon in the slightest at chain Delis . 3 . ) The product itself is bad . Factories out there nowadays are using cheaper , more disgusting fillers to sell their product . Avoid the obviously super cheap products . You get what you pay for . ( usually ) . My own store has brands that are slimey the moment you open the bag . How to help avoid this : 1 . ) Ask to see the piece of meat , so that you may inspect it / try it yourself . Most obvious way is the smell . If you can smell a pungent stank from your side of the counter , avoid it or ask for a new one to be opened . ( Be warned , depending on your relations with the workers , asking for a new one to be opened 24/7 may agitate them & make fun of you / disrespect you behind your back . Do n't be " that guy " . but ensure your own / others ' health comes first ) . 2 . ) Go during busy hours . Yes you will run into a line , but you 'll be giving yourself a better chance at receiving a fresher product with everything being opened in the time window . 3 . ) Stick to the popular items . If you ask for the imported , nitrate - free , low - sodium , raised on a farm in Africa , caught using sterile butterfly nets ; you 'll have to be expecting to be receiving an old product . Sticking to the popular stuff again , will help keep you with the more recently opened , fresher products . Unfortunately you miss out on these premium , overall healthier & better products , but unless they are actually edible , you 'll be happier if you avoided it for the time being . Use Step # 1 often . Also know that it 's no ones purpose to sell customers slimey products . It 's just how they are made & handled . Unfortunately one of the problems is the lack of health & saftey enforcement in Delis . When it 's a busy Sunday afternoon , workers do not like to be stared down while cleaning . Customers start yelling at workers when they do not receive help at these times , so workers are almost forced to put off cleaning & safety guidelines to push the sales . Impatient customers & wimpy bosses who ca n't stand up to said customers is one of the main reasons people receive bad product .
Wow this fucking thread is making me sick i always thought it was a fat or oil that just became more liquid ...
All meat , no matter how clean , contains some bacteria on it . bacteria multiply quickly , but refrigeration slows down the process , so what would take hours , takes several days in your fridge . Do n't eat slimy meat .
rejected: Cold cuts, also known as deli meats, can become slimy over time because of bacteria that grows on their surface. Bacteria are tiny organisms that are present everywhere in the environment, including on the surface of food. When cold cuts are left out at room temperature for too long, or when they are not stored properly in the refrigerator, the bacteria can grow and multiply. This can cause the surface of the cold cuts to become slimy and slippery.It's important to remember to store cold cuts in the refrigerator at a temperature of 40°F (4°C) or below, and to use them within a few days of opening the package. If you notice that your cold cuts have become slimy, it's best to throw them away to prevent the risk of food poisoning.

chosen:

Truth is , a lot of people do n't make any money . You 've got to have something people want to see , and way to get eyeballs on your content . It 's very possible to spend money trying to get eyeballs on your content and never make any of the money back .
Giigu explained it perfectly , but a little FYI about ad revenue . Do n't expect to become rich by having a few hundred or even a few thousand people visiting your site . You need hundreds of thousands of unique daily page views before you will generate any significant ad revenue . But , once you 're at those levels , the pay - off is fairly well . The best way to profit from a website is to take money from users through either donations , merchandise or premium features . This is why I suspect reddit does n't deploy ads but offers reddit gold . Though , if the owners of reddit were really smart , they 'd allow users to choose to activate google or project wonder ads on that nice 200x200 pixel banner off on the right as a way to bring in additional financially support the site .

When someone clicks on an ad, the website owner gets paid a small amount of money. This is called "pay-per-click" advertising. Sponsored content: Another way is by creating content that is sponsored by a company. This means that the company pays the website owner to write about their product or service. Affiliate marketing: Website owners can also make money by promoting products or services from other companies and earning a commission on each sale. Subscription model: Some websites charge a fee for access to exclusive content or features. Donations: Some website owners ask their readers to donate money to help support their website. Overall, the goal is to attract a large audience to the website and then find ways to monetize that audience through these various methods.

query: Why is cat5 wire used for data ? Could regular 18/2 wire do the same thing ? Since data is just 1s and 0s can you use any kind of wire transmit data ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
When you start transmitting data at radio frequency ( CAT5 supports up to to 100MHz ) , copper is not just copper and the electrical characteristics of the cable start to limit how much data you can transmit and how far it can propogate . CAT5 is nifty because it has four twisted pairs of conductors ( which minimizes some of the effects of long cabling ) and it 's standard therefore cheap to rely on . So no , you ca n't just use 18/2 wire . You could if you twisted 8 conductors into pairs , at which point you have a CAT5 cable .
It would work ( TCP / IP has been successfully used across barbed wire before ) , but for maximum efficiency CAT5E uses the wire pairs and the twists that each pair have around the individual four pair to help reduce the possibility of crosstalk .
You ca nt think of the signals as digital in a wire when you transmit at high speed . You have to think of the signal as analog signal . Signals do nt change instantly from 1 to 0 and they that time to travel in a cable . A signal in a cat 5 cable travel at 0.64 time the speed of light or 4.8 to 5.3 ns / meter 100Mbit ethernet so 100 * 10 ^ 6 bits per second = 100 bits in 1000 ns = 1 bit in 10 ns . So if the ethernet cables is longer then 2 meters there are more then one bit in the cable . A cable cab be 100 m long so there will be 50 bits in the cable . So the signal is obviulsy the the same in the while cable . The bitrate in the calbe is in practice higher because of encoding . This a a image of how a 100Mbit ethernet signal looks like . URL_1 Here you have a eye diagram where you overlap different signals . URL_0 The way to look at is is that the system has to determine if the signal is in the at the top in the middle or at the bottom where the black gaps are . You can see that is is noisy and the holes(eyes ) are quite small . The smaller the eyes are the harder it is to determine where the signal is and if they dissiperar you ca nt detect the signal . So not when you see that the signal is noisy and analog you are starting to realiste that you need a cables that behave in certain ways . How a signal propagate depends on the distance between the distance between the wire and the thickness and other material along the wire and by other factors . There will be interference from the surrounding and the other wire pairs so the wire has to be twisted is rates relative to each other to minimize interference . So to have a wire with known parameter that works with the hardware you have to have standard . You can run the signal over other wires if you now what you do . Look at this demonstration with barbed wire URL_2 there is a reason that the barbed wire pars is close to other and parallell and the pares are fare apart . I have read a article about is that I ca nt find that explained that barbed wire in that configuration have similar effect on the signal as a cat 5 cable . If the wires in the pairs would have not been parallell or the pairs been closer it would have not worked .
rejected:
Yes, you can use any kind of wire to transmit data, but different types of wire are better suited for different tasks. Cat5 wire, which stands for category 5, is a type of twisted pair cable that is commonly used for carrying data. The "twisted pair" part of the name refers to the way that the wires inside the cable are arranged: they are twisted together in pairs.

There are a few reasons why cat5 wire is often used for data transmission:

It can transmit data at fast speeds: Cat5 wire is designed to support data transmission speeds of up to 100 megabits per second (Mbps). This is fast enough for many common uses, such as connecting computers to the internet or transmitting video and audio.

It is resistant to interference: The twists in the wire pairs help to cancel out electrical interference from other sources, such as other cables or appliances. This makes cat5 wire more reliable for transmitting data over long distances.

It is relatively cheap: Cat5 wire is relatively inexpensive compared to some other types of data cable, making it a popular choice for many applications.

Now, regular 18/2 wire (which is a type of electrical wire used for household wiring) could technically be used to transmit data, but it is not really designed for that purpose. It is not capable of transmitting data at the same speeds as cat5 wire, and it is more prone to interference from other sources. It would also be more expensive to use 18/2 wire for data transmission, since you would need to buy more of it to cover the same distance as cat5 wire.

So, to sum up: you can use any kind of wire to transmit data, but different types of wire are better suited for different tasks. Cat5 wire is a good choice for transmitting data at fast speeds over long distances because it is fast, reliable, and inexpensive.
