query: Why do Etymologies of words only go back to Latin or Greek ? Do we just not have records of older languages ? Or are these languages not based on other languages ? Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
While linguists can reconstruct ancestor languages , those are just educated guesses as to what people used to say . There are no actual written records of Proto - Indo - European ( or any proto - languages ) . So etymologies of many words in any language are traced back to the ' prestige ' language . Whatever language was spoken by the ' elites ' and ' academics ' is used whenever they needed fancy words . Western European languages use Latin and Greek , Japanese uses Chinese , Hindi uses Sanskrit , Turkish uses Arabic , etc . English uses lots of Latin and Greek roots because European scholars all studied Latin ( and some of them studied Greek ) in the neoclassical era . Academic texts were written in Latin , and it was a big deal when people like Newton or Galileo wrote in their native languages . English also has a lot of Latin roots because the Normans invaded in 1066 and introduced French vocabulary into English . The Norman - French aristocrats called cow meat ' beef ' ( cf . French ' boeuf ' ) , while the farmers who raised the cows called them ' cows ' ( cf . German ' Kuh ' ) . Being that French is a daughter language of Latin , we trace the etymologies of these words back to Latin .
Beyond that tracing a word 's etymology is increasingly just plain guesswork . There are n't many scriptures of older languages ( compared to the amount of Latin and Greek writing ) .
The big problem is that older languages do n't have many records , if at all . Still , though , linguists have reconstructed proto - Indo - European to a certain extent , which would be the ancestor language of Latin , Greek , Old English , and a bunch of other languages . A lot of more common words can be traced back to what they probably were in PIE , even if dictionaries do n't always print them .
rejected: Etymologies are the histories of words, and they can go back to any language, not just Latin or Greek. It's just that Latin and Greek are two of the oldest and most studied languages, so we know a lot about their histories and the histories of the words that come from them. There are many other ancient languages that we don't know as much about, and some of them don't have as many written records as Latin and Greek do. So it's not that we don't have records of older languages, it's just that we don't have as much information about them. Does that make sense?

query: Please explain what is "Signal processing"

Signal processing involves the use of algorithms to manipulate and analyze signals in order to extract useful information or to enhance the signal in some way.



There are many different types of signal processing, including digital signal processing (DSP), which involves the use of digital computers to process signals, and analog signal processing, which involves the use of analog circuits and devices to process signals. Signal processing techniques can be used in a wide range of applications, including telecommunications, audio and video processing, image and video analysis, medical imaging, radar and sonar, and many others.



Some common tasks in signal processing include filtering, which removes unwanted frequencies or noise from a signal; compression, which reduces the size of a signal by removing redundant or unnecessary information; and transformation, which converts a signal from one form to another, such as converting a sound wave into a digital signal. Signal processing techniques can also be used to improve the quality of a signal, such as by removing noise or distortion, or to extract useful information from a signal, such as identifying patterns or features.

query: Why exactly am I " color blind ? " As someone who is red / green color blind , I 've always been curious as to what made me this way . Is it an issue in the brain , eyes , both , neither ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Humans normally have 3 kinds of color photoreceptors ( aka cones ) . S , M , and L , for Short wavelength , M for medium wavelength and L , Long wavelength . This sort of but not really corresponds to blue , green and red . Your genes ( thanks mom ! ) code for a defective protein that effectively knocks out one of your 3 categories of color receptors . The human brain is designed to interpret the relative strengths of the three channels as different colors . Those with the normal complement of 3 kinds of cones can recognize approximately 11 million colors . Those with only two functional types of cones can only see approximately 10 - 20 % of that ( I 'm going by ( old ) memory on the numbers ... ) The most common is red / green colorblindness . There are other more exotic kinds of color blindness . Some people have two defective sets of cones . Some have no functional cones at all . True complete color blindness is extremely rare . In the US , 8 or 9 % of males are colorblind . A small fraction of 1 % of females are affected . It is thought that there are a few very specific examples where " colorblind " people can distinguish some shades that normally sighted people can not . There are also some camouflage that they can easily see that are very difficult for normally sighted folk . Hey , you have a minor superpower ... The military experimented with a special red contact that you wear on one eye only that allows you to perceive different shades that you are incapable of discriminating between now . It was called the x - chrome lens . It wo n't really give you true color vision , and the ability to now " pass " the Ishahara color test is really just an interesting artifact . Your brain is fine , but one of your three color channels never sends anything . You ca n't fly an airplane , at least not commercially . You ca n't be a licensed electrician . You should n't be on the bomb squad , since sometimes distinguishing the red wire and the green wire really really matter . You should n't be an interior decorator , since you ca n't distinguish different shades of color very well or at all in some wavelengths . URL_0 I 'm an optometrist hth

Let me explain . You are a male , therefore you rexieved an x chromosome from your mother and a y chromosome from your father . If you can imagine those letters as being laid out on a table and being made of yarn , the y looks much like the x but instead of having 4 dangling ends , there are only 3 . This colorblindness gene was passed on to you by your mother and this gene just so happens to be locared on the part of the x that the y does not have . Because of this , the gene passed on to you by your father were unable to " over ride " so to say , the gene passed on by your mother . The colorblindness gene is a recessive trait and had you been female you would have recieved an x from your mother and an x from your father . Since the x 's have the same amount of genes on them this allows the recessive gene passed to you by your mother to be overpowered by the dominant noncolorblind gene passed to you by your father . Hence why colorblindness is more common in males . In order to be a colorblind female both parents would have to have passed on the colorblindness gene . This is possible , just much less common . So if you sre infact female , you are rare ! :)

Being color blind means that you have difficulty seeing certain colors, or shades of colors, correctly. This can be caused by problems with the way your eyes or brain process colors.

In your case, you are red/green color blind, which means that you have difficulty distinguishing between red and green colors. This type of color blindness is usually inherited, which means it is passed down from your parents.

Your eyes have special cells called cones that help you see colors. There are three types of cones, each sensitive to a different range of colors. People with red/green color blindness have a problem with the cones that are sensitive to either red or green light. This means that they have trouble distinguishing between these colors, and they may see them as the same color or a different color altogether.

The severity of color blindness can vary. Some people have mild color blindness and can still see colors, but they might have trouble distinguishing between certain shades. Others have more severe color blindness and have a harder time seeing colors at all.

I hope that helps to explain why you are color blind. Is there anything else you would like to know?

query: Why is Canada still technically a constitutional monarchy ? Why has n't Canada separated itself officially from the UK after years of independence ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Think of it like growing up with your parents . When you 're young your parents tell you what to do , but as you get older you need them less and less . Now some people or countries in this case do n't really like their parents so they decide they want to get emaciated ( a fancy word for them not having any control over you ) and they become republics . Canada on the other hand really did n't mind it 's parents and as it got older decided it could be more and more independent without them . Canada basically got to the point where our parents do n't really have any input on our decisions . Any time Canada makes a law we still ask our parents if it 's ok but we 're adults now so they really ca n't ever say no . So we operate as if our parents are n't in our lives . Every once in a while our parents come to visit and a lot of people really like having them here . The reason we have n't emancipated is because now there really is n't a point , it would just be a lot of work to separate from someone we really do n't mind .
Like Australia , Canada is not " attached " to the UK . It is an independent nation that shares a monarch ( Queen Elizabeth II ) with many other countries . The UK has no authority over Canada . QEII is referred to as " The Queen of Canada " , * as well as * " The Queen of Australia " or " The Queen of Great Britain , etc ... " To answer the rest of your question , there simply is n't a very strong republican movement in Canada . Despite the ( in my opinion ) compelling arguments for a republic , the public and political enthusiasm is n't there . Even a place like Australia , which has a much more robust republican movement , has failed to become a republic so far ( including a failed referendum in 1999 ) . Becoming a republic is a very big step , and it takes a long time to bring about such a change .
The ' legal ' basis for Canada 's government to even exist was granted by the authority of the Queen . It 's purely symbolic but it rests on a long tradition - tradition going back before written law . At this point , the Queen is purely symbolic so there 's no real incentive to change it .
rejected:
Canada is a country that is made up of different provinces and territories, each with its own government. The country is ruled by a prime minister and a parliament, but it is also part of the British Commonwealth, which means that it is connected to the United Kingdom in some ways.

One way that Canada is connected to the UK is through its system of government, which is called a "constitutional monarchy." This means that the Queen of the UK is also the Queen of Canada, and she is the symbolic head of state. The Queen does not make decisions about how the country is run, but she is a symbol of unity and stability.

Even though Canada is an independent country and makes its own decisions, it has chosen to keep the Queen as its head of state because it has a long history with the UK and values the traditions and connections that come with being part of the British Commonwealth.

query: Why can I drink water and eat salty food but not drink saltwater ? Always thought about this but never gotten any answer . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
You can drink salt water , it just has a LOT more salt than you think . A liter of seawater has around 35 g of salt . That 's about 5 or 6 teaspoons of salt . Seawater is about 3 times as salty as the human body , so if you drank a cup of seawater , you 'd want to drink 2 more cups of freshwater to balance everything .
If you 've ever salted your soup or your casserole or your stew you 've consumed salt water . Salt+water will have sloshed together around your digestive system before now . What we ca n't drink is water high in salt content , for the same reason we ca n't just eat piles of salt . Because it 's toxic in large quantities . Salt is an irritant to the stomach which triggers vomiting in large quantities - this may be partially an evolutionary response to discourage you from drinking dehydrating salty - water as your body really does n't want you to do that . So if you drink sea - water your body should just purge you ASAP to get rid of the excess salt . If you 're otherwise healthy it should just be unpleasant but if you 're already dehydrated or malnourished it can be fatal . Your body requires salt in small doses , it can deal with a certain amount at a time but a large concentration spells danger because it can rapidly dehydrate you . Too much salt over a long period carries more insidious dangers , In simple terms salt retains water ( through a process called osmosis ) and so having too much entering your blood stream thickens the blood and causes high blood pressure . There are also disorders associated with having too little salt , but salt is so omnipresent in our western diet that it is rarely deficiency that 's the issue . TIP : For a sore throat or any wound in the mouth salt water gargles - half a teaspoon of salt to an 8 ounce cup of water - do wonders - boil water , add salt , allow to cool to room temp . gargle mouthfulls at a time and spit do n't swallow as you would for mouthwash . The salt dries out your mouth and throat effectively cleaning wounds and giving germs a bad day .
Because the amount of salt in seawater is VERY HIGH , its not just a sprinkle of salt in the water , there 's a metric shit - ton of salt per cubic mother - fucking inch of water .
rejected: Sure! When you drink water, it helps your body stay hydrated and helps your body's organs and systems work properly. Water is made up of just hydrogen and oxygen atoms, and it doesn't have any other substances dissolved in it like salt does. Saltwater, on the other hand, has a lot of salt dissolved in it. While your body does need some salt to function properly, it needs just the right amount. Drinking saltwater can actually make you more thirsty because the salt in the water can cause your body to lose more water. This is because when there is too much salt in your body, your body tries to get rid of the excess salt by producing more urine, which can also lead to dehydration. So, while it's okay to drink water and eat food with a small amount of salt in it, it's not a good idea to drink saltwater because it can cause your body to lose more water and become dehydrated.

query: How to find SEC filings that are important to stock market
chosen: 10-Q is the quarterly report, and accordingly is filed quarterly. Similarly, 10-K is the annual report. 8-K is a general form for notification of material events. It is filed every time a material event is required to be reported to the shareholders. It may accompany the periodical reports, but doesn't have to. It can be filed on its own. If you're only interested in the financial statements, then you should be looking for the 10K/10Q forms. SEC will tell you when the forms were filed (dates), but it won't tell you what's more material and what's less. So you can plot a stock price graph on these dates, and see what was deemed more material by the investors based on the price fluctuations, but be prepared to find fluctuations that have no correlation to filings - because the market as a whole can drag the stock up or down. Also, some events may not be required to be reported to SEC, but may be deemed material by the investors. For example, a Cupertino town hall meeting discussing the zoning for the new AAPL HQ building may be deemed material by the investors, based on the sentiments, even if no decision was made to be reported to SEC.
rejected: SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) filings are important for stock market investors because they provide information about a company's financial performance, management, and ownership. There are several types of SEC filings that may be of interest to stock market investors, including:Form 10-K: This is an annual report that provides a comprehensive overview of a company's financial performance, including its revenues, expenses, profits, and losses.Form 10-Q: This is a quarterly report that provides similar information to the Form 10-K, but on a more frequent basis.Form 8-K: This is a report that must be filed by a company when it has significant events or changes, such as a change in management, a change in financial condition, or a material contract.Proxy statement: This is a report that must be filed by a company before its annual shareholder meeting. It provides information about the company's management, financial performance, and other matters that will be voted on at the meeting.To find SEC filings, you can visit the SEC's website (www.sec.gov) and use the EDGAR (Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval) system to search for a company's filings. You can search by company name or ticker symbol. You can also use a financial website or app, such as Yahoo Finance or Bloomberg, to access SEC filings for a company. Some of these websites may also provide tools for filtering and analyzing the filings to help you identify the most relevant and important information.

query: In morse code , how do you know when a letter ends and another one begins ? When do you know if the dot or dash is the end of word A and not the beginning of word B Please explain like I'm five.
chosen:
URL_0 > The letters of a word are separated by a space equal to three dots ( one dash ) , and the words are separated by a space equal to seven dots .
Piggy - backing on this question , can you really send Morse with just some kind of percussive sound ? You see in movies people trapped will rap out SOS by just banging on something and I can see how that will work , just because it 's such a familiar pattern . The thing is that the bang only gives you the start of a dot or a dash so how can you tell which is which . It would be very difficult to tell the difference between a word ending with " e " and a word ending with " t " . It seems to me that reading arbitrary messages in Morse with just a banging noise would be very difficult with ambiguity and guesswork everywhere . I know the original telegraph operators discovered that they could hear the letters just from the clicking mechanism but those devices made different clicks to start and stop each dot or dash . I learned Morse myself long ago ( for my amateur radio licence ) but was too slow reading to pass the test ( had to take a limited licence ) . My guess is that the movies are about as accurate in this respect as they are in most other technical areas ; not very . In case the other explanations of OP 's question are not clear enough ... Morse is sent by turning your transmitter on and off rapidly . Depending on how fast you want to send , choose one standard unit of time . Then : * transmitting for one unit is a dot * transmitting for three units is a dash * silence for one unit is separates dots and dashes within a letter * silence for three units separates letters * silence for seven units separates words The transmitter does n't actually send a beeping tone ; it 's just full on or full off . The receiver generates the audible tone , essentially based on how far the tuner is off the transmitting frequency . If you 're 1kHz off you 'll hear a 1kHz tone . If there are multiple transmitters close to the same frequency then you might be able to hear them all and have to distinguish them with your ear . And finally , thinking of the letters as sequences of dots and dashes is bad . Memorising them by writing down symbols on paper is a waste of time . You have to practice by listening to each letter 's rhythm and learning those .
Ham radio operator here . There is a standard for the amount of space between letters , and between words . It is slightly longer than the space between the dot and dash elements that make up letters and punctuation marks . Someone who sends really beautiful code sends these elements precisely and accurately , making it easier for listeners to pick up where letters and words end . " The time taken to produce the sound equivalent to one ' di ' or ' dit ' ( dot ) is taken as unit time and called a dot unit . A ' dah ' is approximately of three dot units length and the space between two sound elements of a letter is one dot unit , i.e. silence period is one dot unit . The space between two letters or characters is equal to three dot units . The space between two words is equal to five dot units . " From here : URL_0 And by the way , in the United States , Morse code is no longer required in order to earn a ham radio license . However it sure comes in handy when you are trying to exchange messages with a station in Antarctica :-) ... -.-
rejected:
In Morse code, there is a short pause between dots and dashes within a single letter, and a longer pause between letters in a word. The length of the pause between words is even longer. 
For example, if you wanted to spell the word "hello" in Morse code, it would be: 
H - dot dash dot dot
E - dot
L - dash dot dot dot
L - dash dot dot dot
O - dash dash dash 
Each letter is separated by a short pause, and the word is separated by a longer pause. 
So, if you are listening to someone sending Morse code, you can tell when a letter ends and another one begins by listening for the pause between the dots and dashes. And you can tell when a word ends and another one begins by listening for the longer pause between the letters.

query: Why is there a specific order for solving maths and how did it come to be that way ? Talking about PEMDAS here . Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Math can be thought of as a language . The same as you know " a car " refers to the four - wheeled vehicle , you can say the word " a car " and know that me too will then understand and associate it with that four wheel metal box . Language could not work if we had differing opinions about what " a car " means . If you thought it meant lunch box , then we 'd be really confused both of us , and ultimately we probably could n't understand each other at all . Mathematics is the same . To be able to communicate calculation of adding two to three , and multiplying that result with 5 , you could write it out like that . However , because we have mathematical symbols , you could just write that as ( 2 + 3 ) * 5 , which is much easier once you understand the language of these symbols . You could , if you wanted to , leave it at that , always using parenthesis to indicate which order to do operations in . But actually , it turns out you can save the effort of writing bunch of parenthesis if everyone agrees on some order of operations in which one should calculate these operations in . The default way . You would still sometimes need parenthesis , sure , but for some cases the order of operations would be the same as the order you wanted to calculate the numbers in . Like , 2 + 2 * 3 ^ 2 could be written as 2 + ( 2 * ( 3 ^ 2 ) ) if you did n't have order of operations(other than that parenthesis first ) . But the first rule of mathematics is , everyone is lazy , and all extra effort is to be avoided . Writing lots of parenthesis is tiresome , and if there 's a way to avoid doing that , mathematicians will jump on that . Agreeing on order of operations allows that laziness , at least occasionally . For why this particular order , that 's to do with algebra . Algebra was for a long time mostly about polynomial equations . Polynomials are expressions like x^3 + 2 * x^2 + 17x - 11 . And because these expressions were such central focus , it makes much sense to make our order of operations such that you never need a single parenthesis to write out a polynomials . If you really wanted to , you could use parenthesis for that too , to indicate order of operations : ( x^3 ) + ( 2 * ( x^2 ) ) + ( 17x ) - 11 . I 'm just trying to demonstrate how much pointless extra effort you 'd have to use when dealing with these kinds of expressions if you did n't have PEMDAS specifically . tl;dr : Math is a language , and languages have rules . This rule is because of polynomials .
There 's a specific order so that everyone gets the same answers when they evaluate the same expression . ( Barring people purposefully writing ambiguous statements on Facebook ) The reason the operations are done in that particular order is because that 's the order you go in when you 're evaluating a polynomial . Setting it as the default order means that you do n't need to use any parentheses when writing polynomials . For instance , we can write 3 * x^(2 ) + 8 * x + 4 without using any parentheses at all . If multiplication and division took precedence over exponents and roots , we 'd need to write that expression as 3 * ( x^(2 ) ) + 8 * x + 4 . If addition and subtraction took precedence over multiplication and division , we 'd need to write it as ( 3 * x^(2 ) ) + ( 8 * x ) + 4 . And if the operations were done in completely opposite order ( parentheses indicate that an operation needs to be done out of order , so in any order of operations , parentheses automatically have top precedence ) , we would need to write the expression as ( 3 * ( x^(2 ) ) ) + ( 8 * x ) + 4 . Thus , the order we 've settled on is PEMDAS because that 's the order you have to go in when evaluating polynomials . ( Insert obligatory " PEMDAS is misleading because it implies multiplication and addition take precedence over their respective inverses " rant )
First , to be clear , the order of operations is more accurately PE(M|D)(A|S ) . Subtraction is just a different kind of addition , and division is the same thing as multiplying by the inverse . So you do P , then E , and the M or D from left to right , then A or S from left to right . This is a convention , it does n't have to be this way . It was devised due to historic reasons and convenience . With it , equations can be terse and concise . Without it , we would have to surround everything in parenthesis to explicitly define the sequence , and that would make equations verbose and difficult to understand .
rejected:
PEMDAS is a mnemonic that stands for Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication and Division, and Addition and Subtraction. It is a set of rules that help us know the correct order to solve mathematical equations.

The specific order for solving math problems has been developed over time to make it easier for people to solve equations accurately and consistently. It's important to follow the order because some operations are done before others. For example, it's important to do any calculations inside parentheses first because those are done before anything else. Similarly, exponents (like squares or cubes) are done before multiplication and division, and multiplication and division are done before addition and subtraction.

Following this order helps us make sure that we solve equations correctly and get the right answer. It's like a recipe for solving math problems - if you follow the steps in the right order, you'll end up with the correct result.

query: Why do Americans say " zee " for " z " while British say " zed " ? I 'm Australian and this has always confused me . Also , which one is right ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
It 's not America vs Britain , really . It 's the US vs the entire English speaking world . We say zed in Canada too , as they do in Australia , New Zealand , South Africa , Ireland , etc . It 's generally believed to have resulted from American drives to simplify usage in the 19th century , by analogy with bee , cee , dee , etc . But nobody is really all that certain why the US changed the pronunciation .
The British pronounce z as zed by way of the French word zede , from the Greek zeta . Early Americans , being British subjects , mostly pronounced it zed also . Both German and Spanish speakers also pronounce it closer to zed . German zet and Spanish zeta . So if you want to be technical , zed is older so it 's more " correct " . But dialects are very malleable things . As long as you communicate the message , nothing is really wrong . From what I have read , the American pronunciation of z as zee probably came about as a result of rhyming with bee , cee , dee , eee , pee , tee and vee . It makes the alphabet song rhyme at the end . ;)
There 's no really " right " way to pronounce anything . It 's just what 's common for the dialect . Most english dialects say ' zed ' as it derives from the greek letter ' zeta ' . At some point around the late 17th century , Americans , isolated from the rest of the English speaking world , started saying ' zee ' . URL_0 It 's just how language evolves .
rejected:
In the English language, the letter "z" can be pronounced two different ways: "zee" or "zed." The pronunciation "zee" is used in American English, while the pronunciation "zed" is used in British English.

The reason for this difference has to do with the history of the English language. The letter "z" is derived from the Greek letter "zeta," which was pronounced with a "zee" sound. When the Romans adopted the Greek alphabet, they kept the "zee" pronunciation for the letter "z."

However, when the English language developed, it borrowed many words from Latin, which had a different pronunciation for the letter "z." In Latin, the letter "z" was pronounced with a "zed" sound, which is similar to the "dz" sound in the word "adze."

As a result, the letter "z" was pronounced with a "zed" sound in some parts of the English-speaking world, while it was pronounced with a "zee" sound in others.

There is no "right" or "wrong" way to pronounce the letter "z" in English. It simply depends on which English-speaking region you are in. In the United States, the letter "z" is pronounced as "zee," while in the United Kingdom, it is pronounced as "zed."

query: Why is Internet Explorer so bad ? I 've always used Firefox or Chrome just because everyone says they 're faster and that IE totally sucks . But why is IE actually so bad ? Explain like I'm five.
chosen:
Try [ this thread ] ( URL_2 ) . > IE has , historically , not been standards - compliant . Web developers often have to code around IE peculiarities to get their sites to work correctly . And , unfortunately , IE also historically has the largest market share due to it coming pre - installed and default on a majority of the world 's computers . So it 's not something web developers can just easily ignore . > IE has also historically been very insecure - susceptible to things like drive - by downloads and other exploits which can give your computer viruses . > Also , IE6 was the latest version of IE for ... quite some time ( citation needed ) ... and lacked several features of all other modern browsers , most visually tabbed browsing . > Fortunately , IE7/8/9 have been getting progressively better . I 'd say 8 and 9 are actually pretty usable . I 'd say most people are just still sore over IE6 at this point . These two posts are also really good : URL_0 URL_1 > The real issue with Internet Explorer is that old versions stick around forever . The number of people running IE6 ( more than 10 years old ) is mind boggling . That means they are running a browser that does not have the last 10 years of security fixes , ability to handle plugins , show webpages correctly , etc .
Historically , it has had poorer support for html / css standards ( making it painful to develop for ) , and has had a much poorer track record for security vulnerabilities .
Most people will accuse Microsoft of a litany of offenses : resting on their laurels , attempting to dominate and retard the Internet , security woes . Those are deliberate misunderstandings . The truth is a lot simpler , but also a lot more nuanced : Internet Explorer is bundled with Windows . Other browsers are not . The fact that IE is bundled with Windows has a number of implications , many of which have made and continue to make IE less attractive to the " savvy " internet user than other browsers . Anti - trust trial notwithstanding , IE is still considered a component of Windows within Microsoft . Its development is tied directly to Windows ; new releases of the browser closely follow new releases of Windows . Consider the following table : IE Version|Windows version ----:|:---- 1.0|Windows 95 1.5|Windows NT 4 2.0|Windows 95 OSR 1 3.0|Windows 95 OSR 2 4.0|Windows 98 5.0|Windows 98se 5.5|Windows ME 6.0|Windows XP 7.0|Windows XP SP2 8.0|Windows Vista 9.0|Windows 7 10.0|Windows 8 Because IE is tied to the operating system that powers 90+% of the world 's PCs , Microsoft is forced to be a lot more conservative and careful about the features it introduces to IE and the way it is developed . Every move it makes has huge repercussions on the Internet - at - large , for good or ill , whether Microsoft wants it to or not . They know this , and they take their responsibility ( and their power ) very seriously . And everyone else knows it as well . For many , their hatred of IE stems from their envy of what it represents : the power Microsoft has over personal computing . Take , for example , the huge issues that occurred with IE6 and Windows XP in the early 00s . Prior to that point , Microsoft 's sole focus with Windows and IE was to introduce as many new features as quickly as possible . They were competing fiercely with Netscape on the browser side , because they considered Netscape to be the gravest threat to their business . The Internet was just starting to truly become mainstream , but even then no - one really knew what that meant . Everyone was transitioning from Windows 98 , a DOS - based consumer OS to Windows XP which , being based on Windows NT , at least had some intrinsic security features . But Windows XP also had IE , a browser developed originally for Windows 95 , a browser with crazy shit like ActiveX , embedded fonts , a wonky but functional version of JavaScript and so on . Massive security vulnerabilities . And Windows itself had not been built to expect the kinds of threats the Internet would pose , simply because Microsoft did n't know what the threats * were * ; they had n't been conceived yet . Microsoft took a massive PR beating in the early 00s , as vulnerability after vulnerability was ruthlessly exploited in Windows XP and IE 6 , the latter having reached the height of its popularity after its only competition -- Netscape -- basically gave up . So Microsoft retreated . Instead of releasing the next version of Windows ( then codenamed Longhorn ) and with it , the next version of IE , they went to work adding the security features that Windows desperately needed . This delayed the release of Longhorn -- another huge PR problem for Microsoft -- but more importantly , it also delayed the release of IE 7 . The internet - at - large felt that delay . IE6 became entrenched . People got used to it . It had no real competition ; Netscape gave up , Firefox was too immature ( but was popular among the cool kids ) , Safari only ran on Macs and Chrome did n't exist yet . Technologies and standards for websites continued to advance ; CSS 2 got fleshed out , HTML 4 , XHTML 1 , new features in JavaScript . But IE 6 , frozen as it was , did n't support them . It became an anchor , holding everything back , its sheer popularity causing the Web to stagnate . Half a decade later , Web developers and savvy web users still have n't forgiven Microsoft for that . As a web developer , I count myself among them . It remains to be seen how long it will take before we forgive . Maybe we never will . I do n't , but at least I understand . Microsoft is trying mightily to win us back , of course . It largely completed the security retrofit with Windows Vista , which allowed it to focus on adding features again . IE9 represented a massive catch - up effort for IE , bringing it up to support the more stable chunks of HTML 5 and CSS 3 . But the ground it lost in the 00s allowed two new competitors -- Firefox and Chrome -- to step in and beat it to the punch . And they are n't as conservative as Microsoft has to be ; they can rapidly iterate their browsers , introducing new , whiz - bang features at a rapid pace , winning fans and users as they go . While Microsoft is forced to tread slowly and steadily , measuring every move they make , testing every feature they add , knowing that they ca n't afford any more major blunders . rejected: Internet Explorer, or IE, was once a popular web browser made by Microsoft. However, it has since fallen out of favor with most users because it is slower, less secure, and less compatible with modern websites than other browsers like Google Chrome and Mozilla Firefox. One reason for this is that Internet Explorer is no longer updated or supported by Microsoft, so it does not have the latest security features or support for new web technologies. This means that using Internet Explorer can make your computer more vulnerable to security threats and make it more difficult to access certain websites or features. Another reason that Internet Explorer is considered "bad" is that it can be slow and clunky to use. This is because it is an older browser that was not designed with modern computers and websites in mind. As a result, it may not work as smoothly or quickly as newer browsers that have been optimized for the latest web standards. Overall, there are many better alternatives to Internet Explorer that offer faster, more secure, and more feature-rich browsing experiences. If you are still using Internet Explorer, it is a good idea to switch to a different browser to take advantage of these benefits.
